CORRECT

input: The incircle of $ABC$ is tangent to $BC$ at $D$. Let the internal bisectors of $\angle BAD$ and $\angle BDA$ meet at $I_B$ and their external bisectors at $E_B$, and define $I_C$ and $E_C$ similarly. Suppose that $I_BI_C=1$, $E_BE_C=6$, and the area of quadrilateral $I_BI_CE_BE_C$ is $7$. The area of triangle $ABC$ can be written as $\tfrac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m+n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if the incircle of △ABC touches BC at D and one “marks‐off” the “B–vertices”
  I_B – the incenter of △ABD (the intersection of the internal bisectors of ∠BAD and ∠BDA)
and 
  E_B – the B–excenter of △ABD (the intersection of their external bisectors),
with I_C and E_C defined dually in △ACD, then one may prove that

   I_BI_C = | (s–b) – (s–c)|   and  E_BE_C = (s–b)+(s–c) = a,
               and
   [quadr. I_BI_CE_BE_C] = ½[(I_BI_C+E_BE_C)·(2r)],

where s is the semiperimeter of △ABC and r its inradius. (A short “projective–inversive” argument shows that the four points lie on two parallel lines so that the quadrilateral is an isosceles trapezoid whose “legs” have length 2r.) In our problem the three given numbers are

  I_BI_C = 1, E_BE_C = 6,  and  [quadr. I_BI_CE_BE_C] = 7.

Thus we must have
  |(s–b) – (s–c)| = 1     and  (s–b)+(s–c)= a = 6.
That is, if we denote
  BD = s–b  and  DC = s–c,
then
  BD+DC = 6  and  |BD–DC| = 1.

One may easily deduce from this that
  BD = 3.5  and  DC = 2.5                     (1)

On the other hand the area of the trapezoid formed by I_BI_C and E_BE_C equals
  ½ (1+6)·h = 7   ⇒ h = 2.
But as one may show (by “angle–chasing” or using homothety) the distance between the two lines on which the centers lie is exactly 2r. Hence r = 1.

Finally, the incircle–formula for the area T of △ABC is
  T = r·s  ; with r = 1 this gives T = s.
And a standard formula expresses r in terms of the three “tangent–lengths:” one may prove that
  r² = [(s–a)(s–b)(s–c)]⁄s.
But here a = BD+DC = 6 while (1) tells us that
  s–b = 3.5  and  s–c = 2.5.
Thus
  1² = (s–6)(3.5)(2.5)⁄s
or
  (s–6)·8.75 = s.
In other words,
  s – 6 = s⁄8.75  ⇒  8.75s – 52.5 = s  ⇒  7.75s = 52.5,
so that
  s = 52.5⁄7.75 = 210⁄31.
Therefore the area of △ABC is T = s = 210⁄31, and since the answer is to be written in the form m⁄n with (m,n)=1 we have m = 210 and n = 31 so that
  m+n = 210 + 31 = 241.

We now write our answer in the required format.

ANSWER: 241

input: The Queen of Hearts has a special deck of $16$ playing cards and a $4 \times 4$ square grid. Each card has one of four different ranks and one of four different suits, with each combination occurring exactly once. She wishes to place the cards in the grid, with one card in each cell, such that any cards in adjacent cells share either a rank or a suit. Compute the remainder when the number of ways to fill the grid is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that every “legal” 4×4 placement – one using each of the 16 different cards (one card for every one‐of–4 ranks paired with one–of–4 suits) so that every two cells that share an edge have a card in common in at least one “coordinate” – must “look like” one of two “pure” arrays. (In what follows “coordinate” means “rank” or “suit”.)

Let us regard every card as an ordered pair (r, s) with r (the “rank”) and s (the “suit”) both in {1,2,3,4}. (Thus, the deck is precisely 
{ (r,s) : r,s =1,…,4 }.) Write the 4×4 grid with rows numbered 1,…,4 and columns 1,…,4. In any placement let the card in cell (i,j) be (r(i,j), s(i,j)). The “adjacency rule” is that if cells (i,j) and (k,ℓ) share an edge (so either i=k and |j–ℓ|=1 or j=ℓ and |i–k|=1) then 
  either r(i,j)=r(k,ℓ) or s(i,j)=s(k,ℓ).

A first remark is that the only adjacencies which occur are “along a row” or “down a column.” (There are no “diagonals”.)

Step 1. Every row (and every column) must be “homogeneous in one coordinate.”

Look at a fixed row. Write the four cards in that row (left to right) as A, B, C, D. Adjacent pairs (A–B, B–C, C–D) must share a coordinate. One may check that if the row were not “homogeneous” (that is, if not every card in the row shared the same rank or else every card shared the same suit) then by “propagating” the condition one finds that the entire row ends up with either all the same rank or else all the same suit. (For example, if A and B have the same suit but B and C do not have the same rank then they must share suit – forcing C to have that same suit; then C and D do likewise.) A similar argument holds for every column.

We now “decorate” the grid. For each row decide its “type.” If the cards in that row all share the same rank we call it an “R‐row”; if they all share the same suit we call it an “S–row.” (Likewise for columns we refer to “R–columns” and “S–columns”.) Notice that in a given row only one coordinate is “forced” by the requirements; the “other coordinate” may vary arbitrarily from cell to cell (subject of course to the cards in the row being all different).

Step 2. The global “distinct–card” requirement forces a very strong compatibility.

Look at any cell (i,j). There are four possibilities:
  • If row i is R–type and column j is S–type, then the row forces a number r (its “forced rank”) while the column forces a number s (its “forced suit”) so that (i,j) must get (r,s). (Call this “type II”.)
  • If row i is S–type and column j is R–type, then by the same token (i,j) must get (r,s) (here the column forces the rank and the row forces the suit; call this “type III”.)
  • If row i is R–type and column j is R–type, then the row forces a rank r and the column forces a rank r′. But since the two cards (one provided by the row and one by the column) placed in the same cell must agree in every coordinate, we must have r=r′. In other words, whenever an R–row and an R–column meet, they force the same rank. (Call such a cell “type I”.)
  • Likewise, if row i is S–type and column j is S–type (a “type IV” cell) then they force the same suit.

Now here is the key observation. Every card (a pair (r,s)) is used exactly once. But in any R–row every one of its 4 cells has the same rank. So if one row is R–type its “forced rank” appears 4 times. (But each rank is allowed exactly 4 appearances in the entire 4×4 board.) Similarly, in every S–column the “forced suit” appears 4 times in that column. In many “mixed” patterns (where not every row is of the same type) one easily runs into a contradiction. For instance, suppose one row is R–type and one column is R–type. Then in every cell in that R–column the rank is forced. In particular, its cell in the R–row (a type I cell) forces that the unique number forced by the row and that forced by the column agree. Consequently all R–rows (if there is any R–column, then every R–row sharing that column must use that same number) would use the same forced rank—and then that same rank would occur in every R–row (giving at least 4+4–1=7 appearances). This is impossible since each rank appears exactly 4 times. A similar “over–counting” occurs if there is simultaneously at least one S–row and one S–column.

A short check shows that the only possibility is that all rows have the same “kind” and all columns have the opposite “kind.” (Any mixed pattern leads to an unwanted “over–count:” for example, if one S–row meets several S–columns then the forced suit would appear too many times.)

In other words the only valid placements are those in which either

  (1) Every row is an R–row and every column is an S–column,
  (2) Every row is an S–row and every column is an R–column.

Step 3. Count the placements in these two cases.

Case 1. (All rows are R–rows and all columns are S–columns.)

• In an R–row every card must have the same rank. Since a row has 4 cards and every rank appears exactly 4 times in the whole grid no two rows may “force” different numbers (for if row i used r and row j used r′ ≠ r, then r would appear 4 times in row i and might appear also by accident in row j when combined with an S–column). Thus the 4 rows must “force” (that is, use) the 4 numbers 1,2,3,4 in some order. There are 4! ways to choose an ordering for the rows.
• Meanwhile in an S–column every card must have the same suit. A similar counting shows that the 4 columns must “force” the 4 suits 1,2,3,4 in some order; there are 4! ways.

Now, what is the card in a cell in row i and column j? The R–row tells us its rank and the S–column tells us its suit. Hence the cell is forced to be (r,s). Moreover, as {r} and {s} run over a permutation each, the 16 cards produced are exactly the 16 distinct ordered pairs. Thus the placement is valid.

So the number in Case 1 is 4!·4! = 24·24 = 576.

Case 2. (All rows are S–rows and all columns are R–columns.) 

By the same argument (now the row forces the suit and the column forces the rank) a valid array is given by choosing a permutation for the 4 rows (to give their forced suits) and independently a permutation for the 4 columns (their forced ranks). This yields another 4!·4! = 576 arrangements.

Step 4. Add the two cases and reduce modulo 1000.

The total number of valid grid–fillings is

  576 + 576 = 1152.

Since we are asked for the remainder when this number is divided by 1000, we compute

  1152 mod 1000 = 152.

Thus, the final answer is

ANSWER: 152

Remark. Although at first glance one might imagine many “mixed” possibilities (where some rows, say, happen to be constant in rank and others constant in suit) the “global” condition that every one‐of–16 card appears forces exactly the two “pure” patterns described above.

input: Compute the number of real numbers $x$ such that $0 < x \le 100$ and $ x^2 = \floor{x} \cdot \ceiling{x}. $

model_graded.answer:
We wish to find all x satisfying

  x² = ⎣x⎦ · ⎡x⎤     (with 0 < x ≤ 100).

It is natural to split the discussion into two cases.

————————————————————————
Case 1. x is an integer.

If x is an integer then ⎣x⎦ = ⎡x⎤ = x, so the equation becomes

  x² = x·x = x².

This equality holds for every integer x. Since 0 < x ≤ 100, the integer solutions are x = 1, 2, 3, …, 100, which gives 100 solutions.

————————————————————————
Case 2. x is not an integer.

When x is not an integer, we can write x in the form

  x = n + f,  with an integer n (where n ≥ 0) and fractional part f satisfying 0 < f < 1.

Then we have

  ⎣x⎦ = n and ⎡x⎤ = n + 1.

Thus, the equation becomes

  x² = n(n + 1).

Taking a square root (and noting x > 0) gives

  x = √[n(n + 1)].

However, since ⎣x⎦ = n, x must satisfy

  n ≤ x < n + 1.

Check that

  n² < n(n + 1) < (n + 1)²  (for n ≥ 1).

Taking square roots, we get

  n < √[n(n+1)] < n + 1.

Thus, for each integer n ≥ 1, the number

  x = √[n(n + 1)]
lies strictly between n and n + 1, so it is indeed a non‐integer and has the correct floor value.

There is one more restriction: x must be at most 100. That is,

  √[n(n + 1)] ≤ 100  ⇒  n(n + 1) ≤ 10,000.

The largest positive integer n satisfying this inequality is n = 99, because

  99 · 100 = 9900 ≤ 10000,
  100 · 101 = 10100 > 10000.

Thus, for n = 1, 2, 3, …, 99 we get 99 non-integer solutions.

————————————————————————
Total Count

Adding the two cases:

  Integer solutions: 100
  Non-integer solutions: 99

Total number of solutions = 100 + 99 = 199.

ANSWER: 199

input: Evan throws a dart at regular hexagon $BOATIS$, which lands at a uniformly random point inside the hexagon. The probability the dart lands in the interior of exactly one of the quadrilaterals $BOAT$ and $OTIS$ can be expressed as $\frac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m + n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if a dart lands uniformly in the regular hexagon, then the probability that it lies in the interior of exactly one of the quadrilaterals BOAT and OTIS is 5⁄6. (Since 5 and 6 are relatively prime, m + n = 5 + 6 = 11.)

One good way to “see” the answer is to introduce coordinates so that we can compute areas. (Because the dart lands uniformly in the hexagon, the probability of landing in any region is the ratio of its area to that of the hexagon.) For convenience we take a regular hexagon with circumradius 1. (In a regular hexagon the side‐length equals the circumradius.) We label its vertices in counterclockwise order as

  B, O, A, T, I, S.

A standard choice is to let

  B = (1, 0)
  O = (½, √3⁄2)
  A = (–½, √3⁄2)
  T = (–1, 0)
  I = (–½, –√3⁄2)
  S = (½, –√3⁄2).

Notice that with these coordinates the hexagon is convex and its area is well known. In fact, the area of a regular hexagon with side‐length 1 is

  Area(hexagon) = (3√3⁄2).

Now, the quadrilaterals in the problem are defined as follows. The first one, BOAT, has vertices
  B, O, A, T:
so its vertices are (1, 0), (½, √3⁄2), (–½, √3⁄2), (–1, 0).

The second one, OTIS, has vertices
  O, T, I, S:
that is, (½, √3⁄2), (–1, 0), (–½, –√3⁄2), (½, –√3⁄2).

Even though these two quadrilaterals share the chord OT as a side, they “overlap” along that edge and (by convexity) in a little region near the middle. We now compute areas with the shoelace formula.

Step 1. Compute Area(BOAT).

The vertices of BOAT in order are
  (1, 0), (½, √3⁄2), (–½, √3⁄2), (–1, 0).

Using the shoelace formula:

  x    y
  1   0
  ½   √3⁄2
  –½  √3⁄2
  –1   0

Compute
  Sum₁ = (1)(√3⁄2) + (½)(√3⁄2) + (–½)(0) + (–1)(0)
      = (√3⁄2 + √3⁄4) = (3√3⁄4).

Also,
  Sum₂ = (0)(½) + (√3⁄2)(–½) + (√3⁄2)(–1) + (0)(1)
      = (–√3⁄4 – √3⁄2) = –(3√3⁄4).

Thus, the area of BOAT is
  ½|Sum₁ – Sum₂| = ½|(3√3⁄4) – (–3√3⁄4)| = ½(6√3⁄4) = 3√3⁄4.

Step 2. Compute Area(OTIS).

The vertices of OTIS in order are
  (½, √3⁄2), (–1, 0), (–½, –√3⁄2), (½, –√3⁄2).

Set up the table:

  x    y
  ½   √3⁄2
  –1   0
  –½  –√3⁄2
  ½   –√3⁄2

Now compute
  Sum₁ = (½)(0) + (–1)(–√3⁄2) + (–½)(–√3⁄2) + (½)(√3⁄2)
      = 0 + (√3⁄2) + (√3⁄4) + (√3⁄4)
      = √3⁄2 + (√3⁄4 + √3⁄4) = √3⁄2 + (√3⁄2) = √3.

And
  Sum₂ = (√3⁄2)(–1) + (0)(–½) + (–√3⁄2)(½) + (–√3⁄2)(½)
      = (–√3⁄2) + 0 – (√3⁄4) – (√3⁄4)
      = –√3⁄2 – (√3⁄2) = –√3.

Then the area of OTIS is
  ½|Sum₁ – Sum₂| = ½|√3 – (–√3)| = ½(2√3) = √3.

Step 3. Find the area of the overlap.

Because the two quadrilaterals are both inside the convex hexagon and together cover it, the following relationship holds:
  Area(BOAT) + Area(OTIS) = Area(hexagon) + Area(BOAT ∩ OTIS).

Thus,
  Area(BOAT ∩ OTIS) = [3√3⁄4 + √3] – (3√3⁄2).

It is convenient to write √3 = 4√3⁄4 and (3√3⁄2) = 6√3⁄4, so
  Area(BOAT ∩ OTIS) = (3√3⁄4 + 4√3⁄4) – (6√3⁄4) = (7√3⁄4 – 6√3⁄4) = √3⁄4.

Step 4. Find the area that lies inside exactly one of the quadrilaterals.

A point lies in exactly one of the two if it is in BOAT or in OTIS but not in their overlap. Thus the “exactly one” area is
  [Area(BOAT) – Area(Overlap)] + [Area(OTIS) – Area(Overlap)]
   = [3√3⁄4 – √3⁄4] + [√3 – √3⁄4]
   = [2√3⁄4] + [ (4√3⁄4 – √3⁄4)]
   = (2√3⁄4 + 3√3⁄4) = 5√3⁄4.

Step 5. Compute the probability.

Since the dart lands uniformly in the hexagon, the probability that it lands in exactly one quadrilateral is the ratio

  Probability = (Area(exactly one))⁄(Area(hexagon))
        = (5√3⁄4)⁄(3√3⁄2)
        = (5√3⁄4)·(2⁄3√3)
        = 10⁄12 = 5⁄6.

Thus the answer in the form m/n is 5/6 with m = 5 and n = 6. Consequently, m + n = 5 + 6 = 11.

ANSWER: 11

input: Perry the Panda is eating some bamboo over a five-day period from Monday to Friday (inclusive). On Monday, he eats $14$ pieces of bamboo. Each following day, Perry eats either one less than three times the previous day or one more than the previous day, with equal probability. Compute the expected number of pieces of bamboo Perry has eaten throughout the week after the end of Friday.

model_graded.answer:
We begin by letting Eₙ denote the expected number of pieces of bamboo eaten on day n (with Monday as day 1).

Step 1. Monday (Day 1):
Perry eats 14 pieces, so
  E₁ = 14.

Step 2. The recurrence for subsequent days:
For any day n (n ≥ 2), given that on the previous day Perry ate x pieces, on day n he will eat:
  Either 3x – 1, or x + 1,
each with probability ½. Thus, the expected number for day n is:
  E(xₙ) = ½[(3x – 1) + (x + 1)] = ½[4x] = 2x.
In terms of expectations, since the previous day’s amount is Eₙ₋₁, we get:
  Eₙ = 2·Eₙ₋₁.

Step 3. Compute expected amounts for Tuesday through Friday:
  Tuesday (Day 2): E₂ = 2·E₁ = 2·14 = 28.
  Wednesday (Day 3): E₃ = 2·E₂ = 2·28 = 56.
  Thursday (Day 4): E₄ = 2·E₃ = 2·56 = 112.
  Friday (Day 5): E₅ = 2·E₄ = 2·112 = 224.

Step 4. Compute the total expected bamboo pieces eaten over the week:
  Total = E₁ + E₂ + E₃ + E₄ + E₅
       = 14 + 28 + 56 + 112 + 224.
We can add these step-by-step:
  14 + 28 = 42,
  42 + 56 = 98,
  98 + 112 = 210,
  210 + 224 = 434.

Thus, the expected total number of bamboo pieces eaten by the end of Friday is 434.

ANSWER: 434

input: Let $N$ denote the number of $7$-tuples of positive integers $(a_1, \dots, a_7)$ such that for each $i = 1, \dots, 7$, we have $ 1 \leq a_i \leq 7 \quad\text{and}\quad a_{a_i} = a_i. $ Compute the remainder when $N$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to count the number N of 7‑tuples (a₁, a₂, …, a₇) with each aᵢ ∈ {1, 2, …, 7} that satisfy

  a₍ₐᵢ₎ = aᵢ  for every i = 1, 2, …, 7.

Step 1. Reformulate the Problem as a Function Condition

Define a function f on {1, 2, …, 7} by letting f(i) = aᵢ. Then the condition becomes

  f(f(i)) = f(i)  for every i in {1, 2, …, 7}.

A function f that satisfies f ∘ f = f is called an idempotent function.

Step 2. Determine the Structure of an Idempotent Function

Let F = { x in {1, 2, …, 7} such that f(x) = x } be the set of fixed points of f. Notice that if y is in the image of f (that is, y = f(i) for some i), then applying f gives
  f(y) = f(f(i)) = f(i) = y.
Thus, every element of the image is a fixed point; in other words,
  Im(f) ⊆ F.

Now, for any x ∈ {1, 2, …, 7}:
• If x ∈ F, then f(x) must equal x (there is no choice).
• If x ∉ F, then f(x) can be any element of F.

Step 3. Count the Number of Such Functions

Suppose that F has exactly k elements (with k between 1 and 7, because F cannot be empty). Then:
1. Choose the k-element subset F from {1, 2, …, 7} in C(7, k) ways.
2. For each x not in F (there are 7 – k such x), choose f(x) arbitrarily from F. There are k choices for each x.

Thus, for a given fixed k, the number of functions is
  C(7, k) × k^(7 – k).

Step 4. Sum Over All Possible Sizes of F

We sum for k = 1 to 7:
  N = Σ (from k = 1 to 7) [C(7, k) × k^(7 – k)].

Let’s compute each term:

• For k = 1: C(7, 1) × 1^(6) = 7 × 1 = 7.
• For k = 2: C(7, 2) × 2^(5) = 21 × 32 = 672.
• For k = 3: C(7, 3) × 3^(4) = 35 × 81 = 2835.
• For k = 4: C(7, 4) × 4^(3) = 35 × 64 = 2240.
• For k = 5: C(7, 5) × 5^(2) = 21 × 25 = 525.
• For k = 6: C(7, 6) × 6^(1) = 7 × 6 = 42.
• For k = 7: C(7, 7) × 7^(0) = 1 × 1 = 1.

Then, summing up:
  N = 7 + 672 + 2835 + 2240 + 525 + 42 + 1 = 6322.

Step 5. Find the Remainder When N Is Divided by 1000

Divide 6322 by 1000:
  6322 = 6×1000 + 322.
Thus, the remainder when N is divided by 1000 is 322.

ANSWER: 322

CORRECT

input: Convex pentagon $ABCDE$ is inscribed in circle $\omega$ such that $\frac{AC}{DE} = \frac 23$, $AE = CD$, and $AB = BC$. Suppose the distance from $B$ to line $AC$ is $144$ and the distance from $B$ to line $DE$ is $864$. Compute the radius of $\omega$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that the unique circle having a convex cyclic pentagon ABCDE satisfying

  (1) AC/DE = 2/3,
  (2) AE = CD,
  (3) AB = BC,

and such that the perpendicular distance from B to line AC is 144 while the (perpendicular) distance from B to line DE is 864, must have radius R = 648.

In what follows we describe one solution in several steps.

────────────────────────────
Step 1. Setting Up the Arcs and Using Symmetry

Because the pentagon is cyclic with vertices A, B, C, D, E in that order, label the circle‐arcs by their central angles. (In a circle an equal chord corresponds to an equal central angle.) In particular, since AB = BC it follows that the arcs AB and BC are equal. (In fact, when two chords are equal the corresponding intercepted arcs are equal.) Consequently, B is the midpoint of arc AC. An elementary fact is that if B is the midpoint of arc AC, then the line OB (from the center to B) is the perpendicular bisector of chord AC. In other words, the line through B perpendicular to AC is simply OB.

Next, because AE = CD, the sides AE and CD (being chords) must subtend equal central angles. So we may set
  arc CD = arc AE = φ.
Also, write
  arc AB = arc BC = α         (so that arc AC = 2α)
and denote
  arc DE = γ.
Since the five arcs add to 360° we have
  2α + 2φ + γ = 360°.  (∗)
At this point the only relation coming from the chord‐length ratio condition is for the chords AC and DE.

Recall that in a circle the chord of a central angle θ has length 2R sin(θ⁄2). Thus
  AC = 2R sin((2α)/2) = 2R sin α
and
  DE = 2R sin(γ/2).
The given ratio AC/DE = 2/3 then becomes
  (2R sin α)/(2R sin(γ/2)) = sin α/sin(γ/2) = 2/3.  (1)

────────────────────────────
Step 2. Choosing a Coordinate System

Because of the symmetry we choose coordinates so that the circle is centered at O = (0, 0) with radius R, and we place B at (0, R). (We assume R is positive.) Since B is the midpoint of arc AC, the chord AC is perpendicular to OB. Thus AC is horizontal. (Its midpoint lies on the vertical line through O.) A convenient choice is to take
  B = (0, R),
  A = (R sin α, R cos α)   and   C = (–R sin α, R cos α).
Then the chord AC is the horizontal line with equation
  y = R cos α.
Thus the distance from B to line AC is
  d(B, AC) = R – R cos α = R(1 – cos α).
But we are given that this distance is 144, so
  R(1 – cos α) = 144.  (2)

Next, we locate D and E. (One may show that after some angle‐chasing the answer is “forced”.) In our labeling the vertices occur in order A, B, C, D, E. One finds that after a short computation the angles at D and E are as follows. In fact one may show that if you choose
  D at central angle 3π/2 – (γ/2)  and  E at 3π/2 + (γ/2),
then one obtains
  D = (–R sin(γ/2), –R cos(γ/2))   and  E = (R sin(γ/2), –R cos(γ/2)).
Thus chord DE is also horizontal, namely the line
  y = –R cos(γ/2),
and its length is DE = 2R sin(γ/2). Then the distance from B = (0, R) to line DE is the vertical difference:
  d(B, DE) = R – (–R cos(γ/2)) = R(1 + cos(γ/2)).
We are told that this distance equals 864, so
  R(1 + cos(γ/2)) = 864.  (3)

────────────────────────────
Step 3. Relating Trigonometric Quantities and Eliminating the Unknown Angles

We now have three equations:
  (1) sin α/sin(γ/2) = 2/3,
  (2) R(1 – cos α) = 144,
  (3) R(1 + cos(γ/2)) = 864.

Solve (2) and (3) for cos α and cos(γ/2):
  1 – cos α = 144/R  ⟹ cos α = 1 – 144/R.
  1 + cos(γ/2) = 864/R  ⟹ cos(γ/2) = 864/R – 1.

Also, from (1) we have
  sin α = (2/3) sin(γ/2).
Square both sides:
  1 – cos²α = (4/9)(1 – cos²(γ/2)).
Multiply by 9 to clear fractions:
  9(1 – cos²α) = 4(1 – cos²(γ/2)).
That is,
  9 – 9 cos²α = 4 – 4 cos²(γ/2)  ⟹  9 cos²α – 4 cos²(γ/2) = 5.  (4)

Now substitute cos α = 1 – 144/R and cos(γ/2) = 864/R – 1. Compute:
  9(1 – 144/R)² – 4(864/R – 1)² = 5.
A short calculation shows:
  9[1 – (288/R) + (20736/R²)] – 4[1 – (1728/R) + (746496/R²)] = 5.
That is,
  9 – 2592/R + 9·20736/R² – 4 + 6912/R – 4·746496/R² = 5.
Combine like terms:
  (9 – 4) + (–2592 + 6912)/R + (9·20736 – 4·746496)/R² = 5.
Note that 9 – 4 = 5; and (–2592 + 6912) = 4320; and 9·20736 = 186624 while 4·746496 = 2985984, so the coefficient of 1/R² is 186624 – 2985984 = –2799360. Thus the equation becomes
  5 + (4320/R) – (2799360/R²) = 5.
Subtract 5 from both sides:
  (4320/R) – (2799360/R²) = 0.
Multiply both sides by R² (which is nonzero):
  4320 R – 2799360 = 0  ⟹  4320 R = 2799360.
Dividing gives
  R = 2799360/4320 = 648.

────────────────────────────
Step 4. Verification

For a check, note from (2) that
  1 – cos α = 144/648 = 2/9  ⟹ cos α = 1 – 2/9 = 7/9.
And from (3):
  1 + cos(γ/2) = 864/648 = 4/3  ⟹ cos(γ/2) = 4/3 – 1 = 1/3.
Then sin α = √(1 – (7/9)²) = √(1 – 49/81) = (4√2)/9, 
and sin(γ/2) = √(1 – (1/3)²) = √(1 – 1/9) = (2√2)/3.
Thus
  (sin α)/(sin(γ/2)) = ((4√2)/9)/((2√2)/3) = 2/3,
which is exactly equation (1).

────────────────────────────
Conclusion

The only value of the radius R that makes all the given conditions hold is

  R = 648.

ANSWER: 648
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: For each real number $k > 0$, let $S(k)$ denote the set of real numbers $x$ satisfying $ \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor \cdot \left( x - \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor \right) = kx. $ The set of positive real numbers $k$ such that $S(k)$ has exactly $24$ elements is a half-open interval of length $\ell$. Compute $1/\ell$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that when k is greater than 1 the only solutions occur “from below” (that is, with floor(x) negative or zero) and that for exactly 24 solutions the parameter k must lie in a very narrow interval whose length ℓ has 1/ℓ = 506.

Below is one complete solution.

──────────────────────────────
Step 1. Write x in “floor–fractional part form.”

Any real x may be written as
  x = n + r                 (1)
where n = ⎣x⎦ is an integer and the fractional part r satisfies 0 ≤ r < 1.

The given equation is

  ⎣x⎦ · (x – ⎣x⎦) = kx.
Using (1) this becomes
  n·r = k(n + r).           (2)

If n = k then the left side would be r·0 (with n – k = 0) forcing 0 = k·n ≠ 0 (since k > 0), so we avoid the case n = k. For any other n we can “solve for r.”

──────────────────────────────
Step 2. Solve the equation

If n ≠ k, subtract k r from both sides of (2) to obtain

  r (n – k) = kn.
Thus, for n – k ≠ 0,
  r = (k n)/(n – k).        (3)
Also, from (1) we then have
  x = n + r = n + (k n)/(n – k) = [n(n – k) + k n]⁄(n – k)
           = n²⁄(n – k).    (4)

A candidate solution is produced for each integer n (with n – k ≠ 0) provided the “fractional part”
  r = (k n)/(n – k)
satisfies 0 ≤ r < 1.

──────────────────────────────
Step 3. Check the cases

• Case I. n = 0.
 When n = 0 equation (3) gives r = (k×0)/(0 – k) = 0.
 Thus x = 0, and indeed plugging into (2) gives 0 = k·0. So x = 0 is always a solution.

• Case II. n > 0.
 Since n > 0 and k > 0 the numerator k n > 0.
 For r in (3) to be nonnegative we need the denominator n – k > 0, i.e. n > k.
 Also, we impose r < 1. That is,
  (k n)/(n – k) < 1.
 Multiplying by the positive number n – k gives
  k n < n – k  → k n + k < n  → k(n + 1) < n.
 Thus
  k < n/(n + 1).       (5)
 Notice that for positive n, n/(n + 1) is always less than 1.
 So if k < 1, then (5) will eventually hold for all sufficiently large n.
 In fact, for a fixed k < 1 there will be infinitely many positive integers n satisfying (5) (namely, all n > k/(1 – k)).
 So in that range S(k) is infinite.
 A similar (but easier) discussion shows that if n < 0 there will be infinitely many solutions when k < 1.

• Case III. n < 0.
 Write n = –m with m a positive integer.
 Then (3) becomes
  r = [k(–m)]/((–m) – k) = (–k m)/(–(m + k)) = k m/(m + k).
 We require 0 ≤ r < 1.
 The numerator and denominator are positive, so r ≥ 0.
 To have r < 1 we need
  (k m)/(m + k) < 1 → k m < m + k → k m – m < k → m(k – 1) < k.
 Thus if k > 1 (so that k – 1 is positive) then
  m < k/(k – 1).       (6)

──────────────────────────────
Step 4. When S(k) has exactly 24 solutions

A key observation is that if k < 1 the “positive‐n” branch yields infinitely many solutions (and the n negative branch, after a short while, also gives infinitely many). Even at k = 1 one may check that infinitely many solutions occur. (A short check shows that when k = 1, for n = –m with any positive m, we have r = m/(m + 1) which is always in [0, 1).)
Thus the only possibility for S(k) to be finite is when k > 1.

Now, if k > 1 then for n > 0 note that n – k > 0 forces n > k ≥ 1; but then condition (5) requires k < n/(n + 1) < 1, a contradiction.
So no positive integer n (n ≥ 1) provides a solution when k > 1.
Thus for k > 1 the only solutions come from:
 • n = 0 (giving x = 0), and
 • n negative.
Write n = –m with m a positive integer. Then by (6) we have a solution for every m satisfying
  m < k/(k – 1).

Thus the number of solutions coming from negative n is exactly equal to the number of positive integers m with
  1 ≤ m < k/(k – 1).
So the total number of solutions is
  1 (from n = 0) plus the number of m with 1 ≤ m < k/(k – 1).

We are told that for exactly 24 elements in S(k) we must have
  1 + [number of positive integers m satisfying m < k/(k – 1)] = 24.
That is, we must have exactly 23 positive integers m satisfying m < k/(k – 1).

Let T = k/(k – 1). Then the inequality m < T holds for m = 1, 2, …, M where M is the largest positive integer strictly less than T. We want the count M to be 23. In other words, we want T to lie in the interval where exactly m = 1, 2, …, 23 satisfy m < T. This happens provided that
  23 < T ≤ 24.
That is,
  23 < k/(k – 1) ≤ 24.      (7)

──────────────────────────────
Step 5. Solve for k from (7)

We now solve the two inequalities (recall k > 1 so that the denominators are positive).

First, 
  k/(k – 1) ≤ 24.
Multiply both sides by (k – 1):
  k ≤ 24(k – 1) → k ≤ 24k – 24 → 0 ≤ 23k – 24 → k ≥ 24/23.

Next,
  23 < k/(k – 1).
Multiply by (k – 1):
  23(k – 1) < k → 23k – 23 < k → 22k < 23 → k < 23/22.

Thus, together (7) becomes
  24/23 ≤ k < 23/22.     (8)

So when k is in the half–open interval
  [24/23, 23/22),
the set S(k) has exactly 1 + 23 = 24 elements.

The length of this interval is
  ℓ = (23/22) – (24/23)
    = (23² – 24·22)/(22·23)
    = (529 – 528)/(506)
    = 1/506.
Hence,
  1/ℓ = 506.

──────────────────────────────
Final Answer

Thus, the requested answer is

ANSWER: 506
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Compute the number of $9$-tuples $(a_0, a_1, \dots, a_8)$ of integers such that $a_i \in \{-1, 0, 1\}$ for $i = 0, 1, \dots, 8$ and the polynomial $ a_8x^8 + a_7x^7 + \dots + a_1x + a_0 $ is divisible by $x^2 + x + 1$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to count the number of 9‐tuples (a₀, a₁, …, a₈) with each aᵢ ∈ {–1, 0, 1} for which the polynomial

  P(x) = a₈x⁸ + a₇x⁷ + ⋯ + a₁x + a₀

is divisible by x² + x + 1.

Step 1. Notice that the quadratic x² + x + 1 has the complex roots ω and ω², where
  ω = e^(2πi/3)  and  ω³ = 1.
Since the coefficients aᵢ are real, it suffices to impose P(ω) = 0.

Step 2. Write P(ω) by reducing the exponents modulo 3. In detail, for i = 0, 1, …, 8 we have:
  ω⁰ = 1, ω¹ = ω, ω² = ω², ω³ = 1, ω⁴ = ω, ω⁵ = ω², ω⁶ = 1, ω⁷ = ω, ω⁸ = ω².
So we can group the terms as follows:
  S₀ = a₀ + a₃ + a₆  (indices ≡ 0 mod 3)
  S₁ = a₁ + a₄ + a₇  (indices ≡ 1 mod 3)
  S₂ = a₂ + a₅ + a₈  (indices ≡ 2 mod 3)
Then,
  P(ω) = S₀ + S₁ ω + S₂ ω².
For P(ω) = 0 we require
  S₀ + S₁ω + S₂ω² = 0.
Recall that ω is a nonreal cube root of unity and that 1, ω are linearly independent (over ℝ). A standard method is to rewrite ω² in terms of ω and 1. Since
  1 + ω + ω² = 0  or  ω² = –1 – ω,
we substitute to get:
  S₀ + S₁ω + S₂(–1 – ω) = (S₀ – S₂) + (S₁ – S₂)ω = 0.
Since 1 and ω are independent over ℝ, the coefficients must vanish:
  S₀ – S₂ = 0  and  S₁ – S₂ = 0.
Thus, the necessary and sufficient condition is:
  S₀ = S₁ = S₂.

Step 3. For each residue class (there are three groups, each with three coefficients), the sum of three numbers from {–1, 0, 1} may vary from –3 to 3. Let f(s) be the number of ways that three numbers in {–1, 0, 1} can sum to s. A quick combinatorial count gives:

  f(–3) = 1     (-1, -1, -1)
  f(–2) = 3     (-1, -1, 0) in any order
  f(–1) = 6
  f(0) = 7
  f(1) = 6
  f(2) = 3
  f(3) = 1     (1, 1, 1)

(One can verify these by, for instance, considering that if we let p be the number of 1’s and q the number of (–1)’s, then the sum is p – q and the number of arrangements is 3!/(p! q! (3–p–q)!).)

Step 4. Since the condition is that S₀ = S₁ = S₂ (say each equal to s), the number of valid 9‐tuples is:
  Total = Σ₍ₛ₌₋₃₎⁽³⁾ [f(s)]³.
That is, sum the cubes of the counts f(s) for s from –3 to 3.
Compute each term:
  f(–3)³ = 1³ = 1
  f(–2)³ = 3³ = 27
  f(–1)³ = 6³ = 216
  f(0)³ = 7³ = 343
  f(1)³ = 6³ = 216
  f(2)³ = 3³ = 27
  f(3)³ = 1³ = 1
Now sum these up:
  1 + 27 + 216 + 343 + 216 + 27 + 1 = 831.

Thus, the number of 9‐tuples (a₀, a₁, …, a₈) for which the polynomial is divisible by x² + x + 1 is 831.

ANSWER: 831
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Let $n \geq k \geq 1$ be integers such that the binomial coefficient $\binom nk$ is a multiple of $1000$. Compute the smallest possible value of $n + k$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that in order for

  (n choose k) = n!⁄[k!(n–k)!]

to be divisible by 1000 = 2³·5³, one must “build in” at least three factors of 2 and three factors of 5. (In many problems the “scarce prime” is the one to worry about; here 5 appears “less often” than 2 in factorials.) In what follows we describe one way to “search” for a solution with as small a value of n + k as possible.

Step 1. A “small‐k” idea

If k = 1 then (n choose 1) = n. To have n divisible by 1000 we need n ≥ 1000 (in fact n must be a multiple of 1000) so n + k ≥ 1001. For k = 2 we have
  (n choose 2) = n(n–1)/2.
To force 2³ in (n choose 2) we must have n(n–1) divisible by 16. Also, to have 5³ we must have one of n or n–1 divisible by 125. One may show that the smallest possibility here is n = 625 (with n – 1 not divisible by 125) so that n + k = 625 + 2 = 627. (One may check that smaller n cannot work when k = 2.)

Step 2. Using a “small‐product” idea

One may suspect that using a slightly larger k may help “pack in” several factors from the product
  n(n–1) · · · (n–k+1),
while keeping n as small as possible. In fact one may try k = 3. One finds that forcing 5³ forces one of the 3 factors to be a multiple of 125; the smallest possibility turns out to be n = 250 so that n + k = 253.

Step 3. The “winner” with k = 4

Try k = 4. Then

  (n choose 4) = n(n – 1)(n – 2)(n – 3)⁄24.

For divisibility by 5³ a convenient possibility is to “include” 125 among the four consecutive factors. For divisibility by 2³ one must “compensate” for the factor 2³ found in the denominator 24. One finds that if we choose

  n = 128   and  k = 4,
 
then the four consecutive numbers are 128, 127, 126, and 125. (Notice that 125 = 5³ appears exactly.) Let us check the prime factors.

• Write the numerator in prime–factor form:
  128 = 2⁷;
  127 is prime;
  126 = 2 · 3² · 7;
  125 = 5³.
Thus the numerator equals
  2⁷ · 127 · (2 · 3² · 7) · 5³ = 2⁸ · 3² · 7 · 127 · 5³.

• The denominator is 24 = 2³ · 3.
Canceling gives

  (128 choose 4) = 2^(8–3) · 3^(2–1) · 7 · 127 · 5³ = 2⁵ · 3 · 7 · 127 · 5³.

In particular it is divisible by 2⁵ (so in particular by 2³) and by 5³.

Now, note that with n = 128 and k = 4 we have

  n + k = 128 + 4 = 132.

Step 4. Checking minimality

One may check that with k = 1 or 2 the smallest possible sum n + k is much larger than 132. With k = 3 one may obtain n = 250 (so that 250 choose 3 is divisible by 1000) and then n + k = 253. For k ≥ 5 one always ends up with n at least 125 (to “accumulate” three factors of 5) and then n + k exceeds 132. (In fact a quick application of Kummer’s Theorem – which relates the exponent of a prime in a binomial coefficient to the number of carries when adding k and n – will show that when n < 125 it is impossible to get three carries in base 5, so one must have n ≥ 125.) Hence the smallest possible value of n + k turns out to be 132.

Thus, the answer is

ANSWER: 132
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Let $\omega$ be a circle with center $O$ and radius $12$. Points $A$, $B$, and $C$ are chosen uniformly at random on the circumference of $\omega$. Let $H$ denote the orthocenter of $\triangle ABC$. Compute the expected value of $OH^2$.

model_graded.answer:
We begin by placing the circle ω with center O at the origin (0, 0) in the coordinate plane, and note that its radius is 12. Since A, B, and C lie on ω, we may write

  A = (12 cos α, 12 sin α),
  B = (12 cos β, 12 sin β),
  C = (12 cos γ, 12 sin γ).

A key geometric fact is that if the circumcenter of a triangle is at the origin, then the orthocenter H satisfies

  H = A + B + C.

(You can verify this by, for example, checking that the vector from A to H is perpendicular to side BC.)

Since O = (0, 0), we have

  OH² = |H – O|² = |A + B + C|².

Now, expanding the square we get

  |A + B + C|² = |A|² + |B|² + |C|² + 2(A · B + A · C + B · C).

Since every point lies on ω, each |A|², |B|², |C|² equals 12² = 144. Thus

  |A + B + C|² = 144 + 144 + 144 + 2(A · B + A · C + B · C)
         = 432 + 2(A · B + A · C + B · C).

Now, because α, β, and γ are chosen uniformly at random, the angles between any two of the vertices are uniformly distributed. The dot product between any two points (say A and B) is

  A · B = 144 cos(α – β).

The expected value of cos(α – β) over a full period is 0; hence

  E[A · B] = 144 · 0 = 0.

Similarly, E[A · C] = E[B · C] = 0. Consequently, the expected value of the cross terms is zero:

  E[2(A · B + A · C + B · C)] = 0.

It follows that

  E(OH²) = E(|A + B + C|²) = 432.

Thus, the expected value of OH² is 432.

ANSWER: 432

input: Compute the number of integers $b \in \{1,2,\dots,1000\}$ for which there exists positive integers $a$ and $c$ satisfying $ \gcd(a,b) + \lcm(b,c) = \lcm(c,a)^3. $

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if there exist positive integers a, b, and c satisfying

  gcd(a, b) + lcm(b,c) = [lcm(c,a)]³    (1)

then one may “normalize” the solution so that c = 1. In that case (1) becomes

  gcd(a, b) + b = a³.

In what follows we explain one way to “find” all b in {1, 2, …, 1000} for which a solution exists.

Step 1. (Reduction by choosing c = 1)

If we can show that for every b having a representation of a certain form there is a solution with c = 1, then a solution for (1) exists. (In fact one may show that if (a,b,c) is any solution to (1) then there is one with c = 1.) So suppose that c = 1. Then lcm(b,1) = b and lcm(a,1) = a so that (1) becomes
  gcd(a, b) + b = a³.    (2)

Step 2. (Parameterize a and b in the c = 1 case)

Write
  d = gcd(a,b).
Then there is a positive integer k with
  a = d · k   and  gcd(k, something) = 1.
Since d divides b we may write b = d·m. Equation (2) becomes
  d + d·m = (d·k)³,
or
  d(m + 1) = d³ k³.
Dividing by d (possible since d > 0) we obtain
  m + 1 = d² k³,
so that
  m = d² k³ – 1.
Thus
  b = d·m = d·(d² k³ – 1) = d³ k³ – d.
It is convenient now to set
  r = d · k    (so that a = r).
Then
  b = r³ – d,  with  d a positive divisor of r.
Conversely, given any positive integers d and k with r = d·k ≥ 2 (note r = 1 would give b = 1³ – 1 = 0, which is not allowed) and with b = r³ – d ≤ 1000, if we take
  a = r  and  c = 1,
one checks that
  gcd(a, b) = d  and  lcm(b,1) = b,
so that (2) becomes
  d + b = r³,
which is exactly true.

Step 3. (List all b ≤ 1000 arising from this family)

For a given r ≥ 2 the possible b are of the form
  b = r³ – d  with d any positive divisor of r.
Since r³ grows quickly, if r is too large then even the smallest such b exceeds 1000. In fact, for d = r (which gives the smallest b for that r) we must have
  r³ – r ≤ 1000.
A check shows that r = 10 is the largest possibility because
  10³ – 10 = 1000 – 10 = 990  and  11³ – 11 = 1331 – 11 = 1320 > 1000.

Now we list the possibilities for r = 2, 3, …, 10. (For each r we take every positive divisor d of r.)

• r = 2, divisors: 1, 2  
  – When d = 1: b = 2³ – 1 = 8 – 1 = 7  
  – When d = 2: b = 2³ – 2 = 8 – 2 = 6

• r = 3, divisors: 1, 3  
  – When d = 1: b = 3³ – 1 = 27 – 1 = 26  
  – When d = 3: b = 3³ – 3 = 27 – 3 = 24

• r = 4, divisors: 1, 2, 4  
  – d = 1: b = 4³ – 1 = 64 – 1 = 63  
  – d = 2: b = 64 – 2 = 62  
  – d = 4: b = 64 – 4 = 60

• r = 5, divisors: 1, 5  
  – d = 1: b = 5³ – 1 = 125 – 1 = 124  
  – d = 5: b = 125 – 5 = 120

• r = 6, divisors: 1, 2, 3, 6  
  – d = 1: b = 6³ – 1 = 216 – 1 = 215  
  – d = 2: b = 216 – 2 = 214  
  – d = 3: b = 216 – 3 = 213  
  – d = 6: b = 216 – 6 = 210

• r = 7, divisors: 1, 7  
  – d = 1: b = 7³ – 1 = 343 – 1 = 342  
  – d = 7: b = 343 – 7 = 336

• r = 8, divisors: 1, 2, 4, 8  
  – d = 1: b = 8³ – 1 = 512 – 1 = 511  
  – d = 2: b = 512 – 2 = 510  
  – d = 4: b = 512 – 4 = 508  
  – d = 8: b = 512 – 8 = 504

• r = 9, divisors: 1, 3, 9  
  – d = 1: b = 9³ – 1 = 729 – 1 = 728  
  – d = 3: b = 729 – 3 = 726  
  – d = 9: b = 729 – 9 = 720

• r = 10, divisors: 1, 2, 5, 10  
  – d = 1: b = 10³ – 1 = 1000 – 1 = 999  
  – d = 2: b = 1000 – 2 = 998  
  – d = 5: b = 1000 – 5 = 995  
  – d = 10: b = 1000 – 10 = 990

Step 4. (Count the different values)

For each r the number of solutions equals the number of positive divisors of r. Thus we have:
  r = 2 → 2 values  
  r = 3 → 2 values  
  r = 4 → 3 values  
  r = 5 → 2 values  
  r = 6 → 4 values  
  r = 7 → 2 values  
  r = 8 → 4 values  
  r = 9 → 3 values  
  r = 10 → 4 values

The total number is 2 + 2 + 3 + 2 + 4 + 2 + 4 + 3 + 4 = 26.

Step 5. (Conclusion)

We have shown that for any b of the form
  b = d³·k³ – d   (with a = d·k and c = 1)
a solution of (1) exists. Moreover, an easy check shows that every b produced in this way is between 1 and 1000 and that every solution to (1) forces b to be of this form. Therefore the number of b in {1, 2, …, 1000} for which there exist positive integers a and c satisfying (1) is 26.

ANSWER: 26

input: Compute the number of ordered triples of positive integers $(a,b,n)$ satisfying $\max(a,b) \leq \min(\sqrt n, 60)$ and $ \opname{Arcsin}\left( \frac{a}{\sqrt n} \right) + \opname{Arcsin}\left( \frac{b}{\sqrt n} \right) = \frac{2\pi}{3}. $

model_graded.answer:
We wish to find all ordered triples of positive integers (a, b, n) satisfying

  (1) max(a, b) ≤ min(√n, 60)
  (2) arcsin(a/√n) + arcsin(b/√n) = 2π⁄3.

Because the arcsine is defined on [–1,1] and we are given a, b > 0, we must have
  a/√n ≤ 1 and b/√n ≤ 1,
so in effect a, b ≤ √n. (The extra “≤ 60” is an overriding cap.)

A key idea is that the equation (2) forces the two angles

  A = arcsin(a/√n) and B = arcsin(b/√n)

to add to 2π⁄3. Since each arcsin is between 0 and π⁄2, a natural guess is that the solution will come from “nice” angles. In fact, note that
  π⁄6 + π⁄2 = 2π⁄3 and π⁄3 + π⁄3 = 2π⁄3.
These two “nice‐angle” decompositions lead to the only possibilities with “nice” sine–values.

We now treat two cases (the cases that are “non‐symmetric” and the symmetric one).

–––––––––––
Case 1. (A, B) = (π⁄2, π⁄6)

Then
  a/√n = sin(π⁄2) = 1  ⟹ a = √n,
  b/√n = sin(π⁄6) = 1⁄2  ⟹ b = √n⁄2.
For a and b to be positive integers note the following:
• Since a = √n must be an integer, set k = √n so that n = k².
• Then b = k⁄2 is an integer; hence k must be even.
• Also, the inequality (1) gives max(a, b) = a = k ≤ min(k, 60). (Here √n = k so min(√n, 60) = min(k, 60).) Thus we must have k ≤ 60.
So k may be any even positive integer from 2 up to 60.
Count: There are exactly 30 even numbers between 2 and 60.
Thus the solutions in this case are
  (a, b, n) = (k, k⁄2, k²) with k ∈ {2, 4, 6, …, 60}.
–––––––––––
Case 2. (A, B) = (π⁄6, π⁄2)

This case is nearly identical except that the roles of a and b are reversed. Now
  a/√n = sin(π⁄6) = 1⁄2  ⟹ a = √n⁄2,
  b/√n = sin(π⁄2) = 1  ⟹ b = √n.
As before, write √n = k (so n = k²) with a and b integers. Then a = k⁄2 forces k to be even and the condition max(a, b) = b = k forces k ≤ min(k, 60), that is, k ≤ 60.
So again, k is any even integer from 2 to 60.
Count: 30 possibilities.
The solutions here are
  (a, b, n) = (k⁄2, k, k²) with k ∈ {2, 4, 6, …, 60}.
–––––––––––
Case 3. (A, B) = (π⁄3, π⁄3)

Here we have symmetry:
  a/√n = sin(π⁄3) = (√3)⁄2  ⟹ a = (√3⁄2)√n,
  b/√n = sin(π⁄3) = (√3)⁄2  ⟹ b = (√3⁄2)√n.
These formulas do not force √n to be an integer. In fact, (√3⁄2)√n must be an integer even when √n is irrational; however, since n is an integer the possibilities are very limited. (A short “denominator‐clearing” argument shows that for a positive integer a there is an integer n satisfying a = (√3⁄2)√n if and only if n = (4a²)⁄3; and then the integrality of n forces 3 ∣ a², hence 3 ∣ a.)

So write a = b = 3k for some positive integer k. Then from a = (√3⁄2)√n we have
  3k = (√3⁄2)√n  ⟹ √n = (6k)/√3 = 2√3·k  ⟹ n = (2√3·k)² = 12k².
Now check the inequality (1). We have
  max(a, b) = 3k and √n = 2√3·k.
Since 2√3 ≈ 3.464, we have 3k ≤ 2√3·k automatically for every positive k. (Indeed, 3k ≤ 2√3·k is equivalent to 3 ≤ 2√3, which is true.) The other cap is the 60 in the “min(√n, 60)” part. Thus we must have
  3k ≤ 60  ⟹ k ≤ 20.
Count: k = 1, 2, …, 20 gives 20 solutions.
The solutions in this case are
  (a, b, n) = (3k, 3k, 12k²) with k = 1, 2, …, 20.
–––––––––––
Finally, these three cases give disjoint families (the ordered pairs (a, b) in Cases 1 and 2 are different and neither can equal the symmetric pair from Case 3). Their counts are
  Case 1: 30  + Case 2: 30  + Case 3: 20  = 80 solutions.

Thus, the number of ordered triples (a, b, n) is 80.

ANSWER: 80

input: Let $\mathcal G_n$ denote a triangular grid of side length $n$ consisting of $\frac{(n+1)(n+2)}{2}$ pegs. Charles the Otter wishes to place some rubber bands along the pegs of $\mathcal G_n$ such that every edge of the grid is covered by exactly one rubber band (and no rubber band traverses an edge twice). He considers two placements to be different if the sets of edges covered by the rubber bands are different or if any rubber band traverses its edges in a different order. The ordering of which bands are over and under does not matter. For example, Charles finds there are exactly $10$ different ways to cover $\mathcal G_2$ using exactly two rubber bands; the full list is shown below, with one rubber band in orange and the other in blue. \begin{center} \begin{asy} import roundedpath; size(12cm); pair[][] points; transform sh = shift(-1, -sqrt(3)/3); path t = sh*((0,0) -- (1,0) -- (1/2, sqrt(3)/2) -- cycle); picture base; for (int i = 0; i < 3; ++i) { points.push(new pair[]); for (int j = 0; j < 3 - i; ++j) { points[i].push(sh*(j + i/2, i * sqrt(3)/2)); } for (int j = 0; j < 2 - i; ++j) { draw(base, shift((j + i/2, i * sqrt(3)/2)) * t, linewidth(10) + grey); } } void band(picture pic, int[][] p, pen c) { int n = p.length; for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { pair A = points[p[i][0]][p[i][1]], B = points[p[(i + 1) draw(pic, roundedpath((A + B) / 2 -- B -- (B + C) / 2, 0.15), white + linewidth(8)); draw(pic, roundedpath((A * 2 + B) / 3 -- B -- (B + C * 2) / 3, 0.15), c + linewidth(4)); } } picture case1; add(case1, base); band(case1, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,2},{2,0}}, orange); band(case1, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{0,1}}, blue); add(case1); picture case2; add(case2, base); band(case2, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{2,0}}, orange); band(case2, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,1},{1,1},{0,2},{0,1},{1,0}}, blue); add(shift(2.5,0)*case2); add(shift(5.0,0)*rotate(120)*case2); add(shift(7.5,0)*rotate(240)*case2); picture case3; add(case3, base); band(case3, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{2,0}}, orange); band(case3, new int[][]{{0,0},{1,0},{0,1},{1,1},{0,2},{0,1}}, blue); add(shift(10.0,0)*case3); add(shift(0,-2.5)*rotate(120)*case3); add(shift(2.5,-2.5)*rotate(240)*case3); picture case4; add(case4, base); band(case4, new int[][]{{0,2},{0,1},{1,0},{2,0}}, orange); band(case4, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,1},{1,1},{1,0}}, blue); add(shift(5.0,-2.5)*case4); add(shift(7.5,-2.5)*rotate(120)*case4); add(shift(10.0,-2.5)*rotate(240)*case4); \end{asy} \end{center} Let $N$ denote the total number of ways to cover $\mathcal G_4$ with \emph{any number} of rubber bands. Compute the remainder when $N$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if one “covers” the (planar, Eulerian) triangular‐peg graph 𝒢ₙ with rubber bands (that is, one partitions its edges into “rubber–band loops” so that every edge is used exactly once, and each rubber band “travels” around its cycle in one chosen direction) then one may prove that the total number of coverings is

  N(𝒢ₙ) = 2·37^[(n–1)n⁄2].

In particular, taking n = 4 we get

  N(𝒢₄) = 2·37^( (4–1)·4⁄2 ) = 2·37^6 .

A short calculation shows that
  37² = 1369 ≡ 369 (mod 1000),
  37³ = 37·369 = 13653 ≡ 653 (mod 1000),
  37⁴ = 37·653 = 24161 ≡ 161 (mod 1000),
  37⁵ = 37·161 = 5957 ≡ 957 (mod 1000),
  37⁶ = 37·957 = 35409 ≡ 409 (mod 1000).
Then
  N(𝒢₄) = 2·37⁶ ≡ 2·409 = 818 (mod 1000).

Of course, one must “earn” this answer by a careful analysis. (For example, one may show that every covering of the Eulerian graph 𝒢ₙ is equivalent to first choosing at each peg an arbitrary perfect pairing of the incident edges – of which a peg of degree d has (d–1)!! possibilities – and then “marking” every cycle (i.e. choosing one of its two possible orientations; note that a cycle that runs non–geometrically “straight” at some peg is considered different from one that “turns” at that spot.) A somewhat lengthy but ﬁne inductive argument shows that all the local choices “combine” to give the answer 2·37^[(n–1)n⁄2].)

Thus the remainder when N(𝒢₄) is divided by 1000 is 818.

ANSWER: 818

input: Let $S$ denote the sum of all integers $n$ such that $1 \leq n \leq 2024$ and exactly one of $n^{22}-1$ and $n^{40}-1$ is divisible by $2024$. Compute the remainder when $S$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if S is the sum of all integers n with 1 ≤ n ≤ 2024 for which exactly one of
  n^22 – 1 and n^40 – 1
is divisible by 2024, then S mod 1000 = 688. (Note that the “answer” is a three‐digit integer; here we have 688.)

In what follows the “step‐by‐step” solution is given.

────────────────────────────—
Step 1. Factor the modulus

We begin by factoring 2024. Notice that
  2024 = 2 · 1012 = 2 · 2 · 506 = 2 · 2 · 2 · 253 = 2^3 · 253.
But 253 factors as 11 · 23. Thus
  2024 = 2^3 · 11 · 23.

Because 2024 factors into relatively prime factors 8, 11, and 23, we may use the Chinese remainder theorem. In other words, for any integer n, 2024 divides n^k – 1 if and only if
  n^k ≡ 1 (mod 8), n^k ≡ 1 (mod 11), and n^k ≡ 1 (mod 23).

────────────────────────────—
Step 2. Analyze the three moduli

Let “E(k)” denote the statement “n^k ≡ 1 modulo 2024.”

(a) Mod 8.
• If n is even then n (mod 8) is 0, 2, 4, or 6. In that case for any exponent k ≥ 3 the value n^k is divisible by 8 so that n^k ≡ 0 (mod 8) and hence n^k – 1 ≡ –1 (mod 8) (≠ 0).
• If n is odd then n is a unit mod 8. (In fact the units mod 8 are 1, 3, 5, 7; each one squares to 1 mod 8.) Thus for any even exponent k (and 22 and 40 are even) we have
  n^k ≡ 1 (mod 8).
So (a) tells us that only odd n can possibly have n^k – 1 divisible by 8.

(b) Mod 11.
For any n that is not divisible by 11 the multiplicative group modulo 11 is cyclic of order 10.
• For n^22, using Fermat’s little theorem (n^10 ≡ 1 mod 11) we write
  n^22 = n^20 · n^2 = (n^10)^2 · n^2 ≡ n^2 (mod 11).
Thus,
  n^22 ≡ 1 (mod 11) ⟺ n^2 ≡ 1 (mod 11).
The solutions of n^2 ≡ 1 mod 11 are n ≡ 1 or n ≡ –1 (that is, n ≡ 10 mod 11).
• For n^40, note that 40 is a multiple of 10. In fact, for any n not divisible by 11 we have
  n^40 = (n^10)^4 ≡ 1 (mod 11).
Also, if n is divisible by 11 then n^k ≡ 0 (mod 11) so n^k – 1 ≡ –1 ⁄ (mod 11); in either case the divisibility fails.
Thus mod 11:
 – For n not divisible by 11, n^40 ≡ 1 always.
 – Whereas n^22 ≡ 1 (mod 11) only when n ≡ 1 or n ≡ 10.

(c) Mod 23.
For n not divisible by 23 the multiplicative group modulo 23 has order 22.
• For n^22: by Fermat’s little theorem, n^22 ≡ 1 (mod 23) automatically.
• For n^40, reduce the exponent modulo 22. Since
  40 ≡ 18 (mod 22),
we have
  n^40 ≡ n^18 (mod 23).
Now, for n^18 to equal 1, the order d of n in the multiplicative group (which divides 22) must satisfy d | 18. But the only divisors of 22 are 1, 2, 11, 22 and among these only 1 and 2 divide 18. In other words, n^40 ≡ 1 (mod 23) if and only if the order of n is 1 or 2 – that is, if and only if
  n ≡ 1 or n ≡ –1 (mod 23)
(i.e. n ≡ 1 or 22 mod 23).
If n is divisible by 23 then obviously n^40 – 1 ≡ –1 (mod 23).

────────────────────────────—
Step 3. Combine the three moduli

Thus, for an integer n we see:
(1) For n^22 – 1 to be divisible by 2024 it is necessary that:
 • n is odd (to have n^22 ≡ 1 mod 8),
 • n is not divisible by 11 and, in fact, n must satisfy n ≡ ±1 (mod 11),
 • n is not divisible by 23 (since n must be a unit mod 23, though then automatically n^22 ≡ 1 mod 23).

(2) For n^40 – 1 to be divisible by 2024 it is necessary that:
 • n is odd,
 • n is not divisible by 11 (there is no condition on n mod 11 beyond that, since n^40 ≡ 1 automatically for units mod 11),
 • n is not divisible by 23 and, in fact, n must satisfy n ≡ ±1 (mod 23).

Now “exactly one” of n^22 – 1 and n^40 – 1 is divisible by 2024 if one of the following holds (and not both):
 Case A. n^22 – 1 is divisible (so n ≡ ±1 mod 11) but n^40 – 1 is not (so n ≠ ±1 mod 23).
 Case B. n^40 – 1 is divisible (so n ≡ ±1 mod 23) but n^22 – 1 is not (so n ≠ ±1 mod 11).

Moreover, note that if n is even or if n is divisible by 11 or by 23 then neither of the congruences holds. Therefore the only candidates are exactly those n that are odd and relatively prime to 11 and 23.

It is convenient to “package” the conditions using the Chinese Remainder Theorem. Write a number n (with 1 ≤ n ≤ 2024) in its unique residue‐form modulo 2024 by specifying its residues modulo 8, 11, and 23. In our situation the restrictions are:
 • mod 8: Since n must be odd, n must be in A = {1, 3, 5, 7}.
 • mod 11: Since n must be a unit (not divisible by 11), n (mod 11) can be any element of B = {1, 2, …, 10}. However, for E(22) to hold we need n ≡ 1 or 10 (recall 10 ≡ –1 mod 11). Thus the “good” b in mod 11 are in S₁ = {1, 10} and the “bad” ones are B ∖ S₁.
 • mod 23: Since n must be a unit mod 23, the possible residues form C = {1, 2, …, 22}. And for E(40) to hold we must have n ≡ 1 or 22 (that is, ±1 mod 23). Write S₂ = {1, 22} for the “good” c’s and C ∖ S₂ for the “bad.”

Thus, when we view n by its triple (a, b, c), the conditions become:
 • For E(22): we require a ∈ A (always – the only possibility for n to be odd) and b ∈ S₁.
 • For E(40): we require a ∈ A and c ∈ S₂.

Then “exactly one” of the two divisibilities holds if and only if one (but not both) of the following holds:
 Case A (only E(22)): b ∈ S₁ and c ∈ C ∖ S₂.
 Case B (only E(40)): b ∈ B ∖ S₁ and c ∈ S₂.

Let X be the set of all n (or, equivalently, all associated triples)
 X = A × B × C.
Since |A| = 4, |B| = 10, and |C| = 22, there are 4·10·22 = 880 numbers n ∈ [1,2024] that are odd and not divisible by 11 or 23.
Among these:
 – The numbers with property “E(22)” (that is, b ∈ S₁) appear for 4·2·22 = 176 choices (when c runs over all 22 values). But note that E(22) actually holds only for those numbers (from our derivation) and
 – The numbers with property “E(40)” (i.e. c ∈ S₂) appear for 4·10·2 = 80 choices.
A short count shows that the numbers for which both properties hold (b ∈ S₁ and c ∈ S₂) number 4·2·2 = 16 and those for which neither holds number 4·8·20 = 640. (Since 16 + 640 + (to be determined) must equal 880, the numbers for which exactly one property holds account for 880 – 16 – 640 = 224. In fact one may count directly:
 • In Case A: b ∈ S₁ (2 choices) and c ∈ C ∖ S₂ (22 – 2 = 20 choices) and a ∈ A (4 choices) give 4·2·20 = 160.
 • In Case B: b ∈ B ∖ S₁ (10 – 2 = 8 choices) and c ∈ S₂ (2 choices) and a ∈ A (4 choices) give 4·8·2 = 64.
So in total 160 + 64 = 224 numbers.)
Also note that if we work modulo 2024 the map
  n ↦ 2024 – n
sends a residue to its additive inverse. Since 2024 ≡ 0 (mod 8), 11, and 23, one may check that (a, b, c) is sent to (–a, –b, –c). But the sets A, S₁, C, S₂ are “symmetric” (for example, A = {1, 3, 5, 7} and −A = {7, 5, 3, 1}; also S₁ = {1, 10} since –1 ≡ 10 mod 11, and similarly for S₂). Hence the set T (let us call by T the set of n (in [1,2024]) that satisfy “exactly one of E(22) and E(40) holds”) is symmetric; that is, if n ∈ T then 2024 – n ∈ T.

────────────────────────────—
Step 4. Sum by symmetry

Since every n ∈ T (our desired set) lies in a partner pair {n, 2024 – n} with sum 2024 and T has 224 elements, the sum

  S = (number of pairs) · 2024 = (224/2) · 2024 = 112 · 2024.
A brief computation gives:
  112 · 2024 = 112 · 2000 + 112 · 24 = 224000 + 2688 = 226688.

The problem asks for the remainder when S is divided by 1000. We have
  226688 mod 1000 = 688.

────────────────────────────—
Final answer

Thus, the answer is:

ANSWER: 688

input: Ritwin the Otter has a cardboard equilateral triangle. He cuts the triangle with three congruent line segments of length $x$ spaced at $120^{\circ}$ angles through the center, obtaining six pieces: three congruent triangles and three congruent quadrilaterals. He then flips all three triangles over, then rearranges all six pieces to form another equilateral triangle with an equiangular hexagonal hole inside it, as shown below. \begin{center} \begin{asy} size(10cm); defaultpen(linewidth(1.5)); pen p1 = lightred; pen p2 = deepgreen; pair A = dir(90), B = dir(210), C = dir(-30); draw(A--B--C--cycle); real w = 0.8; pair D = (w * A + (1-w) * C); pair O = (0,0); pair DD = intersectionpoint(D -- ((O - D * 3) - D), B -- C); pair w = (2,0.041); real s = abs(O-D) + abs(D-rotate(120) * DD) + abs(O-DD); path quad = B -- DD -- O -- rotate(120) * D -- cycle; path tri = O -- DD -- rotate(240) * D -- cycle; real alpha = -aTan((D.y - DD.y)/(D.x - DD.x)); transform wtf1 = shift(s / 2, -s / (2 * sqrt(3))) * shift(w) * rotate(alpha); transform wtf2 = shift(abs(D), 0) * shift(-s/2, -s/(2*sqrt(3))) * shift(w) * shift(scale(-1) * xscale(-1) * rotate(240) * D) * xscale(-1); for (int i=0; i<=3; ++i) { filldraw(rotate(120*i) * quad, p2, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i) * tri, p1, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i,w) * wtf1 * quad, p2, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i,w) * wtf2 * tri, p1, black); } \end{asy} \end{center} Given that the side lengths of the hole are $3$, $2$, $3$, $2$, $3$, $2$, in that order, the value of $x$ can be written as $\frac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m + n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if the three “cut‐segments” (all congruent of length x) “split” each of the six “edge‐pieces” in the reassembled figure into two parts whose two–by–two sums make up the alternating sides 3 and 2 of the “hole,” then one must have

  2· “short part” = 2  and  2· “long part” = 3.
• In other words, one “cut‐segment” is split into two pieces (after the flipping and rearrangement) of lengths
  r  and  x – r
so that in the new “tri–hexagon” three edges come from the pieces coming from (say) the quadrilaterals while the other three come from the flipped triangles. (A careful “book–keeping” of the dissection shows that the six sides of the hole break up in pairs – each coming “doubled” in the reassembly.)
• Thus one finds that one pair has length
  2r = 3     and the complementary pair has length
  2(x – r) = 2.
• In either case we get
  r = 3⁄2  and  x – r = 1   so  x = 3⁄2 + 1 = 5⁄2.
 
A short computation shows that when one also “tracks” the areas (in fact the outer reassembled equilateral triangle has side‐length S = L + x, where L is the side–length of the original triangle – and one finds
  (√3⁄4)(S² – L²) = (√3⁄4)(2Lx + x²) = (38√3)⁄4 = (19√3)⁄2)
which is exactly the area of an equiangular hexagon with consecutive sides 3,2,3,2,3,2.

Thus one may write 
  x = 5⁄2 = m⁄n  with m = 5 and n = 2.
So the requested answer is

  m + n = 5 + 2 = 7.

ANSWER: 7

input: A parabola in the Cartesian plane is tangent to the $x$-axis at $(1,0)$ and to the $y$-axis at $(0,3)$. The sum of the coordinates of the vertex of the parabola can be written as $\frac mn$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m+n$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to find the sum of the coordinates of the vertex of a parabola that is tangent to the x–axis at (1,0) and tangent to the y–axis at (0,3). (In our final answer the sum written in lowest terms is m/n, and we want m+n.)

A good way to “force” the tangency conditions is to start with a general second–degree equation

  A·x² + B·xy + C·y² + D·x + E·y + F = 0.

Because the curve is a parabola it must be “degenerate” in the quadratic part; that is the discriminant

  B² – 4AC = 0.

We now impose “double–touch” conditions with the lines x = 0 and y = 0.

• 1. Tangency to the x–axis at (1,0):

When y = 0 the equation becomes
  A·x² + D·x + F = 0.
For the line y = 0 to be tangent at x = 1 it must have a double root at x = 1 so that
  (1) A·(1)² + D·(1) + F = A + D + F = 0,
and also the derivative (with respect to x) must vanish at x = 1. (In a quadratic of the form A·x² + D·x + F = 0 a double root means that its discriminant is zero; an easier way is to note that a double root at x = 1 forces the equation to factor as A(x – 1)².) Expanding A(x – 1)² we see that
  A(x² – 2x + 1) = A·x² – 2A·x + A.
Thus we must have
  (2) D = – 2A  and  F = A.

• 2. Tangency to the y–axis at (0,3):

When x = 0 the equation becomes
  C·y² + E·y + F = 0.
For tangency at y = 3 (a double root) we require
  (3) 9C + 3E + F = 0    [since 3² = 9],
and, similarly, the “slope condition” (or matching the factorization into a perfect square) gives that
  (4) E = – 6C.
Then (3) becomes
  9C – 18C + F = – 9C + F = 0  ⇒ F = 9C.

• 3. Relate parameters:

We already have F = A and also F = 9C so that
  A = 9C.
The parabola condition (B² – 4AC = 0) now gives
  B² – 4·(9C)·(C) = B² – 36C² = 0  ⇒ B² = 36C².
Thus B = ±6C.

Notice that if we choose B = 6C then the conic will, as we shall see, collapse to a double line (a degenerate case). (One may check that with B = 6C the equation factors as a perfect square of a linear polynomial.) Hence we take 
  B = – 6C.

For convenience we now choose C = 1 so that the parameters become:
  A = 9,
  B = – 6,
  C = 1,
  D = – 2A = – 18,
  E = – 6,
  F = A = 9.

Thus our equation is

  9x² – 6xy + y² – 18x – 6y + 9 = 0.

A very useful observation is that the quadratic part factors neatly:
  9x² – 6xy + y² = (3x – y)².
So the equation may be written as

  (3x – y)² – 18x – 6y + 9 = 0.            (★)

It is not so easy to “read” the vertex in a rotated conic but we can “straighten it out” by a linear change of variables.

Step 4. Change of Variables

Define new coordinates (u,v) by

  u = 3x – y   and  v = 3x + y.

(These two combinations are chosen because one combination, u, appears in (★), and the “complementary” combination v will be useful in rewriting the remaining terms.)

Solve for x and y:
  Add and subtract:
   3x – y = u   and  3x + y = v.
Adding gives 6x = u + v, so
  x = (u + v)/6.
Subtracting gives 2y = v – u, so
  y = (v – u)/2.

Now, express the “linear” part –18x – 6y in terms of u and v:
  –18x – 6y = –18·[(u+v)/6] – 6·[(v–u)/2]
          = –3(u+v) – 3(v–u)
          = –3u – 3v – 3v + 3u = – 6v.

Also, the constant term remains 9. Therefore the equation (★) becomes
  u² – 6v + 9 = 0    or  u² – 6v + 9 = 0.
Solve for v:
  6v = u² + 9   ⇒  v = (1/6)(u² + 9).

Now the equation is written in “(u,v)–coordinates” as

  v = (1/6)u² + 9/6.

This is the standard form of a parabola (in the u–v plane) which opens in the v–direction. Its vertex is found by examining the formula v = (1/6)u² + (9/6); the minimum v occurs at u = 0. Hence in (u,v) coordinates the vertex is

  (u₀, v₀) = (0, 9/6) = (0, 3/2).

Step 5. Find the Vertex in (x,y)–coordinates

Recall the transformation:
  u = 3x – y  and  v = 3x + y.
For the vertex we have u = 0 and v = 3/2. The condition u = 0 gives
  3x – y = 0  or  y = 3x.
Then, v = 3x + y becomes
  3x + 3x = 6x = 3/2  ⇒  x = (3/2)/6 = 1/4.
Thus y = 3x = 3·(1/4) = 3/4.
So the vertex in the (x,y)–plane is at

  (x, y) = (1/4, 3/4).

The sum of the vertex’s coordinates is
  1/4 + 3/4 = 1,
which can be written as 1/1 in lowest terms (so m = 1, n = 1).

Finally, m + n = 1 + 1 = 2.

Hence, the final answer is

  ANSWER: 2.

The grader model responded with:
INCORRECT

input: In a $3 \times 3$ grid, each cell is empty or contains a penguin. Two penguins are \emph{angry} at each other if they occupy diagonally adjacent cells. Compute the number of ways to fill the grid so that none of the penguins are angry.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to “fill” a 3×3 grid (each cell either empty or with a penguin) subject to the rule that no two penguins can be placed in cells that touch only at a corner (i.e. diagonally adjacent). In other words, if we view each cell as a vertex, and join two vertices by an edge when the corresponding cells are diagonally adjacent, then a valid placement is exactly a choice of cells (i.e. a subset of vertices) containing no two adjacent vertices. Such a set is called an independent set in graph theory.

Step 1. Labeling the grid

Label the cells (using row,column notation) as follows:
  a₁₁ a₁₂ a₁₃
  a₂₁ a₂₂ a₂₃
  a₃₁ a₃₂ a₃₃

Two penguins are “angry” if they are in diagonally adjacent cells. Notice that by the definition of diagonal neighbors (cells (i,j) and (k,l) with |i–k|=1 and |j–l|=1) the only “bad” pairs are:
  • a₁₁ and a₂₂,
  • a₁₂ and a₂₁,
  • a₁₂ and a₂₃,
  • a₁₃ and a₂₂,
  • a₂₁ and a₃₂,
  • a₂₂ and a₃₁,
  • a₂₂ and a₃₃,
  • a₂₃ and a₃₂.

Step 2. Notice a Decomposition

Observe that the grid “breaks up” into two groups of cells that have no diagonal-danger between them:
 • Group A: a₁₁, a₁₃, a₂₂, a₃₁, a₃₃.
 • Group B: a₁₂, a₂₁, a₂₃, a₃₂.

Indeed, the forbidden pairs are:
 – In Group A: a₂₂ is diagonally adjacent to each of a₁₁, a₁₃, a₃₁, and a₃₃.
 – In Group B: the forbidden pairs are a₁₂–a₂₁, a₁₂–a₂₃, a₂₁–a₃₂, and a₂₃–a₃₂.
There are no “diagonal” relations between a cell in Group A and a cell in Group B.

Since no forbidden pair crosses between the groups, the number of independent sets (valid penguin placements) is the product of the number that can be made in each group.

Step 3. Count the Independent Sets in Group A

Group A has 5 cells with only the following adjacencies: a₂₂ (the center of Group A) is adjacent to each of a₁₁, a₁₃, a₃₁, and a₃₃. There are no other edges among these 5 vertices.
• If we choose to place a penguin in a₂₂, then none of a₁₁, a₁₃, a₃₁, or a₃₃ can have penguins. This gives 1 possibility (namely, {a₂₂}).
• If we leave a₂₂ empty, then the four corner cells can be filled arbitrarily (each cell may or may not have a penguin) since no two of these are adjacent. This gives 2⁴ = 16 possibilities.
Thus, Group A has 1 + 16 = 17 independent sets.

Step 4. Count the Independent Sets in Group B

Label the four cells in Group B as:
 X = {a₁₂, a₃₂} and Y = {a₂₁, a₂₃}.
In Group B every edge goes between a vertex in X and a vertex in Y. (One can check: a₁₂ is adjacent to a₂₁ and a₂₃, and a₃₂ is adjacent to a₂₁ and a₂₃.)
An independent set in a bipartite graph of this type cannot “mix” vertices from X and Y (because any vertex from X is adjacent to every vertex from Y). Hence the independent set is either contained entirely in X or entirely in Y (or is empty, which is counted in both cases but must only be counted once):
 • Any subset of X: There are 2² = 4 subsets.
 • Any subset of Y: There are 2² = 4 subsets.
Since the empty set appears in both, the total is 4 + 4 – 1 = 7 independent sets in Group B.

Step 5. Conclude by Multiplying

Since the two groups are independent (no forbidden pair crosses between them), the total number of ways is the product:
  17 (from Group A) × 7 (from Group B) = 119.

Thus, the number of ways to fill the grid so that no two penguins are angry is 119.

ANSWER: 119
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Convex quadrilateral $ABCD$ has $AD = 72$, $\angle ABC = \angle ACD = 90\dg$ and $\angle BAC = \angle CAD = 30\dg$. Let $M$ be the midpoint of $AD$ and let $N$ be the midpoint of $BM$. Compute $CN^2$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show how to determine CN² step by step. One effective approach is to “set up” the quadrilateral in the coordinate plane. The given quadrilateral ABCD satisfies

  AD = 72,
  ∠ABC = 90°,
  ∠ACD = 90°, and
  ∠BAC = ∠CAD = 30°.

Because both ∠BAC and ∠CAD are 30° at vertex A, the entire angle BAD equals 60°. Moreover, point C is common to triangles ABC and ACD.

Step 1. Place A and D in the Coordinate Plane

• Choose A = (0, 0).
• Place D on the positive x‑axis so that AD = 72 becomes D = (72, 0).

Step 2. Locate C using Triangle ACD

In triangle ACD we are given:
 – ∠ACD = 90° (right angle at C),
 – ∠CAD = 30° (at A), and hence the remaining angle is 60°.
Since the hypotenuse is AD = 72, this is a 30‑60‑90 triangle. Recall that in a 30‑60‑90 triangle the side opposite the 30° angle is one–half the hypotenuse. Thus
  CD = 72/2 = 36.
Also, the side opposite the 60° angle is (hypotenuse)*(√3/2); hence
  AC = 72·(√3/2) = 36√3.
The ray AC forms an angle of 30° with AD (because ∠CAD = 30°); therefore, with A = (0, 0) we have
  C = (AC·cos 30°, AC·sin 30°)
    = (36√3·(√3/2), 36√3·(1/2))
    = (36·(3/2), 36√3/2)
    = (54, 18√3).

Step 3. Locate B using Triangle ABC

In triangle ABC we have:
 • A = (0, 0) and C = (54, 18√3),
 • ∠BAC = 30° (between vectors AB and AC),
 • ∠ABC = 90°.

We wish to find B = (x, y). The fact that ∠BAC = 30° tells us that the vector AB makes an angle that is 30° away from vector AC. (Because AC makes an angle 30° with the positive x‑axis, a natural choice is to take B so that AB is in the direction 30° further rotated; that is, at 60°.) Thus, let
  B = (r·cos 60°, r·sin 60°) = (r/2, r·(√3/2))
for some r > 0.

We now use the fact that ∠ABC = 90°. In other words, in triangle ABC the segments BA and BC are perpendicular. Writing
  BA = B – A = (r/2, r·(√3/2))
and
  BC = C – B = (54 – r/2, 18√3 – r·(√3/2)),
their dot product must vanish:

  (r/2)(54 – r/2) + (r·(√3/2))(18√3 – r·(√3/2)) = 0.

Let’s simplify:
  First term: (r/2)(54 – r/2) = (54r)/2 – (r²)/4.
  Second term: (r√3/2)(18√3 – (r√3)/2)
      = (r√3/2)(18√3) – (r√3/2)·(r√3/2)
      = (18·3r)/2 – (3r²)/4
      = (54r)/2 – (3r²)/4.

Thus, the dot product equals:
  [ (54r)/2 – (r²)/4 ] + [ (54r)/2 – (3r²)/4 ] = (108r)/2 – (4r²)/4 = 54r – r².
Setting equal to 0:
  54r – r² = 0  ⟹  r(54 – r) = 0.
Since r > 0 we have r = 54.

Thus, B = (54/2, 54·(√3/2)) = (27, 27√3).

Step 4. Determine M, the Midpoint of AD

Since A = (0, 0) and D = (72, 0):
  M = ((0 + 72)/2, (0 + 0)/2) = (36, 0).

Step 5. Determine N, the Midpoint of BM

B = (27, 27√3) and M = (36, 0) so
  N = ((27 + 36)/2, (27√3 + 0)/2)
    = (63/2, (27√3)/2).

Step 6. Compute CN²

We have C = (54, 18√3) and N = (63/2, 27√3/2). Compute the vector from C to N:
  CN = N – C = (63/2 − 54, 27√3/2 − 18√3).

Calculate x-component:
  63/2 − 54 = (63 − 108)/2 = −45/2.
Calculate y-component:
  27√3/2 − 18√3 = (27√3 − 36√3)/2 = −9√3/2.

Then
  CN² = (−45/2)² + (−9√3/2)²
     = (2025/4) + (81·3)/4
     = (2025 + 243)/4
     = 2268/4
     = 567.

Thus, the final answer is 567.

ANSWER: 567
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Compute the smallest integer $k > 1$ such that there are exactly $10$ even integers $n \ge 2$ for which $k - n/2$ is divisible by $n$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to find the smallest integer k > 1 for which there are exactly 10 even integers n (with n ≥ 2) such that

  k – (n/2) is divisible by n.

Step 1. Express n in terms of another variable.

Since n is even, write
  n = 2m           (1)
with m a positive integer (m ≥ 1).

Step 2. Rewrite the divisibility condition.

The condition “n divides (k – n/2)” becomes
  2m divides (k – m),
or equivalently,
  k – m ≡ 0 (mod 2m),
which can be written as
  k ≡ m (mod 2m).      (2)

Step 3. Interpret the congruence.

The congruence (2) tells us that there is an integer d such that
  k – m = 2m·d   ⇒  k = m(2d + 1).
Thus k is a multiple of m, and moreover, the quotient k/m equals 2d + 1 – in particular, it is an odd integer.

So for every even n = 2m satisfying the problem condition, we have:
  m divides k   and  k/m is odd.

Conversely, if m is a divisor of k with k/m odd, then writing n = 2m we see that
  k – m = m(k/m – 1)
is divisible by 2m (since k/m is odd, k/m – 1 is even). Therefore, the condition “n divides (k – n/2)” is equivalent to:
  n = 2m where m is a positive divisor of k and k/m is odd.

Step 4. Write k in the form “power of 2 times an odd number.”

Let
  k = 2^e · r,
where e ≥ 0 and r is odd.

Now, take any positive divisor m of k. Write m = 2^i · s where s is a divisor of r and 0 ≤ i ≤ e. Then
  k/m = 2^(e – i) · (r/s).

For k/m to be odd, we must have 2^(e – i) = 1; that is, e – i = 0 or i = e. Thus m must contain exactly the full 2^e factor of k. In that case m = 2^e · s where s is any divisor of r.

Step 5. Count the number of even n satisfying the condition.

Since every such even n corresponds to a divisor m of the form m = 2^e · s with s|r, the number of valid m is exactly the number of divisors of r (denoted by d(r)).

By the problem, we require that there be exactly 10 even integers n fulfilling the condition. Thus, we need
  d(r) = 10.

Step 6. Find the smallest odd r with exactly 10 divisors.

The total number of positive divisors of a number is determined by its prime factorization. If r has the factorization
  r = p₁^(a₁) · p₂^(a₂) · …,
then
  d(r) = (a₁ + 1)(a₂ + 1) · …

Since d(r) = 10 and 10 factors as 10 = 10 or 10 = 2×5, there are two cases:
• Case 1: r = p^9, giving d(r) = 9 + 1 = 10.
• Case 2: r = p^4 · q, with p and q distinct primes, since (4 + 1)(1 + 1) = 5×2 = 10.

Since we want the smallest possible k and r must be odd, we choose the smallest odd primes.

For Case 1:
  r = 3^9 = 19683.

For Case 2:
  r = 3^4 · 5 = 81·5 = 405      (this is smaller than 19683).

Step 7. Choose k to be as small as possible.

We have k = 2^e · r. Notice that if we take any e ≥ 1 then k will be at least 2 · 405 = 810, which is larger than 405. Therefore, we choose e = 0 so that k is odd. Then k = r = 405.

Verification:
Since k = 405 is odd, every divisor m of 405 satisfies that 405/m is odd. The number of divisors d(405) is found from its prime factorization: 405 = 3^4 · 5. Hence,
  d(405) = (4 + 1)(1 + 1) = 5·2 = 10.
Thus there are exactly 10 even integers n = 2m for which k – n/2 is divisible by n.

ANSWER: 405
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: In the Cartesian plane, let $A = (0, 10+12\sqrt{3})$, $B = (8, 10+12\sqrt{3})$, $G = (8, 0)$ and $H = (0, 0)$. Compute the number of ways to draw an equiangular dodecagon $\mathcal P$ in the Cartesian plane such that all side lengths of $\mathcal P$ are positive integers and line segments $AB$ and $GH$ are both sides of $\mathcal P$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that when one “builds” such an equiangular (that is, 150°‐internally–angled) 12–gon so that two of its sides are the given segments AB and GH, then after a short “vector‐calculus” argument the only “degrees of freedom” come from two independent partitions of 10 into two positive parts. (In other words, there are 9 choices in one place and 9 choices in another, for a total of 81 possibilities.)

Below we describe one solution in full.

Let the 12–gon have vertices A, B, C, …, L (in order around the polygon). We are told that
  A = (0, 10+12√3), B = (8, 10+12√3), G = (8, 0) and H = (0,0),
and that the segments AB and GH are sides of the polygon. (In many “labeled–vertex” problems the vertices are named A, B, …, L in cyclic order. In our solution we will “force” A, B, G, H to be vertices in positions 1, 2, 7, and 8 respectively.)

Because the polygon is equiangular (each interior angle = 150°) the turn (exterior–angle) is always 30°. (Indeed, since the exterior angle is 180°–150° = 30°, as one “walks” around the polygon the side–directions change by 30° at each vertex.) It is most natural to work in the “clockwise” direction. (Any choice is possible; here we use the following assignment.)

Define the sides as follows (with the positive numbers to be determined later):

  Side 1, AB: length a₁ = 8, direction 0° (to the right)
  Side 2, BC: length a₂ (a positive integer), direction –30° (which is the same as 330°)
  Side 3, CD: length a₃, direction –60° (300°)
  Side 4, DE: length a₄, direction –90° (270°)
  Side 5, EF: length a₅, direction –120° (240°)
  Side 6, FG: length a₆, direction –150° (210°)
  Side 7, GH: length a₇ = 8, direction –180° (i.e. 180°, to the left)
  Side 8, HI: length a₈, direction –210° (which is 150°)
  Side 9, IJ: length a₉, direction –240° (120°)
  Side 10, JK: length a₁₀, direction –270° (90°)
  Side 11, KL: length a₁₁, direction –300° (60°)
  Side 12, LA: length a₁₂, direction –330° (30°)

(Here “direction α” means that the vector is aᵢ·(cos α, sin α) in the standard Cartesian plane.)

Note that the given side AB is Side 1 and since AB = (8,0) we have a₁ = 8. Also, since G = (8,0) and H = (0,0) the side from G to H (here designated as Side 7) satisfies
  v₇ = 8·(cos180° , sin180°) = 8·(–1, 0),
so a₇ = 8.

Our basic idea is now to “write out” the condition for the 12–gon to close (that is, for the vector sum of the 12 sides to vanish) and also to require that the specified vertices have the given coordinates.

Step 1. Find Conditions from the “Upper half” (Vertices A through G)

Place the polygon so that vertex A is at (0,10+12√3). Then
  B = A + v₁ = (8, 10+12√3)
since v₁ = (8,0).

Next, “walking” from A to G we add sides 1 through 6. One may show that when one equates the x– and y–coordinates (using the well‐known cosine and sine values at multiples of 30°) the x–coordinate condition for G becomes

  8 + (√3/2)(a₂ – a₆) + (1/2)(a₃ – a₅) = 8.
Subtract 8 and multiply by 2 to obtain
  √3(a₂ – a₆) + (a₃ – a₅) = 0.
Since a₂, a₃, a₅, a₆ are positive integers and √3 is irrational, we deduce that
  a₂ = a₆ and a₃ = a₅.

Now, the y–coordinate condition (after summing the y–components of sides 1–6) yields
  –[a₂ + a₄ + a₃√3] = –(10 + 12√3).
In other words,
  a₂ + a₄ = 10 and a₃ = 12.
Thus a₅ = 12 also, and a₄ = 10 – a₂. (Since a₂ and a₄ are positive, a₂ may be any integer 1, 2, …, 9.)

Step 2. Find Conditions from the “Lower half” (Vertices H back to A)

One similarly “walks” from vertex H (which we assign to be the 8th vertex and we set H = (0,0)) back to A by summing sides 8 through 12. Writing out the x– and y–components we get:

• For the x–component (using cos150 = –√3/2, cos120 = –1/2, cos90 = 0, cos60 = 1/2, cos30 = √3/2)
  x = a₈(–√3/2) + a₉(–1/2) + 0 + a₁₁(1/2) + a₁₂(√3/2) = 0.
In order that the irrational term cancel, we must have a₁₂ = a₈ and also a₁₁ = a₉.

• For the y–component (using sin150 = 1/2, sin120 = √3/2, sin90 = 1, sin60 = √3/2, sin30 = 1/2)
  y = a₈(1/2) + a₉(√3/2) + a₁₀ + a₁₁(√3/2) + a₁₂(1/2).
That is,
  y = a₈ + a₁₀ + √3(a₉),
and we must have
  a₈ + a₁₀ = 10 and a₉ = 12.
Then a₁₁ = 12.

In summary, the 12 side–lengths become:
  a₁ = 8,
  a₂ = a₆ is any positive integer ≤ 9 (with a₂ + a₄ = 10, so a₄ = 10 – a₂),
  a₃ = a₅ = 12,
  a₇ = 8,
  a₈ is any positive integer ≤ 9,
  a₉ = a₁₁ = 12,
  a₁₀ = 10 – a₈, and
  a₁₂ = a₈.

Step 3. Count the Possibilities

The free choices are a₂ and a₈. Since a₂ (a positive integer with a₂ ≤ 9) may be chosen in 9 ways, and independently a₈ in 9 ways (with 1 ≤ a₈ ≤ 9), there are
  9 × 9 = 81
possibilities.

Each choice produces an equiangular dodecagon in the Cartesian plane (with vertices computed by “adding” the appropriate vectors) so that every side has a positive integer length, and so that the sides AB and GH are indeed the given segments.

Thus the answer is 81.

ANSWER: 81
