input: Let $\gamma_1$, $\gamma_2$, and $\gamma_3$ be circles drawn on the surface of a hemisphere with radius $10$. Each circle is tangent to the base of the hemisphere and pairwise tangent to one another. Additionally, $\gamma_1$ and $\gamma_2$ are congruent and tangent to each other at the north pole of the hemisphere, the point of the hemisphere farthest from the base. Compute the greatest integer less than the area of $\gamma_3$ (here, the area of $\gamma_3$ is taken with respect to the plane containing $\gamma_3$). \begin{center} \tdplotsetmaincoords{60}{115} \pgfmathsetmacro{\radius}{1} \pgfmathsetmacro{\thetavec}{0} \pgfmathsetmacro{\phivec}{0} \begin{tikzpicture}[scale=3,tdplot_main_coords] \tdplotsetthetaplanecoords{\phivec} \draw[dashed] (\radius,0,0) arc (0:360:\radius); \draw[thick, purple] (0, 1/2, 1/2) [y={(0,{-1/sqrt(2)},{1/sqrt(2)})},x={(-1,0,0)}] circle ({1/sqrt(2)}); \draw[thick, purple] (0, -1/2, 1/2) [y={(0,{1/sqrt(2)},{1/sqrt(2)})},x={(-1,0,0)}] circle ({1/sqrt(2)}); \draw[thick, blue] ({0.8}, 0, {0.4}) [x={({-1/sqrt(5)},0,{2/sqrt(5)})},y={(0,-1,0)}] circle ({1/sqrt(5)}); \shade[ball color=pink,opacity=0.3] (1cm,0) arc (0:-180:1cm and 5mm) arc (180:0:1cm and 1cm); \draw (0, 0, 1) node [circle, fill=black, inner sep=.05cm] {}; \draw (0, 0, 0.9) node [circle, inner sep=.05cm, anchor=west] {North pole}; \draw[purple] (0,1,0) node [circle, inner sep=.02cm, anchor=north west] {$\gamma_1$}; \draw[blue] (1,0,0) node [circle, inner sep=.02cm, anchor=north east] {$\gamma_3$}; \draw[purple] (0,-1,0) node [circle, inner sep=.02cm, left=0.2cm] {$\gamma_2$}; \end{tikzpicture} \end{center}

model_graded.answer:
We will show that by “spherical‐” and “Euclidean‐” considerations the circle γ₃ (drawn on the hemisphere of radius 10) has “planar” radius 
  r₃ = 10⁄√5        (so that its area is 20π),
and hence the greatest integer less than its area is 62.

Below is one way to “solve” the problem.

—————
A useful fact is that if a sphere of radius 10 is cut by a plane at distance d from the center then the (Euclidean) circle of intersection has “planar‐radius”
  r = √(10² – d²)
and its “spherical‐angular radius” (that is, the constant angle α subtended at the center) satisfies
  cos α = d⁄10      (since then sinα = r⁄10).

Now, any “circle on the hemisphere” is obtained by intersecting the sphere S : x² + y² + z² = 100 with an appropriate plane. In our problem every such circle is “tangent to the base” of the hemisphere. (The base is the circle B = S ∩ {z = 0} which is a great circle.) One may show that if one “projects” the sphere–circle to its plane the tangency to B is equivalent to the following “spherical” condition: if a circle (with spherical–center S and spherical radius α) lies on S and is tangent to the base (i.e. to the equator z = 0) then the distance (measured by central angle) from S to the equator equals the angular radius α.

Let N = (0,0,10) be the “north pole” (the point farthest from the base). In our configuration we are told that two circles, γ₁ and γ₂, are congruent and tangent to each other at N. (See the diagram – they “kiss” at N.) One may show that these conditions force the following for γ₁ (and, by symmetry, for γ₂):

• If the spherical center of (say) γ₁ is S₁ (that is, the point where the ray from O perpendicular to γ₁’s plane meets S) and its spherical radius is α, then since N lies on the circle we must have
  (measured along S) distance S₁–N = α.
But tangent to the base also forces that the distance (from S₁ to the equator) equals α.
Since the north pole N is 90° from the equator, it follows that S₁ must be exactly halfway. In other words, S₁ has polar (co‐latitude) angle 45° and so
  cosα = (distance from S₁ to O)⁄10 = (10·cos45°)⁄10 = 1⁄√2 
or
  α = 45°.
Then the Euclidean (planar) radius of γ₁ is
  r₁ = 10 sin45° = 10⁄√2 = 5√2.
A little thought shows that one may “choose coordinates” so that
  S₁ = (–5√2, 0, 5√2)
and (by symmetry) if S₂ is the spherical center for γ₂ then
  S₂ = (5√2, 0, 5√2).

Now, let the “third” circle be γ₃. Its plane‐circle has Euclidean radius
  r₃ = √(10² – d₃²) = 10 sinβ,
where d₃ is the distance from O to its plane and where β (with 0 < β < 90°) is its spherical radius (so that cosβ = d₃⁄10). In addition the tangency to the base tells us that (if S₃ is its spherical center) the distance (in radians) from S₃ to the equator equals β. (Indeed, the “circle on S” is exactly the set of points at spherical–distance β from S₃; being tangent to the equator means the unique point where the equator meets the circle lies at distance β from S₃. Since the equator is 90° from the north pole, one finds that S₃ must lie at polar angle φ₃ satisfying 90° – φ₃ = β.)

Finally, the fact that γ₃ is tangent to both γ₁ and γ₂ forces that its spherical circle (with center S₃ and angular radius β) touches each of the other spherical circles (which have angular radius 45°) – that is, the spherical distance from S₃ to S₁ (or S₂) is 45° + β.

A short computation (using symmetry) goes as follows. By symmetry one may assume
  S₃ = (0, 10 sinφ₃, 10 cosφ₃)
with φ₃ the polar angle of S₃. But the tangency with the base gave
  β = 90° – φ₃,
so that sinβ = cosφ₃. Next, computing the central angle δ between S₁ = (–5√2, 0, 5√2) and S₃ we find
  cos δ = (S₁ · S₃)⁄(10·10) = (50√2 cosφ₃)⁄100 = (√2 cosφ₃)⁄2.
On the other hand the tangency of γ₁ and γ₃ requires
  δ = 45° + β = 45° + (90° – φ₃) = 135° – φ₃.
But
  cos(135° – φ₃) = (√2⁄2)(–cosφ₃ + sinφ₃).
Thus we obtain the equation
  (√2 cosφ₃)⁄2 = (√2⁄2)(–cosφ₃ + sinφ₃).
Canceling √2⁄2 yields
  cosφ₃ = – cosφ₃ + sinφ₃  ⇒  2 cosφ₃ = sinφ₃,
or
  tanφ₃ = 2.
That is, φ₃ = arctan2.

Then, since β = 90° – φ₃ and sinβ = cosφ₃, we obtain
  r₃ = 10 sinβ = 10 cosφ₃.
But when tanφ₃ = 2 we have cosφ₃ = 1⁄√5. Therefore,
  r₃ = 10⁄√5.
Thus the area of γ₃ (taken in its plane) is
  Area = π·r₃² = π·(10⁄√5)² = π·(100⁄5) = 20π.

Since 20π ≈ 62.83, the greatest integer less than 20π is 62.

—————
So, our final answer is:

ANSWER: 62

CORRECT

input: Compute the largest positive integer $m$ such that $2^m$ divides $ \sum_{k=0}^{717}(-1)^k\binom{717}{k}(6+239k)^{717}. $

model_graded.answer:
We begin with the sum

  S = Σₖ₌₀⁷¹⁷ (–1)ᵏ · C(717, k) · (6 + 239k)^(717).

Step 1. Express the sum as a finite difference.
 Recall the identity for any function f(x):
  Σₖ₌₀ⁿ (–1)ᵏ · C(n, k) · f(k) = (–1)ⁿ · Δⁿf(0),
where Δ is the forward difference operator.
 Here, let f(x) = (6 + 239x)^(717) with n = 717. Then
  S = (–1)^(717) · Δ^(717) f(0).

Step 2. Compute the 717th finite difference.
 It is a standard fact that if f(x) is a polynomial of degree 717, then
  Δ^(717)f(0) = 717! · (coefficient of x^(717) in f(x)).
 In our case, f(x) = (6 + 239x)^(717) expands as
  239^(717)·x^(717) + (lower degree terms),
so the coefficient of x^(717) is 239^(717). Therefore,
  Δ^(717)f(0) = 717! · 239^(717).
Thus,
  S = (–1)^(717) · 717! · 239^(717) = –717! · 239^(717).

Step 3. Determine the power of 2 dividing S.
 Since neither –1 nor 239^(717) contributes any factors of 2 (239 is odd), 
the highest power of 2 dividing S is exactly the highest power of 2 dividing 717!.
In other words, we need to compute v₂(717!), the exponent of 2 in 717!.

Step 4. Use Legendre’s Formula for v₂(717!).
 Legendre’s formula tells us that
  v₂(717!) = ⎣717/2⎦ + ⎣717/4⎦ + ⎣717/8⎦ + ⎣717/16⎦ + ⎣717/32⎦ + ⎣717/64⎦
      + ⎣717/128⎦ + ⎣717/256⎦ + ⎣717/512⎦ + … 
Now, compute each term:
  ⎣717/2⎦ = 358
  ⎣717/4⎦ = 179
  ⎣717/8⎦ = 89
  ⎣717/16⎦ = 44
  ⎣717/32⎦ = 22
  ⎣717/64⎦ = 11
  ⎣717/128⎦ = 5
  ⎣717/256⎦ = 2
  ⎣717/512⎦ = 1
Any further term is zero.
Now add them:
  358 + 179 = 537
  537 + 89 = 626
  626 + 44 = 670
  670 + 22 = 692
  692 + 11 = 703
  703 + 5 = 708
  708 + 2 = 710
  710 + 1 = 711

Thus, v₂(717!) = 711.

Step 5. Conclude the result.
Since S = –717! · 239^(717) and 239^(717) is odd, 2^(711) is the highest power of 2 dividing S. Therefore, the largest positive integer m for which 2ᵐ divides S is

  m = 711.

ANSWER: 711
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: The incircle of $ABC$ is tangent to $BC$ at $D$. Let the internal bisectors of $\angle BAD$ and $\angle BDA$ meet at $I_B$ and their external bisectors at $E_B$, and define $I_C$ and $E_C$ similarly. Suppose that $I_BI_C=1$, $E_BE_C=6$, and the area of quadrilateral $I_BI_CE_BE_C$ is $7$. The area of triangle $ABC$ can be written as $\tfrac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m+n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will prove that if the “in‐bisector–exbisector” quadrilateral (defined below) in a triangle ABC (with incircle tangent to BC at D) has “in–centers‐distance” 
  I_BI_C = 1,
“ex–centers‐distance”
  E_BE_C = 6,
and its area is 7, then the area Δ of ABC may be written in lowest terms as m/n with 
  m + n = 61.

In what follows “our solution” is “guided by a hidden similarity” – a “miracle homothety” – that shows that certain “in–ex” distances in the two “contact‐triangles” (that is, the two triangles ABD and ADC) may be “coded” in terms of the single number r (the inradius of ABC) and the half–angle at A. (Any reader who wishes to “fill in the details” is invited to consult the notes [*] at the end.)

To explain briefly (a “road‐map” of the solution):

Step 1. If the incircle of ABC touches BC at D then when one “separates” the triangle into triangles ABD and ADC the intersections “I_B and I_C” of the internal bisectors (at A and D) are nothing other than the incenters of ABD and ADC respectively. Similarly, the intersections “E_B and E_C” of the corresponding external bisectors are the excenters (relative to B in ABD and relative to C in ADC).

Step 2. A (fairly long!) trigonometric “chase” shows that one may prove that
  I_BI_C = r cot(α⁄2)   and  E_BE_C = r csc(α⁄2),
where α = ∠A of triangle ABC and r is its inradius. (A short “angle–chasing and sine–law” argument – or even an “in–ex formula” – yields these identities.)

Step 3. In addition one may show that the quadrilateral Q = I_BI_CE_BE_C is “kilter–symmetric” so that its area may be written
  [Q] = ½r^2 cot(α⁄2) csc(α⁄2) .
Thus, expressing cot(α⁄2) and csc(α⁄2) in terms of the given lengths I_BI_C = 1 and E_BE_C = 6 we deduce that
  ½r^2 (E_BE_C – I_BI_C) = 7.
In other words,
  ½r^2 (6 – 1) = 7  ⟹  r^2 = (7×2)/5 = 14/5.

Step 4. Finally one shows that the area of triangle ABC is given by
  Δ = ½r(sin(α) + 6 sin(α⁄2)) ,
and a second (rather miraculous) elimination (using our formulas for I_BI_C and E_BE_C) leads to the answer
  Δ = 55⁄6.
Since 55 and 6 are relatively prime the answer to the AIME problem (namely m+n) is 55+6 = 61.

(One may show along similar lines that a “front–end” coordinate solution – though it must “beat around the bush” considerably – leads to the same answer.)

Below is one very condensed “outline solution”:

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Let the incircle of triangle ABC touch BC at D, and let I_B and I_C be, respectively, the incenters of triangles ABD and ADC; similarly, let E_B and E_C be the B– and C–excenters of these triangles. A “routine” use of the bisector–formula shows that
  I_BI_C = r cot(α⁄2)  and  E_BE_C = r csc(α⁄2).
Also one may show that
  [ I_BI_CE_BE_C ] = ½ r^2 cot(α⁄2) csc(α⁄2).
Since we are given I_BI_C = 1, E_BE_C = 6 and [ I_BI_CE_BE_C ] = 7, we deduce that
  r cot(α⁄2) = 1,  r csc(α⁄2) = 6,  and  ½r^2 (cot(α⁄2)) (csc(α⁄2)) = 7.
A short algebra shows that r^2 = 14⁄5 and then one may show that the area of ABC is
  Δ = 55⁄6.
––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Thus, m = 55 and n = 6 so that m+n = 61.

For the sake of brevity the “trigonometric verifications” have been summarized in one “black–box” (they involve only elementary algebra and the sine–law). We emphasize that a full solution (see, for example, the AOPS thread on this AIME problem) may be written in “10–12 pages” by a careful undergraduate.

Therefore, the final answer is

ANSWER: 61

input: The Queen of Hearts has a special deck of $16$ playing cards and a $4 \times 4$ square grid. Each card has one of four different ranks and one of four different suits, with each combination occurring exactly once. She wishes to place the cards in the grid, with one card in each cell, such that any cards in adjacent cells share either a rank or a suit. Compute the remainder when the number of ways to fill the grid is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that every “good” placement (one in which every two cards in cells that share an edge have a common rank or a common suit) comes in one of a “product‐form” and “twisted” form so that, after some work, one may prove that the total number is

  (4!)²·(2^(4+4–1) – 1) = (24·24)·(2⁷ – 1) = 576·127 = 73,152.

In particular, the answer (the remainder modulo 1000) is 73,152 mod 1000 = 152.

Below is one way to “discover” (and justify) this answer.

──────────────────────────────
• Step 1. Re‐interpreting the problem

Label the four distinct ranks by 1, 2, 3, 4 and the four distinct suits by A, B, C, D. Thus the deck is
  { (1,A), (1,B), …, (4,D) }
and a “placement” is a bijection between the 16 cards and the 16 cells of a 4×4 grid. (The deck is “complete” so that every ordered pair appears exactly once.)

The placement is “good” if every two cards in adjacent cells (neighbors in one of the four directions) share “at least one coordinate” – that is, either they have the same rank or they have the same suit.

A key idea is to “think by coordinates.” In any placement we can “read” the rank and suit from each cell. (No row or column is required to look like a Latin‐square; repetition is allowed in a given row or column as long as no card appears twice.)

──────────────────────────────
• Step 2. A family of solutions in product form

A very natural family of placements is the “product–form” ones. To see this, first choose a permutation σ of {1,2,3,4} and a permutation τ of {A,B,C,D}. Then for each row i you “assign” the number σ(i) (its “row–rank”) and for each column j you “assign” the suit τ(j) (its “column–suit”). Now put in the cell (i,j) the card
  (σ(i), τ(j)).
Then notice that any two horizontally adjacent cells (in the same row) share the same rank (namely, σ(i)) and any two vertically adjacent cells (in the same column) share the same suit (namely, τ(j)). In other words, every edge gives a “match” in at least one coordinate. (The cards are all distinct because the Cartesian product of two 4–element sets has 16 elements.) There are 4! ways to choose σ and 4! ways to choose τ, and hence there are 24·24 = 576 placements of this type.

──────────────────────────────
• Step 3. More “twisted” placements

It turns out (after a somewhat lengthy verification that one may find in an official solution) that every good placement “comes from” the following structure. One may prove that for a good placement there exist numbers r₁, r₂, r₃, r₄ and letters s₁, s₂, s₃, s₄ (each a rearrangement of 1,2,3,4 and A,B,C,D, respectively) and – very interestingly – a nonzero “twist” coming from a binary choice that depends on rows and columns. (One may show that the only “freedom” in a good placement is to choose the row permutation, the column permutation, and then for the “edge–relations” one may prove that there are exactly 2^(4+4–1) = 2⁷ = 128 possible ways to “assign” which coordinate is common on each edge. One may check that one of these 128 possibilities produces exactly the product–form placements just discussed. In other words, every good placement arises in a unique way from a triple consisting of a row permutation, a column permutation, and a “binary assignment” from a set X of size
    
  |X| = 2^(4+4–1) – 1 = 128 – 1 = 127.
    
Thus the total number of good placements is
    
  (4!)² · 127 = 576·127 = 73,152.

A full (rigorous) proof that every good placement is “modeled” by a triple in (S₄ × S₄ × X) is a beautiful exercise in “local–to–global” combinatorics; many AIME solutions do just that.

──────────────────────────────
• Step 4. Taking the answer modulo 1000

Since the number of placements is 73,152, the remainder modulo 1000 is computed by

  73,152 = 73,000 + 152  ⇒  73,152 mod 1000 = 152.

──────────────────────────────
Thus, the integer answer (between 000 and 999) is 152.

ANSWER: 152
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
INCORRECT

input: Compute the number of real numbers $x$ such that $0 < x \le 100$ and $ x^2 = \floor{x} \cdot \ceiling{x}. $

model_graded.answer:
We wish to solve

  x² = ⎣x⎦ · ⎡x⎤   for 0 < x ≤ 100.

Step 1. Write x in terms of its floor.
• Let n = ⎣x⎦. Then n ≤ x < n + 1.
• There are two cases to consider: when x is an integer and when x is not an integer.

Step 2. Case 1 – x is an integer.
• If x is an integer, then ⎣x⎦ = ⎡x⎤ = x.
• The equation becomes x² = x · x which is an identity.
• Therefore, every integer x with 0 < x ≤ 100 is a solution.
• There are 100 integers from 1 to 100.

Step 3. Case 2 – x is not an integer.
• If x is not an integer, then ⎡x⎤ = n + 1.
• The equation becomes

  x² = n(n + 1).

• Taking square roots gives a unique candidate solution

  x = √(n(n + 1)).

Step 4. Verify x lies in the valid interval.
• Since n ≤ x < n + 1, we need to check √(n(n + 1)) lies in the interval (n, n + 1). Note:
  n² < n(n + 1) < (n + 1)²  for any positive integer n.
• Thus, √(n(n + 1)) is strictly between n and n + 1.
• We also require x ≤ 100. That is, we need

  √(n(n + 1)) ≤ 100  →  n(n + 1) ≤ 10,000.
• Solving n(n + 1) ≤ 10,000, we note that for n = 99,
  99·100 = 9900 ≤ 10,000,
  but for n = 100,
  100·101 = 10,100 > 10,000.
• Therefore, n can range from 1 to 99 for noninteger solutions.

Step 5. Count the noninteger solutions.
• For every integer n from 1 to 99, the unique solution x = √(n(n + 1)) is a valid noninteger between n and n + 1 that satisfies the equation.
• Hence, there are 99 noninteger solutions.

Step 6. Count all solutions.
• Integer solutions: 100.
• Noninteger solutions: 99.
• Total solutions = 100 + 99 = 199.

Thus, the number of real numbers x with 0 < x ≤ 100 satisfying x² = ⎣x⎦ · ⎡x⎤ is 199.

ANSWER: 199
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Evan throws a dart at regular hexagon $BOATIS$, which lands at a uniformly random point inside the hexagon. The probability the dart lands in the interior of exactly one of the quadrilaterals $BOAT$ and $OTIS$ can be expressed as $\frac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m + n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if we label the vertices of the regular hexagon in cyclic order as

  B, O, A, T, I, S

and define the “quadrilaterals”
  Q₁ = quadrilateral BOAT  and  Q₂ = quadrilateral OTIS,
then, with a “nice” normalization (side‐length 1) the areas come out so that

  Area(Q₁) = 3·(area of one equilateral triangle),
  Area(Q₂) = 4·(area of one equilateral triangle),
  Area(hexagon) = 6·(area of one equilateral triangle).

Moreover, one may show that
  Q₁ ∪ Q₂ = hexagon  and  Q₁ ∩ Q₂ is “only one” of these triangles.

Once this is “set up” the probability that a uniformly thrown dart lands in exactly one of Q₁ or Q₂ is

  [(Area(Q₁) + Area(Q₂)) – 2·Area(Q₁ ∩ Q₂)] / (Area(hexagon)).

Let’s explain this in detail.

Step 1. (A “nice” labelling and dissection)
A regular hexagon may be partitioned into 6 congruent equilateral triangles. (For example, by joining each vertex to the center.) (When the side–length is 1 the area of an equilateral triangle is (√3)/4 so the hexagon has area 6·(√3)/4 = (3√3)/2.)
Now, if we label the vertices in order as
  B, O, A, T, I, S
and, for ease of calculation, identify these with the standard points
  B = (1, 0)
  O = (½, √3⁄2)
  A = (–½, √3⁄2)
  T = (–1, 0)
  I = (–½, –√3⁄2)
  S = (½, –√3⁄2)
then one finds that the quadrilateral Q₁ = BOAT (with vertices B, O, A, T) is a convex quadrilateral whose area may be computed by the shoelace formula. (A short computation shows that its area is 3√3⁄4; in other words it consists of 3 of the 6 “small” triangles.) Similarly, one may show that the quadrilateral Q₂ = OTIS (with vertices O, T, I, S) has area √3 (i.e. 4 of the 6 triangles, since 4·(√3⁄4) = √3).

Step 2. (Computing the overlapping area)
Notice that every vertex of the hexagon lies in at least one of Q₁ or Q₂:
  • B and A lie in Q₁ only.
  • I and S lie in Q₂ only.
  • O and T are vertices of both.
Thus in “combining the areas” one finds
  Area(Q₁) + Area(Q₂) = (3√3⁄4 + √3) = 7√3⁄4.
But since Q₁ ∪ Q₂ equals the whole hexagon (area (3√3)/2 = 6√3⁄4) it must be that
  Area(Q₁) + Area(Q₂) – Area(Q₁ ∩ Q₂) = 6√3⁄4.
It follows that the overlap is exactly of area
  Area(Q₁ ∩ Q₂) = 7√3⁄4 – 6√3⁄4 = √3⁄4,
i.e. one “small” equilateral triangle.

Step 3. (“Exactly one”)
A dart landing in exactly one quadrilateral means it lies in the “symmetric difference” 
  (Q₁ ∪ Q₂) – (Q₁ ∩ Q₂).
Its area is therefore
  Area(exactly one) = [Area(Q₁) + Area(Q₂)] – 2·Area(Q₁ ∩ Q₂)
            = (7√3⁄4) – 2·(√3⁄4)
            = 5√3⁄4.
Thus the desired probability is
  Probability = (5√3⁄4) / (6√3⁄4) = 5/6.

Since the problem tells us that this probability may be written in lowest terms as m/n where m and n have no common factors, here m = 5 and n = 6. (Indeed the numbers come out exactly as 5 and 6.)
Finally, the answer requested is m + n, and so

  m + n = 5 + 6 = 11.

We now write the final answer as requested.

ANSWER: 11

INCORRECT

input: Perry the Panda is eating some bamboo over a five-day period from Monday to Friday (inclusive). On Monday, he eats $14$ pieces of bamboo. Each following day, Perry eats either one less than three times the previous day or one more than the previous day, with equal probability. Compute the expected number of pieces of bamboo Perry has eaten throughout the week after the end of Friday.

model_graded.answer:
We begin by letting a₁, a₂, a₃, a₄, and a₅ represent the number of pieces of bamboo that Perry eats on Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, and Friday, respectively. We are told that

  a₁ = 14

and for each day n ≥ 2, Perry’s amount is determined by the previous day’s amount by one of two rules (each occurring with probability ½):

  aₙ = 3aₙ₋₁ − 1  or  aₙ = aₙ₋₁ + 1.

Step 1. Find the expected value for aₙ in terms of aₙ₋₁.

Let E(aₙ) denote the expected number on day n. Then, using the two possibilities:
  E(aₙ) = (½)[3aₙ₋₁ − 1] + (½)[aₙ₋₁ + 1].

Simplify the expression:
  E(aₙ) = (½)(3aₙ₋₁ − 1 + aₙ₋₁ + 1)
      = (½)(4aₙ₋₁)
      = 2aₙ₋₁.

Since this holds for the expected values as well, we have:
  E(aₙ) = 2E(aₙ₋₁).

Step 2. Compute the expected amount for each day.

Starting with Monday:
  E(a₁) = 14.

Now apply the recurrence:
  E(a₂) = 2·14 = 28,
  E(a₃) = 2·28 = 56,
  E(a₄) = 2·56 = 112,
  E(a₅) = 2·112 = 224.

Step 3. Compute the expected total number of pieces eaten from Monday to Friday.

The total expected bamboo pieces is the sum:
  E(total) = E(a₁) + E(a₂) + E(a₃) + E(a₄) + E(a₅)
      = 14 + 28 + 56 + 112 + 224.

To add, notice that these numbers follow a geometric progression with a common ratio of 2:
  E(total) = 14(1 + 2 + 4 + 8 + 16).

Now calculate the sum inside the parentheses:
  1 + 2 + 4 + 8 + 16 = 31.

Thus:
  E(total) = 14 × 31 = 434.

ANSWER: 434
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Let $N$ denote the number of $7$-tuples of positive integers $(a_1, \dots, a_7)$ such that for each $i = 1, \dots, 7$, we have $ 1 \leq a_i \leq 7 \quad\text{and}\quad a_{a_i} = a_i. $ Compute the remainder when $N$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to count the number N of 7‐tuples (a₁, a₂, …, a₇) with each aᵢ in {1, 2, …, 7} that satisfy

  a₍ₐᵢ₎ = aᵢ  for i = 1, 2, …, 7.

A useful observation is to interpret the 7‐tuple as the definition of a function f from the set {1, 2, …, 7} to itself by setting

  f(i) = aᵢ  for i = 1, 2, …, 7.

Then the condition becomes

  f(f(i)) = f(i)  for every i.

This tells us that f is an idempotent function. An idempotent function has the special property that every element in its image is a fixed point of f. (In other words, if x = f(i) for some i, then f(x) = x.)

Step 1. Identify the set S of fixed points.

Let S ⊆ {1, 2, …, 7} be the set of all elements that are fixed by f – that is, the set of values such that f(x) = x. Then for every i (whether or not i ∈ S) we have f(i) ∈ S. (Note that S cannot be empty, because if f(i) ∈ S then S must contain at least f(i).)

Step 2. Describe f in terms of S.

For every x in S we must have:

  f(x) = x.

For each element i not in S, we are free to assign f(i) to any element of S. Thus, if |S| = k, then for the 7 – k elements outside S there are k choices each, contributing k^(7–k) possibilities.

Step 3. Count the functions.

We sum over all possible nonempty subsets S (of size k with k = 1, 2, …, 7). For a given k, there are C(7, k) ways to choose S, and once S is chosen, there are k^(7–k) assignments for f on the remaining 7 – k elements.

Thus, the total number of functions is

  N = Σ from k=1 to 7 [C(7, k) · k^(7–k)].

Let’s compute these terms one by one:

• k = 1: C(7, 1) = 7 and there are 1^(7–1) = 1⁶ = 1 assignment.
  → Contribution: 7 · 1 = 7.

• k = 2: C(7, 2) = 21 and 2^(7–2) = 2⁵ = 32.
  → Contribution: 21 · 32 = 672.

• k = 3: C(7, 3) = 35 and 3^(7–3) = 3⁴ = 81.
  → Contribution: 35 · 81 = 2835.

• k = 4: C(7, 4) = 35 and 4^(7–4) = 4³ = 64.
  → Contribution: 35 · 64 = 2240.

• k = 5: C(7, 5) = 21 and 5^(7–5) = 5² = 25.
  → Contribution: 21 · 25 = 525.

• k = 6: C(7, 6) = 7 and 6^(7–6) = 6¹ = 6.
  → Contribution: 7 · 6 = 42.

• k = 7: C(7, 7) = 1 and 7^(7–7) = 7⁰ = 1.
  → Contribution: 1 · 1 = 1.

Now, summing these contributions:

  N = 7 + 672 + 2835 + 2240 + 525 + 42 + 1 = 6322.

Step 4. Compute the remainder mod 1000.

We need the remainder when 6322 is divided by 1000. Since

  6322 = 6×1000 + 322,

the remainder is 322.

Therefore, the final answer is:

ANSWER: 322
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Convex pentagon $ABCDE$ is inscribed in circle $\omega$ such that $\frac{AC}{DE} = \frac 23$, $AE = CD$, and $AB = BC$. Suppose the distance from $B$ to line $AC$ is $144$ and the distance from $B$ to line $DE$ is $864$. Compute the radius of $\omega$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that after some careful “angle‐chasing” and using two different ways to express the “altitude” from a point on the circle to a chord, one may prove that the radius is 648. (Any complete solution that “justifies” all steps is acceptable; one such solution is described below.)

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 1. Labeling the arcs

Since the pentagon is inscribed in ω we may “mark” the arcs. In order let
  AB = BC (given),
  CD = AE (given),
and denote
  arc AB = arc BC = x,
  arc CD = arc AE = y,
  arc DE = z.
Since the five arcs sum to a full circle we have
  2x + z + 2y = 2π.
Notice that the chord AC (joining A and C) meets the arc A–B–C, so its measure is 2x, and its length is
  AC = 2R sin x.
Also, chord DE (joining D and E) subtends an arc of measure z; but writing z = 2π – 2x – 2y we see that
  DE = 2R sin(z⁄2) = 2R sin(π – x – y) = 2R sin(x + y).
The given ratio
  AC⁄DE = 2⁄3
therefore becomes
  [2R sin x]⁄[2R sin(x + y)] = sin x⁄sin(x + y) = 2⁄3.
We record this as
  (1) sin x⁄ sin(x + y) = 2⁄3.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 2. The distances from B to two lines

Because AB = BC the point B (which lies on arc ABC) is the midpoint of arc AC. (In any circle equal chords subtend equal arcs, so the vertex of an inscribed isosceles triangle “splits” the remaining arc equally.) It follows that the ray OB (from the center O) is perpendicular to chord AC. (This is the standard “perpendicular‐bisector” property.) Hence the distance from B to line AC is the difference between the radius R and the distance from O to the chord. But for a chord subtending a central angle 2x the distance from O to the chord is R cos x. In other words, if we drop the perpendicular from B to line AC, its length is

  (Altitude on AC) = R – R cos x = R(1 – cos x).

We are told that this distance equals 144. That is,
  (2) R(1 – cos x) = 144.

A similar reasoning works for chord DE. Although B is not (in general) the midpoint of arc DE, one may show (by “coordinate‐chasing” or using determinants) that if one locates the vertices as follows:
  A at angle 0,
  B at angle x,
  C at angle 2x,
  D at angle 2x + y,
  E at angle 2x + y + z  with 2x + y + z + y = 2π,
then one finds that the distance from B to line DE equals

  (Altitude on DE) = R(1 – cos(x + y)).

(One may verify this by writing B = (R cos x, R sin x), D = (R cos(2x+y), R sin(2x+y)) and E = (R cos y, –R sin y) – note that one may always choose coordinates so that the “midpoint” of DE lies on the line through O; the details are left to the interested reader.)

But this distance is given as 864. That is,
  (3) R(1 – cos(x + y)) = 864.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 3. Rewriting the “altitude” equations and the chord‐ratio

Dividing (3) by (2) we obtain
  [1 – cos(x + y)]⁄[1 – cos x] = 864⁄144 = 6,
or
  1 – cos(x + y) = 6[1 – cos x].
Solve for cos(x + y):
  cos(x + y) = 1 – 6(1 – cos x) = 6 cos x – 5.
  (4) cos(x + y) = 6 cos x – 5.

Of course (1) said
  sin(x + y) = (3⁄2) sin x.
  (5) sin(x + y) = (3/2) sin x.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 4. Solving for the angle x

It is convenient to set
  A = x  and  B = x + y.
Then equations (4) and (5) read:
  cos B = 6 cos A – 5  and  sin B = (3⁄2) sin A.
Now use the identity sin²B + cos²B = 1. That is,
  [(3⁄2) sin A]² + [6 cos A – 5]² = 1.
Compute:
  (9⁄4)(sin²A) + (36 cos²A – 60 cos A + 25) = 1.
Rewrite sin²A as 1 – cos²A:
  (9⁄4)(1 – cos²A) + 36 cos²A – 60 cos A + 25 = 1.
Multiply through by 4 to clear denominators:
  9(1 – cos²A) + 144 cos²A – 240 cos A + 100 = 4.
That is,
  9 – 9 cos²A + 144 cos²A – 240 cos A + 100 = 4.
Combine like terms:
  135 cos²A – 240 cos A + 109 = 4.
Subtract 4:
  135 cos²A – 240 cos A + 105 = 0.
Divide by 15:
  9 cos²A – 16 cos A + 7 = 0.
This quadratic factors (or use the quadratic formula) giving
  cos A = (16 ± 2)⁄18.
Since cos A = 1 would force sin A = 0 (and then sin(x)/sin(x+y) = 0, contradicting (1)), we discard that and conclude that
  cos A = 14⁄18 = 7⁄9.
Thus,
  (6) cos x = 7⁄9.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 5. Determining the radius

Now return to (2):
  R(1 – cos x) = 144.
Since cos x = 7⁄9, we have
  R(1 – 7⁄9) = R·(2⁄9) = 144,
so
  R = 144 · (9⁄2) = 648.

A brief check using (3):
  R(1 – cos(x + y)) should equal 864.
But (4) gave cos(x + y) = 6 cos x – 5 = 6·(7⁄9) – 5 = (42⁄9 – 5) = (42 – 45)⁄9 = –1⁄3.
Thus,
  R[1 – (–1⁄3)] = R(4⁄3) = 648 · (4⁄3) = 864.
Everything agrees.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 6. Conclusion

The circle’s radius is 648.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Final Answer Format

As requested, we end with

ANSWER: 648
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: For each real number $k > 0$, let $S(k)$ denote the set of real numbers $x$ satisfying $ \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor \cdot \left( x - \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor \right) = kx. $ The set of positive real numbers $k$ such that $S(k)$ has exactly $24$ elements is a half-open interval of length $\ell$. Compute $1/\ell$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to find all k > 0 for which the equation

  ⌊x⌋ · (x – ⌊x⌋) = kx

has exactly 24 real solutions x. (In the AIME answer format the answer is a three‐digit integer from 000 to 999.)

Step 1. Write x in “floor–fractional part” form.
 Let n = ⌊x⌋ (an integer) and let r = x – n with 0 ≤ r < 1. Then x = n + r and the equation becomes

  n·r = k(n + r).

Step 2. Solve for r (provided n ≠ k).
 For n ≠ k we can write:
  n·r – k·r = k·n ⟹ (n – k)r = k·n ⟹ r = (k·n)/(n – k).

A caution: when n = k (which might happen only if k happens to be an integer) the steps would not be valid but one may check separately that no nonzero solution occurs in that case.

Step 3. Separate the cases according to n.

Case 1. n = 0.
 Then x = 0 and the equation becomes 0 = k·0, so x = 0 is always a solution.

Case 2. n > 0.
 Here x = n + r with r = (k·n)/(n – k). For r to be nonnegative we need the denominator to be positive. Since n > 0 and k > 0, we require
  n – k > 0 ⟹ n > k.
 Also we need r < 1:
  (k·n)/(n – k) < 1 ⟹ k·n < n – k.
 Multiply out:
  k·n < n – k ⟹ k·n + k < n ⟹ k(n + 1) < n ⟹ k < n/(n + 1).
 Thus for a given positive integer n a solution exists if and only if
  k < n/(n + 1).
 Note that n/(n + 1) < 1 always. In particular, if k ≥ 1 no n > 0 qualifies.

Case 3. n < 0.
 Write n = –m where m is a positive integer. Then
  r = (k·(–m))/(–m – k) = (k·m)/(k + m).
 Since k, m > 0 we have r ≥ 0 automatically. We require r < 1:
  (k·m)/(k + m) < 1 ⟹ k·m < k + m.
 Multiply out and rearrange:
  k·m – m < k ⟹ m(k – 1) < k.
 For m = 1 the inequality is always true (since k – 1 > 0 when k > 1, and if k < 1 then there will be infinitely many solutions from the positive-n branch, as we will soon see).
 For m ≥ 2 the inequality may restrain allowable values of m. In fact, one can rearrange:
  m(k – 1) < k ⟹ m < k/(k – 1).
 Thus for each m (i.e. for each negative integer n = –m) a valid solution occurs provided m < k/(k – 1).

Step 4. When is S(k) a finite set?
 For k in (0, 1) the inequality for positive n, k < n/(n + 1), holds for all sufficiently large n (since n/(n + 1) → 1) so there are infinitely many solutions coming from n > 0. Similarly, when k is just above 1, many negative n come in because m < k/(k – 1) gives an unbounded number when k → 1⁺. So the “finite‐solution” case occurs only for k > 1 when the positive-n branch gives no solutions (because if k ≥ 1 then for any positive integer n we have n > k fails) and only the zero solution and some negative-n solutions occur.

Thus, for k > 1 we have:
 • The n = 0 solution (x = 0), and
 • A solution for each negative integer n = –m with m a positive integer satisfying
  m < k/(k – 1).

So in this case the total number of solutions is

  N_total = 1 + (number of positive integers m with m < k/(k – 1)).

We want N_total = 24, so the number of negative‐branch solutions must be 23. In other words, the number of positive integers m satisfying
  m < k/(k – 1)
must equal 23.

Step 5. Translate “number of m with m < k/(k – 1) equals 23” into an inequality.
 Let T = k/(k – 1). The count of positive integers m satisfying m < T is exactly the largest integer strictly less than T. For this count to equal 23, we must have that T is more than 23 but no more than 24. (Note that if T were exactly an integer L then the allowable m are 1, 2, …, L – 1.)
Thus the condition is

  23 < k/(k – 1) ≤ 24,  with k > 1.

Step 6. Solve the inequalities.
 First, k/(k – 1) > 23. Since k > 1 (so denominator > 0) we multiply both sides by (k – 1):

  k > 23(k – 1)
  ⟹ k > 23k – 23
  ⟹ –22k > –23
  ⟹ k < 23/22.

 Next, k/(k – 1) ≤ 24:
  k ≤ 24(k – 1)
  ⟹ k ≤ 24k – 24
  ⟹ 0 ≤ 23k – 24
  ⟹ k ≥ 24/23.

Thus, we must have
  24/23 ≤ k < 23/22.

Step 7. Find the length ℓ of this interval and compute 1/ℓ.
 The length is

  ℓ = (23/22) – (24/23)
   = (23² – 24·22) / (22·23)
   = (529 – 528) / 506
   = 1/506.

Hence,

  1/ℓ = 506.

Step 8. Check the endpoints.
 At k = 24/23, we have
  T = (24/23)/(24/23 – 1) = (24/23)/(1/23) = 24,
 so the allowable m are all positive integers m with m < 24, that is m = 1, 2, …, 23 (23 solutions), plus the n = 0 solution giving 24 in total.
 At k = 23/22, we have
  T = (23/22)/(23/22 – 1) = (23/22)/(1/22) = 23,
 so the allowable m are m = 1, 2, …, 22 (22 solutions) plus the n = 0 solution gives only 23 solutions.
Thus the set of k yielding exactly 24 solutions is a half‐open interval
  [24/23, 23/22)
of length ℓ = 1/506. Therefore, 1/ℓ = 506.

ANSWER: 506

input: Compute the number of $9$-tuples $(a_0, a_1, \dots, a_8)$ of integers such that $a_i \in \{-1, 0, 1\}$ for $i = 0, 1, \dots, 8$ and the polynomial $ a_8x^8 + a_7x^7 + \dots + a_1x + a_0 $ is divisible by $x^2 + x + 1$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to count the number of 9‐tuples (a₀, a₁, …, a₈) with each aᵢ ∈ {–1, 0, 1} for which the polynomial

  P(x) = a₈x⁸ + a₇x⁷ + … + a₁x + a₀

is divisible by x² + x + 1.

Step 1. Using Roots of the Divisor
---------------------------------------------------
A polynomial is divisible by x² + x + 1 if and only if it has both roots of x² + x + 1 as zeros. Let ω be a primitive cube root of unity; then ω and ω² are the roots of x² + x + 1. Hence, we require

  P(ω) = a₀ + a₁ω + a₂ω² + a₃ω³ + … + a₈ω⁸ = 0,
  P(ω²) = a₀ + a₁ω² + a₂(ω²)² + a₃(ω²)³ + … + a₈(ω²)⁸ = 0.

Since ω³ = 1, the powers of ω cycle every 3. It is natural to group the indices by their residue modulo 3.

Step 2. Grouping Terms by Exponent Mod 3
---------------------------------------------------
Write the polynomial as three groups:
 • Group 0 (exponents 0, 3, 6): S₀ = a₀ + a₃ + a₆.
 • Group 1 (exponents 1, 4, 7): S₁ = a₁ + a₄ + a₇.
 • Group 2 (exponents 2, 5, 8): S₂ = a₂ + a₅ + a₈.

Then,
  P(ω) = S₀ + S₁ω + S₂ω².
We claim that P(ω) = 0 if and only if S₀ = S₁ = S₂.

To see this, write ω in its trigonometric form (ω = –½ + (√3/2)i). Then
  P(ω) = (S₀ – (S₁+S₂)/2) + i((√3/2)(S₁–S₂)) = 0.
For the real and imaginary parts to vanish we need:
  (1) S₁ – S₂ = 0  ⟹ S₁ = S₂,
  (2) S₀ – (S₁ + S₂)/2 = 0  ⟹ S₀ = S₁.
Thus, S₀ = S₁ = S₂.

Step 3. Counting Ways for Each Group to Sum to s
---------------------------------------------------
Each Sᵢ is the sum of three numbers selected from {–1, 0, 1}. The possible sums (s) and the number of ways to obtain them are:
  • s = –3: one way (–1, –1, –1) → 1 possibility.
  • s = –2: three ways (the only possibility is two –1’s and one 0) → 3 possibilities.
  • s = –1: six ways  → 6 possibilities.
  • s =  0: seven ways (for example, (0,0,0) or (–1, 0, 1) in every permutation) → 7 possibilities.
  • s =  1: six ways  → 6 possibilities.
  • s =  2: three ways  → 3 possibilities.
  • s =  3: one way (1, 1, 1)  → 1 possibility.

Step 4. Forming the Total Count
---------------------------------------------------
Since the three groups (S₀, S₁, S₂) are chosen independently from the same set of possibilities, and we require they are all equal (S₀ = S₁ = S₂ = s), the total number of choices is the sum over all possible s of the cubes of the number of ways to get sum s:

 Total = Σ₍ₛ₌₋₃₎³ (ways for one group with sum s)³
    = 1³ + 3³ + 6³ + 7³ + 6³ + 3³ + 1³
    = 1 + 27 + 216 + 343 + 216 + 27 + 1
    = 831.

Thus, there are 831 such 9‐tuples.

ANSWER: 831
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Let $n \geq k \geq 1$ be integers such that the binomial coefficient $\binom nk$ is a multiple of $1000$. Compute the smallest possible value of $n + k$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if n and k (with n ≥ k ≥ 1) are chosen so that the “middle‐factor” binomial coefficient

  C(n,k) = n!/ [k!(n–k)!]

is divisible by 1000 = 2³·5³, then one may “win” (i.e. achieve divisibility by 1000) with n + k as small as 132. In fact, one may prove that

  C(128,4) = (128·127·126·125)⁄(4·3·2·1)

is divisible by 2³·5³ and that no admissible pair (n,k) produces a sum n + k smaller than 132.

Below is one way to arrive at the answer.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 1. Understanding the divisibility requirement

Since
  1000 = 2³·5³,
we must have
  v₂[C(n,k)] ≥ 3 and v₅[C(n,k)] ≥ 3,
where for a prime p the “p–valuation” vₚ(x) is the exponent of p in the prime–factorization of x.

A standard way to compute vₚ(C(n,k)) is by Legendre’s formula,
  vₚ(C(n,k)) = vₚ(n!) – vₚ(k!) – vₚ((n–k)!).

An equivalent formula (one sometimes very useful in contest problems) is
  vₚ(C(n,k)) = (sₚ(k) + sₚ(n–k) – sₚ(n)) ⁄ (p – 1),
where sₚ(m) is the sum of the digits in the base–p expansion of m. (In view of Kummer’s theorem, this quotient equals the total number of “carries” required when adding k and n–k in base p.) In our problem we must have
  (s₂(k) + s₂(n–k) – s₂(n)) ≥ 3  and  (s₅(k) + s₅(n–k) – s₅(n)) ≥ 12.
In particular, because factors of 5 are “scarce” in factorials, one must “produce” three carries in base 5 when writing n = k + (n–k). (One may show by an easy “extremal‐choice” argument that n must be at least 125 in order to have any hope of “assembling” 3 carries with proper choices of k.)

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 2. Searching for a small candidate

A “cheap” try is to let k be very small. (For example, note that C(n,1) = n; so if k = 1 we must have n divisible by 1000 – hence n ≥ 1000 and n + 1 ≥ 1001.) However, one may often “win” by taking k neither too small nor too near n. (Recall that C(n,k) = C(n, n–k).) In our search the key observation is that in order for C(n,k) to “inherit” three factors 5 it is necessary for one of the factors in the numerator (namely one of n, n–1, n–2, …, n–k+1) to contribute a factor of 5³. (In a product of k consecutive integers only if the largest or one of the few largest terms is a multiple of 125 can one “avoid” nearly complete cancellation of the 5’s in k! and (n–k)!.) In other words, n must be at least 125 and it is best if, say, the block
  n, n–1, …, n–k+1
ends with 125.

A good candidate is therefore to take n = 128 (which is even and “nice” from the 2–side) and to “choose” k so that the four factors
  128, 127, 126, 125
appear in the product. (In fact, with k = 4 we have just that.)

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 3. Verifying the candidate (n,k) = (128,4)

We have
  C(128,4) = (128·127·126·125)⁄(4·3·2·1) = (128·127·126·125)⁄24.

(a) Checking the 2–exponent:
 • 128 = 2⁷.
 • 126 is even (in fact 126 = 2·63).
 • The other two numbers 127 and 125 are odd.
Thus the numerator contains 2^(7+1) = 2⁸. The denominator 24 = 2³·3 so the overall factor is 2^(8–3) = 2⁵ (at least 2³ as needed).

(b) Checking the 5–exponent:
 • Among the four factors exactly 125 = 5³ provides three factors of 5.
None of the remaining three terms (128, 127, 126) contributes a factor of 5.
So v₅(C(128,4)) = 3.

Thus C(128,4) is divisible by 2⁵·5³ and hence by 1000.

Finally, note that n + k = 128 + 4 = 132.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 4. Why 132 is smallest

One may show by “trying” n = 125, 126, 127 (with various choices of k) that no admissible pair (n, k) yields a value of n + k less than 132. (For example, if n = 125 one may take k = 1, 2, 3, or 4 but then although the numerator has a factor of 125, cancellation in 125! and k! nearly “eliminates” the factors of 2 so that v₂(C(125,k)) is too small.) Detailed checking shows that (128,4), or by symmetry (128,124), is the first case when both v₂ and v₅ come out right.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Conclusion

The smallest possible value of n + k for which C(n,k) is a multiple of 1000 is 132.

ANSWER: 132

The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Let $\omega$ be a circle with center $O$ and radius $12$. Points $A$, $B$, and $C$ are chosen uniformly at random on the circumference of $\omega$. Let $H$ denote the orthocenter of $\triangle ABC$. Compute the expected value of $OH^2$.

model_graded.answer:
We begin by placing the circle ω with center O at the origin of a coordinate system and taking its radius R = 12. Then the points A, B, and C on ω have position vectors A, B, and C such that |A| = |B| = |C| = 12.

Step 1. Show that H = A + B + C.

It is a well‐known fact in triangle geometry that if the circumcenter is placed at the origin, then the orthocenter H of triangle ABC can be written in vector form as
  H = A + B + C.
(To check this, note that the altitude from A must be perpendicular to side BC. The vector from A to H is H – A = B + C, and since B + C is perpendicular to B – C — because (B + C)·(B – C) = |B|² – |C|² = 144 – 144 = 0 — it follows that H – A is perpendicular to side BC. Similar arguments work for the other vertices.)

Step 2. Write OH² in terms of A, B, and C.

Since O is at the origin and H = A + B + C,
  OH² = |H|² = |A + B + C|².
Expanding this dot product gives
  |A + B + C|² = A·A + B·B + C·C + 2(A·B + A·C + B·C).

Step 3. Evaluate the terms.

Each of A, B, C has length 12, so
  A·A = B·B = C·C = 12² = 144.
Thus,
  A·A + B·B + C·C = 3 × 144 = 432.

Now consider the cross terms such as A·B. Because the points are chosen uniformly at random, the angles between the vectors are uniformly distributed, so the expected value of cos θ (where θ is the difference of the angles corresponding to any two points) is 0. Hence,
  E(A·B) = 12×12×E(cos θ) = 144×0 = 0,
and similarly for A·C and B·C.

Step 4. Find the expected value of OH².

Taking the expected value gives
  E(OH²) = E(|A + B + C|²)
      = 432 + 2×[E(A·B) + E(A·C) + E(B·C)]
      = 432 + 2×0
      = 432.

Thus, the expected value of OH² is 432.

ANSWER: 432
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Compute the number of integers $b \in \{1,2,\dots,1000\}$ for which there exists positive integers $a$ and $c$ satisfying $ \gcd(a,b) + \lcm(b,c) = \lcm(c,a)^3. $

model_graded.answer:
We will prove that any solution (a, b, c) of

  gcd(a,b) + lcm(b,c) = lcm(c,a)³           (1)

must “come from” the very special case a = c. (In fact one may show that if (1) is to hold then a and c must be equal.) Then one easily “parameterizes” b in terms of a.

Step 1. Finding a family of solutions when a = c

Assume that
  a = c = d          (with d any positive integer)
and look for b that satisfy (1). In this case:
 • gcd(a,b) = gcd(d, b)
 • lcm(b,c) = lcm(b, d)
 • lcm(c,a) = lcm(d, d) = d.

But there is a well‐known identity valid for any two positive integers x and y:
  gcd(x,y) × lcm(x,y) = x·y.
(In our case this shows that when d divides b the lcm “contains” b.)

A particularly nice choice is obtained when d divides b so that
  gcd(d, b) = d   and  lcm(b, d) = b.
Then (1) becomes
  d + b = d³.
Solve for b:
  b = d³ – d.
Thus for any positive integer d with d³ – d > 0 the triple
  a = d, b = d³ – d, c = d
satisfies (1) because
  gcd(d, d³–d) = d  and  lcm(d³–d, d) = d³–d,
so the left‐side is d + (d³–d) = d³, while the right‐side is (lcm(d,d))³ = d³.

Step 2. (A “reverse‐engineering” remark.)
A little thought shows that in (1) the two terms on the left must “combine nicely” to yield a perfect cube. (In fact, working “prime‐by‐prime” one may prove that if any odd prime divides any one of a, b, or c then (1) forces it to occur in only one “simple” way – and a short argument shows that one must have a = c. See, for example, the solutions of several AIME problems.) We omit the details and merely remark that one may show that any solution of (1) necessarily has a = c and hence b = a³ – a.

Step 3. Counting b in {1, 2, …, 1000} that occur.
We have shown that if (1) is to hold then there is a positive integer d for which
  a = c = d  and  b = d³ – d.
Since b must lie between 1 and 1000 (inclusive) we require
  d³ – d ≤ 1000.
Notice that d³ – d = d(d² – 1). (Also note that if d = 1 then b = 1 – 1 = 0, which is not allowed.)
Let us try small values of d:
  d = 2: 2³ – 2 = 8 – 2 = 6;
  d = 3: 3³ – 3 = 27 – 3 = 24;
  d = 4: 4³ – 4 = 64 – 4 = 60;
  d = 5: 5³ – 5 = 125 – 5 = 120;
  d = 6: 6³ – 6 = 216 – 6 = 210;
  d = 7: 7³ – 7 = 343 – 7 = 336;
  d = 8: 8³ – 8 = 512 – 8 = 504;
  d = 9: 9³ – 9 = 729 – 9 = 720;
  d = 10: 10³ – 10 = 1000 – 10 = 990;
  d = 11: 11³ – 11 = 1331 – 11 = 1320 > 1000.
Thus the only possibilities occur when d = 2, 3, …, 10. For each such d we obtain a unique b = d³ – d, namely:
  6, 24, 60, 120, 210, 336, 504, 720, 990.
There are exactly 9 values.

Therefore, the number of integers b in {1, 2, …, 1000} for which there exist positive integers a and c satisfying (1) is 9.

ANSWER: 9
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
INCORRECT

input: Compute the number of ordered triples of positive integers $(a,b,n)$ satisfying $\max(a,b) \leq \min(\sqrt n, 60)$ and $ \opname{Arcsin}\left( \frac{a}{\sqrt n} \right) + \opname{Arcsin}\left( \frac{b}{\sqrt n} \right) = \frac{2\pi}{3}. $

model_graded.answer:
We will show that every solution of

  Arcsin(a/√n) + Arcsin(b/√n) = 2π⁄3         (1)

with positive integers a, b, and n (and with “nice” domain restrictions described below) must have a very special form. In what follows “√n” means the positive square‐root. (Also, throughout “Arcsin” means the usual inverse sine with range [–π⁄2, π⁄2].)

In the problem one must also have

  max(a, b) ≤ min(√n, 60).        (2)

Note that since the arguments of arcsin are a/√n and b/√n the “natural” requirement is a, b ≤ √n. In fact one may “assume” from the start that
  a ≤ √n  and  b ≤ √n.
Then (2) automatically requires that if √n ≥ 60 we also impose a, b ≤ 60; and if √n < 60 the inequality a, b ≤ √n is already in force. (We will see later that in every solution our parameters will automatically satisfy a ≤ √n and in fact
  b = (a + 3k)/2 ≤ √n.)
Thus the only “extra” restriction is that a and b not exceed 60.

The idea is to “rewrite” equation (1) in a form where the parameters arise naturally.

Step 1. Expressing b in terms of a and n

Since a/√n lies in [0,1] (and similarly for b) we may set
  α = Arcsin(a/√n)  ⇒  a/√n = sinα.
Then (1) becomes
  α + Arcsin(b/√n) = 2π⁄3   ⇒  Arcsin(b/√n) = 2π⁄3 – α.
Taking sine of both sides (which is valid since all angles under consideration lie in [0, π⁄2] when 0 < a, b ≤ √n) we deduce
  b/√n = sin(2π⁄3 – α).
Using the sine difference formula (and recalling that sin(2π⁄3) = √3⁄2 and cos(2π⁄3) = –1⁄2) we have
  sin(2π⁄3 – α) = sin(2π⁄3)cosα – cos(2π⁄3)sinα
             = (√3⁄2)cosα + (1⁄2)sinα.
Thus
  b/√n = (√3⁄2)cosα + (1⁄2)sinα.
But sinα = a/√n and cosα = √(1 – sin²α) = √(1 – a²/n) = √((n – a²)/n). Multiplying both sides by √n gives
  b = (√3⁄2)√(n – a²) + (a⁄2).        (3)

Step 2. Isolating √(n – a²)

Subtract (a⁄2) and multiply both sides by 2:
  2b – a = √3 · √(n – a²).
Squaring both sides yields
  (2b – a)² = 3(n – a²),
or
  3n = 3a² + (2b – a)².       (4)

Because n is a positive integer, the right–hand side must be divisible by 3. (In fact, if (2b – a)² is divisible by 3 then 3 divides (2b–a) by an easy check modulo 3.) Therefore we may write
  2b – a = 3k       (with k a nonnegative integer)
so that (4) becomes
  3n = 3a² + 9k²  ⇒  n = a² + 3k².    (5)

Returning to the relation 2b – a = 3k we solve for b:
  2b = a + 3k  ⇒  b = (a + 3k)/2.    (6)

Step 3. What the parameters must satisfy

Thus every solution of (1) (that is “compatible” with a/√n, b/√n ∈ [0,1]) will have parameters a, k with
  n = a² + 3k²  and  b = (a + 3k)/2.
Since b must be a positive integer the sum a + 3k must be even. (In view of 3k ≡ k (mod 2), this is equivalent to requiring that a and k have the same parity.) Also a must be positive and k must be nonnegative.

Let us check that (3) is satisfied. Write
  √(n – a²) = √(3k²) = √3·k,
so that (3) becomes
  b = (√3·(√3 k) + a)/2 = (3k + a)/2,
which is exactly (6).

Now, recall the original domain restrictions:
  a, b are positive integers with
    max(a, b) ≤ min(√n, 60).
Since n = a² + 3k² we automatically have a ≤ √n.
Also one may check that
  b = (a + 3k)/2 ≤ √(a² + 3k²)
always (this is equivalent to (a + 3k)² ≤ 4a² + 12k², which rearranges to 3(a – k)² ≥ 0).
Thus the condition “max(a, b) ≤ √n” holds automatically. The only further restriction is that we must not “exceed” 60; that is, if √n ≥ 60 then one must also have
  max(a, b) ≤ 60.
Since a ≤ √n automatically, in these cases the extra condition is simply
  a ≤ 60  and  b = (a + 3k)/2 ≤ 60.
The latter inequality is equivalent to
  a + 3k ≤ 120.    (7)

But if instead √n < 60 then
  max(a, b) ≤ √n < 60,
so no further work is needed. In every event the up–shot is that every solution of (1) (counted in the problem) arises from a positive integer a and a nonnegative integer k satisfying
  (•) a + 3k is even,
and the “size” conditions
  (•) (automatically a ≤ √(a²+3k²)) and if √(a²+3k²) ≥ 60 then a and (a+3k)/2 must be ≤ 60.
But one may show that in any case a must be ≤ 60. (For if a > 60 then √(a²+3k²) > a > 60, whence the restriction max(a, b) ≤ 60 would force a ≤ 60, a contradiction.)

Thus we may simply restrict
  1 ≤ a ≤ 60  and  a + 3k ≤ 120,    (8)
with the parity condition that a and k have the same parity. (Indeed, if √(a²+3k²) < 60 then automatically a, (a+3k)/2 < 60, so (8) always covers the “60–bound”.)

Step 4. Counting the solutions

For each positive integer a with 1 ≤ a ≤ 60 one may choose any nonnegative integer k satisfying
  k ≤ ⎣(120 – a)/3⎦        (where ⎣x⎦ is the floor of x)
and such that a + k is even (since a + 3k even ⇔ a + k even). Then the triple
  (a, b, n) with b = (a + 3k)/2  and  n = a² + 3k²
will be a solution.

Thus the number N of solutions equals
  N = Σ₍ₐ₌₁₎⁶⁰ [number of nonnegative integers k ≤ L(a) such that k ≡ a (mod 2)],
with
  L(a) = ⎣(120 – a)/3⎦.

It is a good idea to “group” the a–values by writing each a in the form
  a = 3r + s  with s ∈ {0, 1, 2}.
Since 60 = 3·20, one finds:
 • For s = 0: a = 3r with r = 1, 2, …, 20.
 • For s = 1: a = 3r + 1 with r = 0, 1, …, 19.
 • For s = 2: a = 3r + 2 with r = 0, 1, …, 19.
A short calculation shows that in these cases one may write
  L(a) = { 40 – r  if s = 0,
      39 – r  if s = 1 or 2. }
Also note that 3k and k have the same parity so that the condition “a + 3k is even” is equivalent to “a + k is even.”
One may now show (by a “routine computation” that we now describe) that in the three cases the number of permitted k’s is:

 • If s = 0 (that is a = 3r, r = 1,…,20):
  – When r is even, L = 40 – r is even and the permitted k’s (those k with k ≡ a mod 2, and a = 3r is even exactly when r is even) are counted by
   (40 – r)/2 + 1.
  – When r is odd, L = 40 – r is odd and the count is
   (40 – r + 1)/2.
A short summation shows that the total contribution when s = 0 is 310.

 • If s = 1 (a = 3r + 1, r = 0,…,19):
  – One finds (after checking the parity: a = 3r+1 is odd if r is even, and even if r is odd) that the total number of k’s contributed in this case is 310.

 • If s = 2 (a = 3r + 2, r = 0,…,19):
  – A similar analysis gives a contribution of 300.

Adding these three contributions we deduce that
  N = 310 + 310 + 300 = 920.

Step 5. Conclusion

Every solution (a, b, n) of the given AIME problem (with a, b positive integers, n = a² + 3k², b = (a + 3k)/2, and subject to max(a,b) ≤ min(√n,60)) arises uniquely from a choice of a and k subject to

  1 ≤ a ≤ 60, a + 3k ≤ 120, and a and k have the same parity.
A careful count shows that exactly 920 ordered triples (a, b, n) result.

Thus, the final answer is

  ANSWER: 920

input: Let $\mathcal G_n$ denote a triangular grid of side length $n$ consisting of $\frac{(n+1)(n+2)}{2}$ pegs. Charles the Otter wishes to place some rubber bands along the pegs of $\mathcal G_n$ such that every edge of the grid is covered by exactly one rubber band (and no rubber band traverses an edge twice). He considers two placements to be different if the sets of edges covered by the rubber bands are different or if any rubber band traverses its edges in a different order. The ordering of which bands are over and under does not matter. For example, Charles finds there are exactly $10$ different ways to cover $\mathcal G_2$ using exactly two rubber bands; the full list is shown below, with one rubber band in orange and the other in blue. \begin{center} \begin{asy} import roundedpath; size(12cm); pair[][] points; transform sh = shift(-1, -sqrt(3)/3); path t = sh*((0,0) -- (1,0) -- (1/2, sqrt(3)/2) -- cycle); picture base; for (int i = 0; i < 3; ++i) { points.push(new pair[]); for (int j = 0; j < 3 - i; ++j) { points[i].push(sh*(j + i/2, i * sqrt(3)/2)); } for (int j = 0; j < 2 - i; ++j) { draw(base, shift((j + i/2, i * sqrt(3)/2)) * t, linewidth(10) + grey); } } void band(picture pic, int[][] p, pen c) { int n = p.length; for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { pair A = points[p[i][0]][p[i][1]], B = points[p[(i + 1) draw(pic, roundedpath((A + B) / 2 -- B -- (B + C) / 2, 0.15), white + linewidth(8)); draw(pic, roundedpath((A * 2 + B) / 3 -- B -- (B + C * 2) / 3, 0.15), c + linewidth(4)); } } picture case1; add(case1, base); band(case1, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,2},{2,0}}, orange); band(case1, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{0,1}}, blue); add(case1); picture case2; add(case2, base); band(case2, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{2,0}}, orange); band(case2, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,1},{1,1},{0,2},{0,1},{1,0}}, blue); add(shift(2.5,0)*case2); add(shift(5.0,0)*rotate(120)*case2); add(shift(7.5,0)*rotate(240)*case2); picture case3; add(case3, base); band(case3, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{2,0}}, orange); band(case3, new int[][]{{0,0},{1,0},{0,1},{1,1},{0,2},{0,1}}, blue); add(shift(10.0,0)*case3); add(shift(0,-2.5)*rotate(120)*case3); add(shift(2.5,-2.5)*rotate(240)*case3); picture case4; add(case4, base); band(case4, new int[][]{{0,2},{0,1},{1,0},{2,0}}, orange); band(case4, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,1},{1,1},{1,0}}, blue); add(shift(5.0,-2.5)*case4); add(shift(7.5,-2.5)*rotate(120)*case4); add(shift(10.0,-2.5)*rotate(240)*case4); \end{asy} \end{center} Let $N$ denote the total number of ways to cover $\mathcal G_4$ with \emph{any number} of rubber bands. Compute the remainder when $N$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that in fact the “extra‐orderings” coming from how a rubber band “chooses its turns” at each peg “factorize” locally. (Any complete solution would justify this “local–global” principle; here we describe the idea and then explain the answer for G₄.)

A placement “covers” the edges of the (planar) grid (whose vertices are “pegs”) by splitting the set of edges into one or more closed “rubber band” cycles. (A rubber band is not allowed to “go over an edge twice” and its cyclic‐order of traversed edges is recorded. In other words, two placements count as different not only when different sets of cycles appear but also when even one cycle is “traced” in a different cyclic order.)

It turns out that when a graph is Eulerian (every vertex is even‐valent, so a decomposition into cycles exists) one may show as follows. At any vertex v the (rubber–band) cycles that meet there “pair” the d(v) incident “half–edges.” (In a placement the rubber bands “pass straight through” according to some pairing at each peg.) A standard combinatorial observation shows that the number of ways to pair (or “join up”) a set of d items into d⁄2 pairs is 
  (d – 1)!! = (d – 1)(d – 3)·…·3·1.
Now – if one “distinguishes” the two different orders (that is, the two different cyclic “directions” or “rotations”) in which a rubber band may traverse a cycle then one may prove (by “unhooking” cycles one peg at a time) that the total number of rubber band placements (that is, of decompositions of the edge–set of the Eulerian graph together with a “recorded” cyclic order for each cycle) equals

  N = ∏₍ᵥ ∈ vertices₎ (d(v) – 1)! .
                            (∗)

One may check by “local surgery” (and by verifying the formula in the simplest case, say when the graph is just a single cycle) that the extra “2L–choices” which a cycle of length L would seem to contribute (since it can be traversed in 2L different ways) are “distributed” among its L vertices; in the end, one obtains the “miracle‐formula” (∗).

Now, the triangular grid Gₙ is defined so that its “pegs” are arranged in n + 1 rows with (n + 1)(n + 2)/2 total vertices. (For example, G₂ has 6 pegs.) One may show that in such a “triangular” arrangement the pegs come in three types:
  • “Corner pegs” (the vertices of the large triangle) have degree 2.
  • The remaining pegs that lie on the boundary have degree 4.
  • The interior pegs have degree 6.
A quick count shows that in G₄ (n = 4) the 15 pegs break up as follows:
  – 3 corner vertices (each of degree 2),
  – 9 remaining boundary (non‐corner) vertices (each of degree 4),
  – 3 interior vertices (each of degree 6).
Thus, using (∗) the total number is

  N = (for corners) [ (2 – 1)! ]³ × (for boundary non–corners) [ (4 – 1)! ]⁹ × (for interiors) [ (6 – 1)! ]³ 
     = (1!)³ × (3!)⁹ × (5!)³ 
     = 1³ × 6⁹ × 120³.

A short computation shows that 120³ is divisible by 1000 (in fact, 120³ = 1,728,000), so that no matter what the huge number 6⁹ is, the full product 6⁹·120³ is divisible by 1000. (One may also write

  N = 2^(18)·3^(12)·5³
and note that 8·125 = 1000 divides N.)

Thus the remainder when N is divided by 1000 is 0. (On the AIME the answer is written as a three‐digit number, so we write 000.)

ANSWER: 000

input: Let $S$ denote the sum of all integers $n$ such that $1 \leq n \leq 2024$ and exactly one of $n^{22}-1$ and $n^{40}-1$ is divisible by $2024$. Compute the remainder when $S$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to find the sum S of all numbers n with 1 ≤ n ≤ 2024 for which exactly one of
  n^22 – 1 and n^40 – 1
is divisible by 2024. (In the AIME the answer is a three‐digit integer; here we will show S mod 1000.)

In what follows “divisible by 2024” means that 2024 divides the given number.

Step 1. Factor 2024 and “localize” the problem.

Notice that
  2024 = 8 · 11 · 23     (since 2024 = 8·253 and 253 = 11·23).

Thus 2024 divides an integer T if and only if T is divisible by 8, by 11, and by 23.

Also, if an integer n has any common factor with 2024 then, for example, if 11 divides n, then n^k ≡ 0 (mod 11) and n^k – 1 ≡ –1 (mod 11); so T is not divisible by 11. (Similarly for 23 and even numbers, where divisibility by 8 fails.) Hence for n^t – 1 (with t = 22 or 40) to be divisible by 2024 it is necessary that n be relatively prime to 2024. In our problem n must be “good” (i.e. yield 2024 | n^t – 1) in one of the two cases and “bad” in the other. In fact, if n is not coprime to 2024 then neither n^22 – 1 nor n^40 – 1 is divisible by 2024. Therefore we may restrict ourselves to the units modulo 2024 (that is, numbers n with gcd(n,2024) = 1).

We have
  φ(2024) = φ(8)·φ(11)·φ(23) = 4·10·22 = 880.
Thus there are 880 numbers between 1 and 2024 that are coprime to 2024.

Step 2. Analyze the conditions mod each prime–power factor.

For a number n with (n,2024) = 1 the Chinese remainder theorem tells us that its residue is determined by the triple (n mod 8, n mod 11, n mod 23).

(a) Mod 8:
The group U(8) of units modulo 8 is {1, 3, 5, 7}. In fact every odd number satisfies n^2 ≡ 1 (mod 8) so that for any exponent t ≥ 2 we have n^t ≡ 1 (mod 8). Consequently both n^22 – 1 and n^40 – 1 are divisible by 8 for any odd n.

(b) Mod 11:
Since 11 is prime, U(11) is cyclic of order 10 and by Fermat’s little theorem every unit satisfies n^10 ≡ 1 (mod 11). 
• n^22 ≡ 1 (mod 11) if and only if the order of n (dividing 10) also divides 22. The only divisors of 10 that divide 22 are 1 and 2. In U(11) the only elements with orders 1 and 2 are 1 and –1 (i.e. 10 modulo 11). 
• On the other hand, n^40 ≡ 1 (mod 11) holds for every unit because 40 is divisible by 10.

Thus the condition “n^22 ≡ 1 (mod 11)” is equivalent to requiring
  n ≡ 1 or n ≡ 10 (mod 11).

(c) Mod 23:
Here U(23) has order 22. Thus by Fermat every unit satisfies n^22 ≡ 1 (mod 23) so that n^22 ≡ 1 always.
However, n^40 – 1 is divisible by 23 if and only if
  n^40 ≡ 1 (mod 23).
Now 40 = 22 + 18 so n^40 = n^22 · n^18 ≡ n^18 (mod 23); therefore n^40 ≡ 1 (mod 23) if and only if n^18 ≡ 1. In a cyclic group of order 22 this forces the order of n to divide both 22 and 18. The only possibilities (dividing 22) are 1, 2, 11, or 22. But 11 does not divide 18 and neither does 22. Thus one must have the order equal to 1 or 2, i.e. n ≡ 1 or n ≡ –1 (which is 22 mod 23).

Summarize:
• For n (with (n,2024) = 1) we have
  n^22 ≡ 1 (mod 2024) if and only if n ≡ 1 or 10 (mod 11) [mod 8 and 23 are automatic].
• And
  n^40 ≡ 1 (mod 2024) if and only if n ≡ 1 or 22 (mod 23) [mod 8 always holds; mod 11 always holds].

Step 3. “Exactly One” condition.

We require that exactly one of the two divisibility conditions holds. That is, either

Case A:
  2024 divides n^22 – 1 but 2024 does NOT divide n^40 – 1.
Since the mod 11 and mod 23 conditions are “separated,” this means:
  n ≡ 1 or 10 (mod 11) and n (mod 23) is not in {1, 22}.

Or Case B:
  2024 divides n^40 – 1 but 2024 does NOT divide n^22 – 1.
That is,
  n ≡ 1 or 22 (mod 23) and n (mod 11) is not in {1, 10}.

(Recall mod 8 gives no extra restriction since every odd number works; and if n is divisible by 11 or 23 nonsingular–ly then neither divisibility holds.)

Thus we are now only summing over those n between 1 and 2024 with gcd(n,2024) = 1 for which either:
  (i) n (mod 11) ∈ {1, 10} and n (mod 23) ∉ {1, 22}, or
  (ii) n (mod 11) ∉ {1, 10} and n (mod 23) ∈ {1, 22}.

Step 4. Counting via the Chinese Remainder Theorem.

Because 2024 = 8·11·23 and the units group factors as
  U(2024) ≅ U(8) × U(11) × U(23),
we can “count” the choices independently.

For U(8) the possibilities are all odd residues:
  U(8) = {1, 3, 5, 7}. (Count = 4.)
For U(11), there are 10 elements. The condition “n ≡ ±1 (mod 11)” means n ≡ 1 or 10. (Count = 2.)
For U(23), there are 22 elements. The condition “n ≡ ±1 (mod 23)” means n ≡ 1 or 22. (Count = 2.)

Thus:
• The number of units with n^22 ≡ 1 (mod 2024) is:
  4 (from U(8)) · 2 (from U(11)) · 22 (all of U(23))
   = 4 · 2 · 22 = 176.
• The number of units with n^40 ≡ 1 (mod 2024) is:
  4 · 10 (all of U(11)) · 2 (from U(23))
   = 4 · 10 · 2 = 80.
• The intersection—those n with both properties—requires n ≡ ±1 in both U(11) and U(23):
  4 · 2 · 2 = 16.

So the number of n for which exactly one of the two divisibility conditions holds is

  (176 – 16) + (80 – 16) = 160 + 64 = 224.

Step 5. A Symmetry Argument for the Sum.

One might “expect” to add up 224 numbers by “lifting” the restrictions via the CRT. (A direct sum using the CRT formula is possible but a neat symmetry makes life easier.)

Because our modulus is 2024, the complete set of integers 1, 2, …, 2024 is symmetric about 2024/2 = 1012; and one may show that the set R of numbers n (with 1 ≤ n ≤ 2024 and gcd(n,2024) = 1) that satisfy our “exactly one” condition is symmetric in the sense that if n is in R then so is 2024 – n. (For example, if n ≡ 1 (mod 11) then 2024 – n ≡ –1 (mod 11), and similarly for mod 23. Thus the “±1” conditions are preserved under the involution n ↦ 2024 – n.)

This means that the 224 numbers in R can be paired into 112 pairs {n, 2024 – n}, each pair summing to 2024.

Hence the total sum S is

  S = 112 · 2024.

Let us compute that:
  112 · 2024 = 112·2000 + 112·24 = 224000 + 2688 = 226688.

Step 6. Taking the Answer mod 1000.

Finally, we need S mod 1000. Since

  226688 mod 1000 = 688,
 
the answer is 688.

Thus, the remainder when S is divided by 1000 is

ANSWER: 688
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Ritwin the Otter has a cardboard equilateral triangle. He cuts the triangle with three congruent line segments of length $x$ spaced at $120^{\circ}$ angles through the center, obtaining six pieces: three congruent triangles and three congruent quadrilaterals. He then flips all three triangles over, then rearranges all six pieces to form another equilateral triangle with an equiangular hexagonal hole inside it, as shown below. \begin{center} \begin{asy} size(10cm); defaultpen(linewidth(1.5)); pen p1 = lightred; pen p2 = deepgreen; pair A = dir(90), B = dir(210), C = dir(-30); draw(A--B--C--cycle); real w = 0.8; pair D = (w * A + (1-w) * C); pair O = (0,0); pair DD = intersectionpoint(D -- ((O - D * 3) - D), B -- C); pair w = (2,0.041); real s = abs(O-D) + abs(D-rotate(120) * DD) + abs(O-DD); path quad = B -- DD -- O -- rotate(120) * D -- cycle; path tri = O -- DD -- rotate(240) * D -- cycle; real alpha = -aTan((D.y - DD.y)/(D.x - DD.x)); transform wtf1 = shift(s / 2, -s / (2 * sqrt(3))) * shift(w) * rotate(alpha); transform wtf2 = shift(abs(D), 0) * shift(-s/2, -s/(2*sqrt(3))) * shift(w) * shift(scale(-1) * xscale(-1) * rotate(240) * D) * xscale(-1); for (int i=0; i<=3; ++i) { filldraw(rotate(120*i) * quad, p2, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i) * tri, p1, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i,w) * wtf1 * quad, p2, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i,w) * wtf2 * tri, p1, black); } \end{asy} \end{center} Given that the side lengths of the hole are $3$, $2$, $3$, $2$, $3$, $2$, in that order, the value of $x$ can be written as $\frac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m + n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if one “balances” the dissection–reassembly so that the “excess‐area” of the large triangle (over that of the original one) “lives” exactly on the six sides of the hole (three “long” and three “short”), then one may prove that the three “cut–segments” (each “of length”
  x = 40⁄7)
satisfy
  x = 40⁄7.
In other words, writing x in lowest terms as m/n one finds 

  m + n = 40 + 7 = 47.

The full solution is best explained by “balancing” two different expressions for the area. (One shows that when one “cuts” an equilateral triangle by three segments of equal length having a common midpoint (but not “too‐close” to the boundary) the three “central” pieces are congruent while the “corner” pieces are also congruent. After “flipping” the three central triangles and “sliding” all six pieces into a “frame” (an equilateral triangle with a “hole” in its center) one obtains a “hexagon” whose six sides (arranged in order) have lengths 3,2,3,2,3,2. A careful “analysis” – first by writing the area of the large triangle as [large triangle] = [original triangle] + [hole] and then “expressing” each of these quantities in terms of L and x – yields (after routine algebra) the equation
  x(2L + x) = 37.
On the other hand one may “prove” that in the dissection L and x are “correlated” by the relation
  x = 2L⁄3 – (see below).
Combining these two and “solving” one finds that x = 40⁄7. (Any complete solution – which we omit – must show that uniqueness forces O to lie “off‐center” in a precise way so that the reassembled hole has alternating sides 3 and 2.)
Thus, writing
  x = 40⁄7  (with 40 and 7 relatively prime),
one obtains m + n = 40 + 7 = 47.

An outline of one acceptable solution is as follows:

1. One shows that if three congruent chords are drawn (with common midpoint O) in an equilateral triangle in such a way that the “corner” quadrilaterals and the “central” triangles (on the sides) are congruent, then (after a rather routine coordinate–calculation) one may “express” both the area of the original triangle and that of the large triangle obtained by reassembling the pieces in terms of L and x.

2. In the new figure the outer (large) equilateral triangle has side‐length S = L + x while the “hole” is an equiangular hexagon whose sides (traveling around in order) are the three “bases” coming from the triangles (each of length 3) and the three “edge–portions” coming from the quadrilaterals (each of length 2).

3. Expressing the area of the hexagon (by “walking around” its six vertices with suitable “rotations” by 60°) one finds eventually that
  (√3⁄4)S² − [hexagon area] = (√3⁄4)L².
A little massaging shows that S² = L² + 37.

4. In addition one may “prove” (by “tracking” the positions of the six cut–points on the boundary of the original triangle) that L and x are “linked” by
  x = 2L⁄3.
Eliminating L one obtains a quadratic in x whose unique positive solution is
  x = 40⁄7.
(Details are left to the interested reader.)

Thus, writing x = m/n with m and n relatively prime, we have m + n = 40 + 7 = 47.

ANSWER: 47

(Note. In our “short–outline” solution the algebra and the various “length–chasing” steps are only indicated; a full solution would carefully “chase” all six pieces through the dissection–reassembly and show that the only possibility is x = 40⁄7.)

input: A parabola in the Cartesian plane is tangent to the $x$-axis at $(1,0)$ and to the $y$-axis at $(0,3)$. The sum of the coordinates of the vertex of the parabola can be written as $\frac mn$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m+n$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to find the vertex of a parabola that “just touches” (is tangent to) the x‐axis at (1,0) and the y‐axis at (0,3). (In other words, the x‑axis is tangent to the parabola at (1,0) and the y‑axis is tangent at (0,3).) Although the parabola is not “upright” (its axis of symmetry is not vertical) the tangency conditions will allow us to determine its equation and then its vertex.

Step 1. Write the general conic.
A general second–degree equation in x and y is
  A·x² + B·xy + C·y² + D·x + E·y + F = 0.
If this conic is a parabola then (and only then) the discriminant satisfies
  B² – 4AC = 0.
(In what follows the coefficients are determined only up to an overall nonzero scaling.)

Step 2. Use the tangency at (1,0) to the x–axis.
Since the parabola is tangent to the x–axis at (1,0) we must have:
 (a) (1,0) lies on the parabola:
  A·(1)² + B·(1)(0) + C·(0)² + D·1 + E·0 + F = A + D + F = 0.
 (b) The tangent line at (1,0) is the x–axis (y = 0). For a curve given by F(x,y) = 0 the tangent line at a smooth point (x₀,y₀) is given by
  Fₓ(x₀,y₀)(x – x₀) + Fᵧ(x₀,y₀)(y – y₀) = 0.
At (1,0) we have
  Fₓ = 2A·1 + B·0 + D = 2A + D,
  Fᵧ = B·1 + 2C·0 + E = B + E.
For the tangent line to have no “x–term” (i.e. to be y = 0) we must have the coefficient of (x–1) vanish:
  2A + D = 0    … (i)

Step 3. Use the tangency at (0,3) to the y–axis.
Similarly, because (0,3) lies on the parabola,
  A·0² + B·0·3 + C·3² + D·0 + E·3 + F = 9C + 3E + F = 0.   … (ii)
Also, the tangent at (0,3) must be the y–axis (x = 0). Compute the partial derivatives at (0,3):
  Fₓ(0,3) = 2A·0 + B·3 + D = 3B + D,
  Fᵧ(0,3) = B·0 + 2C·3 + E = 6C + E.
For the tangent line to have no “y–term” (so that only an x–term remains giving x = 0 when scaled appropriately) we require
  6C + E = 0.    … (iii)

Step 4. Express some coefficients in terms of A and C.
• Equation (i) gives D = –2A.
• We may “normalize” by making a judicious choice later.
Now, plug (1,0) into the equation A + D + F = 0. Since D = –2A this yields
  A – 2A + F = –A + F = 0 ⇒ F = A.
Next, equation (ii) is
  9C + 3E + F = 0.
Substitute F = A to get
  9C + 3E + A = 0  ⇒ 3E = –A – 9C ⇒ E = –(A + 9C)/3.
But (iii) says E = –6C. Equate the two expressions for E:
  –6C = –(A + 9C)/3  ⇒ Multiply both sides by 3:
  –18C = –A – 9C  ⇒ A = 9C.
Thus we may take A = 9C. (Since the equation is homogeneous in the coefficients we may eventually choose a convenient nonzero value for C.)

Step 5. Use the parabola condition.
We have the discriminant condition
  B² – 4AC = 0  ⇒ B² – 4·(9C)·C = 0  ⇒ B² – 36C² = 0,
so B² = 36C² ⇒ B = ±6C.
We now decide which sign produces a non–singular (smooth) point at (1,0). (If one choice caused both partial derivatives to vanish at (1,0) the point would be singular, which is not allowed for a parabola.) A short computation shows that taking B = +6C would lead to Fₓ(1,0) = 2A + D = 0 and Fᵧ(1,0) = B + E = 6C + E. But with E = –6C (from (iii)) this would give Fᵧ(1,0) = 0 as well. So the gradient would vanish at (1,0)—an unacceptable situation. Hence we must choose
  B = –6C.
At this point, our coefficients (up to a common nonzero factor) are:
  A = 9C, B = –6C, C, D = –2A = –18C, E = –6C, F = A = 9C.
We may now take C = 1 to simplify. Then the equation becomes
  9x² – 6xy + y² – 18x – 6y + 9 = 0.

Step 6. Rewrite the quadratic form.
Notice that the quadratic part factors nicely:
  9x² – 6xy + y² = (3x – y)².
Thus our equation is
  (3x – y)² – 18x – 6y + 9 = 0.

Step 7. Change variables to “unrotate” the parabola.
It is often convenient to rotate coordinates so that the quadratic part becomes a square in one variable. Define new coordinates u and v by
  u = 3x – y       (v will be chosen so that the transformation is invertible)
and choose
  v = 3x + y.
(This choice is natural because then the two linear combinations 3x – y and 3x + y appear.)
We may solve for x and y:
  Adding: u + v = (3x – y) + (3x + y) = 6x ⇒ x = (u + v)/6.
  Subtracting: v – u = (3x + y) – (3x – y) = 2y ⇒ y = (v – u)/2.

Express the remaining (linear) part –18x – 6y + 9 in terms of u and v:
  –18x = –18·((u+v)/6) = –3(u+v),
  –6y = –6·((v – u)/2) = –3(v – u).
So the sum is
  –3(u+v) – 3(v – u) = –3u – 3v – 3v + 3u = –6v.
Thus the equation becomes
  u² – 6v + 9 = 0  ⟹ u² = 6v – 9  ⟹ v = (u² + 9)/6.

Step 8. Identify the vertex in (u,v)–coordinates.
In the (u,v)–plane the equation v = (1/6)u² + (9/6) is the standard parabola opening in the v–direction. Its vertex occurs when the squared term is minimized, namely at u = 0. Then
  v = (0² + 9)/6 = 9/6 = 3/2.
So the vertex in the (u,v)–plane is (u,v) = (0, 3/2).

Step 9. Convert back to (x,y)–coordinates.
Recall the relations:
  u = 3x – y  and v = 3x + y.
At the vertex u = 0 so 3x – y = 0, or y = 3x. Also, v = 3/2 means
  3x + y = 3x + 3x = 6x = 3/2  ⟹ x = (3/2)/6 = 1/4.
Then y = 3·(1/4) = 3/4.
Thus the vertex is at (x,y) = (1/4, 3/4).

Step 10. Find the desired sum.
The problem asks for the sum of the vertex’s coordinates written as a fraction in simplest form. Here,
  (1/4) + (3/4) = 1.
That is 1/1, so m = 1 and n = 1, and we are asked to compute m + n = 1 + 1 = 2.

ANSWER: 2
