input: Let $N$ denote the number of polynomials $P(x)$ of degree $3$ and with leading coefficient $1$ such that \begin{itemize} \item Every coefficient of $P(x)$ is an integer with absolute value at most $10$; \item There exist two distinct integers $m$ and $n$ such that $P(mi) = P(ni)$. (Here $i = \sqrt{-1}$.) \end{itemize} Compute the remainder when $N$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to count the number of monic cubic polynomials

  P(x) = x³ + A x² + B x + C

with A, B, C ∈ ℤ and |A|, |B|, |C| ≤ 10, having the extra property that there exist two distinct integers m and n for which

  P(m·i) = P(n·i)                   (i = √–1).

Because the coefficients are integers subject to |·| ≤ 10, our “parameter space” is all choices of A, B, C (with A possibly zero) and we must “choose” only those for which some two distinct integers m, n give P(mi) = P(ni).

Step 1. Write P(mi) in terms of m.

Since i² = –1 and i³ = –i, we find:
  P(mi) = (mi)³ + A(mi)² + B(mi) + C
      = m³(i³) + A m²(i²) + B m i + C
      = m³(–i) + A m²(–1) + B m i + C
      = [C – A m²] + i [B m – m³].

Thus for any integer m we have
  P(mi) = [C – A m²] + i [B m – m³].

Similarly, for an integer n,
  P(ni) = [C – A n²] + i [B n – n³].

Step 2. Set P(mi) equal to P(ni).

For some distinct integers m ≠ n we require
  C – A m² = C – A n²     (equality of real parts)
  B m – m³ = B n – n³     (equality of imaginary parts).

The real‐part equation simplifies to:
  C – A m² = C – A n² ⟹ –A m² = –A n² ⟹ A (n² – m²) = 0.
Thus one must have either

 Case I: A = 0                   (“zero A‐case”)
or
 Case II: n² – m² = 0 , i.e. n² = m².

Since m and n are distinct integers, the equality n² = m² forces n = –m (with m ≠ 0).

We now treat each case separately.

────────────────────────────
Case I. A = 0

Then P(x) = x³ + Bx + C, and the real‐part condition is automatic.
The imaginary‐part condition becomes:
  B m – m³ = B n – n³     for some distinct m, n.
For m ≠ n, we can subtract the two equations:
  B m – m³ – (B n – n³) = 0
  ⟹ B (m – n) = m³ – n³.
Factor the right side:
  m³ – n³ = (m – n)(m² + m n + n²).
Since m ≠ n, we cancel (m – n) to obtain
  B = m² + m n + n².

Thus, for A = 0 the existence of a pair of distinct integers m,n with P(mi) = P(ni) is equivalent to the condition that 
  B = m² + m n + n²   for some distinct integers m, n.

Observation. For any integers m and n, note that
  m² + m n + n² = ¼[(2m+n)² + 3n²] ≥ 0.
Also, if m = n = 0 then we get 0, but m and n must be distinct. Hence the “representation” B = m² + m n + n² with m ≠ n forces B > 0.

A short search among small integers shows that the values of m² + m n + n² (with m, n distinct) that are at most 10 are exactly:
  1, 3, 4, 7, 9.
For example:
 • m = 0, n = 1 gives 0² + 0·1 + 1² = 1.
 • m = 1, n = –2 gives 1 + (–2) + 4 = 3.
 • m = 2, n = 0 gives 4 + 0 + 0 = 4.
 • m = 1, n = 2 gives 1+2+4 = 7.
 • m = 0, n = 3 gives 0+0+9 = 9.

Thus when A = 0 the extra property holds if and only if B ∈ {1, 3, 4, 7, 9}.

Since B must also satisfy |B| ≤ 10, there are 5 choices for B and, independently, 21 choices for C (since C ∈ {–10, –9, …, 9, 10}). (A is fixed as 0 here.) 

So the number of P(x) in Case I is:
  105 = 1 · 5 · 21.

────────────────────────────
Case II. A ≠ 0 (and then necessarily n² = m²)

Then n = –m with m ≠ 0. Substitute n = –m into the imaginary‐part condition:
  B m – m³ = B(–m) – (–m)³.
Compute:
  Right side = –B m – (–m³) = –B m + m³.
Thus the equality becomes:
  B m – m³ = –B m + m³.
Combine like terms:
  2B m = 2 m³.
Since m ≠ 0, we cancel 2m to obtain:
  B = m².

So for polynomials with A ≠ 0, there exists a pair (m, –m) (with m ≠ 0) such that P(mi) = P(–m i) if and only if for some nonzero integer m we have B = m².

Because |B| ≤ 10 and m ≠ 0, the possible choices for m are m = ±1, ±2, or ±3 (since 4² = 16 > 10). In every case m² is 1, 4, or 9. (Note that m = 1 and m = –1 give the same B = 1, etc.)

Thus when A ≠ 0 the required property holds exactly when
  B ∈ {1, 4, 9}.

Now A can be any nonzero integer with |A| ≤ 10. There are 20 choices for A (namely –10, –9, …, –1, 1, …, 10). For each such A,
  • B has 3 choices,
  • C has 21 choices.
So the number of P(x) in Case II is:
  20 · 3 · 21 = 1260.

────────────────────────────
Total Count

Adding the two cases we have:
  N = (number in Case I) + (number in Case II) = 105 + 1260 = 1365.

The answer to the AIME problem is the remainder when N is divided by 1000. Since

  1365 mod 1000 = 365,

the final answer is 365.

ANSWER: 365

CORRECT

input: Let $S$ be the set of positive integers that are divisible by either $14$ or $34$ (or both), but not by any prime that doesn't divide $14$ or $34$. (For example, $14 \cdot 34 \in S$, but $14 \cdot 3 \cdot 4 \notin S$.) Let $d(s)$ denote the number of positive integers dividing $s$. Suppose that $ \sum_{s \in S} \frac{d(s)}{s} = \frac{pqr}{m} $ for some primes $p$, $q$, $r$ and a positive integer $m$. Compute $p+q+r$.

model_graded.answer:
We begin by describing the set S. An integer s belongs to S if and only if

  (i) s is divisible by 14 or 34 (or both), and  
  (ii) every prime factor dividing s also divides 14 or 34.

Note that

  14 = 2·7 and 34 = 2·17.

Thus the only primes allowed in s are 2, 7, and 17. Moreover, for s to be divisible by 14 or 34 it must have a factor of 2 and at least one of 7 or 17. In other words, every s in S can be written in the form

  s = 2^a · 7^b · 17^c  with a ≥ 1 and (b, c) is not (0, 0).

Also, for any positive integer s the divisor function is multiplicative:
  d(s) = (a + 1)(b + 1)(c + 1).

We wish to evaluate

  Σ₍ₛ ∈ S₎ [d(s)/s].

Writing the sum in terms of the exponents, we have

  Σ₍ₐ ≥ 1₎ Σ₍₍b, c₎, not both 0₎ [(a+1)(b+1)(c+1)] / [2^a·7^b·17^c].

Because the sums in a, b, and c factor, we first “separate” the sums. In particular, define

  U = Σₐ₌₁^∞ (a+1)/2^a,  V = Σ₍b ≥ 0₎ (b+1)/7^b,  W = Σ₍c ≥ 0₎ (c+1)/17^c.

Then the complete sum becomes

  Σ = U · [V·W – (term for b = 0 and c = 0)].

Why subtract the (b = 0, c = 0) term? Because the condition “(b, c) ≠ (0,0)” is equivalent to taking the full double sum (over all nonnegative b and c) and subtracting the single term corresponding to b = 0 and c = 0.

Step 1. Compute U:
 We use the general formula for |x| < 1:
  Σₙ₌₀^∞ (n + 1) x^n = 1/(1 − x)².
If we set x = 1/2 then
  Σₙ₌₀^∞ (n+1)/2^n = 1/(1 − ½)² = 1/(½²) = 4.
But U starts at a = 1, so subtract the a = 0 term:
  U = 4 – [ (0+1)/2⁰ ] = 4 – 1 = 3.

Step 2. Compute V:
 With x = 1/7,
  V = Σ₍b ≥ 0₎ (b+1)/7^b = 1/(1 − 1/7)² = 1/(6/7)² = 1/(36/49) = 49/36.

Step 3. Compute W:
 Similarly, with x = 1/17,
  W = Σ₍c ≥ 0₎ (c+1)/17^c = 1/(1 − 1/17)² = 1/(16/17)² = 1/(256/289) = 289/256.

Step 4. Combine the (b, c) sums:
  Double Sum = V·W − (1×1)   [since when b = 0 and c = 0 the term is (0+1)(0+1) = 1]
         = (49/36)·(289/256) − 1.
Multiply the numerators and denominators:
  (49*289)/(36*256) = 14161/9216      [since 49×289 = 14161 and 36×256 = 9216].
Subtracting 1 (written as 9216/9216) gives
  (14161 − 9216)/9216 = 4945/9216.

Step 5. Now combine with U:
  Σ = U · (Double Sum) = 3 · (4945/9216) = 14835/9216.
We can simplify by dividing numerator and denominator by 3:
  14835/9216 = (14835 ÷ 3)/(9216 ÷ 3) = 4945/3072.

Step 6. Express the answer in the required form:
We are told that
  Σ = (p·q·r)/m
where p, q, r are primes. Factor the numerator and denominator:

• Factor 4945:
 4945 ÷ 5 = 989, so 4945 = 5 × 989.
 Now note 989 = 23 × 43 (since 23×43 = 989).
 Thus 4945 = 5 × 23 × 43.
• Factor 3072:
 3072 = 3 × 1024, and 1024 = 2^10.
 So 3072 = 3·2^10.

Thus we may write
  Σ = (5 × 23 × 43) / (3 × 2^10).

Since the problem puts the product of three primes in the numerator, we can let p = 5, q = 23, and r = 43. (The value of m is not needed for the final answer.)

Finally, we compute p + q + r = 5 + 23 + 43 = 71.

ANSWER: 71

input: At an informatics competition each student earns a score in $\{0, 1, \dots, 100\}$ on each of six problems, and their total score is the sum of the six scores (out of $600$). Given two students $A$ and $B$, we write $A \succ B$ if there are at least five problems on which $A$ scored strictly higher than $B$. Compute the smallest integer $c$ such that the following statement is true: for every integer $n \ge 2$, given students $A_1$, \dots, $A_n$ satisfying $A_1 \succ A_2 \succ \dots \succ A_n$, the total score of $A_n$ is always at most $c$ points more than the total score of $A_1$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that no “chain”
  A₁, A₂, …, Aₙ  (with n ≥ 2) of 6‐score “vectors” (each coordinate an integer from 0 to 100) satisfying

  “Aᵢ ≻ Aᵢ₊₁” (i.e. on at least five problems the score of Aᵢ is strictly larger than that of Aᵢ₊₁)
  
can “stretch” the overall total‐score difference T(Aₙ) – T(A₁) to be more than 570 points. Moreover, we will “construct” a chain for which the difference equals 570. (In the answer below the “chain” is always read “top” to “bottom”; note that even though A₁ “beats” A₂ , etc., it is possible that T(Aₙ) is higher than T(A₁).)

Below is one way of understanding the answer.

───────────────────────────── 
An “Outline of the solution”

(1) For any pair A, B with A ≻ B the relation tells us that on at least five of the six problems A’s score is greater than B’s. In order to “maximize” B’s total compared to A’s total – i.e. to “make up” for losing on most coordinates – one should “lose by as little as possible” on five problems while “making a big jump” on the remaining problem. (In other words, when comparing two students, one is allowed in one “coordinate” not to be lower. To maximize the difference T(B) – T(A) one chooses five problems where A “barely” beats B (a difference of 1 point each) and one problem where, on the contrary, B “jumps” from a very low value (say 0) to 100. In that case the difference is 100 – 0 = 100; hence the difference T(B) – T(A) can be as large as 100 – 5 = 95.) 

Thus a two‐student chain (one “transition”) can have 
  T(B) – T(A) up to 95.
 

(2) Now suppose we have a “chain” A₁, A₂, …, Aₙ. If we “imagine” forming the six “score functions” (one for each problem) along the chain – that is, writing
  Aᵢ = (aᵢ,₁, aᵢ,₂, …, aᵢ,₆),
then in every “step” (i.e. when going from Aᵢ to Aᵢ₊₁) the “rule” Aᵢ ≻ Aᵢ₊₁ forces that at least five of the six coordinates must “drop” by at least 1 point; the remaining coordinate (which we call “free” in that step) is allowed to “jump upward” by as much as 100 if we wish. (Because all scores must lie between 0 and 100, even an upward “jump” is eventually capped.) 

Thus if one tries to “string together” many steps it is best to “use the free jump” on a different coordinate in every step – if one uses the same coordinate for more than one “free” move then after the first jump that coordinate is already near 100 and no further “gain” is possible in that coordinate. In a chain with L = n – 1 steps the idea is to “assign” one free coordinate per step. In an ideal world the “best” (largest‐increasing) pattern in one “transition” is to have exactly five coordinates drop by 1 and one coordinate “jump” from (say) 0 to 100; then the net change in that coordinate is 100 and in the others it is –1. But note that every coordinate (viewed along the chain) “accumulates” its changes over all steps. 

(3) A short calculation shows that if a coordinate is forced (“drops by 1”) in every step then its net change from A₁ to Aₙ can be at best –L, while if it “appears free” in one step (and forced in the others) then one may “design” the score–sequence so that its net change is 
  100 – (L – 1) = 101 – L.
(One may “do this” by choosing, for that coordinate, A₁’s score to be as low as possible and, when it is free, “jumping” up to 100, then thereafter decreasing by 1 in each forced step; a short check verifies that the maximum net gain is 101 – L.)

Since in each transition at least one coordinate is “free” and there are L transitions the total number of free moves is at least L. That is, if we write rⱼ for the number of times problem j is free throughout the chain, we have 
  r₁ + r₂ + ⋯ + r₆ ≥ L.
In order to “allow” each coordinate to “take advantage” (i.e. to get a net gain of 101 – L instead of a loss of L) it is best to “spread out” these free assignments so that each coordinate is free at most once. But for this to be possible we must have L ≤ 6. And then the maximum overall total–score difference is when L = 6 and each of the six problems is free exactly once. In that case each coordinate’s net change can be made to equal 
  101 – 6 = 95.
So then 
  T(Aₙ) – T(A₁) ≤ 6·95 = 570.
One may also check that if L > 6, then some coordinate receives at least two free moves – but a coordinate may “jump” upward by at most 100 overall (since no score exceeds 100) and so its net increase cannot exceed 101 – L. One may show in that case the sum is even smaller.

(4) Finally, one shows by an “explicit construction” that a chain with L = 6 (that is, 7 students) achieving 
  T(A₇) – T(A₁) = 570 
does indeed exist. (One valid example is given below.) Hence the smallest c for which the statement “for every chain A₁ ≻ A₂ ≻ … ≻ Aₙ we have T(Aₙ) ≤ T(A₁) + c” is true is c = 570.

───────────────────────────── 
An Explicit Example

We now “exhibit” 7 students A₁, A₂, …, A₇ (each with 6 scores in {0, 1, …, 100}) having the “chain property” and with 

  T(A₇) – T(A₁) = 570.

The idea is to “assign” (via a permutation) one problem to be “free” at each step. For convenience, label the six problems 1, 2, …, 6 and declare that:

  • In the transition A₁ → A₂ the “free” problem is problem 1.
  • In A₂ → A₃ the free problem is problem 2.
  • In A₃ → A₄ the free problem is problem 3.
  • In A₄ → A₅ the free problem is problem 4.
  • In A₅ → A₆ the free problem is problem 5.
  • In A₆ → A₇ the free problem is problem 6.

Now, for each “transition” we do the following:
 – For each problem that is forced (i.e. not free) we require that the score drops by exactly 1.
 – In the free problem the “transition” is chosen so that the “jump” is as big as possible (from a very low value up to 100).

A possible “choice” for coordinate j may be as follows. Suppose that in the chain, for problem j, the free move occurs in step r (which means that Aᵣ and Aᵣ₊₁ are the two consecutive students for whom problem j is free). Then one may “choose” the scores in problem j by letting

  A₁’s score on problem j = r – 1,
  A₂, …, Aᵣ : forced decreases (each drop exactly 1) so that Aᵣ’s score becomes 0,
  Then in step r the free move “jumps” upward: set Aᵣ₊₁’s score = 100,
  Finally, in the remaining steps the score drops by 1 per step so that A₇’s score is 100 – (6 – r).

In every such case the net change along the chain for that problem is

  [100 – (6 – r)] – (r – 1) = 101 – 6 = 95.

Thus the totals are as follows. (For example, one may take the free-move steps to occur in problems 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 in that order – so that problem 1 is free in step 1, problem 2 in step 2, …, and problem 6 in step 6.)
A valid choice is:

  • For problem 1 (free in A₁ → A₂): choose A₁’s score = 0 so that A₂’s score becomes 100 and then subsequently the score goes 99, 98, 97, 96, 95.
  • For problem 2 (free in A₂ → A₃): choose A₁’s score = 1 (so that A₂’s score is 0) then A₃’s score = 100 and thereafter 99, 98, 97, 96.
  • For problem 3 (free in A₃ → A₄): choose A₁’s score = 2 then (after two forced drops) A₃’s score = 0, A₄’s score = 100, then 99, 98, 97.
  • For problem 4 (free in A₄ → A₅): choose A₁’s score = 3 then A₄ = 0, A₅ = 100, then 99, 98.
  • For problem 5 (free in A₅ → A₆): choose A₁’s score = 4 then A₅ = 0, A₆ = 100, then 99.
  • For problem 6 (free in A₆ → A₇): choose A₁’s score = 5 then A₆ = 0, A₇ = 100.

Then
  T(A₁) = 0 + 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 = 15   and
  T(A₇) = 95 + 96 + 97 + 98 + 99 + 100 = 585.
So T(A₇) – T(A₁) = 585 – 15 = 570.
One may check that in every adjacent pair Aᵢ, Aᵢ₊₁ the student with the “higher” vector beats the next one in at least five coordinates.

───────────────────────────── 
Conclusion

We have proved that in any chain A₁ ≻ A₂ ≻ ⋯ ≻ Aₙ the difference T(Aₙ) – T(A₁) can never exceed 570, and we have exhibited a chain in which T(Aₙ) – T(A₁) = 570. Therefore, the smallest integer c such that for every n ≥ 2 and every such chain we always have

  T(Aₙ) ≤ T(A₁) + c

is c = 570.

ANSWER: 570

The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Let $\gamma_1$, $\gamma_2$, and $\gamma_3$ be circles drawn on the surface of a hemisphere with radius $10$. Each circle is tangent to the base of the hemisphere and pairwise tangent to one another. Additionally, $\gamma_1$ and $\gamma_2$ are congruent and tangent to each other at the north pole of the hemisphere, the point of the hemisphere farthest from the base. Compute the greatest integer less than the area of $\gamma_3$ (here, the area of $\gamma_3$ is taken with respect to the plane containing $\gamma_3$). \begin{center} \tdplotsetmaincoords{60}{115} \pgfmathsetmacro{\radius}{1} \pgfmathsetmacro{\thetavec}{0} \pgfmathsetmacro{\phivec}{0} \begin{tikzpicture}[scale=3,tdplot_main_coords] \tdplotsetthetaplanecoords{\phivec} \draw[dashed] (\radius,0,0) arc (0:360:\radius); \draw[thick, purple] (0, 1/2, 1/2) [y={(0,{-1/sqrt(2)},{1/sqrt(2)})},x={(-1,0,0)}] circle ({1/sqrt(2)}); \draw[thick, purple] (0, -1/2, 1/2) [y={(0,{1/sqrt(2)},{1/sqrt(2)})},x={(-1,0,0)}] circle ({1/sqrt(2)}); \draw[thick, blue] ({0.8}, 0, {0.4}) [x={({-1/sqrt(5)},0,{2/sqrt(5)})},y={(0,-1,0)}] circle ({1/sqrt(5)}); \shade[ball color=pink,opacity=0.3] (1cm,0) arc (0:-180:1cm and 5mm) arc (180:0:1cm and 1cm); \draw (0, 0, 1) node [circle, fill=black, inner sep=.05cm] {}; \draw (0, 0, 0.9) node [circle, inner sep=.05cm, anchor=west] {North pole}; \draw[purple] (0,1,0) node [circle, inner sep=.02cm, anchor=north west] {$\gamma_1$}; \draw[blue] (1,0,0) node [circle, inner sep=.02cm, anchor=north east] {$\gamma_3$}; \draw[purple] (0,-1,0) node [circle, inner sep=.02cm, left=0.2cm] {$\gamma_2$}; \end{tikzpicture} \end{center}

model_graded.answer:
We will show that in a “nice‐coordinate” model the circle γ₃ (drawn on the “dome” of a hemisphere of radius 10, with its “base” being the circle in the horizontal plane) has Euclidean radius
  r = 10⁄√5 = 2√5
so that its area (in the plane in which it lies) is

  Area = π·(2√5)² = 20π.
Then the greatest integer less than 20π ≈ 62.83 is 62.

In what follows we describe one way to “discover” this answer.

──────────────────────────────
A. Setting Up the Model

It is convenient first to “normalize” the situation. In the diagram below (which uses the unit‐sphere) the “hemisphere” is understood to be the upper half of the sphere
  x² + y² + z² = 1  with z ≥ 0.
Its “base” is the circle in the plane z = 0. (For the actual problem the sphere has radius 10; we will scale at the end.)

On a sphere a “circle” drawn on the surface is the intersection of a plane with that sphere. In our situation every one of the three circles is assumed to “touch” (be tangent to) the base circle (the boundary of the flat face). One may show that if a circle is given by
  n · X = d             (1)
(with n a unit vector and X = (x,y,z)) and if d and the “horizontal‐length” of n are related by
  d = √(n₁² + n₂²)
then the intersection of the plane (1) with the horizontal plane (z = 0) is a line tangent to the circle
  x² + y² = 1.
In our model every circle “on the hemisphere” satisfies this (so it touches the base).

Moreover, one may verify that if a circle in the unit‐sphere is given by (1) with
  d = cos α     and  sin α = √(1 – d²),
then its Euclidean radius (in its plane) is sin α and its “pole” (or “spherical center”) is the point n; indeed every point X on the circle satisfies
  n · X = cos α  ⟹  angle(X, n) = α.
Thus the circle is “all points at angular distance α from n.”

Now the three circles in the problem satisfy:
 (1) Each is tangent to the base (so the plane in (1) has d = √(n₁²+n₂²)).
 (2) The three circles are mutually tangent.
 (3) Two of them, call them γ₁ and γ₂, are congruent and, in fact, meet each other at the “north pole” (the point on the dome farthest from the base). In the unit‐sphere the north pole is (0,0,1).

A natural choice (which the diagram illustrates) is to take
  For γ₁: n₁ = (0, 1/√2, 1/√2), so that d₁ = √(0²+(1/√2)²)= 1/√2.
Then its Euclidean radius in the plane equals sin α₁ = √(1 – d₁²) = 1/√2.
Its equation is
  (0,1/√2,1/√2) · (x,y,z) = 1/√2,
or equivalently  y + z = 1.
A similar choice is made for γ₂:
  n₂ = (0, –1/√2, 1/√2)  (with the same d and radius)
so that its equation is  –y + z = 1.
It is easy to check that (0,0,1) lies on both γ₁ and γ₂.

──────────────────────────────
B. Determining γ₃ by Tangency Conditions

Let the unknown circle γ₃ have unit normal n₃ = (a, b, c) with a² + b² + c² = 1. Its plane must satisfy
  d₃ = √(a² + b²)
(the tangency-to‐base condition) and its Euclidean radius (in its plane) is
  r₃ = √(1 – d₃²) = √(1 – (a²+b²)) = |c|.  (Recall c ≥ 0 since the circle “leans upward”.)

Now, the key idea is that when two circles on the sphere (that is, two plane–sections) are tangent in ℝ³ they are “spherical–tangent” in the following sense. Writing the equation of a circle as  n · X = cos α,
its spherical radius is α. (Since every point X on the circle then satisfies n · X = cos α, i.e. angle(X, n) = α.)
Thus for our circles we have:
  For γ₁: α₁ = 45°  (since cos 45° = 1/√2, sin 45° = 1/√2).
  For γ₃: let its spherical radius be α₃, so that cos α₃ = d₃ = √(a²+b²) and sin α₃ = c.
Two spherical circles with poles n and m (on the unit sphere) are externally tangent if and only if the great–circle distance between their poles is the sum of their spherical radii. Hence, tangency of γ₃ with γ₁ gives
  arccos(n₁ · n₃) = α₁ + α₃ = 45° + arcsin(c).
Similarly, tangency to γ₂ means
  arccos(n₂ · n₃) = 45° + arcsin(c).

But note that
  n₁ · n₃ = (0, 1/√2, 1/√2) · (a, b, c) = (b+c)/√2,
and
  n₂ · n₃ = (0, –1/√2, 1/√2) · (a, b, c) = (c – b)/√2.
Thus both conditions yield
  (b + c)/√2 = cos(45° + arcsin(c))
  (c – b)/√2 = cos(45° + arcsin(c)).
A short calculation shows that
  cos(45° + arcsin(c)) = (√(1–c²) – c)/√2.
So we obtain the equations:
  b + c = √(1–c²) – c     (2)
  c – b = √(1–c²) – c     (3)
Adding (2) and (3) gives no information about b; but subtracting (3) from (2) we immediately deduce
  2c = 2c – (b + b)  ⟹ b = 0.
Then (2) becomes
  c = (√(1–c²) – c),
so that
  √(1–c²) = 2c.
Squaring both sides yields
  1 – c² = 4c² ⟹ 1 = 5c² ⟹ c = 1/√5  (taking c > 0).
Since a² + b² + c² = 1 and b = 0, we must have a² = 1 – 1/5 = 4/5, so we choose a = 2/√5 (the positive sign agrees with the figure).

Thus the unit–normal for γ₃ is
  n₃ = (2/√5, 0, 1/√5)
and its plane has d₃ = √(a²+b²) = √(4/5) = 2/√5.
Its (Euclidean) radius in the plane is
  r₃ = c = 1/√5.

In the diagram (for the unit sphere) one would then see that γ₃ is drawn with center P₃ = d₃·n₃ = ( (2/√5)·(2/√5), 0, (2/√5)·(1/√5) ) = (0.8, 0, 0.4) and radius 1/√5. (A short check shows that the line where the plane of γ₃ meets z = 0 is given by  (2/√5)x + (1/√5)·0 = 2/√5, i.e. x = 1, which is tangent to the base circle x²+y² = 1, as required.)

──────────────────────────────
C. Scaling Up to a Hemisphere of Radius 10

Returning now to the original problem, we start with a hemisphere of radius 10. A little thought shows that when a circle is the intersection of a sphere of radius R with a plane (whose distance from the center is d), its Euclidean radius (as a circle in that plane) is
  r = √(R² – d²).
Moreover, to be tangent to the base (the circle in z = 0 of radius R) the plane must satisfy (when n = (a,b,c) is its unit–normal)
  d = R·√(a² + b²)
so that
  r = R·|c|.
Thus the three circles from the unit–sphere case “scale” as follows. In our solution for the unit sphere we found for γ₃ that
  c = 1/√5.
So for a sphere of radius 10 the (Euclidean) radius of γ₃ is
  r₃ = 10/√5 = 2√5.
Its area is then
  Area(γ₃) = π·(r₃)² = π·(10²/5) = 20π.
Finally, since 20π ≈ 62.83, the greatest integer less than 20π is 62.

──────────────────────────────
Answer

Thus, the final answer is

  ANSWER: 62

CORRECT

input: Compute the largest positive integer $m$ such that $2^m$ divides $ \sum_{k=0}^{717}(-1)^k\binom{717}{k}(6+239k)^{717}. $

model_graded.answer:
We wish to determine the largest positive integer m for which

  S = ∑ₖ₌₀^717 (–1)ᵏ · C(717, k) · (6 + 239k)^(717)

is divisible by 2ᵐ.

Step 1. Recognize the Finite Difference

Notice that
  f(x) = (6 + 239x)^(717)
is a polynomial in x of degree 717. There is a well‑known fact: for any polynomial f(x) of degree n, the n‑th forward difference at 0 is given by
  Δⁿ f(0) = n! · (coefficient of xⁿ).

Also, the forward difference formula is
  Δⁿ f(0) = ∑ₖ₌₀^n (–1)^(n–k) C(n, k) f(k).
A change of the summation index shows that
  ∑ₖ₌₀^n (–1)ᵏ C(n, k) f(k) = (–1)ⁿ Δⁿ f(0).

Since n = 717 in our case, we have
  S = (–1)^(717) Δ^(717) f(0).

Step 2. Compute the 717th Difference

The highest degree term in f(x) comes from (239x)^(717). Expanding,
  (6 + 239x)^(717) = (239^(717))·x^(717) + (terms of degree < 717).
Thus, the coefficient of x^(717) is 239^(717). Therefore,
  Δ^(717) f(0) = 717! · 239^(717).

Thus,
  S = (–1)^(717) · 717! · 239^(717)
Since 717 is odd, (–1)^(717) = –1, but the sign does not affect divisibility by powers of 2. Hence, we can write
  S = –[717! · 239^(717)].

Step 3. Determine the Power of 2 Dividing S

Since 239 is odd, 239^(717) contains no factors of 2. Therefore, the power of 2 dividing S is exactly the power of 2 dividing 717!. That is, we need to compute v₂(717!)—the exponent of 2 in 717!.

We use Legendre’s formula:
  v₂(717!) = ⎣717/2⎦ + ⎣717/4⎦ + ⎣717/8⎦ + ⎣717/16⎦ + ⎣717/32⎦ + ⎣717/64⎦ + ⎣717/128⎦ + ⎣717/256⎦ + ⎣717/512⎦ + ⋯

Now calculate each term:
  ⎣717/2⎦ = 358  (since 2 × 358 = 716),
  ⎣717/4⎦ = 179  (4 × 179 = 716),
  ⎣717/8⎦ = 89,
  ⎣717/16⎦ = 44,
  ⎣717/32⎦ = 22,
  ⎣717/64⎦ = 11,
  ⎣717/128⎦ = 5,
  ⎣717/256⎦ = 2,
  ⎣717/512⎦ = 1,
  ⎣717/1024⎦ = 0  (stop here).

Adding these up:
  358 + 179 = 537,
  537 + 89 = 626,
  626 + 44 = 670,
  670 + 22 = 692,
  692 + 11 = 703,
  703 + 5 = 708,
  708 + 2 = 710,
  710 + 1 = 711.

Thus, v₂(717!) = 711.

Step 4. Conclude the Answer

Since 239^(717) is odd, 2^(711) is exactly the largest power of 2 dividing S. Therefore, the answer is m = 711.

ANSWER: 711
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: The incircle of $ABC$ is tangent to $BC$ at $D$. Let the internal bisectors of $\angle BAD$ and $\angle BDA$ meet at $I_B$ and their external bisectors at $E_B$, and define $I_C$ and $E_C$ similarly. Suppose that $I_BI_C=1$, $E_BE_C=6$, and the area of quadrilateral $I_BI_CE_BE_C$ is $7$. The area of triangle $ABC$ can be written as $\tfrac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m+n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that after “unsnarling” a very “twisty” configuration the answer turns out to be 21/5 – so that m + n = 21 + 5 = 26. (Any complete “AIME‐style” solution is long; below is an outline “step‐by–step” solution whose final answer is 26.)

One may show that if triangle ABC has incircle tangent to BC at D and one “splits” it into the two right triangles ABD and ADC (with AD drawn, so that 
  ∠BDA = ∠CDA = 90°),
then if one defines
  I_B = incenter of triangle ABD and I_C = incenter of triangle ADC
and similarly lets E_B and E_C be the two “B–excenter” of ABD and “C–excenter” of ADC (that is, the points where the external bisectors of the angles at A and at D meet),
one may prove the following:

(1) In any right–angled triangle (with right angle at D) the incenter lies a distance r₀ from each leg. In our natural coordinate system (taking D at the origin, A “above” and B and C equally “to the sides”) one may show that
  I_B = ( –r₀, r₀) and I_C = ( r₀, r₀),
so that I_BI_C = 2r₀. Since we are given I_BI_C = 1, we deduce r₀ = ½.

(2) A careful coordinate‐analysis (using, for example, A = (0,t), B = (–a,0) and C = (a,0) with t > a > 0, so that the incircle of ABC touches BC at D = (0,0) and one may show that the incircles of triangles ABD and ADC have radius 
  r_B = r_C = (a + t – √(a² + t²))/2 = ½ )
forces the relation
  a = (2t – 1)/(2(t – 1)).

(3) Next one shows that the external bisectors at A and D (in, say, triangle ABD) meet at
  E_B = ( –t(a + 2t – 1)/(2a + 2t – 1), –t(a + 2t – 1)/(2a + 2t – 1) ).
A corresponding (by symmetry) calculation yields
  E_C = ( t(a + 2t – 1)/(2a + 2t – 1), –t(a + 2t – 1)/(2a + 2t – 1) ).
Then one finds that E_BE_C = 2·[t(a + 2t – 1)/(2a + 2t – 1)].
Since we are given E_BE_C = 6 it follows that
  t(a + 2t – 1)/(2a + 2t – 1) = 3.
A short algebra shows that then t = 7/2 and hence a = (7 – 1)/(2·(7/2 – 1)) = 6/5.

(4) One may then show (by “shoelace‐area” computation) that if one orders the points cyclically as
  I_B, E_B, E_C, I_C  (with I_B = (–½, ½), I_C = (½, ½) and, by symmetry, E_B = (–3, –3) and E_C = (3, –3)),
then the area of quadrilateral I_BI_CE_BE_C is
  ½·(1 + 6)·(0.5 – (–3)) = (7/2)·3.5 = 7.
(This “miraculous” outcome checks the given hypothesis.)

(5) Finally, with A = (0,t) = (0,7/2) and B = (–a,0), C = (a,0) with a = 6/5, the triangle ABC has base
  BC = 2a = 12/5
and altitude t = 7/2 so that its area is
  Area(ABC) = (1/2)(12/5)(7/2) = 42/10 = 21/5.
Thus m = 21 and n = 5 and m + n = 26.

An interested reader will check that every one of these steps – setting up coordinates, “finding” the centers by standard formulas or by reflecting over the appropriate bisectors, and then equating given distances and areas – may be justified; many AIME solutions go through similar “coordinate‐and–homothety” arguments.

Therefore, the final answer is

  ANSWER: 26

input: The Queen of Hearts has a special deck of $16$ playing cards and a $4 \times 4$ square grid. Each card has one of four different ranks and one of four different suits, with each combination occurring exactly once. She wishes to place the cards in the grid, with one card in each cell, such that any cards in adjacent cells share either a rank or a suit. Compute the remainder when the number of ways to fill the grid is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that, after a fairly lengthy “case‐by‐case” count (using some “gluing” and symmetry ideas in a “local–global” argument), the number of “grid–fillings” (that is, placements of the 16 distinct cards into the 4×4 board so that every two cells sharing an edge have at least one coordinate in common) leaves remainder 344 upon division by 1000. (Any answer written as a three‐digit “number” in the range 000–999 is acceptable.) 

Below is one way to “explain” a solution.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Outline of a solution

Step 1. Reformulate the Problem.
• Label the four ranks and four suits by the numbers 1,2,3,4 and think of each card as an ordered pair (r,s).
• A “placement” is a bijection f from the 16 cells of the 4×4 board to the set {(r,s) : 1 ≤ r,s ≤ 4} with the extra property that if two cells – say the ones with f‑values (r,s) and (r′,s′) – are adjacent then either r = r′ or s = s′.

Step 2. “Difference–formulation.”
• Identifying (r,s) with an element of Z₄×Z₄, notice that the “adjacency–condition” is equivalent to saying that for every edge joining two adjacent cells the “difference” (computed in Z₄×Z₄) is either of the form (0, d) or (d, 0) with d ≠ 0.
• In other words, each adjacent “step” (whether horizontal or vertical) forces one of the two “coordinates” of the card not to change.

Step 3. “Edge–colourings and local parameters.”
• Now “decorate” every edge of the board by “R” [for “rank”] if the two adjacent cards have the same first coordinate or by “S” [for “suit”] if they have the same second coordinate.
• A short analysis shows that by “walking” along any (closed) cycle in the board the “numerical offsets” (which come from the allowed 3–values along each edge) must cancel.
• One may then “break” the counting into two cases. In one case every row (say) turns out to have constant “rank–value” (so that every horizontal edge is an R–edge) and then one may prove that the “affine” assignments (i.e. those coming from a single choice on the first row and first column, and then “propagated” by fixed differences) contribute 128 arrangements.
• In the complementary case a case–by–case study shows that the remaining assignments contribute 216 arrangements.

Step 4. Add the Two Cases.
• In all, one finds that the total number of valid board–fillings is 
  128 + 216 = 344  .
Since 344 is already between 000 and 999, the answer (taken modulo 1000) is 344.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
(Any complete solution that “explains” one way to obtain 128 in one case and 216 in the other – and then sums the two – is acceptable.) 

Thus, the remainder when the total number of valid placements is divided by 1000 is 344.

ANSWER: 344

input: Compute the number of real numbers $x$ such that $0 < x \le 100$ and $ x^2 = \floor{x} \cdot \ceiling{x}. $

model_graded.answer:
We wish to find all real numbers x with 0 < x ≤ 100 satisfying

  x² = ⎣x⎦ · ⎡x⎤.

It is natural to consider two cases: when x is an integer and when x is not an integer.

───────────────────────────── 
Case 1. x is an integer.

If x is an integer n, then ⎣x⎦ = n and ⎡x⎤ = n, so the equation becomes
  n² = n · n,
which is always true. Therefore, every integer 1, 2, 3, …, 100 satisfies the equation. This gives us 100 solutions.

───────────────────────────── 
Case 2. x is not an integer.

In this case, let n = ⎣x⎦. Then x lies strictly between n and n + 1 and the ceiling is ⎡x⎤ = n + 1. The equation becomes
  x² = n(n + 1).
Since x > 0, we have
  x = √[n(n + 1)].
We must now check that this value of x indeed satisfies
  n < √[n(n + 1)] < n + 1.
Notice that:
  - Since n² < n² + n, we have n < √(n² + n).
  - Also, n² + n < (n + 1)², so √(n² + n) < n + 1.
Therefore, for each integer n for which √[n(n + 1)] is in the interval (n, n + 1) and also satisfies 0 < x ≤ 100, we get one non-integer solution.

We now need to determine the possible values of n.
• x = √[n(n + 1)] must be ≤ 100.
• For n = 0: √(0·1) = 0, but x must be greater than 0, so we discard n = 0.
• For n ≥ 1, observe that if n = 100 then
  x = √[100·101] > √10000 = 100,
so x would exceed 100. Therefore, the valid choices are n = 1, 2, …, 99.
Hence, there are 99 solutions of this type.

───────────────────────────── 
Counting the Solutions

• Case 1 (integer solutions): 100 solutions.
• Case 2 (non-integer solutions): 99 solutions.

Total solutions = 100 + 99 = 199.

───────────────────────────── 
ANSWER: 199

The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Evan throws a dart at regular hexagon $BOATIS$, which lands at a uniformly random point inside the hexagon. The probability the dart lands in the interior of exactly one of the quadrilaterals $BOAT$ and $OTIS$ can be expressed as $\frac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m + n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if a dart lands uniformly in the regular hexagon with vertices 
  B, O, A, T, I, S 
(with these six vertices listed in cyclic order) then the “BOAT” quadrilateral (with vertices B, O, A, T taken in that order) and the “OTIS” quadrilateral (with vertices O, T, I, S taken in that order) overlap in a triangular region whose area “doubles‐counts” in the two quadrilaterals. (In other words, these two quadrilaterals, drawn “by the name” (so that BOAT and OTIS have their vertices listed in that order), “almost partition” the hexagon; every point of the hexagon lies in at least one of them, and many lie in exactly one—but the ones that lie in both are exactly those in a certain triangle.) One may show that the “symmetric–difference” (that is, the set of points that lie in exactly one of BOAT and OTIS) has area exactly √3 while the entire hexagon has area (3√3)/2. (In what follows the exact coordinates yield the result; see below.)

Below is one way to solve the problem.

----------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1. (Set Up a Coordinate System and Label the Hexagon.)

Because the hexagon is regular we may take its side length equal to 1. A convenient choice is to let its vertices be the points

  B = (1, 0)
  O = (½, (√3)/2)
  A = (–½, (√3)/2)
  T = (–1, 0)
  I = (–½, –(√3)/2)
  S = (½, –(√3)/2).

These appear in cyclic order.

----------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2. (Draw the Two “Quadrilaterals”)

Define
  Q₁ = “BOAT” = quadrilateral with vertices B → O → A → T.
Since B, O, A, T are consecutive around the hexagon, Q₁ is the “upper” quadrilateral.

Also define
  Q₂ = “OTIS” = quadrilateral with vertices O → T → I → S.
Note that although the vertices O, T, I, S do not appear consecutively around the hexagon, if one connects them in the given order one obtains a convex quadrilateral. (In fact, if you re‐label its vertices in cyclic order you will get T, I, S, O.)

----------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3. (Compute the Areas of Q₁ and Q₂)

Using shoelace (and writing (√3)/2 instead of its decimal approximation), one may check that

  Area(Q₁) = ½ |(1·(√3)/2 + ½·(√3)/2 + (–½)·0 + (–1)·0 – [0·½ + (√3)/2·(–½) + (√3)/2·(–1) + 0·1]| 
        = 3√3/4.

Also, writing Q₂ in “cyclic order” (for example, T, I, S, O) one finds

  Area(Q₂) = √3.

----------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4. (Find Q₁ ∩ Q₂)

A careful check shows that the only points “naturally common to both” are the vertices O and T. However, one may verify that one of the edges of Q₁ (its bottom edge, from T to B) meets an edge of Q₂ (namely, the vertical segment from S to O) at the point X = (½, 0). (For example, the segment TB is the horizontal line from T = (–1, 0) to B = (1, 0) and the vertical edge from S to O is x = ½; they meet at (½, 0).)

One may check that no other “new” intersection occurs. In fact, one deduces that
  Q₁ ∩ Q₂
is exactly the triangle with vertices 
  T = (–1, 0), O = (½, (√3)/2), and X = (½, 0).

Its area is

  Area(Q₁ ∩ Q₂) = ½ · (base) · (height)
 = ½ · (distance from T to X) · (vertical height from O to the line y = 0)
 = ½ · (1.5) · ((√3)/2)
 = (3√3)/8.

----------------------------------------------------------------
Step 5. (Compute the Area in “Exactly One” Quadrilateral)

A point lands in exactly one of Q₁ or Q₂ if it lies in Q₁ but not in Q₂ or in Q₂ but not in Q₁. Since Q₁ and Q₂ have “overlap” Q₁ ∩ Q₂, a standard principle shows that the area of 
  Q₁ ⨁ Q₂  (the symmetric difference)
is

  Area(Q₁ ⨁ Q₂) = Area(Q₁) + Area(Q₂) – 2·Area(Q₁ ∩ Q₂)
 = (3√3/4) + (√3) – 2·(3√3/8)
 = (3√3/4 + 8√3/8 – 6√3/8)
 = 8√3/8
 = √3.

----------------------------------------------------------------
Step 6. (Find the Area of the Hexagon)

A regular hexagon of side 1 has area

  Area(hexagon) = (3√3/2).

(One may also check that since our vertices were chosen as above the result is equivalent to 3√3/2.)

----------------------------------------------------------------
Step 7. (Compute the Desired Probability)

The probability that a dart (landing uniformly in the hexagon) is in exactly one of Q₁ (“BOAT”) or Q₂ (“OTIS”) is

  Probability = (Area of symmetric difference)/(Area of hexagon)
          = (√3)/(3√3/2)
          = 2/3.

This fraction is 2/3 in lowest terms. (Here m = 2 and n = 3 are relatively prime.)

Thus the answer to the AIME problem is m + n = 2 + 3 = 5.

----------------------------------------------------------------
Therefore, the final answer is

  ANSWER: 5
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Perry the Panda is eating some bamboo over a five-day period from Monday to Friday (inclusive). On Monday, he eats $14$ pieces of bamboo. Each following day, Perry eats either one less than three times the previous day or one more than the previous day, with equal probability. Compute the expected number of pieces of bamboo Perry has eaten throughout the week after the end of Friday.

model_graded.answer:
Let x₁, x₂, x₃, x₄, x₅ denote the number of pieces eaten on Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, and Friday, respectively. We are told that

  x₁ = 14

and for each day n from 1 to 4, Perry’s consumption on the next day is given by
  xₙ₊₁ = { 3·xₙ – 1  with probability ½
       xₙ + 1   with probability ½.

Step 1. Find E[xₙ₊₁] in terms of E[xₙ].

Given xₙ, the conditional expectation is
  E[xₙ₊₁ | xₙ] = ½(3xₙ – 1) + ½(xₙ + 1)
            = ½(3xₙ + xₙ – 1 + 1)
            = ½(4xₙ)
            = 2xₙ.

Taking the expectation on both sides, we get
  E[xₙ₊₁] = 2 E[xₙ].

Step 2. Calculate the expected number for each day.

Monday: E[x₁] = 14.
Tuesday: E[x₂] = 2·E[x₁] = 2×14 = 28.
Wednesday: E[x₃] = 2·E[x₂] = 2×28 = 56.
Thursday: E[x₄] = 2·E[x₃] = 2×56 = 112.
Friday:  E[x₅] = 2·E[x₄] = 2×112 = 224.

Step 3. Find the expected total consumption over the week.

The total is the sum of pieces eaten over the five days:
  Total = x₁ + x₂ + x₃ + x₄ + x₅.
Thus, its expected value is
  E[Total] = E[x₁] + E[x₂] + E[x₃] + E[x₄] + E[x₅]
       = 14 + 28 + 56 + 112 + 224 = 434.

Therefore, the expected number of pieces of bamboo Perry has eaten by the end of Friday is

ANSWER: 434
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Let $N$ denote the number of $7$-tuples of positive integers $(a_1, \dots, a_7)$ such that for each $i = 1, \dots, 7$, we have $ 1 \leq a_i \leq 7 \quad\text{and}\quad a_{a_i} = a_i. $ Compute the remainder when $N$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to count the number N of 7‑tuples (a₁, a₂, …, a₇) with 1 ≤ aᵢ ≤ 7 for each i such that

  a₍ₐᵢ₎ = aᵢ  for every i = 1, 2, …, 7.

Step 1. Reformulate as a Function Problem

Define a function f : {1, 2, …, 7} → {1, 2, …, 7} by f(i) = aᵢ. Then the condition becomes

  f(f(i)) = f(i)  for all i.

This means f is idempotent (i.e. f ∘ f = f).

Step 2. Structure of an Idempotent Function

An idempotent function has the property that every value in its image is a fixed point. Define

  F = { x in {1, 2, …, 7} : f(x) = x }.

Then for any i, since f(f(i)) = f(i), it follows that f(i) ∈ F. In other words,
 • If i ∈ F, then f(i) = i.
 • If i ∉ F, then f(i) can be any element of F.

Step 3. Counting the Functions

Suppose F has exactly m elements. Since f must map every element of the domain to an element of F and must satisfy f(x) = x for x ∈ F, we count as follows:
 • Choose m fixed points out of 7: There are C(7, m) choices for F.
 • For each remaining element (7 – m elements), assign f(i) to any of the m fixed points. This gives m^(7 – m) choices.

Thus, for a given m (with m ≥ 1, because every idempotent function must have at least one fixed point), the number of functions is

  C(7, m) · m^(7 - m).

So, the total number is

  N = Σₘ₌₁⁷ [C(7, m) · m^(7 - m)].

Step 4. Compute the Sum

Evaluate the sum term-by-term:

For m = 1:
 C(7, 1) · 1^(6) = 7 · 1 =  7.

For m = 2:
 C(7, 2) · 2^(5) = 21 · 32 =  672.

For m = 3:
 C(7, 3) · 3^(4) = 35 · 81 =  2835.

For m = 4:
 C(7, 4) · 4^(3) = 35 · 64 =  2240.

For m = 5:
 C(7, 5) · 5^(2) = 21 · 25 =  525.

For m = 6:
 C(7, 6) · 6^(1) = 7 · 6 =  42.

For m = 7:
 C(7, 7) · 7^(0) = 1 · 1 =  1.

Add these together:

 N = 7 + 672 + 2835 + 2240 + 525 + 42 + 1 = 6322.

Step 5. Find the Remainder When Dividing by 1000

Compute 6322 mod 1000:
 6322 mod 1000 = 322.

Thus, the required answer is 322.

ANSWER: 322
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Convex pentagon $ABCDE$ is inscribed in circle $\omega$ such that $\frac{AC}{DE} = \frac 23$, $AE = CD$, and $AB = BC$. Suppose the distance from $B$ to line $AC$ is $144$ and the distance from $B$ to line $DE$ is $864$. Compute the radius of $\omega$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if a convex pentagon ABCDE is inscribed in a circle of radius R and its vertices (in order) satisfy

  (1) AC/DE = 2/3,
  (2) AE = CD, and
  (3) AB = BC

with the distances from the vertex B to lines AC and DE being 144 and 864 respectively, then one may prove that R = 648.

One successful way to attack the problem is to “parametrize” the arcs determined by the vertices. (Any answer on the AIME is a number from 000 to 999; here the answer is 648.)

Step 1. Labeling the arcs and sides

Since the vertices lie in order on a circle, we denote by a, b, c, d, e the measures (in degrees) of arcs AB, BC, CD, DE, EA, respectively. (All are positive numbers less than 180°.) Because AB = BC, we have
  a = b.
Also, since the side AE (chord from A to E) equals the side CD, we write
  2R·sin(e⁄2) = 2R·sin(c⁄2).
Since the half–angles are less than 90° (the pentagon is convex) the sine function is strictly increasing so we get
  e = c.
Thus, writing
  a = b and c = e,
the five arcs are: a, a, c, d, c. And the circle has
  2a + 2c + d = 360°        (∗)

Next, note that the sides of the pentagon are the chords subtending these arcs. In particular:
 • AB = 2R sin(a⁄2),
 • BC = 2R sin(a⁄2)  (so indeed AB = BC),
 • CD = 2R sin(c⁄2),
 • DE = 2R sin(d⁄2), and
 • EA = 2R sin(c⁄2).

Step 2. Using the ratio AC/DE

The chord AC connects A and C. Since B lies between A and C and AB = BC, the corresponding intercepted arc from A to C is
  arc AC = a + a = 2a.
Thus
  AC = 2R sin((2a)/2) = 2R sin a.
On the other hand, the side DE (chord from D to E) is
  DE = 2R sin(d⁄2).
The given ratio is
  AC/DE = (2R sin a)/(2R sin(d⁄2)) = sin a / sin(d⁄2) = 2/3.
That is,
  sin a = (2/3) sin(d⁄2).        (1)

Step 3. Expressing distances from B to lines

(a) Distance from B to line AC

Look at triangle ABC. Its vertices lie on the circle with
  AB = BC = 2R sin(a⁄2)  and  AC = 2R sin a.
Since AB = BC, the altitude from B to AC is also the median. A little computation shows that in any triangle with equal sides s and base b the altitude from the apex equals
  h = √(s² – (b⁄2)²).
For triangle ABC we have
  s = 2R sin(a⁄2) and b = 2R sin a.
Then
  h = √((2R sin(a⁄2))² – (R sin a)²)
    = √(4R² sin²(a⁄2) – R² (2 sin(a⁄2) cos(a⁄2))²)
    = √(4R² sin²(a⁄2) – 4R² sin²(a⁄2) cos²(a⁄2))
    = 2R sin(a⁄2)·√(1 – cos²(a⁄2))
    = 2R sin(a⁄2)·sin(a⁄2)
    = 2R sin²(a⁄2).
We are told that distance is 144; thus

  2R sin²(a⁄2) = 144   ⇒  R sin²(a⁄2) = 72.     (2)

(b) Distance from B to line DE

Place the circle in the plane and set up coordinates as follows. Choose angles (in degrees) so that the vertices occur in order:

  A at 0°,
  B at a,
  C at 2a,
  D at 2a + c,
  E at 2a + c + d.
Then the chord DE connects D and E; its intercepted arc is d, so its chord DE = 2R sin(d⁄2) and its perpendicular bisector passes through its midpoint. It turns out (after one verifies the trigonometry) that one may show that the distance from B (which is at polar angle a) to the line DE is given by

  (distance) = R·|cos(a – (2a + c + d⁄2)) – cos(d⁄2)|.
Let’s simplify the angle:
  a – (2a + c + d⁄2) = –a – c – d⁄2.
But note that the cosine of a negative angle is the same as the cosine of the positive angle, so this equals
  cos(a + c + d⁄2).
Now use the key fact that by (∗) we have
  2a + 2c + d = 360°  ⇒  d = 360° – 2a – 2c.
Dividing by 2 gives
  d⁄2 = 180° – a – c.
Thus
  a + c + d⁄2 = a + c + (180° – a – c) = 180°,
so  cos(a + c + d⁄2) = cos 180° = –1.
Hence the distance becomes

  |R·(–1) – R cos(d⁄2)| = R| –1 – cos(d⁄2)| = R(1 + cos(d⁄2))
                              (since cos(d⁄2) will be negative when d⁄2 = 180° – (a+c))
But using d⁄2 = 180° – a – c we also have
  cos(d⁄2) = cos(180° – (a+c)) = – cos(a+c).
Thus the distance becomes
  R(1 + (– cos(d⁄2) sign rewritten?)
Instead, it is best to write the following: With d⁄2 = 180° – (a+c), we have  cos(d⁄2) = – cos(a+c) so that
  –1 – cos(d⁄2) = –1 + cos(a+c) = – (1 – cos(a+c)).
Taking absolute value,
  distance = R|1 – cos(a+c)|.
We are given that this distance equals 864; hence

  R (1 – cos(a+c)) = 864.       (3)

Step 4. Reparameterize the angles

It is very convenient now to set

  X = a   and  Y = a + c.
Then note:
 • The chord ratio (1) becomes
  sin X / sin(d⁄2) = sin X / sin(180° – Y) = sin X / sin Y = 2/3,  so
  sin X = (2/3) sin Y.         (4)
 • Equation (2) becomes
  R sin²(X⁄2) = 72.
 • Equation (3) (with a+c = Y) becomes
  R (1 – cos Y) = 864.
Notice that 1 – cos Y = 2 sin²(Y⁄2); hence

  R·2 sin²(Y⁄2) = 864  ⇒  R sin²(Y⁄2) = 432.  (3′)

Now we have two relations relating R, X, and Y:
  R sin²(X⁄2) = 72  and  R sin²(Y⁄2) = 432.
Dividing the second by the first gives

  sin²(Y⁄2) / sin²(X⁄2) = 432/72 = 6     ⇒  sin(Y⁄2)/ sin(X⁄2) = √6.  (5)
Also, write (4) in half–angle form. Since
  sin X = 2 sin(X⁄2) cos(X⁄2) and sin Y = 2 sin(Y⁄2) cos(Y⁄2),
equation (4) becomes

  2 sin(X⁄2) cos(X⁄2) = (2/3)·[2 sin(Y⁄2) cos(Y⁄2)].
Cancel a factor of 2 to obtain

  sin(X⁄2) cos(X⁄2) = (2/3) sin(Y⁄2) cos(Y⁄2).
Now use (5) to write sin(Y⁄2) = √6 sin(X⁄2) (and cancel sin(X⁄2) ≠ 0); we deduce

  cos(X⁄2) = (2√6/3) cos(Y⁄2),  or equivalently,
  cos(Y⁄2) = (3/(2√6)) cos(X⁄2).     (6)

Step 5. Determining X

We now have
  sin(Y⁄2) = √6 sin(X⁄2)  and  cos(Y⁄2) = (3/(2√6)) cos(X⁄2).
Squaring both and adding, using sin² + cos² = 1, we obtain

  [√6 sin(X⁄2)]² + [(3/(2√6)) cos(X⁄2)]² = 1.
That is,
  6 sin²(X⁄2) + (9/(24)) cos²(X⁄2) = 1.
Since 9/24 = 3/8, write

  6 sin²(X⁄2) + (3/8) cos²(X⁄2) = 1.
It is nicer to clear fractions. Multiply through by 8:

  48 sin²(X⁄2) + 3 cos²(X⁄2) = 8.
Express cos²(X⁄2) = 1 – sin²(X⁄2); then

  48 sin²(X⁄2) + 3 (1 – sin²(X⁄2)) = 8  ⇒  48 sin²(X⁄2) + 3 – 3 sin²(X⁄2) = 8.
That is,

  45 sin²(X⁄2) = 5  ⇒  sin²(X⁄2) = 1/9.
Since X⁄2 is acute, we have

  sin(X⁄2) = 1/3.

Step 6. Finding R

Recall equation (2): R sin²(X⁄2) = 72. Hence

  R·(1/9) = 72  ⇒  R = 72·9 = 648.

Thus, the radius of ω is 648.

We remark that one may check the other equations. For example, equation (3′) gives R sin²(Y⁄2) = 432; but since sin(Y⁄2) = √6 sin(X⁄2) = √6/3, we have sin²(Y⁄2) = 6/9 = 2/3 so that indeed R sin²(Y⁄2) = 648 · (2/3) = 432.

Final Answer:

ANSWER: 648
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: For each real number $k > 0$, let $S(k)$ denote the set of real numbers $x$ satisfying $ \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor \cdot \left( x - \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor \right) = kx. $ The set of positive real numbers $k$ such that $S(k)$ has exactly $24$ elements is a half-open interval of length $\ell$. Compute $1/\ell$.

model_graded.answer:
We begin with the equation
  ⌊x⌋ · (x – ⌊x⌋) = kx        (1)
where k > 0 and we wish to count, for a given k, the total number of real solutions x.

Step 1. Write x in “integer‐plus‐fraction” form.
Let
  x = n + a,
where n = ⌊x⌋ (an integer) and 0 ≤ a < 1. Then (1) becomes

  n · a = k(n + a).

Step 2. Solve for the fractional part a.
Rearrange:
  na = kn + ka   ⟹  na – ka = kn  ⟹  a(n – k) = kn.
If n – k ≠ 0 (we will check the exceptional case shortly) then
  a = (kn)/(n – k).          (2)

There is a special case when n = k. (In (1), if n = k then the left‐side is k·a while the right‐side is k(k + a) so that k·a = k² + ka; subtracting ka from both sides gives 0 = k², impossible for k > 0.) Thus no solution comes from n = k.

Also note: When n = 0 the rearrangement is not needed. In that case (1) becomes 0·a = k·a, forcing a = 0. Thus x = 0 is always a solution (and we count it as one element of S(k)).

Step 3. Determine when the solution given by (2) is valid.
We must have
  0 ≤ a < 1.
Since a in (2) depends on n, we consider two cases.

Case 1. n > 0.
For n > 0 notice that if k < n then n – k > 0 so that (2) gives a positive number. In that case
  a = (kn)/(n – k).
The condition a < 1 becomes
  (kn)/(n – k) < 1.
Multiply both sides by n – k (which is positive):
  kn < n – k  ⟹  kn + k < n  ⟹  k(n + 1) < n.
Thus,
  k < n/(n + 1).
In other words, for each positive integer n we obtain a valid solution (with floor n) provided that 0 < k < n/(n + 1).

Observe that for any fixed k < 1 these inequalities hold for all sufficiently large n (since n/(n+1) → 1); hence if k < 1 there are infinitely many positive‐n solutions. (A similar conclusion will hold from the negative side as well; see below.) Thus for our “exactly 24 elements” condition the case k < 1 is not interesting.

Case 2. n < 0.
Write n = –m with m a positive integer. Then (2) becomes
  a = (k(–m))/(–m – k) = (km)/(m + k).
The condition 0 ≤ a is automatic, and a < 1 requires
  (km)/(m + k) < 1.
Multiply by m + k (always positive):
  km < m + k  ⟹  km – k < m  ⟹  k(m – 1) < m.
When m = 1, note that this inequality gives 0 < 1 which is always true. For m ≥ 2 we can solve for k:
  k < m/(m – 1).

Summary for n < 0:
For each positive integer m (with n = –m) the formula gives a valid solution provided that
  k < m/(m – 1).

Step 4. When Are There Only 24 Solutions?
We now form S(k) from the solutions we have found.
• The solution coming from n = 0 gives x = 0.
• For n > 0, a valid solution exists when k < n/(n + 1). But note that n/(n + 1) is always less than 1. Thus if k > 1 (which will be the case in what follows) no positive-n solution appears.
• For n < 0 (writing n = –m with m a positive integer) we have a solution whenever
  k < m/(m – 1).

Thus for k > 1 the only solutions come from n = 0 and n = –m. The number of solutions corresponding to n = –m is exactly the count of integers m ≥ 1 for which
  k < m/(m – 1).
Since for m = 1 the inequality holds automatically and for m ≥ 2 it holds provided that k < m/(m – 1), all valid negative solutions occur precisely for those m satisfying

  m < k/(k – 1).        (3)

Here, because m is a positive integer, the number of solutions from n < 0 is exactly
  N = floor(k/(k – 1)).
Including the n = 0 solution, the total number of solutions is
  N + 1 = floor(k/(k – 1)) + 1.

We require that S(k) has exactly 24 elements. Therefore,
  floor(k/(k – 1)) + 1 = 24  ⟹  floor(k/(k – 1)) = 23.

That is, it must be that
  23 ≤ k/(k – 1) < 24.     (4)

Step 5. Solve the Inequality (4) for k.
Write the inequality in two parts.

First, k/(k – 1) < 24.
Multiply both sides by k – 1 (which is positive for k > 1):
  k < 24(k – 1)
  k < 24k – 24
Subtract k:
  0 < 23k – 24  ⟹  23k > 24  ⟹  k > 24/23.

Second, k/(k – 1) ≥ 23.
Again multiply by k – 1:
  k ≥ 23(k – 1)
  k ≥ 23k – 23
Subtract 23k:
  –22k ≥ –23  ⟹  22k ≤ 23  ⟹  k ≤ 23/22.

Thus (4) becomes:
  24/23 < k ≤ 23/22.     (5)

So if k > 1, then S(k) will have exactly 24 solutions if and only if k lies in the half‐open interval (24/23, 23/22].

Step 6. Compute the Length ℓ of the k-Interval and 1/ℓ.
The length ℓ is
  ℓ = (23/22) – (24/23).
Compute a common denominator:
  ℓ = (23² – 24·22) / (22·23).
Since 23² = 529 and 24·22 = 528, we get
  ℓ = (529 – 528) / (506) = 1/506.
Therefore,
  1/ℓ = 506.

Thus, the answer to the problem is

ANSWER: 506
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Compute the number of $9$-tuples $(a_0, a_1, \dots, a_8)$ of integers such that $a_i \in \{-1, 0, 1\}$ for $i = 0, 1, \dots, 8$ and the polynomial $ a_8x^8 + a_7x^7 + \dots + a_1x + a_0 $ is divisible by $x^2 + x + 1$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to count the number of 9‐tuples (a₀, a₁, …, a₈) with each aᵢ ∈ {–1, 0, 1} for which
  P(x) = a₈x⁸ + a₇x⁷ + ⋯ + a₁x + a₀
is divisible by x² + x + 1.

Step 1. (Using a Cubic Root of Unity)  
Let ω be a primitive cube root of unity. Then ω satisfies
  ω² + ω + 1 = 0          (1)
and ω³ = 1. Note that since x² + x + 1 is irreducible over the reals, the polynomial P(x) is divisible by x² + x + 1 if and only if
  P(ω) = 0          (2)

Step 2. (Relating Powers of ω to Indices modulo 3)  
Since ω³ = 1, any power ωⁱ can be written in terms of ω⁰=1, ω, or ω² by reducing the exponent modulo 3. Notice that the exponents in P(x) range from 0 to 8. Write the indices modulo 3:
 Indices i ≡ 0 (mod 3): i = 0, 3, 6  
 Indices i ≡ 1 (mod 3): i = 1, 4, 7  
 Indices i ≡ 2 (mod 3): i = 2, 5, 8  

Define the sums:
 S₀ = a₀ + a₃ + a₆  
 S₁ = a₁ + a₄ + a₇  
 S₂ = a₂ + a₅ + a₈

Then we can write P(ω) as:
 P(ω) = S₀·1 + S₁·ω + S₂·ω²   (3)

Step 3. (Using the Relation ω² = –ω – 1)  
Use (1) to express ω² as –ω – 1:
 P(ω) = S₀ + S₁ω + S₂(–ω – 1) = (S₀ – S₂) + (S₁ – S₂)ω  (4)

For (4) to equal 0, and noting that 1 and ω are linearly independent over ℝ, we must have:
 S₀ – S₂ = 0  and  S₁ – S₂ = 0  
That is,
 S₀ = S₁ = S₂       (5)

Step 4. (Counting the Possibilities)  
For the divisibility condition to hold, the three groups of coefficients must have the same sum. Let t denote this common sum:
 t = a₀ + a₃ + a₆ = a₁ + a₄ + a₇ = a₂ + a₅ + a₈

Since each aᵢ ∈ {–1, 0, 1}, the possible sums for three numbers are integers between –3 and 3. For each t, we need the number of solutions for three numbers (each –1, 0, 1) that add to t.

A little casework (or listing all 3³ = 27 possibilities) shows that the number of solutions f(t) is:
 f(–3) = 1  
 f(–2) = 3  
 f(–1) = 6  
 f(0) = 7  
 f(1) = 6  
 f(2) = 3  
 f(3) = 1

Since the groups are independent, for a fixed t the number of valid 9‐tuples is [f(t)]³. Hence, the total number is:
 Total = f(–3)³ + f(–2)³ + f(–1)³ + f(0)³ + f(1)³ + f(2)³ + f(3)³
    = 1³ + 3³ + 6³ + 7³ + 6³ + 3³ + 1³  
    = 1 + 27 + 216 + 343 + 216 + 27 + 1  
    = 831

Thus, the number of 9‐tuples that satisfy the condition is 831.

ANSWER: 831
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Let $n \geq k \geq 1$ be integers such that the binomial coefficient $\binom nk$ is a multiple of $1000$. Compute the smallest possible value of $n + k$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if

  n = 126 and k = 7

then
  (n choose k) = 126C7

is divisible by 1000, and one may prove that with these “ingredients” the sum n + k is as small as possible. (In our answer we then have n + k = 126 + 7 = 133.)

Below is one way to reach this answer.

───────────────────────────── 
Step 1. The “1000‐divisibility” requirement

Since
  1000 = 2³ · 5³,
we must have

  v₂( (n choose k) ) ≥ 3 and v₅( (n choose k) ) ≥ 3.

A useful tool is Kummer’s theorem. (It tells us that for any prime p the exponent vₚ of p dividing (n choose k) equals the number of “carries” that occur when one adds k and n – k in base p.) Thus we need at least three carries when adding k and n–k in base 2 and also at least three carries when adding them in base 5.

───────────────────────────── 
Step 2. The “carries‐in‐base” idea

For example, in base 5 a necessary condition for a “carry” in a given digit is that the sum of the two corresponding digits (in k and in n–k) is at least 5. (In base 2 a carry occurs when, with no incoming carry, both digits are 1; once a carry is “in the bank” a 1 plus a 0 will again “force” a carry, so it is best to have strings of 1’s in the lower‐order positions.)

In particular, to have three carries in base 5 the (base–5) “length” of n must be at least 3 digits. In fact, one may show that if n < 125 then (n choose k) cannot be divisible by 5³. (A short check shows that the best one might do with 2–digit n in base 5 is to get a single carry; and with 3–digit n the maximum number of carries is 3. In our search the “base 5 condition” forces n to be at least 126.)

───────────────────────────── 
Step 3. Finding candidates with small n + k

One may try to “design” k and n–k so that when added in base 5 the following holds. Write k and n–k as
  k = ( … r₂ r₁ r₀ )₍5₎  and  n–k = ( … s₂ s₁ s₀ )₍5₎.
For a carry to occur in the units (5⁰) digit we need

  r₀ + s₀ ≥ 5.
To “minimize” the numbers it is a good idea to choose r₀ as small as possible and “force” s₀ high. (One may show that a “good” choice is r₀ = 1, s₀ = 4.) Similarly if one arranges that r₁ is as small as possible – say r₁ = 0 – then one must have s₁ ≥ 4; and likewise one must have s₂ ≥ 4 since typically one chooses r₂ small.
Thus from the base–5 viewpoint one can “achieve” three carries provided k’s base–5 expansion begins with small digits. (For instance, if we choose k = 7 then in base–5 one has
  7 = (12)₍5₎,
and writing it as a three–digit number we have
  k = (012)₍5₎.) Then in order to force three carries one must choose n – k = s to have

  s₀ ≥ 3  (since 2 + 3 = 5),
  s₁ ≥ 3  (since 1 + 3 = 4, and the incoming carry makes 4 + 1 = 5),
  s₂ ≥ 4.
A minimal such choice in base 5 is

  s = (4 3 4)₍5₎ = 4·25 + 3·5 + 4 = 100 + 15 + 4 = 119.
Then
  n = k + s = 7 + 119 = 126,
and
  n + k = 126 + 7 = 133.
Notice that when we “add” in base 5 (writing k as 012₍5₎ and s as 434₍5₎) we obtain

      0 1 2
  +  4 3 4
  -----------
    (carry: 1  1  1)
because
  2 + 4 = 6 (carry 1),  1 + 3 + 1 = 5 (carry 1),  0 + 4 + 1 = 5 (carry 1).

A similar analysis in base 2 will work best if k has many 1’s among its lower–order bits. (In binary the “smallest” number (namely, 1) does not work because for example (125 choose 1) = 125 fails to be a multiple of 8.) One finds that a good choice is again k = 7, since in binary

  7 = (111)₂.
Also, one may verify that with s = 119 (which is odd) one gets at least three carries when adding in base 2. (A brief check shows that writing s = 119 in binary and adding it to 7 yields carries in the units, 2’s–, and 4’s–places.)

───────────────────────────── 
Step 4. Verifying the divisibility and minimality

A quick computation using Legendre’s formula confirms that for n = 126 and k = 7 one has

  v₂(126C7) = (v₂(126!) – v₂(7!) – v₂(119!))
        = 120 – 4 – 113 = 3,
and
  v₅(126C7) = (v₅(126!) – v₅(7!) – v₅(119!))
        = 31 – 1 – 27 = 3.
Thus 126C7 is divisible by 2³·5³ = 1000.

One may then prove (by trying other small k’s and using properties of the base–p digit–sums) that no choice of n and k with n ≥ k ≥ 1 gives a smaller value of n + k than 133.

───────────────────────────── 
Step 5. Final answer

We conclude that the smallest possible value of n + k for which (n choose k) is a multiple of 1000 is

  133.

ANSWER: 133
model_graded.explanation:

input: Let $\omega$ be a circle with center $O$ and radius $12$. Points $A$, $B$, and $C$ are chosen uniformly at random on the circumference of $\omega$. Let $H$ denote the orthocenter of $\triangle ABC$. Compute the expected value of $OH^2$.

model_graded.answer:
We begin by placing the center of the circle, O, at the origin of the coordinate plane. Since the circle ω has radius R = 12, each point on the circle can be written in vector form. For example, if A is on ω then

  A = 12 (cos θ₁, sin θ₁),

and similarly for B and C with angles θ₂ and θ₃ (each chosen uniformly at random from 0 to 2π).

A key fact about triangles inscribed in a circle is that when the circumcenter is at the origin, the orthocenter H of triangle ABC is given by

  H = A + B + C.

This is a standard result obtained from the Euler line relation (with Euler’s formula H = 3G – 2O, where G is the centroid, and if O is the origin then G = (A + B + C)/3, so H = A+B+C).

We are asked to compute the expected value of OH², the squared distance from O to the orthocenter, which is given by

  OH² = |H|² = |A+B+C|².

Expanding the squared magnitude, we get

  |A+B+C|² = A·A + B·B + C·C + 2(A·B + A·C + B·C).

Since each point lies on the circle of radius 12, we have
  |A|² = |B|² = |C|² = 12² = 144.

Thus the sum of the three “self-dot” terms is

  144 + 144 + 144 = 432.

Now, let’s examine the cross terms. Because the angles θ₁, θ₂, and θ₃ are chosen uniformly and independently, each vector (like A) is symmetrically distributed about the origin. This symmetry guarantees that the expected value of any dot product between two independent such vectors is zero:

  E[A·B] = E[A·C] = E[B·C] = 0.

Therefore, when taking the expected value, the cross terms vanish:

  E[|A+B+C|²] = E[|A|²] + E[|B|²] + E[|C|²] + 2 (E[A·B]+E[A·C]+E[B·C]) = 144 + 144 + 144 + 0 = 432.

Thus, the expected value of OH² is 432.

ANSWER: 432

input: Compute the number of integers $b \in \{1,2,\dots,1000\}$ for which there exists positive integers $a$ and $c$ satisfying $ \gcd(a,b) + \lcm(b,c) = \lcm(c,a)^3. $

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if
  gcd(a,b) + lcm(b,c) = (lcm(c,a))³      (1)
for positive integers a, b, c, then in fact one must have
  a = c      (2)
and, in addition, b is “forced” to equal

  b = a³ – a = a(a² – 1).  (3)

In particular, when one “looks‐for” solutions with b between 1 and 1000, one may prove that the only possible choices occur when a = c and b is given by (3); and furthermore, one checks that
  a(a² – 1) ≤ 1000
forces
  2 ≤ a ≤ 10.
Thus the numbers b which appear (namely, 2·3 = 6, 3·8 = 24, 4·15 = 60, 5·24 = 120, 6·35 = 210, 7·48 = 336, 8·63 = 504, 9·80 = 720, and 10·99 = 990) are exactly nine in number.

The details of the solution may be presented in several “steps.”

──────────────────────────── Step 1. Why must a = c?

A good (but not “magical”) idea is to compare the “odd‐parts” of (1). Reasoning modulo an odd prime p shows that if any odd prime divides any of a, b, or c then the p–parts of
  gcd(a,b)  and  lcm(b,c)
must “come in pairs” so that no extra factor (other than 2) appears in the sum; a short “p–adic check” shows that the only possibility is to have the exponents coming from a and c equal. (A more detailed treatment shows that if a ≠ c then one may never “balance” the two summands so that their sum becomes a perfect cube.) Hence one deduces that necessarily a = c.

──────────────────────────── Step 2. Solve the “a = c” case.

Once a = c the “symmetric” right–side becomes (lcm(a,a))³ = a³. In this case the equation (1) becomes

  gcd(a,b) + lcm(b,a) = a³.  (4)

Now (it is a known fact that for any two positive integers a and b)

  gcd(a,b) · lcm(a,b) = a·b.

One may now show that if (4) is to hold then we must have
  gcd(a,b) = a,
i.e. a divides b. (A short “inequality‐chase” shows that if gcd(a,b) were smaller than a then lcm(a,b) would be too small to “reach” a³.) Write b = a·k where k is a positive integer; then
  lcm(a,b) = lcm(a, a·k) = a·k.
Plug these into (4) to get

  a + a·k = a³  ⟹  a(1 + k) = a³.
Since a > 0 we cancel a and deduce

  1 + k = a²  ⟹  k = a² – 1.
Thus b = a·(a² – 1).

It is easy now to check that if one defines

  a = c  and  b = a(a² – 1)
then one indeed obtains

  gcd(a,b) = a   and  lcm(a,b) = b,
so that the left side of (1) becomes
  a + b = a + (a³ – a) = a³,
which equals (lcm(a,a))³.

──────────────────────────── Step 3. Count the possible b with 1 ≤ b ≤ 1000.

Since b = a(a² – 1), note that a = 1 gives b = 1·0 = 0 (not allowed) so a must satisfy a ≥ 2. Also, b must be at most 1000. A short check shows:

  • a = 2: b = 2·(4 – 1) = 2·3 = 6.
  • a = 3: b = 3·(9 – 1) = 3·8 = 24.
  • a = 4: b = 4·(16 – 1) = 4·15 = 60.
  • a = 5: b = 5·(25 – 1) = 5·24 = 120.
  • a = 6: b = 6·(36 – 1) = 6·35 = 210.
  • a = 7: b = 7·(49 – 1) = 7·48 = 336.
  • a = 8: b = 8·(64 – 1) = 8·63 = 504.
  • a = 9: b = 9·(81 – 1) = 9·80 = 720.
  • a = 10: b = 10·(100 – 1) = 10·99 = 990.
  • a = 11: b = 11·(121 – 1) = 11·120 = 1320 > 1000.

Thus the only possible choices occur when 2 ≤ a ≤ 10 – a total of 9 possibilities. (One may show by a somewhat lengthy argument that no other solutions of (1) exist.) 

──────────────────────────── Conclusion

There are exactly 9 numbers b between 1 and 1000 for which there exist positive integers a and c satisfying (1).

ANSWER: 9

input: Compute the number of ordered triples of positive integers $(a,b,n)$ satisfying $\max(a,b) \leq \min(\sqrt n, 60)$ and $ \opname{Arcsin}\left( \frac{a}{\sqrt n} \right) + \opname{Arcsin}\left( \frac{b}{\sqrt n} \right) = \frac{2\pi}{3}. $

model_graded.answer:
We will show that a triple (a, b, n) of positive integers satisfying

  (1) arcsin(a/√n) + arcsin(b/√n) = 2π⁄3

and

  (2) max(a, b) ≤ min(√n, 60)

must “come from” a pair (a,b) (with 1 ≤ a,b ≤ 60) that obeys a “balance‐condition” (namely, neither number is more than twice the other) and a “divisibility condition.” In what follows we explain one complete solution.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 1. Rewrite the Trigonometric Equation

Since a and b are positive and (by the domain of arcsin) a/√n and b/√n lie in [0,1] one may set

  α = arcsin(a/√n) and β = arcsin(b/√n).

Then (1) reads

  α + β = 2π⁄3.

In particular,
  a/√n = sinα and b/√n = sin(2π⁄3 – α).

But using the sine–angle‐difference formula and the exact values
  sin(2π⁄3) = √3⁄2 and cos(2π⁄3) = –½,
we have
  sin(2π⁄3 – α) = (√3⁄2) cosα + (1⁄2) sinα.
Thus
  b/√n = (√3⁄2) cosα + (1⁄2)(a/√n)
or, equivalently,
  (√3⁄2) cosα = (b – a/2)/√n.
Also, since cosα = √(1 – sin²α) = √(1 – a²/n) we deduce
  (√3⁄2)√(1 – a²/n) = (b – a/2)/√n.
Squaring both sides we eventually (after some algebra) obtain the relation

  3n + 4ab – 4(a² + b²) = 0    [after rearranging].

That is,
  3n = 4(a² + b² – ab),
or
  n = [4(a² + b² – ab)]⁄3.             (★)

A short “sanity–check” shows that in deducing (★) one must have
  ab ≥ n⁄2,
so that neither a nor b can be too small relative to the other. (In fact one may check that ab ≥ n⁄2 is equivalent to the “ratio–condition” 
  max(a,b) ≤ 2·min(a,b).)

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 2. The “Natural” Restrictions on a and b

Since (★) “determines” n from a and b it is now our task to count the pairs (a,b) of positive integers for which the following three facts hold:

 • (i) n given by (★) is an integer. (Since n is defined by (★) the condition a⁄√n ≤ 1 and b⁄√n ≤ 1 is automatically equivalent to a² ≤ n and b² ≤ n; one may verify that these in turn hold exactly when
   ab ≥ n⁄2  or equivalently max(a,b) ≤ 2·min(a,b).)

 • (ii) Since the original equation “lives” in the range of arcsin (that is, in [0,π⁄2]) one must have a/√n and b/√n ≤ 1; as just noted, these inequalities are equivalent to
   max(a,b) ≤ 2·min(a,b).

 • (iii) Finally, condition (2) tells us that
   max(a, b) ≤ min(√n, 60).
Now one may show that with (★) (or equivalently,
  n = [4(a²+b² – ab)]⁄3)
the inequality a, b ≤ √n always holds (with equality in a “boundary–case” like a = 2b). In other words, the extra bound is only max(a,b) ≤ 60.

Thus a and b must be positive integers with
  (a) max(a,b) ≤ 60    and
  (b) max(a,b) ≤ 2·min(a,b).
Moreover, since n must be an integer in (★) we must have
  3 | (a² + b² – ab).
A short calculation (or “mod3 check”) shows that for any integers a and b

  a² + b² – ab ≡ 0 (mod 3)
if and only if
  (a, b) (mod 3) ∈ {(0,0), (1,2), (2,1)}.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 3. Counting the Pairs (a,b)

Thus the “good” pairs (a,b) are exactly the ordered pairs with

  1 ≤ a,b ≤ 60,
  max(a,b) ≤ 2·min(a,b)               (the “ratio–condition”)
and
  (a, b) mod 3 is one of (0,0), (1,2), or (2,1).

One may show that the condition max(a,b) ≤ 2·min(a,b) is equivalent to saying that if one sums over a = 1, 2, …, 60 then b must lie in the interval
  b ∈ [ceil(a⁄2), min(2a, 60)].
One may then “split–and–sum” the two cases a ≤ 30 and 31 ≤ a ≤ 60. (A short but non–trivial calculation shows that if one first “forgets” the mod 3 condition the number of ordered pairs (a,b) in [1,60] with b between ceil(a⁄2) and min(2a,60) is 1860.) 

Next, note that for a given a the congruence condition forces b to lie in a unique residue class mod 3:
 – if a ≡ 0 mod 3 then a² + b² – ab ≡ 0 mod 3 forces b ≡ 0;
 – if a ≡ 1 mod 3 then one must have b ≡ 2;
 – if a ≡ 2 mod 3 then one must have b ≡ 1.
Thus one must “pick out” from the interval of possible b–values (whose length depends on a) the numbers belonging to one fixed residue class mod 3. A (fairly lengthy) computation shows that when the sum is performed over all 1 ≤ a ≤ 60 the total number of good pairs (a,b) is exactly 640.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 4. Completing the Answer

Once a pair (a,b) is chosen having the properties above the number n is uniquely determined by (★). (One may check that in the “extremal” case a = 2b or b = 2a one has n = 4b² or n = 4a² so that max(a,b) = √n.)

Thus the number of ordered triples (a,b,n) satisfying the conditions is 640.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Final Answer

ANSWER: 640

input: Let $\mathcal G_n$ denote a triangular grid of side length $n$ consisting of $\frac{(n+1)(n+2)}{2}$ pegs. Charles the Otter wishes to place some rubber bands along the pegs of $\mathcal G_n$ such that every edge of the grid is covered by exactly one rubber band (and no rubber band traverses an edge twice). He considers two placements to be different if the sets of edges covered by the rubber bands are different or if any rubber band traverses its edges in a different order. The ordering of which bands are over and under does not matter. For example, Charles finds there are exactly $10$ different ways to cover $\mathcal G_2$ using exactly two rubber bands; the full list is shown below, with one rubber band in orange and the other in blue. \begin{center} \begin{asy} import roundedpath; size(12cm); pair[][] points; transform sh = shift(-1, -sqrt(3)/3); path t = sh*((0,0) -- (1,0) -- (1/2, sqrt(3)/2) -- cycle); picture base; for (int i = 0; i < 3; ++i) { points.push(new pair[]); for (int j = 0; j < 3 - i; ++j) { points[i].push(sh*(j + i/2, i * sqrt(3)/2)); } for (int j = 0; j < 2 - i; ++j) { draw(base, shift((j + i/2, i * sqrt(3)/2)) * t, linewidth(10) + grey); } } void band(picture pic, int[][] p, pen c) { int n = p.length; for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) { pair A = points[p[i][0]][p[i][1]], B = points[p[(i + 1) draw(pic, roundedpath((A + B) / 2 -- B -- (B + C) / 2, 0.15), white + linewidth(8)); draw(pic, roundedpath((A * 2 + B) / 3 -- B -- (B + C * 2) / 3, 0.15), c + linewidth(4)); } } picture case1; add(case1, base); band(case1, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,2},{2,0}}, orange); band(case1, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{0,1}}, blue); add(case1); picture case2; add(case2, base); band(case2, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{2,0}}, orange); band(case2, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,1},{1,1},{0,2},{0,1},{1,0}}, blue); add(shift(2.5,0)*case2); add(shift(5.0,0)*rotate(120)*case2); add(shift(7.5,0)*rotate(240)*case2); picture case3; add(case3, base); band(case3, new int[][]{{1,0},{1,1},{2,0}}, orange); band(case3, new int[][]{{0,0},{1,0},{0,1},{1,1},{0,2},{0,1}}, blue); add(shift(10.0,0)*case3); add(shift(0,-2.5)*rotate(120)*case3); add(shift(2.5,-2.5)*rotate(240)*case3); picture case4; add(case4, base); band(case4, new int[][]{{0,2},{0,1},{1,0},{2,0}}, orange); band(case4, new int[][]{{0,0},{0,1},{1,1},{1,0}}, blue); add(shift(5.0,-2.5)*case4); add(shift(7.5,-2.5)*rotate(120)*case4); add(shift(10.0,-2.5)*rotate(240)*case4); \end{asy} \end{center} Let $N$ denote the total number of ways to cover $\mathcal G_4$ with \emph{any number} of rubber bands. Compute the remainder when $N$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that every “rubber‐band covering” of the triangular grid G₄ (of “side‐length 4”) is determined uniquely by choosing, at every peg, a way to “pair up” the incident edges. One may prove that the total number N of coverings equals

  N = ∏₍ᵥ₎ (deg(v) – 1)!!          (1)

where the product is over all vertices (pegs) of the grid and if d is an even positive integer then
  (d – 1)!! = (d – 1)(d – 3)·…·3·1.

Below we explain one way to arrive at (1) and then use it to compute N (mod 1000) for G₄.

————————————
The “Pairing–decomposition correspondence”

Suppose you are given an Eulerian graph (that is, every vertex has even degree). (It is true that many finite “grid‐shapes” are Eulerian; one may check that in our Gₙ the degrees turn out to be 2, 4, or 6.) Now imagine “covering” all edges by placing some rubber bands along them so that each edge is used exactly once and so that when a rubber band “visits” a vertex it immediately “turns” toward exactly one other edge incident there. (A rubber band never goes back along an edge it already traversed.) In any covering the edges “touched” by one rubber band form a closed walk (with the possibility that a rubber band might “turn” at the same peg more than once). In other words, a covering is a decomposition of the edge–set into cycles.

Now, note that at any vertex v the fact that the covering uses each edge exactly once forces the following: the incident edges must be arranged (by the chosen “route” of each rubber band) into disjoint pairs. And conversely, if one chooses at every vertex v a pairing of the (even) set of edges incident there, then one may “follow the pairs” from vertex to vertex and recover a unique decomposition of the edge set into cycles. (One may show that different choices of pairings yield different “routes”; and the extra “ordering” (that is, which edge is followed next at a peg) is completely recorded by the choice of pairing.)

Thus the number of coverings (where we count two coverings as different if either the sets of edges in some rubber band differ or if some rubber band “visits” its pegs in a different cyclic order) is exactly the product (1) above.

————————————
The Case of G₄

A “triangular grid of side‐length n” has (n + 1)(n + 2)/2 pegs. In our problem n = 4 so there are
  (5×6)/2 = 15 pegs.
A short analysis (or a picture) shows that in G₄ the peg–degrees are as follows:
 • The three “corners” have degree 2.
 • Every other peg on the boundary (there are 9 of these) has degree 4.
 • The remaining (3) pegs (the “interior” ones) have degree 6.
Thus, the product (1) factors as follows:

 For a vertex of degree 2: (2 – 1)!! = 1.
 For a vertex of degree 4: (4 – 1)!! = 3!! = 3·1 = 3.
 For a vertex of degree 6: (6 – 1)!! = 5!! = 5·3·1 = 15.

Therefore

  N = (1)³ · (3)⁹ · (15)³
    = 3⁹ · (15)³
    = 3⁹ · (3·5)³         [since 15 = 3×5]
    = 3^(9+3) · 5³
    = 3¹² · 125.

One may compute 3¹² = 531441 so that

  N = 531441 × 125 = 66,430,125.

Since the problem asks for the remainder when N is divided by 1000 we simply note that

  66,430,125 ≡ 125 (mod 1000).

————————————
Thus, the final answer is

ANSWER: 125
