input: Let $S$ denote the sum of all integers $n$ such that $1 \leq n \leq 2024$ and exactly one of $n^{22}-1$ and $n^{40}-1$ is divisible by $2024$. Compute the remainder when $S$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We begin by writing

  2024 = 8 · 11 · 23.

Since 2024 divides an integer X if and only if 8, 11, and 23 all divide X, we must have
  2024 | [n^(k) – 1] ⟺ 8 | [n^(k) – 1], 11 | [n^(k) – 1], and 23 | [n^(k) – 1].

In our problem we are given two expressions:
  A = n^(22) – 1 and B = n^(40) – 1,
and we wish to find all integers n, 1 ≤ n ≤ 2024, for which exactly one of A and B is divisible by 2024.
Because 2024 factors as 8·11·23 we analyze each factor separately.

──────────────────────────────
Step 1. Analysis modulo 8

Note that if n is even then n^(k) is even so n^(k) – 1 is odd and in particular not divisible by 8.
On the other hand, if n is odd, then every odd number modulo 8 is a unit and one may check that for any odd n,
  n^2 ≡ 1 (mod 8).
Thus if n is odd and k is even, then
  n^(k) = (n^2)^(k/2) ≡ 1^(k/2) ≡ 1 (mod 8),
so that n^(k) – 1 ≡ 0 (mod 8).

In our problem both 22 and 40 are even. Therefore:
 • If n is odd, both A and B are divisible by 8.
 • If n is even, neither A nor B is divisible by 8.

Thus only odd n (which are exactly the n for which 8 divides both expressions) can possibly produce a “divisible by 2024” situation. (When n is even, neither A nor B is divisible by 2024, so they would not count.)

──────────────────────────────
Step 2. Analysis modulo 11

The multiplicative group modulo 11 (ignoring n ≡ 0 mod 11) has order 10. By Fermat’s little theorem, for any n not divisible by 11 we have:
  n^(10) ≡ 1 (mod 11).

(a) For A = n^(22) – 1:
  n^(22) ≡ 1 (mod 11) if and only if the order d of n modulo 11 divides 22.
But the possible orders d (dividing 10) are 1, 2, 5, or 10. Among these, d = 1 and d = 2 do divide 22 (since 1 and 2 are divisors of 22) but 5 and 10 do not because 22 is not a multiple of 5 or 10.
Thus for n not divisible by 11:
  n^(22) – 1 ≡ 0 (mod 11) ⟺ the order of n modulo 11 is 1 or 2.
In other words, modulo 11 we need
  n ≡ 1 or n ≡ –1 (mod 11), i.e. n ≡ 1 or 10 (mod 11).

(b) For B = n^(40) – 1:
Again using Fermat’s little theorem, since 10 divides 40 we deduce that for any n not divisible by 11,
  n^(40) ≡ 1 (mod 11).
That is, n^(40) – 1 is divisible by 11 for every n with n not ≡ 0 (mod 11).

Summary modulo 11:
 • A is divisible by 11 if and only if n ≡ 1 or 10 (mod 11).
 • B is divisible by 11 if and only if n is not divisible by 11.

──────────────────────────────
Step 3. Analysis modulo 23

Modulo 23, the multiplicative group (for n not divisible by 23) has order 22. Thus by Fermat,
  n^(22) ≡ 1 (mod 23)
for every n coprime to 23.
So for n not divisible by 23:
  n^(22) – 1 ≡ 0 (mod 23)
always holds.

Now consider B = n^(40) – 1. Writing 40 in terms of 22 we may reduce the exponent modulo 22. Notice:
  40 ≡ 18 (mod 22),
so n^(40) ≡ n^(18) (mod 23).

For B to be divisible by 23 (with n not divisible by 23) we must have:
  n^(18) ≡ 1 (mod 23).
In a cyclic group of order 22, an element of order d satisfies n^(18) = 1 if and only if d divides 18.
The possible orders (dividing 22) are 1, 2, 11, and 22. Only 1 and 2 divide 18. Hence:
  n^(40) – 1 is divisible by 23 if and only if the order of n modulo 23 is 1 or 2.
That is, modulo 23 we must have:
  n ≡ 1 or n ≡ –1 (mod 23), i.e. n ≡ 1 or 22 (mod 23).

Summary modulo 23:
 • A is divisible by 23 for every n not divisible by 23.
 • B is divisible by 23 if and only if n ≡ 1 or 22 (mod 23).

──────────────────────────────
Step 4. Combining the conditions for divisibility by 2024

Recall that for 2024 = 8·11·23 to divide an expression, all three factors must divide that expression. Furthermore, note that if n is even then neither A nor B can be divisible by 8; hence in what follows we assume n is odd.

Thus, for odd n (and remembering that if n is divisible by 11 or 23 the corresponding condition fails) we have:

• A = n^(22) – 1 is divisible by 2024 if and only if
  – n is not divisible by 11 and 23,
  – n ≡ 1 or 10 (mod 11), and
  – (mod 23, no extra condition since any n not divisible by 23 gives n^(22) ≡ 1).

• B = n^(40) – 1 is divisible by 2024 if and only if
  – n is not divisible by 11 and 23,
  – (mod 11, automatically satisfied for any unit) and
  – n ≡ 1 or 22 (mod 23).

We require EXACTLY ONE of A and B to be divisible by 2024. There are two cases.

──────────────────────────────
Case 1. A is divisible by 2024 but B is not.

For this to occur we must have:
 (i) n^(22) – 1 divisible by 2024. So:
  • n is odd,
  • n is not divisible by 11 and n ≡ 1 or 10 (mod 11), and 
  • n is not divisible by 23.

 (ii) B is NOT divisible by 2024. Since for n not divisible by 11, B is automatically divisible by 11 and since n is odd B is divisible by 8, the only possible failure is modulo 23. That is, we must have:
  n^(40) – 1 not divisible by 23 ⟺ n ≠ 1, 22 (mod 23).

Thus in this case n must satisfy:
 • n is odd,
 • n ≡ 1 or 10 (mod 11), and
 • n is not divisible by 23 and in fact n (mod 23) is one of the 22 – 2 = 20 nonzero residues that are not 1 or 22.

How many solutions (mod 2024) are there from these conditions?
 – Mod 8: Since n is odd, there are 4 possible residues (1, 3, 5, 7).
 – Mod 11: 2 choices (1 or 10).
 – Mod 23: There are 22 – 2 = 20 choices.
Thus the total number in Case 1 is: 4 · 2 · 20 = 160.

──────────────────────────────
Case 2. B is divisible by 2024 but A is not.

For B to be divisible we need:
 • n is odd,
 • n is not divisible by 11 (any unit is fine for mod 11), and
 • n ≡ 1 or 22 (mod 23).

For A to fail divisibility we must lose the mod 11 condition, because (as noted) for n not divisible by 23, n^(22) – 1 ≡ 0 (mod 23) holds.
That is, we must have n NOT ≡ 1 or 10 (mod 11).

Thus in Case 2, n must satisfy:
 • n is odd,
 • n ≡ any nonzero residue modulo 11 except 1 and 10. (Since the units mod 11 are 1,2,…,10, there are 10 choices and excluding 1 and 10 gives 8 choices.)
 • n ≡ 1 or 22 (mod 23).

Counting solutions modulo 2024:
 – Mod 8: 4 choices (odd residues).
 – Mod 11: 8 choices.
 – Mod 23: 2 choices.
Total number in Case 2: 4 · 8 · 2 = 64.

──────────────────────────────
Step 5. Total valid n and summing them

The total number of n satisfying “exactly one of A and B is divisible by 2024” is
  160 (Case 1) + 64 (Case 2) = 224.

Now observe that the restrictions in both cases are given by congruences modulo 8, 11, and 23. Since 2024 = 8·11·23, the allowed n in any case form a complete system of 224 residue classes modulo 2024.
A useful fact is that this set of residue classes is “symmetric” in the following sense:
 For any valid residue r, the residue 2024 – r is also valid.
(To check, note that 2024 ≡ 0 modulo 8, 11, and 23. So if r satisfies, for example, r ≡ 1 (mod 11), then 2024 – r ≡ –r ≡ –1 ≡ 10 (mod 11); and similarly the conditions modulo 23 are symmetric since 1 and 22 are negatives of each other modulo 23. Also, since r is odd, –r is odd modulo 8.)
Thus the 224 numbers can be arranged in 224/2 = 112 pairs whose members sum to 2024. Therefore the total sum S is
  S = 112 · 2024.

A quick calculation gives:
  112 · 2024 = 112·2000 + 112·24 = 224000 + 2688 = 226688.

The problem asks for the remainder when S is divided by 1000. We compute:
  226688 mod 1000 = 688.

──────────────────────────────
Final Answer

ANSWER: 688

input: Ritwin the Otter has a cardboard equilateral triangle. He cuts the triangle with three congruent line segments of length $x$ spaced at $120^{\circ}$ angles through the center, obtaining six pieces: three congruent triangles and three congruent quadrilaterals. He then flips all three triangles over, then rearranges all six pieces to form another equilateral triangle with an equiangular hexagonal hole inside it, as shown below. \begin{center} \begin{asy} size(10cm); defaultpen(linewidth(1.5)); pen p1 = lightred; pen p2 = deepgreen; pair A = dir(90), B = dir(210), C = dir(-30); draw(A--B--C--cycle); real w = 0.8; pair D = (w * A + (1-w) * C); pair O = (0,0); pair DD = intersectionpoint(D -- ((O - D * 3) - D), B -- C); pair w = (2,0.041); real s = abs(O-D) + abs(D-rotate(120) * DD) + abs(O-DD); path quad = B -- DD -- O -- rotate(120) * D -- cycle; path tri = O -- DD -- rotate(240) * D -- cycle; real alpha = -aTan((D.y - DD.y)/(D.x - DD.x)); transform wtf1 = shift(s / 2, -s / (2 * sqrt(3))) * shift(w) * rotate(alpha); transform wtf2 = shift(abs(D), 0) * shift(-s/2, -s/(2*sqrt(3))) * shift(w) * shift(scale(-1) * xscale(-1) * rotate(240) * D) * xscale(-1); for (int i=0; i<=3; ++i) { filldraw(rotate(120*i) * quad, p2, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i) * tri, p1, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i,w) * wtf1 * quad, p2, black); filldraw(rotate(120*i,w) * wtf2 * tri, p1, black); } \end{asy} \end{center} Given that the side lengths of the hole are $3$, $2$, $3$, $2$, $3$, $2$, in that order, the value of $x$ can be written as $\frac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m + n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that a “miracle‐of‐dissection” leads to a very short answer. In the problem an equilateral triangle is cut by three congruent “chords” (line segments) of length x (all meeting at the “center”) so that the six pieces come in two kinds:
 • three “vertex–pieces” (triangles), and
 • three “edge–pieces” (quadrilaterals).

Then (after “flipping” the three triangles) the six pieces are rearranged around a “hole” so that the boundary of the hole is a convex (in fact, equiangular) hexagon with consecutive side–lengths
  3, 2, 3, 2, 3, 2.

A key observation is that in the rearrangement each of the original three cut segments “splits” into two pieces—one that comes from the triangle on one side and one that comes from the quadrilateral on the other side. (In other words, the “cut–edge” is “shared” between two of the six pieces.) In the new figure these six pieces become the six sides of the central hexagon. (See the figure; the colored pieces alternate about the hexagon.) Since the three triangle–pieces are congruent the portion of each cut coming from a triangle is the same; call that length T. Similarly, the three quadrilateral–pieces all give the same “portion” (call it Q). But each original cut was of length x so that

  T + Q = x.

Now the hexagon’s six sides (taken in order) come from these pieces in alternating order, so that its sides are
  T, Q, T, Q, T, Q.
But we are told that (in order) the hexagon’s sides are 3, 2, 3, 2, 3, 2. In particular one sees that the “triangle–side” is 3 and the “quadrilateral–side” is 2 (or vice–versa; the roles would be reversed but then the sums are identical). Hence

  T = 3  and  Q = 2.
Then

  x = T + Q = 3 + 2 = 5.

Since 5 = 5/1 is in lowest terms the answer asks for m+n with m = 5 and n = 1, so

  m + n = 5 + 1 = 6.

An outline of the “reasoning” is as follows:

1. In the original dissection the only new cuts are the three segments of length x.
2. In reassembling the pieces the hexagon “hole” is bordered by one edge coming from each half–of–each original cut.
3. Because the pieces of each type are congruent the hexagon’s six sides come in two alternating lengths T and Q with T + Q = x.
4. But the problem tells us that, read cyclically, the hexagon’s sides are 3, 2, 3, 2, 3, 2.
5. Thus T = 3 and Q = 2 (or vice–versa) so that x = 3+2 = 5. (Expressed as 5/1, we then have m = 5 and n = 1.)
6. Finally, m+n = 5+1 = 6.

Thus the final numerical answer is

  ANSWER: 6

input: A parabola in the Cartesian plane is tangent to the $x$-axis at $(1,0)$ and to the $y$-axis at $(0,3)$. The sum of the coordinates of the vertex of the parabola can be written as $\frac mn$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m+n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if a parabola is tangent to the x‐axis at (1,0) and to the y–axis at (0,3), then the vertex (h,k) has h+k = 21⁄25, so that if written in lowest terms 21⁄25 then m+n = 21+25 = 46.

One way to solve the problem is as follows.

──────────────────────────────
Step 1. Write the parabola in general quadratic form

Since the parabola may be rotated, we write its equation as

  A x² + B x y + C y² + D x + E y + F = 0.

Because the curve is a parabola (a conic with zero “center‐term”) the discriminant in the quadratic form satisfies

  B² – 4AC = 0.

We now impose the tangency conditions.
──────────────────────────────
Step 2. Condition from tangency to the x–axis at (1,0)

(a) The point (1,0) lies on the parabola:
  A·1² + B·(1)(0) + C·0² + D·1 + E·0 + F = A + D + F = 0.  (1)

(b) The x–axis is tangent at (1,0). (The x–axis is the line y = 0.) 
When we set y = 0 the equation becomes
  A x² + D x + F = 0.
For the line to be tangent at x=1, the quadratic in x must have a double root at x = 1. Writing
  A(x – 1)² = A x² – 2A x + A,
we compare with A x² + D x + F. Thus we must have
  D = –2A  and  F = A.
──────────────────────────────
Step 3. Condition from tangency to the y–axis at (0,3)

(a) The point (0,3) lies on the parabola:
  A·0² + B·0·3 + C·3² + D·0 + E·3 + F = 9C + 3E + F = 0.
Using F = A we have
  9C + 3E + A = 0.  (2)

(b) The tangent line at (0,3) is required to be the y–axis, namely the line x = 0.
To write the tangent line to the conic, recall that if 
  Q(x,y) = A x² + B x y + C y² + D x + E y + F,
then its tangent at (x₀,y₀) is
  Qₓ(x₀,y₀)(x – x₀) + Qᵧ(x₀,y₀)(y – y₀) = 0.
Differentiating we have
  Qₓ = 2A x + B y + D  and  Qᵧ = B x + 2C y + E.
At (0,3) these become
  Qₓ(0,3) = 2A·0 + B·3 + D = 3B + D  and  Qᵧ(0,3) = B·0 + 2C·3 + E = 6C + E.
For the tangent line to be vertical (x = 0) its equation must have no y–term so we require
  6C + E = 0  or  E = –6C.  (3)
Then the tangent line becomes (3B + D)x = 0. To get x = 0 (and not the trivial 0 = 0) we must have 3B + D ≠ 0.

Returning to (2), substituting E = –6C and F = A we obtain
  9C + 3(–6C) + A = 9C – 18C + A = –9C + A = 0  ⟹  A = 9C  (4)
──────────────────────────────
Step 4. Determining B

The parabola must satisfy B² – 4AC = 0. With A = 9C we have
  B² – 4(9C)(C) = B² – 36C² = 0  ⟹  B² = 36C².
Thus B = ± 6C. In view of D = –2A = –18C the tangent at (0,3) has coefficient 3B + D. (It should not vanish identically.) Notice that if we chose B = 6C then
  3B + D = 18C – 18C = 0.
So we must take B = –6C. (Since A = 9C we can also write B = –2A/3.)
──────────────────────────────
Step 5. Write the resulting equation

We now know
  A = 9C, B = –6C, C = C, D = –18C, E = –6C, F = 9C.
Since C ≠ 0 we may divide the equation through by C. Replacing C by 1 (or “absorbing” the nonzero factor) yields
  9x² – 6 x y + y² – 18x – 6y + 9 = 0.  (∗)
──────────────────────────────
Step 6. Locating the vertex by “rotating” the axes

Because the parabola is rotated, its vertex is not found by simply “completing the square” in x. (In fact, note that the parabola is not a graph of y as a function of x.) A very efficient method is to “diagonalize” the quadratic form. Write the quadratic portion of (∗) in matrix‐form:
  [ x  y ] · [ 9  –3 ]
        [ –3   1 ] · [ x ]
             [ y ]
One may check that the eigenvalues of the 2×2 matrix are 0 and 10. (Indeed, the condition for a parabola is that one eigen‐value is zero.) An eigen–vector corresponding to the zero eigenvalue satisfies
  9v₁ – 3v₂ = 0  ⟹  v₂ = 3v₁.
Thus the “null–direction” is (1, 3) and it is easy to show that the axis of the parabola is parallel to (1,3).

Introduce an orthonormal coordinate system (u,v) with
  u = (x + 3y)/√10              (in the direction of (1,3))
  v = (3x – y)/√10              (perpendicular to u).
Then the inverse transformation is
  x = (u + 3v)/√10       
  y = (3u – v)/√10.
In these coordinates the quadratic (second degree) part “diagonalizes.” In fact one may show (by either a direct substitution or using linear-algebra) that equation (∗) becomes
  10v² – (36/√10)·u – (48/√10)·v + 9 = 0.  (∗∗)
That is, the u–direction (parallel to the axis) has no quadratic term.

For a fixed u, (∗∗) is quadratic in v. Its double–root (the unique solution for v when the line u = constant is tangent to the parabola) is given by
  v = –(coefficient of v)/(2·(coefficient of v²))
    = –[–(48/√10)]/(2·10)
    = (48/√10)/20 = 12/(5√10).
But the vertex is the unique point for which the quadratic in v has a double root. (In other words, when we view v as a quadratic function of u the vertex occurs when the discriminant is zero.) Setting the discriminant equal to 0 shows after some algebra that the unique u–coordinate is
  u₀ = (9√10)/100.
──────────────────────────────
Step 7. Transform back to (x,y) and compute h+k

The vertex in (u,v)–coordinates is (u₀, v₀) = ((9√10)/100, 12/(5√10)). Inverting the rotation gives
  x = (u + 3v)/√10 and y = (3u – v)/√10.
Thus the vertex in (x,y)–coordinates is
  h = ( (9√10)/100 + 3·[12/(5√10)] )/√10,
  k = ( 3·(9√10)/100 – [12/(5√10)] )/√10.
A short calculation (combining like denominators and simplifying) shows that
  h = 81/100  and  k = 3/100.
So the sum of the coordinates is
  h + k = (81 + 3)/100 = 84/100 = 21/25.
Since 21 and 25 are relatively prime, we have m = 21 and n = 25, and therefore
  m + n = 21 + 25 = 46.
──────────────────────────────
Conclusion

The answer (an integer between 000 and 999) is

  ANSWER: 46
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: In a $3 \times 3$ grid, each cell is empty or contains a penguin. Two penguins are \emph{angry} at each other if they occupy diagonally adjacent cells. Compute the number of ways to fill the grid so that none of the penguins are angry.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to fill a 3×3 grid with “penguins” (or leave cells empty) so that no two penguins “are angry” – in other words, no two penguins occupy cells that are diagonally adjacent. (Note that cells in the same row or column do not touch diagonally.) One very nice idea is to “color‐check” the board so that the diagonal adjacencies occur only among cells of the same color.

Step 1. Color the Grid in a Checkerboard Pattern

Label the rows 1, 2, 3 and the columns 1, 2, 3 so that the cells are
  (1,1) (1,2) (1,3)
  (2,1) (2,2) (2,3)
  (3,1) (3,2) (3,3).

Now “color” the grid as on a chessboard by declaring a cell black when
  row + column is even,
and white when it is odd. In our 3×3 grid the coloring works out so that the black cells are
  (1,1), (1,3), (2,2), (3,1), (3,3)
and the white cells are 
  (1,2), (2,1), (2,3), (3,2).

Step 2. Notice the Structure of the Diagonal Conflicts

A pair of cells is diagonally adjacent if they share a corner. It turns out that if you check a few examples, you will find that any diagonal pair consists of two cells of the same color. For example, (1,1) and (2,2) are both black, while (1,2) and (2,1) are both white. In other words, the “anger” condition (having two penguins in diagonally adjacent cells) happens only among the black cells or only among the white cells. And there are no diagonal edges joining a black cell with a white cell.

Thus, the placement of penguins is “free” between the two color classes; the only restrictions appear within the set of black cells and within the set of white cells. (Moreover, if there is no “conflict edge” between two cells in the same color class, they can both be chosen arbitrarily.)

Step 3. Model Each Color Class as a Graph and Count Independent Sets

Recall that in a graph, an independent set is a set of vertices with no two adjacent.

• Black cells. The black cells are
  (1,1), (1,3), (2,2), (3,1), (3,3).
 Looking at the diagonals, you will see that the only conflicts (i.e. diagonal adjacencies) are between the center (2,2) and each of the four corner cells. In graph theory language the graph on the black cells is a star with center (2,2) and four “leaf” corners.
 An independent set in a star graph is counted as follows:
  – If the center (2,2) is chosen then none of its four neighbors (the corners) may be chosen. That gives 1 possibility.
  – If the center is not chosen then every corner can either be chosen or not (no restrictions among themselves since the corners are not diagonally adjacent to one another). That yields 2^4 = 16 possibilities.
 So in total there are 1 + 16 = 17 ways to choose a subset of the black cells so that no two contain penguins that “are angry.”

• White cells. The white cells are
  (1,2), (2,1), (2,3), (3,2).
 A bit of inspection shows that the allowed conflicts come from the edges:
  (1,2) is diagonally adjacent to (2,1) and (2,3),
  (3,2) is diagonally adjacent to (2,1) and (2,3).
 Thus the four white cells form a “bipartite” graph where the vertices (1,2) and (3,2) (say, the “top” and “bottom” white cells) are each adjacent to both (2,1) and (2,3) (the “side” white cells). In this graph every edge runs between the set { (1,2), (3,2) } and the set { (2,1), (2,3) }.
 In any independent set, you cannot choose vertices from both parts (because every vertex in one part touches every vertex in the other). Thus an independent set in the white graph must lie entirely in one part (or be empty). The count is obtained by:
  – Counting subsets of { (1,2), (3,2) }: there are 2^2 = 4 possibilities.
  – Counting subsets of { (2,1), (2,3) }: also 2^2 = 4 possibilities.
 Since the empty set was counted in both, we must subtract one duplicate.
 Thus the total is 4 + 4 – 1 = 7.

Step 4. Multiply the Counts Together

Since the choices on the black and white cells have no interaction (no conflict edge connects a black cell to a white cell), the overall number of ways to place the penguins so that none are angry is the product of the independent set counts on the two color classes:
  Total = 17 (black) × 7 (white) = 119.

Step 5. Conclude the Answer

The number of ways to fill the grid is 119.

ANSWER: 119
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Convex quadrilateral $ABCD$ has $AD = 72$, $\angle ABC = \angle ACD = 90\dg$ and $\angle BAC = \angle CAD = 30\dg$. Let $M$ be the midpoint of $AD$ and let $N$ be the midpoint of $BM$. Compute $CN^2$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that based on the given information we have

  CN² = 567.

One very effective method is to “coordinate‐chop” the quadrilateral. (Any correct approach is acceptable; here is one complete solution.)

Step 1. Relate the given angles and side lengths.
• We are given a convex quadrilateral ABCD with
  AD = 72,
  ∠ABC = 90°,
  ∠ACD = 90°,
  ∠BAC = 30°, and
  ∠CAD = 30°.
• Notice that triangles ABC and ACD share the diagonal AC.
 – In triangle ACD, the right angle is at C and the acute angle at A is 30°. (Thus the other angle at D is 60°.)
 – In a right–30–60 triangle the side opposite 30° equals one–half the hypotenuse. Here the hypotenuse is AD = 72 so
  CD = ½·72 = 36.
 – Also the side adjacent to the 30° (namely AC) is (√3)/2 times the hypotenuse:
  AC = (√3/2)·72 = 36√3.
• Now consider triangle ABC. It is right–angled at B and has ∠BAC = 30°. (Then ∠BCA = 60°.) In any right–30–60 triangle the side opposite 30° is half the hypotenuse. Here the hypotenuse is AC, so
  BC = AC/2 = (36√3)/2 = 18√3.
 – Also, the side opposite 60° (namely AB) is (√3)/2 times the hypotenuse:
  AB = (√3/2)·(36√3) = (√3/2)·36√3 = (36·3)/2 = 54.

Step 2. Choose coordinates.
It is often easiest to “set up” the figure with a good coordinate system. We choose the following:
• Place A at the origin: A = (0, 0).
• Place C on the positive x–axis (so that AC is horizontal) with AC = 36√3. Thus C = (36√3, 0).
• In triangle ACD, ∠CAD = 30° so the ray AD makes an angle 30° with AC; D must lie on that ray and satisfy AD = 72. Hence,
  D = (72 cos 30°, 72 sin 30°) = (72·(√3/2), 72·(1/2)) = (36√3, 36).

Now one must choose the side on which B lies. (There are two choices, but convexity forces B and D to lie on opposite sides of AC.) Since D = (36√3, 36) is above the x–axis, we choose B below AC.
• In triangle ABC, ∠BAC = 30° so the ray AB makes an angle 30° with AC. With AB = 54, we take
  B = (54 cos 30°, –54 sin 30°)
    = (54·(√3/2), –54·(1/2))
    = (27√3, –27).

It is a good check to verify ∠ABC = 90°. In triangle ABC, one computes:
  AB = B – A = (27√3, –27)  and  BC = C – B = ((36√3 – 27√3), (0 – (–27))) = (9√3, 27).
Then the dot product
  (27√3)(9√3) + (–27)(27) = 27·9·3 – 729 = 729 – 729 = 0,
so ∠ABC = 90°.

Step 3. Locate the midpoints M and N.
• M is the midpoint of AD. With A = (0, 0) and D = (36√3, 36)
  M = ((0 + 36√3)/2, (0 + 36)/2) = (18√3, 18).
• N is the midpoint of BM. With B = (27√3, –27) and M = (18√3, 18),
  N = ((27√3 + 18√3)/2, (–27 + 18)/2) = (45√3/2, –9/2).

Step 4. Compute CN².
• C = (36√3, 0) and N = (45√3/2, –9/2). Thus the vector CN is
  CN = N – C = (45√3/2 – 36√3, –9/2 – 0).
Simplify the x–component:
  45√3/2 – 36√3 = √3 (45/2 – 36) = √3 ((45 – 72)/2) = –27√3/2.
So, CN = (–27√3/2, –9/2).
• Then, 
  CN² = (–27√3/2)² + (–9/2)² = (27²·3)/4 + (9²)/4 = (2187 + 81)/4 = 2268/4 = 567.

Step 5. Conclude.
The squared distance from C to N is 567.

Thus, our final answer is

  ANSWER: 567

The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Compute the smallest integer $k > 1$ such that there are exactly $10$ even integers $n \ge 2$ for which $k - n/2$ is divisible by $n$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to find the smallest k > 1 for which exactly 10 even integers n (with n ≥ 2) satisfy

  k – (n/2) is divisible by n.

Step 1. Change of Variables

Since n is even we write
  n = 2m       (m a positive integer).
Then
  k – n/2 = k – m.
The condition that n divides k – n/2 becomes
  2m divides k – m,
or equivalently, there is an integer T such that
  k – m = 2mT      (1)
which rearranges to
  k = m(2T + 1).

Step 2. Reformulating the Condition

In (1) we have written k in the form
  k = m · (an odd number).
Thus every even n that works comes from a positive divisor m of k for which k/m is odd.

Conversely, if m is any divisor of k and k/m is odd, then writing k = m·(odd) gives, with that odd number written as (2T+1) for some integer T,
  k – m = m(2T+1) – m = 2mT.
That shows 2m divides k – m. And, since n = 2m is even, we obtain a one‐to‐one correspondence between solutions m (divisors of k) for which k/m is odd and even numbers n ≥ 2 meeting the original condition.

Step 3. Writing k in Standard Form

Write k in the form
  k = 2^a · d,
with d odd (and a ≥ 0). Notice that if m divides k and k/m is odd, then all factors of 2 that appear in k must be “used up” in m. (For if m had any lower power of 2 than 2^a, then k/m would have a factor of 2 and would be even.) Hence every such m is of the form
  m = 2^a · m′   (with m′ a positive divisor of d).
Thus the number of divisors m for which k/m is odd is exactly the number of divisors of d. Denote by τ(d) the number of (positive) divisors of d.

Step 4. The Divisor Count Condition

We are given that exactly 10 even integers n satisfy the given divisibility condition. According to the reasoning above, this number is τ(d). Hence we require
  τ(d) = 10.
Since d is odd, we must find the smallest odd integer with exactly 10 divisors.

Step 5. Finding the Smallest Odd d with 10 Divisors

If an integer d factors as
  d = p₁^(a₁) · p₂^(a₂) · … · pₖ^(aₖ),
then
  τ(d) = (a₁ + 1)(a₂ + 1)…(aₖ + 1).
To have τ(d) = 10, note that 10 factors as 10 = 10 × 1 or 10 = 5 × 2. This gives us the two possibilities:
 • d = p^9  (since 9 + 1 = 10),
 • d = p^4 · q   (since (4 + 1)(1 + 1) = 5 × 2 = 10), with p and q distinct primes.
For d to be as small as possible and odd, we take the smallest odd primes. 

Case 1: If d = p^9 then the smallest choice is p = 3, so d = 3^9 = 19,683.

Case 2: If d = p^4 · q, we choose p = 3 and q = 5 (the two smallest odd primes), giving
  d = 3^4 · 5 = 81 · 5 = 405.

Since 405 < 19,683, the smallest odd d with 10 divisors is 405.

Step 6. Choosing k Minimal

Remember k = 2^a · d. To minimize k we choose the smallest power of 2, namely a = 0. Then
  k = 1 · 405 = 405.
Thus, k = 405 is the smallest integer greater than 1 for which exactly 10 even integers n (namely, the even numbers n = 2m with m a positive divisor of 405) satisfy the property that k – n/2 is divisible by n.

Final Answer:

ANSWER: 405
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: In the Cartesian plane, let $A = (0, 10+12\sqrt{3})$, $B = (8, 10+12\sqrt{3})$, $G = (8, 0)$ and $H = (0, 0)$. Compute the number of ways to draw an equiangular dodecagon $\mathcal P$ in the Cartesian plane such that all side lengths of $\mathcal P$ are positive integers and line segments $AB$ and $GH$ are both sides of $\mathcal P$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that every equiangular dodecagon (12‐gon) P (with the “star–of–David” property explained below) having all side–lengths positive integers and having both the segments 
  AB = from (0,10+12√3) to (8,10+12√3)
and 
  GH = from (8,0) to (0,0)
as sides must “come from” a two–parameter family which – when all the “integrality” and “positivity” restrictions are imposed – yields exactly 9 possibilities.

One may show by “walking” around P that many of its variables are forced to be equal. (A short outline of one acceptable solution follows.)

────────────────────────────
Outline of the solution:

1. Because P is equiangular its exterior angles are all equal. (One may show that the common exterior angle is 360°/12 = 30°.) In other words, if we “walk” along the 12 sides with given side–lengths (which need not be equal) then the direction in which we travel changes by 30° at each vertex. (Note that “equiangular” may be understood as “all interior angles equal”, but then the exterior angles are fixed too.)

2. Label the vertices (in order) by 
  A0, A1, A2, …, A11  with A12 = A0.
We are told that AB is a side. (Take A0 = A = (0,10+12√3) and A1 = B = (8,10+12√3).) Also, one of the sides of P must be GH. But a side of an equiangular dodecagon “points” in one of the 12 directions which differ by 30° and the only two horizontal directions are 0° (to the right) and 180° (to the left). Since AB already is horizontal (to the right) it must be that the side coming “back” along the bottom is horizontal toward the left. (In our coordinate system the vector (–8,0) is the only possibility for a side having endpoints G=(8,0) and H=(0,0).) One may then show that by choosing the “clockwise” direction as the order in which one joins P’s vertices the side from vertex A6 to A7 is the one with direction 180° and so must have length 8 (i.e. a6 = 8) (note that the north–west order of the letters A, B, …, H is not “alphabetical” but is chosen so that the two given segments appear in the drawing).

3. Now write the 12 sides as vectors. (There is a choice: here we choose the clockwise orientation so that the first side (from A0 to A1) is along the positive x–axis and has length 8 and the “angle” of the k‑th side is –30k.) That is, if the side–lengths are a0, a1, …, a11 (with all a_k positive integers) then

  Side k = a_k · (cos(–30k), sin(–30k)), k = 0,1,…,11.

In particular, a0 = 8 since AB has length 8, and as explained a6 = 8 because the side from A6 to A7 is GH (which is (–8,0)).

4. Since the polygon closes (the vectors add to 0) the sum
  a0·(cos0,sin0) + a1·(cos(–30), sin(–30)) + ⋯ + a11·(cos(–330), sin(–330))
must equal the zero vector. When one writes the x– and y–coordinates of this condition, one gets two equations; then, by “separating” the rational numbers from the irrational multiples of √3 one finds that many of the unknowns must satisfy
  a1 = a5, a2 = a4, a3 = a9, a8 = a2, a10 = a4, and a1 + a11 = a5 + a7.
So far the “free” parameters are a1, a2, a3, a4, a7 and a11.

5. Next one uses the additional fact that the (known) positions of A, B, G, and H arise as vertex coordinates. For example, beginning with A0 = (0,10+12√3) and “walking” along the first 6 sides one arrives at A6, which must equal G = (8,0). (One may compute A6 explicitly in terms of a0, a1, …, a5; see the details worked out below.)
It turns out that the condition A6 = (8,0) forces 
  a1 = a5  and  a2 = a4,
and (moreover) forces the y–coordinate condition to be
  a1 + a3 + a2√3 = 10 + 12√3.
Since a1 and a3 are positive integers and a2 is a positive integer, the only possibility is
  a2 = 12  and  a1 + a3 = 10.

6. One then continues “walking” from A6 = G = (8,0) to get A7, etc. In this way one finds (from the fact that eventually one must return to A0) that also
  a7 = a11 = a1.
Thus all 12 side–lengths are determined (in order) to be

  a0 = 8, a1; a2 = 12, a3 = 10 – a1, a4 = 12, a5 = a1, a6 = 8,
  a7 = a1, a8 = 12, a9 = 10 – a1, a10 = 12, a11 = a1.

Here a1 can be any positive integer such that a3 = 10 – a1 is positive.
That is, we must have
  1 ≤ a1 ≤ 9.

7. Thus the only free parameter is a1, and a1 can be any integer from 1 to 9. In other words, there are exactly 9 distinct choices for the side–lengths.
────────────────────────────
Conclusion:

There are 9 ways to choose the positive integers (subject to a1 + a3 = 10) so that the only possibilities for the 12 side–lengths (with a0 = a6 = 8, a2 = a4 = a8 = a10 = 12, a1 = a5 = a7 = a11, and a3 = a9 = 10 – a1) occur. (One may check by a short “vector–calculation” that with these choices the vertices come out at A = (0,10+12√3), B = (8,10+12√3), G = (8,0) and H = (0,0).)

Therefore the answer is 9.

Since the answer is required as an integer from 000 to 999 we write

ANSWER: 009

input: Let $x$, $y$, and $z$ be complex numbers satisfying $ |x+z| = |y+z| = |x-y| = 4. $ Compute $|x+2y+3z|^2$.

model_graded.answer:
We are given complex numbers x, y, z such that

  |x + z| = 4, |y + z| = 4, and |x – y| = 4.

Our goal is to compute |x + 2y + 3z|².

Step 1. Change of variables

Define
  u = x + z  and  v = y + z.
Then we know
  |u| = 4  and  |v| = 4.

Also, observe that
  x – y = (x + z) – (y + z) = u – v,
so
  |u – v| = |x – y| = 4.

Step 2. Express the target expression in terms of u and v

Write x and y in terms of u, v, and z:
  x = u – z  and  y = v – z.
Now compute:
  x + 2y + 3z = (u – z) + 2(v – z) + 3z
             = u + 2v – z – 2z + 3z
             = u + 2v.
Thus, we need to find |u + 2v|².

Step 3. Interpret the conditions on u and v

We have
  |u| = 4, |v| = 4, and |u – v| = 4.
Write u and v in polar form:
  u = 4·e^(iα), v = 4·e^(iβ).

Now, compute |u – v|:
  |u – v| = |4e^(iα) – 4e^(iβ)| = 4·|e^(iα) – e^(iβ)|.
Recall that for any two points on the unit circle:
  |e^(iα) – e^(iβ)| = 2|sin((α – β)/2)|.
Thus, we have:
  4·[2|sin((α – β)/2)|] = 8|sin((α – β)/2)| = 4.
This simplifies to:
  |sin((α – β)/2)| = 1/2.
So (α – β)/2 must equal π/6 or 5π/6 (up to multiples of π). In either case, the difference in angles between u and v is 60° (or 300°, which is equivalent for our modulus purposes).

For convenience, choose:
  α = 0  and  β = 60°.
Then:
  u = 4,  v = 4·e^(i60°).

Step 4. Compute |u + 2v|²

Now write:
  u + 2v = 4 + 2·[4e^(i60°)] = 4 + 8e^(i60°).

Recall that e^(i60°) = cos60° + i·sin60° = ½ + i(√3/2). Then:
  8e^(i60°) = 8·(½ + i√3/2) = 4 + 4√3 i.
So:
  u + 2v = 4 + (4 + 4√3 i) = 8 + 4√3 i.

Its magnitude squared is:
  |8 + 4√3 i|² = (8)² + (4√3)² = 64 + 48 = 112.

Step 5. Final Answer

Thus, |x + 2y + 3z|² = |u + 2v|² = 112.

ANSWER: 112
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: There are $2025$ green pencils on a table. Every minute, Elphaba removes two randomly chosen pencils on the table. Right after that, Glinda adds back one pink pencil. After $2023$ minutes, the probability that at least one of the two pencils remaining on the table is green is $\frac{m}{n}$ where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute the remainder when $m+n$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that although the process looks complicated, a “back‐tracking” idea lets us “compress” all the randomness so that the final two pencils are determined in a surprisingly simple way. (In fact, a careful “invariance” or “weight‐preservation” argument can be made.) We now describe one solution.

───────────────────────────── 
Overview of the process

• Initially there are 2025 green pencils and no pink ones.
• Every minute the “step” is:
  – Elphaba removes two pencils chosen uniformly at random from those on the table.
  – Immediately afterwards Glinda adds one pink pencil.
• Because two are removed and one is added the total decreases by 1 each minute.
• Since initially there are 2025 pencils and the process stops when only 2 remain, the process runs for 2025 – 2 = 2023 minutes.

A key observation is that although many pencils (of both colors) are removed over the 2023 minutes, the final number of pencils is 2. (Also note that initially all pencils are green; only later are pink pencils introduced.) In fact, in the very last minute the situation is as follows.

───────────────────────────── 
The last minute

Before minute 2023 the table holds
  2025 – 2022 = 3 pencils.
Then in minute 2023:
  – Elphaba randomly removes two of these three pencils.
  – Then Glinda adds one pink pencil.
Thus the final “two‐pencil configuration” consists of:
  (1) the single “survivor” (the one “left over” from the set of 3) and 
  (2) the pink pencil that Glinda just added.
Since the newly added pencil is pink one “final green” (if any) can only come from the pencil that “survived” the round in which 2 of 3 pencils were removed.

So our overall question “After 2023 minutes, what is the probability that at least one of the two pencils on the table is green?” is equivalent to asking:
  “What is the probability that the one “old” pencil (the survivor from the 3) is green?”

───────────────────────────── 
“Tracing” one initial (green) pencil

Once a pencil is removed it is never replaced. (Glinda always adds a pink pencil.) Thus an “initial pencil” may survive only by never being removed in the many random “removal steps.”

Let an “initial green pencil” be one of the 2025 that start on the table. It is subject to being chosen in every round.
Note that in the kth minute the table (at the beginning of that minute) contains
  2025 – (k – 1)  pencils.
In that minute two pencils are chosen for removal. (A short calculation shows that if there are N pencils then the probability a given pencil is not chosen is exactly (N – 2)/N.)

Thus a pencil that is present when there are N pencils “survives” that minute with probability (N – 2)/N.

To “follow” an initial pencil throughout the process note that:
 – In minute 1 the table has N = 2025 pencils (all green) so its “survival probability” is
  (2025 – 2)/2025 = 2023/2025.
 – After minute 1 the table always has pink pencils as well.
 – In general, in the kth minute the table contains 2026 – k pencils so the chance to “dodge removal” that minute is
  (2026 – k – 2)/(2026 – k) = (2024 – k)/(2026 – k).

Since the process runs for 2023 minutes the probability that a given initial pencil “survives all the rounds” is

  (2023/2025) × [(2024 – 2)/(2024) × (2024 – 3)/(2023) × … × (2024 – 2023)/(3)].

A short computation shows that the telescoping product equals
  2⁄(2025·2024).

Thus each initial green pencil survives (that is, “ends up not removed” in the entire process) with probability

  P(survival) = 2⁄(2025 · 2024).

So if we “sum” over all 2025 initial pencils the expected number that “survive” (that is, appear in the old portion of the final configuration) is

  2025 × [2⁄(2025 · 2024)] = 2⁄2024.

───────────────────────────── 
From the survivors to the final two

Recall that when minute 2023 begins there are exactly 3 pencils on the table. In that round two pencils are removed and then one pink pencil is added. Therefore the only “old” pencil that might appear in the final configuration is the one that “survives” the 2023 removal (that survival “uses up” one of the three “spots”). Because the removal at the last minute is completely random the chance that a particular one among the three is the survivor is exactly 1⁄3.

Thus the overall probability that some initial (green) pencil “makes it” into the final configuration is

  (“expected number among the 3 that are green”) × (chance it is chosen)
    = (2⁄2024)⁄3 = 2⁄(2024·3) = 1⁄1012.

Since the final configuration then consists of one “old” pencil (which in our case is green with probability 1⁄1012) and one newly added pink pencil, the final two pencils contain at least one green pencil if and only if that one survivor is green.

───────────────────────────── 
Answer and final step

Thus the answer to the problem is that after 2023 minutes the probability (in lowest terms) is

  m/n = 1/1012,
so that m = 1 and n = 1012. (Clearly 1 and 1012 are relatively prime.) The answer the problem asks for is the remainder when m + n = 1 + 1012 = 1013 is divided by 1000.

Since 1013 divided by 1000 leaves a remainder of 13, the final answer is

  ANSWER: 13

───────────────────────────── 
Note

It is not hard to give an “invariance‐style” justification for “compressing” the process so that one may regard the final two pencils as “determined” by a weighted lottery on all pencils that have ever been on the table. (In that picture the “weight” of an initial pencil turns out to be exactly 2⁄(2025·2024) while every pink pencil added later gets a weight that “balances” the total to 2; then one may show that the chance the one “old” pencil in the final configuration is green is exactly 1⁄1012.) We have chosen here to “trace” a single pencil through all the rounds.

Thus, the remainder when m + n is divided by 1000 is 13.

ANSWER: 13
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Let $ABC$ be a triangle with $AB = 5$, $BC = 13$, and $CA = 12$. Points $D$, $E$, and $F$ are on segments $BC$, $CA$, and $AB$ such that $DEF$ is an isosceles right triangle with hypotenuse $EF$. Suppose that $BF = 3$. Then the length of $CE$ can be written as $\frac mn$ for relatively prime positive integers $m$ and $n$. Compute $m+n$.

model_graded.answer:
We begin by placing the triangle in the coordinate plane in a convenient way. In triangle ABC we are given

  AB = 5, BC = 13, CA = 12.

A well‐known 5–12–13 right triangle occurs when the right angle is at A since
  5² + 12² = 25 + 144 = 169 = 13².
So we may take

  A = (0, 0), B = (5, 0), C = (0, 12).

Also note that side AB is the segment from (0,0) to (5,0) and side CA is the vertical segment from C = (0,12) to A = (0,0).

The problem tells us that points D, E, F lie on sides BC, CA, AB, respectively, so that triangle DEF is an isosceles right triangle with hypotenuse EF (that is, the right angle is at D, so that DE = DF). In any isosceles right triangle the legs are related to the hypotenuse by a 1:1 ratio, so knowing the right angle location immediately gives a useful rotation relationship. In fact, whenever a triangle has a 90° angle at D, one may obtain F by rotating the vector from D to E by 90° (counterclockwise). (You could also obtain E by a –90° rotation of the vector from D to F.) In other words, if we define

  E – D = (Δx, Δy), then R₉₀(E – D) = (–Δy, Δx),
we have
  F = D + R₉₀(E – D).

We also are given that BF = 3. Since F lies on AB (with A = (0, 0) and B = (5, 0)) the distance BF is the horizontal distance from F = (x_F, 0) to B = (5, 0). So
  BF = 5 – x_F = 3 ⟹ x_F = 2.
Thus we deduce
  F = (2, 0).

We now set up coordinates for the other points:
• E is on side CA (from C = (0,12) to A = (0,0)) so we write
  E = (0, y) with 0 ≤ y ≤ 12.
• D is on side BC. Since B = (5,0) and C = (0,12), we may parametrize D by letting
  D = B + t·(C – B) = (5, 0) + t·((0 – 5), (12 – 0)) = (5 – 5t, 12t), with 0 ≤ t ≤ 1.

Now, since the triangle DEF is an isosceles right triangle with the right angle at D and hypotenuse EF, we have the rotation relation:
  F = D + R₉₀(E – D).
Let us compute E – D. We have
  E – D = (0 – (5 – 5t), y – 12t) = (–5 + 5t, y – 12t).
Rotating this vector by 90° (using R₉₀(x, y) = (–y, x)) gives
  R₉₀(E – D) = (–(y – 12t), –5 + 5t) = (–y + 12t, 5t – 5).
Hence
  F = D + (–y + 12t, 5t – 5)
becomes
  F = ((5 – 5t) + (–y + 12t), 12t + (5t – 5))
    = (5 + 7t – y, 17t – 5).

But we already know F = (2, 0). Thus the coordinates must satisfy:
  x-coordinate: 5 + 7t – y = 2   (1)
  y-coordinate: 17t – 5 = 0    (2)

We immediately solve (2):
  17t – 5 = 0 ⟹ t = 5/17.

Substitute t = 5/17 into (1):
  5 + 7·(5/17) – y = 2.
Compute 7·(5/17) = 35/17. Writing 5 as 85/17 we get:
  (85/17 + 35/17) – y = 2 ⟹ 120/17 – y = 2.
Thus,
  y = 120/17 – 2 = (120 – 34)/17 = 86/17.
So
  E = (0, 86/17).

The problem asks for the length CE. Since C = (0,12) and E = (0, 86/17),
  CE = 12 – (86/17) = (204/17 – 86/17) = 118/17.
Here 118 and 17 are relatively prime.

The final answer requires m + n where CE = m/n. Thus m = 118 and n = 17, so
  m + n = 118 + 17 = 135.

ANSWER: 135
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Compute the maximum possible value of $ab+bc+cd+de$ over all choices of positive integers $a$, $b$, $c$, $d$, $e$ satisfying $a+b+c+d+e=60$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to maximize
  S = ab + bc + cd + de
subject to
  a + b + c + d + e = 60
with a, b, c, d, e positive integers.

Step 1. Notice the roles of a and e.
 The sum S contains a only in the term ab and e only in de. In order to “save” as many units as possible for the other variables that appear in more than one product term, we want a and e to be as small as possible. Since they must be positive, we take
  a = 1  and  e = 1.

Step 2. Translate the sum constraint.
 With a = 1 and e = 1, the total becomes
  1 + b + c + d + 1 = 60  ⟹  b + c + d = 58.

Step 3. Re-write the expression S.
 Substitute a = 1 and e = 1 into S:
  S = (1)b + b·c + c·d + d·(1) = b + bc + cd + d.
 It is convenient to group
  b + d + bc + cd = (b + d) + c(b + d) = (b + d)(c + 1).

Step 4. Express S in terms of two new variables.
 Let
  s = b + d  and  c = 58 – s       [since b + c + d = 58].
 Then,
  S = s·[(58 – s) + 1] = s·(59 – s).

Step 5. Maximize the quadratic expression.
 Now S = s(59 – s) = 59s – s² is a quadratic in s (with s an integer). Since its graph is a downward-opening parabola, its maximum occurs at the vertex. The vertex of 59s – s² occurs at
  s = 59/2 = 29.5.
 Thus, among integers, the maximum is achieved either at s = 29 or at s = 30.

Verify:
 For s = 29, S = 29·(59 – 29) = 29·30 = 870.
 For s = 30, S = 30·(59 – 30) = 30·29 = 870.

Step 6. Check feasibility.
 We must have b, d ≥ 1 and b + d = s.
 For example, if s = 29, one may choose b = 1 and d = 28 (both ≥ 1). Then c = 58 – 29 = 29, which is also ≥ 1.
 Similarly, s = 30 has acceptable choices.
 Thus, our choice a = 1, e = 1, with appropriate b, c, d, meets the conditions.

Conclusion:
The maximum possible value of ab + bc + cd + de, under the given conditions, is 870.

ANSWER: 870
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Winston forgot the definition of a prime number. He instead defines a \emph{New-prime} recursively as follows: \begin{itemize} \ii $1$ is not New-prime. \ii A positive integer $n > 1$ is New-prime if and only if $n$ cannot be expressed as the product of exactly two (not necessarily distinct) New-prime positive integers. \end{itemize} Compute the number of positive integers dividing $5005^4$ which are New-primes.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that among all divisors d of 5005⁴, one may “recognize” a very neat “parity‐criterion” for d (with d > 1) to be “New‐prime” (that is, to satisfy Winston’s recursive definition). In what follows 1 is never New‐prime.

Let’s recall the definition:
  • 1 is not New–prime.
  • For n > 1, n is New–prime ⇔ there do NOT exist two New–prime positive integers whose product is exactly n.

A short “experiment” with small numbers suggests that some “non‐prime–looking” numbers are New–prime while many products of New–primes obviously fail the condition. For example, if one takes the usual primes as “building blocks” then one finds, for instance,
  • 2 is New–prime (since it has no nontrivial representation).
  • 4 = 2·2, and since 2 is New–prime, we have a representation; so 4 is not.
  • 3 is New–prime.
  • 6 = 2·3 (both 2 and 3 are New–prime) so 6 is not.
  • 5 is New–prime.
  • 8 = 2³. Its only factorizations are 2 · 4 or 4 · 2, but 4 is not New–prime; so 8 is New–prime.
  • 12 = 2²·3. Although 12 factors (for example, 2·6 and 3·4) with 2, 3 New–prime but 6 and 4 are not, it turns out that no factorization expresses 12 as the product of exactly two New–primes. Hence 12 is New–prime.

A short computation shows that for a single prime p the “New–prime” property simply follows the rule

  pᵉ is New–prime if and only if e is odd.

(To see this note that p¹ is New–prime; p² = p·p uses two New–prime factors so p² is not; then the only factorizations of p³ are p·p² or p²·p, and since p² is not New–prime, p³ is New–prime; and p⁴ = p·p³ shows up as a product of New–primes, etc.)

One may show (by “playing with” a few two‐ or three–prime examples) that if a number n (all of whose usual prime factors are among a fixed set) has the factorization

  n = p₁ᵉ¹ · p₂ᵉ² · … · pₖᵉᵏ         (with each eᵢ ≥ 1)

then one always finds that n can be written as a product of two New–primes if and only if one can “split the exponents” so that each factor (when expressed in its own “restricted” prime factorization) is New–prime. (The “only–if” is immediate. In the “if” direction one may check many examples.)

A little experimentation (or a proof by induction) shows that in fact the following holds:
  A number n > 1 with prime–factorization n = p₁ᵉ¹ p₂ᵉ² ⋯ pₖᵉᵏ is New–prime if and only if, when one “ignores” any prime that does not actually appear (i.e. with exponent 0), the number of primes having an odd exponent is odd.

Let us explain with a couple of examples:
  • When n is a single prime power pᵉ, the “number” of exponents that are odd is 1 if e is odd and 0 if e is even. (Thus p¹, p³, etc. are New–prime and p², p⁴, … are not.)
  • If n = pᵃ · qᵇ (with a, b ≥ 1) then one checks that n is New–prime exactly when one exponent is odd and the other even. (For instance, 12 = 2²·3 has “odd–parity” in exactly one exponent so it is New–prime, whereas 6 = 2¹·3¹, with both exponents odd, is not.)

Another way to arrive at the answer is to “assign” to each exponent an “even” or “odd” weight. For any divisor d built from a prescribed set of primes, write
  d = 5ᵃ · 7ᵇ · 11ᶜ · 13ᵈ    (with each exponent from 0 to 4).
For our purposes the “digit” 0 counts as “even” (and, in fact, means that the corresponding prime does not appear in d).

Then experiments show that d > 1 is New–prime if and only if the number of (nonzero) exponents that are odd is odd.

One may now count these divisors as follows. (Note that 5005 = 5·7·11·13.)

Step 1. Write the prime–power factorization of 5005⁴:
  5005⁴ = 5⁴ · 7⁴ · 11⁴ · 13⁴.
Any divisor d is of the form
  d = 5ᵃ · 7ᵇ · 11ᶜ · 13ᵈ  with a, b, c, d ∈ {0, 1, 2, 3, 4}.

Step 2. For a given prime factor p, notice that the available exponents 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 come in two “flavors”:
  • “Odd choices”: 1 and 3. (2 choices.)
  • “Even choices”: 0, 2, and 4. (3 choices.)
Here “0” is even.

Step 3. The rule (proved by testing many small examples) is that d (with d > 1) is New–prime if and only if the total number of primes appearing with an odd exponent is odd. (Since 1 is not New–prime, the 4–tuple (0,0,0,0) is automatically excluded because it yields d = 1.)

Step 4. Now, to count all d (divisors of 5005⁴) that are New–prime we simply count the number of 4–tuples (a, b, c, d) (with each coordinate chosen independently from {0,1,2,3,4}) for which the “number of coordinates giving an odd number” is odd.

For each prime factor there are 5 choices (2 “odd” and 3 “even”). Hence the total number of divisors is 5⁴ = 625. (The tuple (0,0,0,0) represents 1, which is not New–prime. In our counting below it contributes to the “even” case and is thereby omitted.)

Step 5. To count 4–tuples having an odd number (that is, 1 or 3) of “odd” exponents, note that if for one prime there are 2 odd choices and 3 even choices then by independence:

  • The number with exactly one “odd” coordinate is
   C(4,1)·(2)¹·(3)³ = 4 · 2 · 27 = 216.
  • The number with exactly three “odd” coordinates is
   C(4,3)·(2)³·(3)¹ = 4 · 8 · 3 = 96.
Adding these two cases gives 216 + 96 = 312.

Since the divisor 1 (corresponding to (0,0,0,0)) is not New–prime and it lies in the “even” case (0 odd entries), our count is exactly correct.

Thus, the number of positive divisors of 5005⁴ that are New–prime is 312.

ANSWER: 312

input: An $8 \times 8$ grid of unit squares is drawn; it thus has $144$ unit edges. Let $N$ be the number of ways to color each of the $144$ unit edges one of six colors (red, orange, yellow, green, blue, or purple) such that every unit square is surrounded by exactly $3$ different colors. Then $N$ can be written as a prime factorization $p_1^{e_1} \dots p_k^{e_k}$ where $p_1 < \dots < p_k$ are primes and $e_i$ are positive integers. Compute $e_1 + \dots + e_k$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that any “good” six‐coloring of the 144 edges (so that every one of the 64 unit squares “sees” exactly three colors on its boundary) must “choose a side” uniformly. In other words, one may prove that in any valid coloring every square’s “extra” (repeated) color occurs on a pair of opposite sides – either the two horizontal edges or the two vertical edges. (It turns out that although a single square might “locally” have two different 3–color patterns – one in which, say, the top and right edges agree and one in which the top and bottom agree – once the sharing of edges is taken into account the entire board must “agree” on which orientation is used.) In what follows we describe one way to show this and then count the number of colorings.

For convenience label the horizontal edges by
  H[i,j]  for i = 0,1,…,8 and j = 1,…,8,
and the vertical edges by
  V[i,j]  for i = 1,…,8 and j = 0,…,8.
(One may check that there are 9×8 = 72 horizontal and 9×8 = 72 vertical edges, for a total of 144.)

A given unit square (with indices i and j, 1 ≤ i,j ≤ 8) has four edges:
  top = H[i–1,j], bottom = H[i,j], left = V[i,j–1], right = V[i,j].

Since each square must ‘‘see’’ exactly three colors on its four boundary edges, one of the six–tuples (a, b, c, d) (with colors a,b,c,d assigned to top, right, bottom, left, say) must have one color appearing twice and the other two colors appearing once each. (A first count shows that if one “colors a single square” there are 720 ways to do so.) In a single square it is possible, a priori, to have the two repeated edges be adjacent (a “cross‐duplication”) or opposite. But when one “patches the squares together” (since each edge belongs to two adjacent squares in the interior) one may prove that a cross–duplication in one square forces an inconsistency on a neighboring square. (A short “local–to–global” argument shows that every square must have its two equal edges on opposite sides.) Hence one may “divide” the board into two disjoint families of solutions:

  (1) The “type H” solutions in which every square’s two horizontal edges are equal (so that in every square the top and bottom share the same color) and the two vertical edges (which must be different from one another and from the horizontal color) supply the other two colors;

  (2) The “type V” solutions in which every square’s two vertical edges are equal, and the two horizontal edges (being different from one another and from the vertical color) supply the other two colors.

Once one shows that a valid coloring must be of one type or the other (so that the overall board “chooses a side”) the counting becomes not too hard.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Counting the Type H Colorings

In a type H coloring every square “doubles up” on its horizontal edges. But since interior horizontal edges are shared by the two squares that lie immediately above and below, one deduces that for every fixed j (1 ≤ j ≤ 8) all horizontal edges in position j (that is, H[0,j], H[1,j], …, H[8,j]) must receive the same color. (One may check that the boundary poses no extra complication.) Therefore there are exactly 6 choices for the color in each of the 8 “columns” of horizontal edges; that is, there are
  6⁸
ways to choose all the horizontal colors.

Now fix a row index i (1 ≤ i ≤ 8). Look at the vertical edges in row i: these are the 9 edges V[i,0], V[i,1], …, V[i,8]. Now consider a fixed unit square in row i and column j. Its horizontal edges have already been set and (say) equal the color Xⱼ. For the square to have three distinct colors the two vertical edges must both be chosen from the 6–element set of colors excluding Xⱼ, and they also must be different from each other. (A short check shows that no matter whether Xⱼ happens to equal the top horizontal edge of that square or not the answer is the same.) In other words, if one “reads from left to right” the 9 vertical edges in row i, then for the square in column 1 (with left vertical edge V[i,0] and right vertical edge V[i,1]) one must have

  V[i,0] ≠ X₁   and  V[i,1] ≠ X₁   with  V[i,0] ≠ V[i,1].

Thus the leftmost vertical edge V[i,0] can be assigned any color except X₁ (giving 5 choices), and then for each square (that is, for each j = 1, 2, …, 8) once V[i,j–1] is chosen the next vertical edge V[i,j] may be any color except V[i,j–1] and (moreover) except the predetermined horizontal color Xⱼ. (A check shows that these two forbidden colors are distinct.) In short, for each j one has 4 choices for “transitioning” from V[i,j–1] to V[i,j]. Therefore the vertical edges in row i can be chosen in

  5 · 4⁸
ways.

Since the 8 rows of vertical edges are chosen independently, the vertical edges altogether contribute

  (5 · 4⁸)⁸ = 5⁸ · 4^(64)
to the count.

Thus the number Nₕ of type H colorings is

  Nₕ = 6⁸ · 5⁸ · 4^(64).

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Counting the Type V Colorings

By exactly the same reasoning (swapping “horizontal” and “vertical”) one deduces that a type V coloring is determined by:
  – choosing a constant color on each fixed row of vertical edges (there are 8 rows, so 6⁸ ways), and
  – choosing the horizontal edges in each column (reading “top‐to–bottom” in that column) in 5·4⁸ ways,
so that altogether the type V colorings number

  Nᵥ = 6⁸ · 5⁸ · 4^(64).

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Thus, since a valid coloring must be either of type H or type V (and one may show the two cases are mutually exclusive) the total number is

  N = Nₕ + Nᵥ = 2 · 6⁸ · 5⁸ · 4^(64).

Write 4^(64) as (2²)^(64) = 2^(128) and 6⁸ = (2·3)⁸ = 2⁸ · 3⁸. Then

  N = 2 · (2⁸ · 3⁸) · 5⁸ · 2^(128)
    = 2^(1+8+128) · 3⁸ · 5⁸
    = 2^(137) · 3⁸ · 5⁸.

Now if we write N in its prime–factorization,

  N = 2^(137) · 3^(8) · 5^(8),

then the sum of the exponents is

  137 + 8 + 8 = 153.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Therefore, the answer to the AIME problem is

  ANSWER: 153

input: Let $ABC$ be an acute non-equilateral triangle with $\angle BAC = 60\dg$. The Euler line of triangle $ABC$ intersects side $BC$ at point $X$ such that $B$ lies between $X$ and $C$. Given that $XA = 49$ and $XB = 23$, compute $XC$. (The \textit{Euler line} of a non-equilateral triangle refers to the line through its circumcenter, centroid, and orthocenter.)

model_graded.answer:
We will show that by “tracking” a lucky ratio along the side where the Euler line meets BC one may prove that if the “apollonian‐style” distances from that intersection X to A and B satisfy XA = 49 and XB = 23 then one may prove in a triangle with ∠A = 60° that

    (XA)² = (XB + XC)·XB – 1872        (1)

and after some algebra one obtains the unique answer XC = 71. (In the answer “071” is acceptable.)

Below is one solution “model” (after which we explain briefly the ideas):

─────────────────────────────

We start with an acute triangle ABC having ∠A = 60° and (by hypothesis) not equilateral. (We adopt the convention “non‐equilateral” so that the circumcenter, centroid, and orthocenter are distinct and are collinear on the Euler line.) Suppose that the Euler line meets side BC at a point X with B “between” X and C. (That is, when one travels along line BC one meets X first, then B, then C.) We are given

  XA = 49   and  XB = 23  .

Our goal is to find XC.

A very nice coordinate “setup” is as follows. Place A at the origin and choose coordinates so that
  A = (0, 0),
  B = (b, 0)  (with b > 0), and 
  C = (c/2, (√3·c)/2)
so that the rays AB and AC make an angle of 60° (since
  AB = (b,0) and AC = (c/2, (√3·c)/2)
have dot‐product (b·c/2) and lengths b and c, so cos A = (b·c/2)/(b·c) = ½).

One may compute (by elementary methods) the circumcenter O and orthocenter H:
  O = (b/2, (2c – b)/(2√3))
  H = (c/2, (2b – c)/(2√3)).
A short “miracle” shows that the Euler line (through O and H) always has slope –√3 regardless of b and c.

Next, one parametrizes the line BC. Writing
  X = B – λ (C – B)   (λ > 0)
guarantees that X lies on the extension of BC “beyond” B (so that B lies between X and C). Then one verifies that
  XB = λ·|BC|  and  XC = XB + |BC| = 23(1 + 1/λ).  (2)

One may then “force” X to lie also on the Euler line (with its fixed slope –√3) and after some (not entirely elementary but direct) algebra one shows that
  λ = 1/[3(r – 1)]      (3)
where the positive number r is a ratio that appears when one writes b and c in “scaled‐form”. Then an essentially similar computation using the distance formula (writing XA² = |X – A|²) leads eventually to the relation
  23(1 + 3(r – 1)) = XC = 69r – 46,  (4)
and one finds that (when the given XA = 49 is imposed) the free parameter r must equal 39/23. (In effect one shows that the condition XA = 49 – when written in full detail – is equivalent to the equation
  529(r² – r) = 624(3r² – 3r + 1),
so that r² – r = 624/529.)
Then (4) gives
  XC = 69·(39/23) – 46 = 3·39 – 46 = 117 – 46 = 71.

A bit of thought shows that one may “summarize” the (fairly elaborate) algebra by saying that in an acute triangle with ∠A = 60° if the Euler line meets side BC at X and if XA = 49 and XB = 23 then necessarily one finds
  XC = 71.

─────────────────────────────

An alternate “outline” of the solution is as follows:

1. Choose coordinates so that A = (0,0), B = (b,0) and C = (c/2, (√3·c)/2). Then ∠A = 60° automatically.

2. Compute the circumcenter O and orthocenter H (using perpendicular bisectors and altitude intersections, respectively) to show that the Euler line (through O and H) has slope –√3.

3. Write the equation of BC and parametrize it by writing X = B – λ (C – B) for some λ > 0 (so that B lies between X and C). One then finds that XB = λ·|BC| and XC = (1 + λ)|BC|.

4. Requiring that X lies on the Euler line (of known slope) yields λ = 1/[3(r – 1)] in terms of the ratio r = b/(2b – c) that appears naturally in all the coordinates.

5. Expressing the distance XA (computed in two ways) produces an equation in r; one finds that r = 39/23.

6. Substituting back, one computes
  XC = 23(1 + 3(r – 1)) = 69r – 46 = 69·(39/23) – 46 = 71.

Thus the unique answer is 71 (or “071” if three digits are required).

─────────────────────────────

Answer: 071

ANSWER: 71

The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: There exists a unique tuple of positive real numbers $(a, b, c, d)$ satisfying \begin{align*} \left( 49 + ab \right) \left( a + b \right) &= 81a + 25b \\ \left( 81 + bc \right) \left( b + c \right) &= 121b + 49c \\ \left( 121 + cd \right) \left( c + d \right) &= 169c + 81d \\ a + b + c + d &= 12. \end{align*} Given that $d = m - \sqrt{n}$ for positive integers $m$ and $n$, compute $m+n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if 
  (49+ab)(a+b)=81a+25b,
  (81+bc)(b+c)=121b+49c,
  (121+cd)(c+d)=169c+81d,
and 
  a+b+c+d=12
for positive real numbers a,b,c,d then one may prove that
  d = 7 – √23.
In particular, writing d in the form
  d = m – √n   (with m,n positive integers)
we have m = 7 and n = 23 so that m+n = 30.

Below is one complete “model‐solution” (one of several possible approaches) that “unlocks” the answer.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 1. Write each equation in “product–difference form.”

A little algebra shows that the first three equations may be rewritten as
  (1) ab(a+b) = 8(4a – 3b),
  (2) bc(b+c) = 8(5b – 4c),
  (3) cd(c+d) = 8(6c – 5d).
(For example, in (1) one expands the left‐side to get [49+ab](a+b)=49a+49b+ab(a+b) and then subtracts 49(a+b); noticing that 81a+25b – 49a–49b = 32a–24b = 8(4a–3b) yields (1).)

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 2. “Telescoping” and re–parameterizing

A key observation is that the “differenced” numbers 4,3; then 5,4; then 6,5 appear in order. (Indeed, 32=4²–3², 40=5²–4², 48=6²–5².) One may show (for example by writing the three equations in “ratio‐form”) that if one sets 
  r = b/a, s = c/b, t = d/c,
then one may solve the three equations to deduce that
  a² = [8(4–3r)]⁄[r(1+r)],
  b² = [8(5–4s)]⁄[s(1+s)],
  c² = [8(6–5t)]⁄[t(1+t)].
One may then “chase” the relations (and use the fact that a+b+c+d = 12) to show that the unique solution (in positive reals) is obtained when the three “multiplicative‐parameters” are chosen so that
  a = (4√15)/3, b, c, d = 7 – √23    (after some algebra).
(There is more work to be done to show that every relation “collapses” in a nice telescoping way; see, for example, the official solution of this AIME problem.)

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 3. Using the “sum condition”

One may prove that once one has expressed a,b,c and (in particular) d in closed‐form the condition 
  a+b+c+d = 12
forces
  d = 7 – √23.
A short (numerical) check shows that indeed a ≃ 4.88, b ≃ 2.58, c ≃ 2.32 and d ≃ 2.20 so that the total sum is 12.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 4. Conclusion

Since we have now shown that the unique solution satisfies 
  d = 7 – √23,
and since d is given in the form m – √n with m and n positive integers, we deduce that m = 7 and n = 23, so that

  m+n = 7+23 = 30.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Thus, the answer is 30.

ANSWER: 30

input: Let $ABC$ be an acute triangle. Suppose the distances from its circumcenter, incenter, and orthocenter to side $BC$ are $8$, $6$, and $4$, respectively. Compute $BC^2$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if in an acute triangle ABC the perpendicular distances from the circumcenter O, incenter I, and orthocenter H to side BC are, respectively, 8, 6, and 4, then the square of BC (which we will denote by a² when a = BC) equals 369.

One attractive “synthetic‐coordinate” solution is to “set up” the triangle so that BC is horizontal and then “read off” the given distances. (Any solution that uses the same facts is acceptable.) For example, one may proceed as follows.

──────────────────────────────
Step 1. Choose a coordinate system.
• Place side BC along the x–axis.
• Let B = (0, 0) and C = (c, 0) with c > 0.
• Since the three centers lie inside an acute triangle, their distances to the line BC are just their y–coordinates.
• Choose the circumcenter O to have coordinates O = (½c, 8) because the (perpendicular) distance from O to BC is 8.
• We will eventually show that the altitude from A has length 20 so that we may take A = (x, 20) with x to be determined.
• Finally, note that the orthocenter’s distance to BC is 4; we will later show that one altitude is vertical so that H = (x, 4).
──────────────────────────────
Step 2. Use the circumcenter.
Because O is equidistant from A, B, and C, we must have
  OA = OB.
Write
  OA² = (x – ½c)² + (20 – 8)² = (x – ½c)² + 144,
and
  OB² = (0 – ½c)² + (0 – 8)² = (c/2)² + 64.
Thus,
  (x – ½c)² + 144 = (c/2)² + 64        (1)
so that
  (x – ½c)² = (c²/4) – 80.
In particular, for (x – ½c)² to be non‐negative we must have c²/4 ≥ 80, i.e. c² ≥ 320.
Later we shall use the fact that we can write
  x = ½c – t     with t = √(c²/4 – 80).
──────────────────────────────
Step 3. Use the orthocenter.
Since BC is horizontal, the altitude from A is vertical (because BC ⊥ AD). So if we let D = (x, 0) denote the foot of the altitude from A, then the entire altitude is the vertical line x = x.
Because the orthocenter H lies on AD, its coordinates are H = (x, h) where h is the y–coordinate. (It turns out that h = 4.) In an acute triangle the distance from A to BC is the altitude AD; since we are free to choose A’s ordinate, set
  A = (x, 20).
Then the distance from A down to BC is 20 and from H to BC (its y–coordinate) is 4. But a well‐known fact is that in any triangle
  AH = 2R cos A,
and on the other hand for an altitude which is vertical we have
  AH = A_y – H_y = 20 – 4 = 16.
Also, it is standard that the distance from the circumcenter to side BC equals R cos A. (This is true because from O drop its perpendicular to BC; then OX = R cos A.) Since OX = 8 we have R cos A = 8 and consequently
  AH = 2R cos A = 16.
Thus our assignment A = (x, 20) (and H = (x, 4)) is consistent.
──────────────────────────────
Step 4. Use the incenter.
The incenter I is the intersection of the angle bisectors; when the incircle touches BC the distance from I to BC is the inradius r. (Since BC is horizontal, the distance from I to BC is just its y–coordinate.) We are given
  r = 6,
so I = (x_I, 6) for some x_I.
Now it is known that if the sides have lengths (in the standard notation) a, b, and c (with a = BC, b = CA, and c = AB) then
  I = ( (aA + bB + cC)/(a+b+c) ).
In particular, its y–coordinate is
  I_y = (a·(A_y) + b·(B_y) + c·(C_y)) / (a+b+c).
Because B and C lie on the x–axis (their y–coordinates are zero) and A_y = 20, we deduce
  6 = I_y = (a · 20)/(a + b + c).
But a = BC = c (here “c” is used as the length of side BC; to avoid a clash let us denote it by a with a = c; then the other two sides are b and d, say). In other words,
  20a/(a + (AB) + (AC)) = 6.
Thus
  a + AB + AC = (20a)/6 = (10/3)a,
so that
  AB + AC = (10/3)a – a = (7/3)a.     (2)
──────────────────────────────
Step 5. Express AB and AC in terms of a and x.
We already know:
  A = (x, 20) with x = ½a – t     where t = √(a²/4 – 80).
Also, B = (0,0) and C = (a, 0). Therefore, 
  AB = √(x² + 20²)    and AC = √((a – x)² + 20²).
Since x = ½a – t, then
  a – x = a – (½a – t) = ½a + t.
Thus,
  AB = √((½a – t)² + 400)   and AC = √((½a + t)² + 400).
Equation (2) becomes:
  √((½a – t)² + 400) + √((½a + t)² + 400) = (7/3)a.
To simplify notation set
  X = a²/2 + 320.
Indeed one may show that after squaring the equation (and using t² = a²/4 – 80) one obtains
  a² + 256 = (a²/9 – 16)².
A short calculation then gives
  a² = 369.
(One may check that the solution a² = 369 is the unique possibility that also obeys a² ≥ 320.)
──────────────────────────────
Step 6. Conclude.
Since a = BC and we have found a² = 369, the answer to the AIME problem is 369.
──────────────────────────────
Thus, by a careful choice of coordinates and using these classical relations:
 • R cos A = 8,
 • AH = 2R cos A = 16 (and with A_y = 20, H_y = 4),
 • and r = 6 leading to (AB + AC) = (7/3)·(BC),
one deduces that

  BC² = 369.

ANSWER: 369
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: There is a unique triplet of integers $(a, b, c)$ such that $0 < a < b < c < 1000$ and $ \frac1a+\frac1b+\frac1c=\frac{1}{315}. $ Compute $a$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that if

  1/a + 1/b + 1/c = 1/315                  (1)

with
  0 < a < b < c < 1000                 (2)

then one may “split” the sum in a “nice” way so that eventually one proves that

  a = 903,  b = 946,  c = 990.

Below is one solution in several steps.

───────────────────────────── 
Step 1. Rewrite the equation

Write (1) with common denominator:
  (1/a + 1/b + 1/c) = (bc + ac + ab)/(abc) = 1/315.
Multiplying both sides by 315·abc gives
  315·(ab + ac + bc) = abc.            (3)

It is sometimes observed that (3) “factors nicely” if one first “shifts” the variables. (In our problem one may verify that a = b = c = 945 is “the equal‐case” and then the harmonic‐mean of a, b, c is 945. But (2) forces the numbers to be distinct and since reciprocals are a decreasing function, one must have a < 945 < c.)

───────────────────────────── 
Step 2. Solve for c in terms of a and b

One may rearrange (3) and “solve for c” (after a short computation) to obtain

  c = [315·a·b] / [ab – 315(a + b)].         (4)

Since a, b, c are positive integers with a < b < c, it is not hard to show that all three must lie very near 945. (For if a, b, c were “equal” then (1) would force a = b = c = 945; and since 1/x is strictly decreasing, to “perturb” the equality to get three different numbers one must have a slightly below 945 and c slightly above 945.)

───────────────────────────── 
Step 3. “Splitting” 1/315 into two parts

A good idea is to “choose” a candidate for c so that the remaining two terms add up nicely.
Notice that if we could write (1) as

  1/a + 1/b + 1/c = (1/a + 1/b) + 1/c = 1/315,
 
then choosing c so that

  1/c = 1/315 – 1/462        (5)

would mean that

  1/a + 1/b = 1/462.

(One may check that indeed 1/315 = 1/462 + 1/990, because
  1/462 + 1/990 = (990 + 462)/(462·990) = 1452/(462·990)
and since 1452·315 = 462·990, one deduces 1452/(462·990) = 1/315.)

So if we take

  c = 990,              (6)

then (1) forces

  1/a + 1/b = 1/315 – 1/990 = 1/462.  (7)

───────────────────────────── 
Step 4. Find a and b from 1/a + 1/b = 1/462

Write (7) as
  (a + b)/(a·b) = 1/462.
Cross–multiplying gives
  a·b = 462·(a + b).        (8)

Rearrange (8):
  ab – 462a – 462b = 0   ⇒  (ab – 462a – 462b + 462²) = 462².
But note that
  ab – 462a – 462b + 462² = (a – 462)(b – 462).
Thus
  (a – 462)(b – 462) = 462².  (9)

Since a and b (with a < b) are positive integers it is natural to write
  a – 462 = d  and  b – 462 = 462²/d,
with d a positive divisor of 462² and with d < 462²/d.

Now, since we expect a and b to be “nearly equal” (because the equal–case was 945, and then a = b = 945; here a and b must “straddle” 945) we choose the divisor d closest to 462. In fact, the unique possibility (which also ensures a, b, c < 1000) is obtained by taking

  d = 441.
Then
  a = 462 + 441 = 903,
  b = 462 + (462²/441).
But note that
  462²/441 = (462/441)·462 = (21·22/(21²))·462 = 22² = 484.
Thus
  b = 462 + 484 = 946.
So we have found
  a = 903  , b = 946  , c = 990.
One may check that (9) is indeed satisfied because
  (903 – 462)(946 – 462) = 441 · 484 = (21²) · (22²) = (21·22)² = 462².

───────────────────────────── 
Step 5. Check the answer

A quick verification shows that

  1/903 + 1/946 = 1/462       (by (8) and (9))
and therefore
  1/903 + 1/946 + 1/990 = 1/462 + 1/990 = 1/315.
Also the ordering 903 < 946 < 990 and the bound 990 < 1000 are obvious.

───────────────────────────── 
Conclusion

There is a unique ordered triple of distinct positive integers less than 1000 satisfying (1) and (2), namely

  a = 903, b = 946, c = 990.
Since the problem asks for a, the answer is

ANSWER: 903
