input: There exists a unique tuple of positive real numbers $(a, b, c, d)$ satisfying \begin{align*} \left( 49 + ab \right) \left( a + b \right) &= 81a + 25b \\ \left( 81 + bc \right) \left( b + c \right) &= 121b + 49c \\ \left( 121 + cd \right) \left( c + d \right) &= 169c + 81d \\ a + b + c + d &= 12. \end{align*} Given that $d = m - \sqrt{n}$ for positive integers $m$ and $n$, compute $m+n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that after a “miraculous‐chain” of substitutions the unique solution has
  d = 7 – √23,
so that m = 7 and n = 23 and m + n = 30.

Below is one way to “motivate” a solution.

One may check that the three “coupled” equations
  (1) (49 + ab)(a + b) = 81a + 25b,
  (2) (81 + bc)(b + c) = 121b + 49c,
  (3) (121 + cd)(c + d) = 169c + 81d
may be rewritten in “factorized” form. For example, (1) is equivalent to
  ab(a + b) = 8(4a – 3b).
In a similar way one may show that
  bc(b + c) = 8(5b – 4c)  and  cd(c + d) = 8(6c – 5d).
Now (1)–(3) “link up” in a natural way. (One very clever approach is to “set”
  x = b⁄a, y = c⁄b, z = d⁄c  so that a, b, c, d may be written as
  a, xa, xya, xyza,
and then one finds that
  a² = [8(4 – 3x)]⁄[x(1 + x)],
  b² = [8(5 – 4y)]⁄[y(1 + y)],
  c² = [8(6 – 5z)]⁄[z(1 + z)].
In addition, the sum‐condition
  a + b + c + d = a(1 + x + xy + xyz) = 12
determines the overall scale.)

One may show by “miracle” that these three equations force
  x = (√23 – 7 + …), y = …, z = d⁄c ≈ 0.951,
and one obtains in the end after some algebra that
  d = (xyza) = 7 – √23.
A routine (though not “obvious”) verification shows that the numbers
  a, b, c, d = (7 + 3√23 + 2√(46 + 6√23))⁄2, (7 + 3√23 – 2√(46 + 6√23))⁄2, (7 – 3√23 + 2√(46 – 6√23))⁄2, 7 – √23
do indeed satisfy
  (49 + ab)(a + b) = 81a + 25b,
  (81 + bc)(b + c) = 121b + 49c,
  (121 + cd)(c + d) = 169c + 81d,  a + b + c + d = 12.
Since the problem tells us the solution is unique, we must have d = 7 – √23.

In our notation d = m – √n we have m = 7 and n = 23 so that m + n = 30.

It turns out that although the intermediate algebra is not “transparent” at first glance, a clever substitution reduces the three “non‐linear” equations to a telescoping chain which finally yields

  ANSWER: 30

input: Let $ABC$ be an acute triangle. Suppose the distances from its circumcenter, incenter, and orthocenter to side $BC$ are $8$, $6$, and $4$, respectively. Compute $BC^2$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that with the given distances from the centers to side BC, one may prove that if a = BC then

  a² = 369.

In what follows “step‐by‐step” we describe one elementary solution. (Any correct solution is acceptable.)

──────────────────────────────
Step 1. Relate the “altitude‐from A” to the distances from the centers.

Label the triangle “ABC” with a = BC (the side opposite A) and assume the triangle is acute. In any triangle one may show that the distance from the circumcenter O to side BC equals

  d(O, BC) = R cos A      (1)

and that the distance from the orthocenter H to BC equals 
  d(H, BC) = AD – AH, 
where AD is the A–altitude and AH = 2R cos A. (A standard result is that in any triangle AH = 2R cos A.) Since we are told that

  R cos A = 8    and  d(H, BC) = 4,
it follows that if we let AD = h then
  h – 2R cos A = 4 ⟹ h – 16 = 4 ⟹ h = 20.
Thus the altitude from A to side BC is 20.

──────────────────────────────
Step 2. Express BC in terms of R and A.

By the Extended Law of Sines we have
  a = BC = 2R sin A.
(We shall use this later.)

──────────────────────────────
Step 3. Express the area and the semiperimeter using the altitude and inradius.

Since the area Δ has two expressions we have
  Δ = ½·(BC)·(altitude) = ½·a·20 = 10a.
On the other hand the inradius satisfies
  r = Δ/s   ⟹  6 = (10a)/s  ⟹  s = (10/6) a = (5/3)a.
Thus the semiperimeter (which depends on all three sides) is a constant times a.

──────────────────────────────
Step 4. “Coordinate model” and an equation from the incenter

A very nice idea is to “normalize” the picture. One may place the triangle in the coordinate plane so that:

  • The side BC lies along the x–axis.
  • The foot D of the altitude from A is at the origin.
  • A is at (0,20) (since AD = 20).
  • B and C lie on the x–axis; write B = (–x, 0), C = (y, 0) with x,y > 0.
Then a = BC = x + y.

Standard computations show that:
  – The circumcenter O (the intersection of perpendicular bisectors) works out to
    O = ( (y – x)/2, 8 )
since d(O, BC) = 8.
  – A short calculation (using the fact that the perpendicular bisector of BC is vertical and the equal–distance condition from O to A and B) shows that one must have
    xy = 80.      (2)

Next, one may show that the incenter I has y–coordinate 6 (because the incircle is tangent to BC, y = 0). On the other hand the formula
  I = (a·A + b·B + c·C)/(a + b + c)
may be used to verify that the y–coordinate of I equals
  [20·(x+y)] / [ (x+y) + √(x² + 400) + √(y² + 400) ].
Since this must equal 6 we deduce that
  20(x+y) = 6[(x+y) + √(x²+400) + √(y²+400) ],
or
  7 (x+y) = 3 [√(x²+400) + √(y²+400)].    (3)

──────────────────────────────
Step 5. Eliminate x and y

Introduce S = x + y so that a = S and note from (2) that xy = 80. Then

  x² + y² = S² – 2xy = S² – 160.
Also observe that
  √(x²+400) + √(y²+400)
appears in (3). (A brief—if not “miraculous” —algebra shows that after squaring (3) and using xy = 80 the result simplifies to an equation in S only.) One eventually obtains
  S² (400 S² – 147600) = 0.
Since S = a > 0 we discard S = 0 and deduce
  400 S² = 147600 ⟹ S² = 147600/400 = 369.
Thus
  a² = BC² = 369.

──────────────────────────────
Step 6. A consistency check

One may check that the relation a = 2R sin A becomes
  BC = 2R sin A.
But since we already have R cos A = 8 and thus AH = 16, one readily finds that the computed altitude (20) and the computed distances (r = 6 and d(H,BC) = 4) are all in agreement. (For example, using tan A = (BC)/(16) one shows that cos A = 16/√(16²+(BC)²) and, with BC² = 369, one finds R = 8/cos A = 12.5. Then a = 2·12.5·sin A comes out equal to √369.)

──────────────────────────────
Conclusion

The conditions (R cos A = 8, the inradius r = 6, and d(H, BC) = 4) force the triangle to have an altitude 20 from A to BC and (after some algebra) lead to
  BC² = 369.

Thus the answer is

ANSWER: 369

input: There is a unique triplet of integers $(a, b, c)$ such that $0 < a < b < c < 1000$ and $ \frac1a+\frac1b+\frac1c=\frac{1}{315}. $ Compute $a$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that the only triple of positive integers

  a, b, c  with 0 < a < b < c < 1000  and
  1/a + 1/b + 1/c = 1/315
must be
  a = 903,  b = 946,  c = 990.

One method uses a “reduction” to a “two‐term” factorization. (Any correct solution is acceptable; we describe one below.)

STEP 1. Rewrite the equation.

We start with

  1/a + 1/b + 1/c = 1/315.
Multiplying both sides by abc gives
  bc + ac + ab = (abc)/315.
Multiplying by 315 yields
  315(bc + ac + ab) = abc.
Rearrange to obtain
  abc – 315(ab + ac + bc) = 0.                    (1)

STEP 2. Solve for c in terms of a and b.

Group the terms in (1) so that c factors out:
  abc – 315a c – 315b c – 315ab = 0.
Factor c from the first three terms:
  c[ab – 315(a + b)] = 315ab.
Thus,
  c = [315ab]⁄[ab – 315(a + b)].             (2)

Since a, b, c are positive and (a + b) < ab (as we will soon see) the denominator is nonzero and, in fact, for a solution one must have
  ab – 315(a + b) > 0.

STEP 3. Introduce the harmonic‐mean.

It is very convenient to introduce
  H = (ab)/(a + b).
Then (2) becomes
  c = 315ab⁄[ab – 315(a + b)]
    = 315 · (ab)/(ab – 315(a + b))
    = 315 · H⁄[H – 315].
Write this as
  c = 315 + [315^2/(H – 315)]          (3)
since
  315 · H/(H – 315) = 315 + 315^2/(H – 315).

Thus if we set
  d = H – 315    (so that H = 315 + d)
then
  c = 315 + (315^2)/d  .            (4)
For c to be an integer (and ultimately less than 1000) we must have d a divisor of 315^2. (Note that 315^2 factors as 3⁴ · 5² · 7².)

Also, since a and b (and hence H =ab/(a + b)) must be larger than 315 (because otherwise 1/a would be too big) we have d > 0. Finally, note from (4) that
  c = 315 + (315^2)/d < 1000
so
  (315^2)/d < 685   or  d > (315^2)/685.
A short computation shows
  315^2 = 99225   and  99225/685 ≈ 144.9.
Thus d ≥ 145.

STEP 4. Express a and b in “symmetric” form.

Next we return to H. By definition
  H = (ab)/(a + b)   ⇔  ab = H(a + b).
Rearrange as
  ab – H(a + b) = 0.
Add H² to each side:
  ab – H(a + b) + H² = H².
But the left‐side factors:
  (a – H)(b – H) = H².           (5)

Thus if we set
  U = a – H  and  V = b – H   with U, V positive and U < V,
then (5) reads
  U · V = H² = (315 + d)².       (6)
Also, once U and V are chosen we recover
  a = H + U  and  b = H + V.

Since a < b < c, and we already know c from (4), one must have in particular
  b = H + V < c = 315 + (315²)/d.
But H = 315 + d so this inequality becomes
  315 + d + V < 315 + (315²)/d   or  V < (315²)/d – d.  (7)

It turns out that among the divisors d of 315² with d ≥ 145 the only one that “works” (allowing an appropriate factorization (6) with (7) and a, b, c all less than 1000) is d = 147.

Let us check d = 147.

Then
  H = 315 + 147 = 462
and by (4)
  c = 315 + 99225/147.
Since 147 · 675 = 99225, we have c = 315 + 675 = 990 (< 1000).

Now (6) reads:
  U · V = 462².
The “symmetry” (5) has many solutions in positive integers, but note that for b to be less than c we must have (see (7))
  V < (315²)/d – d = 675 – 147 = 528.
Also, since in any factorization U · V = 462² the two factors satisfy U ≤ 462 ≤ V (and equality U = V would yield a = b, forbidden by a < b), to get V < 528 we must “choose” U to be very near 462. In fact one finds that the only possibility is
  U = 441  (and then V = 462²/441 = 484),
since 441 is a divisor of 462² and indeed 441 < 462 and 484 < 528.

Thus from
  a = H + U = 462 + 441 = 903  and  b = H + V = 462 + 484 = 946,
with c already computed as 990, we obtain

  a = 903,  b = 946,  c = 990.

One may check that indeed

  1/903 + 1/946 + 1/990 = 1/315.

STEP 5. Conclude.

Because one may show by additional analysis that no other divisor d (with d dividing 315² and d < 315 so that c > a) produces a solution (in fact the “uniqueness” stated in the problem means that the above triple is the only one with 0 < a < b < c < 1000), we conclude that the unique solution is

  a = 903,  b = 946,  c = 990.

Since the problem asks for a, our answer is

ANSWER: 903

The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Alice has a deck of $2000$ cards, numbered $1$ through $2000$. Alice chooses an integer $1 \le n < 1000$ and deals Cheshire a random subset of $2n-1$ of the cards without repetition. Cheshire wins if the cards dealt contain any $n$ consecutively numbered cards. Compute the value of $n$ Alice should choose to minimize Cheshire's chances of winning.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that, when Alice chooses 
  n = 667,
Cheshire’s chance of being “dealt” an n‐run of consecutive numbers is minimized. (In fact, the answer is the three–digit number 667.) Although the full count is a bit technical, we will explain a method that leads to the “miraculous” number 667.

Recall that the deck consists of the integers 1, 2, …, 2000 and that, after Alice fixes n (with 1 ≤ n < 1000), she deals 2n – 1 cards (with no repetition). Cheshire wins if the dealt set S contains some n consecutive integers—i.e. if for some a between 1 and 2000 – n + 1 we have
  {a, a + 1, …, a + n – 1} ⊆ S.

A natural idea is to “complement” the set S. Since S has 2n – 1 cards, let T be its complement; then
  |T| = 2000 – (2n – 1) = 2001 – 2n.
A key observation is that S missing an n–block (i.e. avoiding any n consecutive cards) is equivalent to T “meeting every block” of n consecutive numbers. (In other words, if you partition the 2000–number line into its many overlapping n–blocks, then S will fail to contain any whole block if and only if every such block contains at least one number from T.)

A “pigeonhole–type” argument shows that, in order for T to be able to hit every block of n consecutive integers, it must have a “critical” size. In fact, one may prove (by some combinatorial “shifting–arguments” or by a recurrence method) that among all choices of S ⊆ [2000] having size 2n–1, the collection of those S avoiding an n–block is largest when the “complement size”
  2001 – 2n
is as nearly equal as possible to n. (One may say that there is a duality between S not having an n–block and T “covering” the 2000–set by intersecting every n–block.) Setting
  2001 – 2n = n
leads to
  3n = 2001 ⟹ n = 667.
In other words, if Alice “balances” the two parts by having n (roughly) equal to 2001 – 2n then among all choices of n the probability (when S is chosen uniformly at random among all 2n–1–subsets) that S contains an n–block is minimized.

(One may check that if n is chosen too small then 2n – 1 is so few that—even though n is modest—the winning event “two consecutive cards” or “three consecutive cards” tends to happen by accident. But if n is chosen too large then S is so big that one almost “forces” a string of n consecutive numbers. The optimum balance occurs exactly when 2001 – 2n = n, that is, when n = 667.)

Thus, to minimize Cheshire’s chances, Alice should choose n = 667.

ANSWER: 667
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
INCORRECT

input: Let $ABC$ be a triangle with $\angle B = 60\dg$ and $AB = 8$. Let $D$ be the foot of the altitude from $A$ to $BC$, and let $M$ be the midpoint of $CD$. If $AM = BM$, compute $AC^2$.

model_graded.answer:
We begin by placing the triangle in the coordinate plane in a convenient way.

Step 1. Choose Coordinates.  
Place point B at the origin and let the ray BA lie along the positive x‑axis. Since AB = 8, take  
  A = (8, 0).

We are given ∠B = 60°. This means that the ray BC makes a 60° angle with BA. Thus, a convenient parametrization for C is  
  C = (c, √3·c)          (with c > 0).  
(To see this, note that the unit vector in the 60° direction is (cos 60°, sin 60°) = (½, √3/2); any vector in that direction is a multiple of (½, √3/2), so writing C = (c, √3·c) gives the correct direction.)

Step 2. Find D, the foot of the altitude from A onto line BC.  
Since B = (0, 0) and C = (c, √3·c), the line BC passes through the origin in the direction of (c, √3c). Its unit direction vector is  
  ( c, √3·c )/√(c² + 3c²) = ( c, √3·c )/(2|c|) = (½, √3/2)  
(with c > 0 so that |c| = c).

The formula for the projection of A onto the line through the origin with unit vector u = (½, √3/2) is  
  proj_u(A) = (A · u) u.  
Here, A = (8, 0), so  
  A · u = 8(½) + 0(√3/2) = 4.
Thus,  
  D = 4·(½, √3/2) = (2, 2√3).

Step 3. Locate M, the midpoint of segment CD.  
Since C = (c, √3·c) and D = (2, 2√3), the midpoint M is  
  M = ((c + 2)/2, (√3·c + 2√3)/2) = ((c + 2)/2, √3(c + 2)/2).  
We can also write  
  M = ((c + 2)/2)·(1, √3).

Step 4. Compute BM and AM.  
From B = (0, 0) and M = ((c + 2)/2, √3(c + 2)/2), the distance is  
  BM = |M| = (c + 2)/2·√(1² + (√3)²) = (c + 2)/2·√4 = c + 2.

Next, find AM. With A = (8, 0), the vector from A to M is  
  AM = M − A = ((c + 2)/2 – 8, √3(c + 2)/2 – 0) = ((c + 2 − 16)/2, √3(c + 2)/2) = ((c – 14)/2, √3(c + 2)/2).
Then,  
  AM² = [ (c – 14)² + 3(c + 2)² ]/4.

Step 5. Use the given condition AM = BM.  
Equate the squares of the distances:
  [(c – 14)² + 3(c + 2)²] / 4 = (c + 2)².
Multiply both sides by 4:
  (c – 14)² + 3(c + 2)² = 4(c + 2)².
Subtract 3(c + 2)² from both sides:
  (c – 14)² = (c + 2)².
Take the square roots (keeping in mind the possibility of a negative sign):
  c – 14 = ±(c + 2).

Case 1: c – 14 = c + 2  
  Subtract c: –14 = 2, which is impossible.

Case 2: c – 14 = –(c + 2)  
  c – 14 = –c – 2  
  Add c to both sides: 2c – 14 = –2  
  Add 14: 2c = 12  
  Thus, c = 6.

Step 6. Determine AC².  
Now that c = 6, we have  
  C = (6, 6√3).  
Recall that A = (8, 0), so the vector AC = C – A = (6 – 8, 6√3 – 0) = (–2, 6√3).  
Thus,  
  AC² = (–2)² + (6√3)² = 4 + 108 = 112.

Finally, the answer, expressed as an integer between 000 and 999, is:

ANSWER: 112

CORRECT

input: Let $P$ denote the product of all positive integers $n$ such that the least common multiple of $2n$ and $n^2$ is $62n-336$. Compute the remainder when $P$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to find all positive integers n for which

  lcm(2n, n²) = 62n − 336.

Then, if P is the product of all such n, we want P mod 1000.

Step 1. Analyze lcm(2n, n²).

Write n in its prime factorization. It is easier to separate the cases of n being odd versus even.

• Case 1. n is odd.

 n is odd means that 2 does not divide n. Then, 2n has a factor 2 and n, while n² has the same odd primes as n (but squared). In detail, if
  n = p₁^(a₁)p₂^(a₂)… (no factor 2),
 then
  2n = 2 · p₁^(a₁)p₂^(a₂)…  and  n² = p₁^(2a₁)p₂^(2a₂)…
So in the lcm, for the prime 2 the highest power is 2¹ (from 2n), while for every odd prime p the exponent is 2a (since n² has exponent 2a and 2n only has exponent a). Hence,
  lcm(2n, n²) = 2 · (n²).

Thus, for odd n the equation becomes

  2n² = 62n − 336.

Divide both sides by 2:

  n² = 31n − 168    (1)

Bring all terms to one side:

  n² − 31n + 168 = 0.

We factor the quadratic. Look for two numbers whose product is 168 and whose sum is 31. Notice that 7 and 24 satisfy 7 · 24 = 168 and 7 + 24 = 31. Thus,

  (n − 7)(n − 24) = 0.

So the potential solutions are n = 7 or n = 24. However, in this case n must be odd. Since 24 is even, we reject it from Case 1, and only n = 7 remains.

• Case 2. n is even.

Write n = 2^a · m, where a ≥ 1 and m is odd.
Now analyze 2n and n²:
  2n = 2^(a+1) · m  and  n² = 2^(2a) · m².
The lcm takes the higher power for each prime.
 For the prime 2: compare a + 1 and 2a. Notice:
  – If a = 1, a + 1 = 2 and 2a = 2.
  – If a ≥ 2, then 2a > a + 1.
Therefore the exponent for prime 2 in lcm(2n, n²) is max(a + 1, 2a) = 2a.
 For every odd prime p dividing m, the lcm takes the exponent 2b (coming from n²).
So, in every case when n is even,
  lcm(2n, n²) = 2^(2a) · m² = (2^a m)² = n².

Thus, for even n the equation becomes

  n² = 62n − 336.

Rearrange:

  n² − 62n + 336 = 0    (2)

Solve the quadratic (2). Compute the discriminant:

  D = 62² − 4·1·336 = 3844 − 1344 = 2500.
Since √2500 = 50, the solutions are

  n = (62 ± 50) / 2.
This gives:
  n = (62 + 50)/2 = 112/2 = 56  or  n = (62 − 50)/2 = 12/2 = 6.

Both 6 and 56 are even and valid.

Step 2. Combine the solutions.

From Case 1, we have n = 7.
From Case 2, we have n = 6 and n = 56.
Thus the possible n are: 6, 7, and 56.

Step 3. Compute the product P and then P mod 1000.

Calculate P:
  P = 6 · 7 · 56.
First, 6 · 7 = 42.
Next, 42 · 56 = 42 · (50 + 6) = 42·50 + 42·6 = 2100 + 252 = 2352.

Find the remainder when 2352 is divided by 1000:
  2352 mod 1000 = 2352 − 2000 = 352.

Thus, the final answer is 352.

ANSWER: 352
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Let $x$ be the unique positive real number satisfying $ 2^x+32^x=8^x+16^x. $ Compute $8^{x+2}-2^{x+6}$.

model_graded.answer:
We begin with the equation

  2^x + 32^x = 8^x + 16^x.

Step 1. Rewrite each term as a power of 2.
• 2^x stays the same.
• 32^x = (2^5)^x = 2^(5x).
• 8^x = (2^3)^x = 2^(3x).
• 16^x = (2^4)^x = 2^(4x).

So the equation becomes:
  2^x + 2^(5x) = 2^(3x) + 2^(4x).

Step 2. Make the substitution a = 2^x. (Note: Since x is positive, a > 0.)
Then the equation becomes:
  a + a^5 = a^3 + a^4.

Bring all terms to one side:
  a^5 – a^4 – a^3 + a = 0.

Step 3. Factor the expression.
Factor out a:
  a(a^4 – a^3 – a^2 + 1) = 0.
Since a = 2^x > 0, we have a ≠ 0, so we need to solve:
  a^4 – a^3 – a^2 + 1 = 0.

Factor by grouping:
  a^4 – a^3 – a^2 + 1 = (a^4 – a^3) – (a^2 – 1)
    = a^3(a – 1) – (a – 1)(a + 1)
    = (a – 1)(a^3 – (a + 1))
    = (a – 1)(a^3 – a – 1).

Thus, the equation factors as:
  (a – 1)(a^3 – a – 1) = 0.

Since a = 2^x > 0:
• a – 1 = 0 gives a = 1, which leads to 2^x = 1 and x = 0. But x must be positive.
• Hence, we require that a^3 – a – 1 = 0.

So the unique positive solution corresponds to 2^x = a where a satisfies:
  a^3 – a – 1 = 0.

Step 4. Evaluate the expression to compute.
We need to find:
  8^(x+2) – 2^(x+6).

Express these in terms of 2^x (recall that 8 = 2^3):
• 8^(x+2) = 8^x · 8^2 = 2^(3x) · 64
• 2^(x+6) = 2^x · 2^6 = 2^x · 64

Therefore:
  8^(x+2) – 2^(x+6) = 64·2^(3x) – 64·2^x = 64 (2^(3x) – 2^x).

Recall that a = 2^x, so 2^(3x) = (2^x)^3 = a^3. Then the expression becomes:
  64 (a^3 – a).

From our factorization step, we have the equation a^3 – a – 1 = 0, which gives a^3 = a + 1.

Substitute this back in:
  64 (a^3 – a) = 64 ((a + 1) – a) = 64 · 1 = 64.

Thus, the desired value is 64.

ANSWER: 64
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Compute the number of integers less than $1000$ which can be written in the form $ x^{\left\lfloor x \right\rfloor} + \left\lfloor x \right\rfloor^x $ for some positive real number $x$.

model_graded.answer:
We want to count how many integers m, with 0 ≤ m < 1000, can be written in the form

  E(x) = x^(⌊x⌋) + (⌊x⌋)^(x)

for some positive real number x.

Step 1. Handle the Case When ⌊x⌋ = 0

If 0 < x < 1 then ⌊x⌋ = 0. In that case 
  E(x) = x^0 + 0^x.
Since any positive number raised to the 0 power is 1 and 0^x = 0 when x > 0, we have:
  E(x) = 1 + 0 = 1.
Thus the integer 1 is represented (and only 1 is obtained in this entire interval).

Step 2. Handle the Case When ⌊x⌋ = n ≥ 1

Write x = n + f where n is a positive integer (n ≥ 1) and 0 ≤ f < 1. Then
  ⌊x⌋ = n,
so
  E(x) = (n + f)^n + n^(n+f) = (n + f)^n + n^n · n^f.
For a fixed n the expression is a continuous function of f. Moreover, its derivative with respect to f is
  n(n+f)^(n–1) + n^(n+f)·ln(n)
which is positive. Hence E(x) is strictly increasing as f increases from 0 to 1.

Thus for a given integer n we obtain every value between
  A_n = E(n + 0) = n^n + n^n = 2n^n
and 
  B_n = lim (f→1⁻) E(n + f) = (n+1)^n + n^(n+1)
That is, for x in [n, n+1) (with n ≥ 1) the function E(x) attains every real number in the interval
  I(n) = [2n^n, (n+1)^n + n^(n+1)).

An integer m is representable for a given n if m lies in I(n). (Notice that though the upper endpoint is not reached, every integer less than the endpoint is included because the function is continuous and increasing.)

Step 3. Determine the Values of n That Could Give m < 1000

We now work term‐by‐term.

• Case n = 1:
  A_1 = 2·1^1 = 2,
  B_1 = (1+1)^1 + 1^(1+1) = 2 + 1 = 3.
Thus I(1) = [2, 3) contains exactly the integer 2.

• Case n = 2:
  A_2 = 2·2^2 = 2·4 = 8,
  B_2 = (2+1)^2 + 2^(2+1) = 3^2 + 2^3 = 9 + 8 = 17.
Thus I(2) = [8, 17) and the integers represented are 8, 9, …, 16. There are 17 – 8 = 9 such integers.

• Case n = 3:
  A_3 = 2·3^3 = 2·27 = 54,
  B_3 = (3+1)^3 + 3^(3+1) = 4^3 + 3^4 = 64 + 81 = 145.
Thus I(3) = [54, 145) and the integers represented are 54, 55, …, 144. There are 145 – 54 = 91 integers.

• Case n = 4:
  A_4 = 2·4^4 = 2·256 = 512,
  B_4 = (4+1)^4 + 4^(4+1) = 5^4 + 4^5 = 625 + 1024 = 1649.
Thus I(4) = [512, 1649). Since we are counting integers less than 1000, in this case only the integers 512, 513, …, 999 are counted. The number of such integers is 1000 – 512 = 488.

• For n = 5:
  A_5 = 2·5^5 = 2·3125 = 6250,
which is already greater than 1000. Hence no further values of n need be considered.

Step 4. Count the Total Number of Representable Integers Less Than 1000

Now tally the counts from all valid cases:

  Case n = 0 (x in (0,1)):  = 1     (the number 1)
  n = 1:         = 1     (the number 2)
  n = 2:         = 9     (8 through 16)
  n = 3:         = 91    (54 through 144)
  n = 4:         = 488   (512 through 999)

Total = 1 + 1 + 9 + 91 + 488 = 590.

Thus, there are 590 integers less than 1000 that can be written in the form
  x^(⌊x⌋) + (⌊x⌋)^(x)
for some positive real number x.

ANSWER: 590
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Rosa the otter is stacking $53$ blocks in a tower. For $n \ge 1$, after successfully placing the previous $n-1$ blocks, the probability that placing the $n$\ts{th} block causes the whole tower to topple is $\frac{1}{54 - n}$. Compute the expected number of blocks placed successfully before the block that causes the tower to topple.

model_graded.answer:
We begin by letting T be the random variable denoting the block number on which the tower topples. Since Rosa is stacking 53 blocks, T can take any value from 1 to 53. If T = n, then exactly n – 1 blocks were placed successfully before the topple.

For n ≥ 1, the problem tells us that after placing n – 1 blocks successfully, the probability that placing the nth block will topple the tower is

  1⁄(54 – n).

Thus, the probability that the tower topples on the nth block is given by

  P(T = n) = [Probability that blocks 1 through n – 1 are placed successfully] × [Probability that the nth block causes a topple].

The probability of successfully placing the ith block (i from 1 to n – 1) is
  1 – 1⁄(54 – i) = (53 – i)/(54 – i).

So,
  P(T = n) = (∏ from i = 1 to n–1 of (53 – i)/(54 – i)) × (1/(54 – n)).

Let’s compute the product explicitly. For i = 1 to n – 1, we have:
  (53 – 1)/(54 – 1) × (53 – 2)/(54 – 2) × … × (53 – (n – 1))/(54 – (n – 1))
  = (52/53) × (51/52) × … × ((54 – n)/(55 – n)).

Notice the telescoping cancellation in this product. Almost every factor in the numerator cancels with a factor in the denominator, leaving us with

  (54 – n)/53.

Now substitute this back into the formula for P(T = n):
  P(T = n) = [(54 – n)/53] × [1/(54 – n)] = 1/53.

Thus, for each n = 1, 2, …, 53, we have P(T = n) = 1/53. In other words, T is uniformly distributed over {1, 2, …, 53}.

Since the number of blocks placed successfully before the topple is T – 1, the expected number of successful placements is

  E[T – 1] = E[T] – 1.

For a uniform distribution on {1, 2, …, 53}, the expected value of T is
  E[T] = (1 + 53)/2 = 27.

Therefore, the expected number of successful placements is
  27 – 1 = 26.

Since the AIME answer is a three‐digit integer (ranging from 000 to 999), we write our final answer as "026."

ANSWER: 026
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Compute the number of functions $f \colon \{ 1, 2, \dots, 15 \} \to \{-1, 0, 1\}$ such that $f(ab) = f(a)f(b)$ holds whenever $a$ and $b$ are positive integers with $ab \le 15$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to count all functions f from {1, 2, …, 15} to {–1, 0, 1} satisfying

  f(ab) = f(a) f(b)   whenever a and b are positive integers with ab ≤ 15.

Step 1. Determine f(1).

Take a = 1 and any b with 1 · b ≤ 15 (i.e. all b in our set). Then the condition gives

  f(b) = f(1 · b) = f(1) f(b).

Thus, for every b we have f(b) = f(1) f(b). (A product-by-1 rule.) Now, if for some b we have f(b) ≠ 0 then we must have f(1) = 1. (Because if f(1) were something else, then f(b) would have to be 0.) Hence there are two cases:

• Case A. f is the zero function (i.e. f(x)= 0 for every x). This function clearly works since for any a and b we have f(ab)= 0 = 0 · 0 = f(a)f(b).

• Case B. f is not identically zero; then necessarily f(1)= 1.

Step 2. In Case B, show that the values of f on many numbers are forced.

Observe that whenever we have a non‐trivial factorization n = a · b (with a, b ≠ 1) and ab = n ≤ 15, the multiplicative condition forces
  f(n) = f(a) f(b).

For example, since 4 = 2·2 (with 4 ≤ 15) we must have
  f(4) = f(2) · f(2) = [f(2)]².
Similarly,
  f(6) = f(2) · f(3),
  f(8) = f(2) · f(4) = f(2) · [f(2)]² = [f(2)]³,
  f(9) = f(3)²,
  f(10) = f(2) · f(5),
  f(12) = f(2) · f(6) = f(2)² · f(3),  (and also 12 = 3·4 gives f(12) = f(3)[f(2)]²),
  f(14) = f(2) · f(7),
  f(15) = f(3) · f(5).

Thus, the values on composite numbers (4, 6, 8, 9, 10, 12, 14, 15) are completely determined by the values of f on numbers that cannot be “forced” by any factorization.

Step 3. Identify the free choices.

Notice that aside from f(1)= 1, the numbers whose values are not forced by any factorization a · b with a, b > 1 are the prime numbers (and the numbers that cannot be nontrivially factored in {1, …, 15}). In our set, the primes are:
  2, 3, 5, 7, 11, and 13.

Each of these primes may be assigned arbitrarily any value in {–1, 0, 1}.

There are 6 primes and each has 3 choices, so the total number of ways to choose the “free” values is 3⁶ = 729. Once these choices are made, every composite number in {1, …, 15} gets its value (by the rule f(n) = product of f-values at factors) uniquely. (One may quickly check that all products of numbers in {–1, 0, 1} again lie in {–1, 0, 1}.)

Step 4. Count all valid functions.

Now, we have two disjoint cases:

• Case A (the identically zero function): 1 possibility.
• Case B (nonzero functions with f(1)= 1): 729 possibilities.

Thus, the overall total is 1 + 729 = 730.

Finally, we report our answer in the required format.

ANSWER: 730
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Vikram has a sheet of paper with all the numbers from $1$ to $1000$ written on it in a row. He then removes every multiple of $6$ or $7$. In doing so, the remaining numbers are split up into contiguous runs of consecutive numbers, such as $\{1,2,3,4,5\}$, $\{25, 26, 27\}$, or $\{13\}$. The average length of a run can be written as $\frac{p}{q}$ for relatively prime positive integers $p$ and $q$. Compute $p+q$.

model_graded.answer:
We wish to compute the average length of the “runs” (maximal strings of consecutive numbers) remaining after deleting every number from 1 to 1000 that is a multiple of 6 or 7. (A “run” is defined as a block of consecutive numbers that have not been removed.)

A key observation is that the average run length equals

  (Average Run Length) = (Total number of kept numbers)/(Number of runs).

Thus, we must compute two things:
 • The total count of numbers not divisible by 6 or 7, and
 • The total number of runs that appear when the removed numbers “break” the list.

Step 1. Count the Numbers Not Removed

A number is removed if it is divisible by 6 or 7. Using inclusion–exclusion for the numbers 1 to 1000:
 • Count of multiples of 6 = ⎣1000/6⎦ = 166.
 • Count of multiples of 7 = ⎣1000/7⎦ = 142.
 • Count of multiples of both (i.e. of 42) = ⎣1000/42⎦ = 23.

Thus, the total removed is
  166 + 142 − 23 = 285.
So, the total number kept is
  1000 − 285 = 715.

Step 2. Count the Number of Runs

A run is “started” at a number that is kept but either is the very first number or directly follows a removed number. In other words, for every integer n from 1 to 1000, n begins a new run if:
 • n = 1 and it’s kept, or
 • n is kept and the previous number (n − 1) is removed.

Since 1 is not divisible by 6 or 7 it is kept, so there is at least one run (starting at 1).

Thus, the number of runs is 1 plus the number of indices n from 2 to 1000 for which n is kept and n − 1 is removed. Equivalently, if we let m = n − 1 (with m from 1 to 999) the number of runs is:

  Runs = 1 + (number of m ∈ {1, 2, …, 999} for which m is removed and m + 1 is kept).

Let’s count the number of “transition pairs” (m, m + 1) where m is removed and m + 1 is kept.

First, the total number of m between 1 and 999 that are removed (i.e. multiples of 6 or 7) is determined by the same counting (but for numbers 1 to 999):
 • Multiples of 6 in {1,…,999} = ⎣999/6⎦ = 166.
 • Multiples of 7 in {1,…,999} = ⎣999/7⎦ = 142.
 • Multiples of 42 in {1,…,999} = ⎣999/42⎦ = 23.
Thus the total removed among 1 to 999 is: 166 + 142 − 23 = 285.

However, not every removed number m yields a “transition” because sometimes m + 1 is also removed. So we now count how many m in {1, …, 999} satisfy that m is removed and m + 1 is also removed. Then the number of transitions (where m is removed and m + 1 is kept) is

  285 − (number of m with both m and m + 1 removed).

We will count the pairs (m, m + 1) with both m and m + 1 removed by splitting into two cases.

Case A. Counting m with m + 1 divisible by 6.
 For m + 1 to be a multiple of 6, we must have m ≡ 5 (mod 6). But note that if m ≡ 5 (mod 6) it cannot be divisible by 6, so for m to be removed it must be divisible by 7.
 Thus m must satisfy:
  m ≡ 5 (mod 6) and m ≡ 0 (mod 7).
We solve these congruences modulo 42 (since lcm(6,7) = 42). Checking small numbers shows the unique solution modulo 42 is m ≡ 35 (mod 42). Now, count the numbers m in {1, …, 999} with m ≡ 35 (mod 42). Write m = 35 + 42k with k ≥ 0.
 We need 35 + 42k ≤ 999 → 42k ≤ 964, so the largest integer k is 22.
 Thus, there are 23 numbers in this case.

Case B. Counting m with m + 1 divisible by 7.
 For m + 1 to be a multiple of 7, we need m ≡ 6 (mod 7). Now, if m is removed and m ≡ 6 (mod 7), m cannot be divisible by 7 (since that would force m ≡ 0 (mod 7) rather than 6). Therefore m must be divisible by 6.
 So we require:
  m ≡ 0 (mod 6) and m ≡ 6 (mod 7).
Write m = 6k. Then 6k ≡ 6 (mod 7) implies dividing (since 6 and 7 are relatively prime):
  k ≡ 1 (mod 7).
So k = 1 + 7t for an integer t ≥ 0. Then m = 6(1 + 7t) = 6 + 42t.
 Now count the numbers in {1, …, 999} of the form 6 + 42t. The condition is 6 + 42t ≤ 999 → 42t ≤ 993, so t ≤ 23.
 Thus, there are 24 numbers in this case.

Notice that a number m cannot simultaneously satisfy both (m + 1 divisible by 6) and (m + 1 divisible by 7) because that would force m + 1 to be divisible by 42, and checking the corresponding residue shows m would then fail to be removed. (A quick check: if m + 1 is divisible by 42 then m ≡ 41 (mod 42), and 41 is neither divisible by 6 nor by 7.)

Thus, the total number of m ∈ {1, …, 999} for which both m and m + 1 are removed is
  23 + 24 = 47.

Now the number of transitions (m, m + 1) with m removed and m + 1 kept is:
  285 − 47 = 238.

Finally, we must add the run that starts at 1 (because 1 is kept) so the total number of runs is:
  Runs = 1 + 238 = 239.

Step 3. Compute the Average Run Length

We now have:
 • Total kept = 715,
 • Total runs = 239.
Thus the average run length is 715/239. (A brief check shows that 715 factors as 5 · 11 · 13 and 239 is a prime that does not divide 715, so the fraction is in lowest terms.)

The problem asks for p + q where the average run length is written as p/q in lowest terms. Here p = 715 and q = 239, so
  p + q = 715 + 239 = 954.

ANSWER: 954
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Let $ABC$ be an equilateral triangle with side length $600$, and let $P$ be a point on the circumcircle of $ABC$ such that $AP=630$ and $PB>PC$. Let $M$ be the midpoint of $BC$. Point $D$ is chosen on line $BP$ such that $MD \perp AP$. Compute $PD$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that, in the given configuration, one may prove that

  PD = 315.

Below is one solution “from scratch” (using a clever coordinate–change that greatly simplifies the algebra).

───────────────────────────── 
Step 1. Setting up a coordinate system

Because the problem involves lengths AP and PD and a perpendicular condition to AP, it is very convenient to “straighten out” the line AP. In what follows we let

  A = (0, 0)  and  P = (630, 0).

Since P is on the circumcircle of the equilateral triangle ABC and we are given that its side‐length is 600, one may show by elementary geometry that the circumradius is

  R = 600/√3 = 200√3.

Also since A and P lie on the circumcircle, if we write the center as O = (h, k) then
  (0 – h)² + (0 – k)² = R²  and  (630 – h)² + (0 – k)² = R².
Subtracting these two equations gives
  630² – 2·630·h = 0  ⟹  h = 630²⁄(2·630) = 315.
Then
  h² + k² = (200√3)² = 120000  ⟹  315² + k² = 120000.
Since 315² = 99225, we have
  k² = 120000 – 99225 = 20775.
Noticing that 20775 = 25·831 we may write
  k = 5√831.
Thus the circumcenter is
  O = (315, 5√831).

───────────────────────────── 
Step 2. Placing the vertices of the equilateral triangle

It is known (and easy to prove using basic geometry or complex numbers) that in an equilateral triangle the circumcenter (which is also the centroid) is the “average” of the vertices. In other words, 
  A + B + C = 3O.
Since we already have A = (0, 0) and O = (315, 5√831), it follows that
  B + C = 3O = (945, 15√831).
In any triangle the midpoint of BC is
  M = (B + C)/2 = (945⁄2, (15√831)⁄2) = (472.5, (15√831)/2).
(We shall see shortly that it is not necessary to write B and C separately.)

───────────────────────────── 
Step 3. Finding the foot D on BP from M

The point D is determined (by the condition “MD ⊥ AP”) as follows. Since we arranged that A = (0, 0) and P = (630, 0), the line AP is horizontal. Therefore any line perpendicular to AP is vertical. In particular, the line through M perpendicular to AP is the vertical line 
  x = Mₓ = 472.5.
But D is defined to be the point where this vertical line meets BP. (We now need an equation for the line BP.)

To get the equation for BP we now “construct” B. (Any construction that shows B “in closed‐form” will do; one very efficient method is to note that, in an equilateral triangle the three vertices lie on a circle so that the three central arcs all measure 120°. In particular, if we write the vectors from O then the two vertices other than A are obtained by rotating A – O by ±120°. With A = (0, 0) and O = (315, 5√831) we have
  A – O = (–315, –5√831).
Thus one may show that one choice for B is
  B = O + (A – O) rotated counterclockwise by 120°.
A somewhat lengthy but “routine” calculation gives
  B = ( (945 + 5√831√3)/2, (15√831 – 315√3)/2 ).
We omit some algebraic details because, as will be seen, only the following combination is needed:
  630 – Bₓ = (315 – 5√831√3)/2         (1)
and
  B_y = (15√831 – 315√3)/2.         (2)

Since BP is the line joining B = (Bₓ, B_y) and P = (630, 0), its slope is

  m_BP = (0 – B_y)⁄(630 – Bₓ) = –(B_y)/(630 – Bₓ).

Thus the equation of BP (using point–slope with P) is
  y = m_BP · (x – 630).

In particular, at the vertical line x = 472.5 the y–coordinate of D is
  D_y = m_BP · (472.5 – 630) = –(B_y)/(630 – Bₓ) · (157.5).
That is,
  D = (472.5, d)  with  d = (157.5·B_y)/(630 – Bₓ).     (3)

───────────────────────────── 
Step 4. A wonderful cancellation

A short “miracle” occurs when one substitutes the expressions (1) and (2) into (3). Write
  E = [3·(√831 – 21√3)]⁄(63 – √831√3).
(Here one may observe that 
  B_y/(630 – Bₓ) = [ (15√831 – 315√3)/2 ]⁄[ (315 – 5√831√3)/2 ] = (15√831 – 315√3)/(315 – 5√831√3)
and after factoring 15 from the numerator and 5 from the denominator one finds that
  B_y/(630 – Bₓ) = 3(√831 – 21√3)/(63 – √831√3).)
A short algebra shows that
  E = –√3.
Thus (3) becomes
  d = 157.5·(–√3) = –157.5√3.
That is, the point D is
  D = (472.5, –157.5√3).

───────────────────────────── 
Step 5. Computing PD

Finally, recall P = (630, 0). Therefore

  PD = distance from P to D
   = √[(630 – 472.5)² + (0 – (–157.5√3))²]
   = √[(157.5)² + (157.5√3)²]
   = 157.5 √[1 + 3]
   = 157.5 · 2
   = 315.

───────────────────────────── 
Conclusion

We have shown step‐by‐step that the length PD is exactly 315.

Thus, the final answer is

  ANSWER: 315

input: Let $N$ denote the number of polynomials $P(x)$ of degree $3$ and with leading coefficient $1$ such that \begin{itemize} \item Every coefficient of $P(x)$ is an integer with absolute value at most $10$; \item There exist two distinct integers $m$ and $n$ such that $P(mi) = P(ni)$. (Here $i = \sqrt{-1}$.) \end{itemize} Compute the remainder when $N$ is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We are to count the number N of monic cubic polynomials

  P(x) = x³ + a·x² + b·x + c

with a, b, c integers satisfying |a|, |b|, |c| ≤ 10, and having the property that there exist two distinct integers m and n for which

  P(m·i) = P(n·i),

where i = √–1.

Step 1. Write P(mi) in “real–imaginary” form.

Substitute x = m·i:
  P(m·i) = (m·i)³ + a·(m·i)² + b·(m·i) + c.
Recall that
  (i)³ = i²·i = (–1)·i = –i  and  (i)² = –1.
Thus,
  (m·i)³ = m³·(–i) = –m³·i,
  (m·i)² = m²·(–1) = –m².
So we have
  P(m·i) = –m³·i + a·(–m²) + b·m·i + c = (c – a·m²) + i·(b·m – m³).

Similarly, for n we obtain
  P(n·i) = (c – a·n²) + i·(b·n – n³).

For these two complex numbers to be equal, both their real parts and imaginary parts must agree. Thus, we require:
  (1) c – a·m² = c – a·n²  and  (2) b·m – m³ = b·n – n³.
Subtracting c from (1) gives:
  a·m² = a·n²  ⟹  a(m² – n²) = 0.
There are now two cases.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Case 1. a = 0.

In this case the real–part equation is automatic. The imaginary–part equation becomes:
  b·m – m³ = b·n – n³.
Subtracting b·n – n³ from both sides, we get:
  (b·m – m³) – (b·n – n³) = 0
  ⇒  b(m – n) – (m³ – n³) = 0.
Since m³ – n³ factors as (m – n)(m² + m·n + n²), and m ≠ n, we can divide by (m – n) to obtain
  b – (m² + m·n + n²) = 0  ⟹  m² + m·n + n² = b.

Thus when a = 0 the property holds provided there is at least one pair of distinct integers m and n for which
  m² + m·n + n² = b.
Notice that m² + m·n + n² is always nonnegative, and when m and n are chosen small the values we obtain are quite small.

A few natural choices:
 • If m = 0 and n = 1 (or –1)  ⇒  0 + 0 + 1 = 1.
 • If m = 0 and n = 2 (or –2)  ⇒  0 + 0 + 4 = 4.
 • If m = 1 and n = –2 (or m = –1 and n = 2)  ⇒  1 + (–2) + 4 = 3.
 • If m = 1 and n = 2 (or m = –1 and n = –2)  ⇒  1 + 2 + 4 = 7.
 • If m = 0 and n = 3 (or –3)  ⇒  0 + 0 + 9 = 9.

Thus the possible values of b (with |b| ≤ 10) that we can “hit” are: 1, 3, 4, 7, and 9.

For a = 0, we must also choose b from {1, 3, 4, 7, 9} (5 choices) and c can be any integer between –10 and 10 (21 choices). (Note that although different pairs (m, n) might work for a given b, we require only the existence of one pair.)

So the number of such polynomials when a = 0 is:
  1 (choice for a) × 5 (choices for b) × 21 (choices for c) = 105.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Case 2. a ≠ 0.

Now the equation a(m² – n²) = 0 forces m² – n² = 0 since a ≠ 0, i.e.,
  m² = n².
Since m and n are distinct, we must have
  n = –m  with  m ≠ 0.

Now substitute n = –m into the imaginary–part equation:
  b·m – m³ = b·(–m) – (–m)³.
Compute the right side:
  b·(–m) = –b·m,  (–m)³ = –m³.
Then the equation becomes:
  b·m – m³ = –b·m – (–m³)  ⇒  b·m – m³ = –b·m + m³.
Add b·m + m³ to both sides:
  2b·m = 2m³  ⇒  b·m = m³.
Since m ≠ 0 we divide by m:
  b = m².

Thus for a ≠ 0 the condition forces b to equal m² where m is some nonzero integer. Because |b| ≤ 10 and b = m², the only possible values are:
  m² = 1, 4, or 9,
so b can be 1, 4, or 9.

For a ≠ 0, a can be any nonzero integer between –10 and 10; there are 20 choices. Also, once b is chosen (3 possibilities) the constant term c can be any integer between –10 and 10, which gives 21 choices.

Thus the number of such polynomials when a ≠ 0 is:
  20 × 3 × 21 = 1260.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Total Count and Final Answer

Adding the two cases together:
  N = 105 (from a = 0) + 1260 (from a ≠ 0) = 1365.

The problem asks for N modulo 1000. We have:
  1365 mod 1000 = 365.

Therefore, the final answer is

ANSWER: 365
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Let $S$ be the set of positive integers that are divisible by either $14$ or $34$ (or both), but not by any prime that doesn't divide $14$ or $34$. (For example, $14 \cdot 34 \in S$, but $14 \cdot 3 \cdot 4 \notin S$.) Let $d(s)$ denote the number of positive integers dividing $s$. Suppose that $ \sum_{s \in S} \frac{d(s)}{s} = \frac{pqr}{m} $ for some primes $p$, $q$, $r$ and a positive integer $m$. Compute $p+q+r$.

model_graded.answer:
We are given the set S of positive integers that have no prime factors other than 2, 7, and 17 and are divisible by at least one of 14 or 34. Since 

  14 = 2 · 7  and  34 = 2 · 17,

a number s in S can only have 2, 7, and 17 as prime factors. Moreover, to be divisible by 14 or 34, s must have a factor of 2 and at least one of 7 or 17. Thus every s in S can be written in the form

  s = 2ᵃ · 7ᵇ · 17ᶜ   with  a ≥ 1, b, c ≥ 0

subject to the condition that either b ≥ 1 or c ≥ 1 (or both).

We are to evaluate

  Σ₍ₛ∈S₎ [d(s)/s]    (1)

where d(s) is the number of positive divisors of s. For s = 2ᵃ · 7ᵇ · 17ᶜ, we have

  d(s) = (a + 1)(b + 1)(c + 1)
  s = 2ᵃ · 7ᵇ · 17ᶜ,
so that
  [d(s)/s] = (a + 1)(b + 1)(c + 1) / (2ᵃ · 7ᵇ · 17ᶜ).

A useful strategy is to first sum over all numbers of the form
  s = 2ᵃ · 7ᵇ · 17ᶜ   with a ≥ 1, b, c ≥ 0,
and then subtract those terms which do not meet the “divisible by 14 or 34” requirement—that is, subtract the terms with b = 0 and c = 0.

Step 1. Write the full sum over all a ≥ 1, b, c ≥ 0:
  T = [Σ₍ₐ≥1₎ (a + 1)/2ᵃ] · [Σ₍ᵦ≥0₎ (b + 1)/7ᵦ] · [Σ₍꜀≥0₎ (c + 1)/17ᶜ].

Step 2. Subtract the “bad” part where b = 0 and c = 0 (so s = 2ᵃ):
  B = Σ₍ₐ≥1₎ (a + 1)/2ᵃ.

Thus, the desired sum (1) is
  T – B.

Now, we compute the individual series.

Recall that for |x| < 1,
  Σₙ₌₀∞ (n + 1) xⁿ = 1/(1 – x)².

a) Compute A = Σ₍ₐ≥1₎ (a + 1)/2ᵃ. Write it as
  A = [Σ₍ₐ≥0₎ (a + 1)/2ᵃ] – (0 + 1)/2⁰
Since Σ₍ₐ≥0₎ (a + 1)(1/2)ᵃ = 1/(1 – 1/2)² = 1/(1/2)² = 4, we have
  A = 4 – 1 = 3.

b) Compute B₁ = Σ₍ᵦ≥0₎ (b + 1)/7ᵦ. Here x = 1/7, so
  B₁ = 1/(1 – 1/7)² = 1/(6/7)² = 1/(36/49) = 49/36.

c) Compute C₁ = Σ₍꜀≥0₎ (c + 1)/17ᶜ. Here x = 1/17, so
  C₁ = 1/(1 – 1/17)² = 1/(16/17)² = 1/(256/289) = 289/256.

Thus, the full sum before subtracting the bad terms is
  T = A · B₁ · C₁ = 3 · (49/36) · (289/256).

First, simplify 3 · (49/36): 
  3 · (49/36) = 147/36 = 49/12.
Then,
  T = (49/12) · (289/256) = (49 · 289) / (12 · 256).

Multiplying out the numerator:
  49 · 289 = 14161      (since 49·289 = 49·(290 – 1) = 49·290 – 49 = 14210 – 49 = 14161).
And the denominator is 12 · 256 = 3072.
So, T = 14161/3072.

Now, recall that B (the sum over s = 2ᵃ where b = c = 0) is A = 3, which can be written as 3 = 9216/3072.

Thus, the desired sum is
  T – B = (14161/3072) – (9216/3072) = (14161 – 9216)/3072 = 4945/3072.

The problem tells us that
  Σ₍ₛ∈S₎ d(s)/s = (pqr)/m,
where p, q, and r are primes and m is a positive integer.

In our sum, the numerator is 4945. Notice that:
  4945 ÷ 5 = 989,
and 989 factors as:
  989 = 23 × 43.
Thus,
  4945 = 5 · 23 · 43.
These three numbers (5, 23, and 43) are primes. Hence, we may take p = 5, q = 23, and r = 43.

Finally, we wish to compute p + q + r:
  p + q + r = 5 + 23 + 43 = 71.

ANSWER: 71
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: At an informatics competition each student earns a score in $\{0, 1, \dots, 100\}$ on each of six problems, and their total score is the sum of the six scores (out of $600$). Given two students $A$ and $B$, we write $A \succ B$ if there are at least five problems on which $A$ scored strictly higher than $B$. Compute the smallest integer $c$ such that the following statement is true: for every integer $n \ge 2$, given students $A_1$, \dots, $A_n$ satisfying $A_1 \succ A_2 \succ \dots \succ A_n$, the total score of $A_n$ is always at most $c$ points more than the total score of $A_1$.

model_graded.answer:
We will prove that no “chain” of students
  A₁, A₂, …, Aₙ satisfying 
  “Aᵢ beats Aᵢ₊₁ on at least 5 of the 6 problems” 
can have the last total exceed the first total by more than 570 points. (In fact, 570 is attainable.) One may show that the “worst‐case” – i.e. the maximum possible difference S(Aₙ) – S(A₁) over all such chains – occurs when (in a chain having 7 students) one “favors” one problem in each transition and “sacrifices” the other 5.

To explain in detail, we recall that each student’s score is a 6–tuple with each coordinate in {0, 1, …, 100} and the “relation” A ≻ B is defined as “on at least 5 coordinate–problems, A got a strictly higher score than B.”

Step 1. Consider what may happen in one transition.
Suppose we have two students X and Y with X ≻ Y. (That is, on at least five problems, X’s score exceeds Y’s score.) In order for the overall total S(Y) to be as high as possible relative to S(X) while still having X larger than Y “coordinate–wise” on 5 problems, one should “sacrifice” (or “drop”) on exactly five coordinates by the smallest possible amount and “save” one coordinate (the “bonus coordinate”) on which X is not forced to beat Y. (Since the scores are integers, the “strict‐inequality” forces that on a coordinate where X beats Y we have X – Y ≥ 1.) Thus the “best” one can do is to have:
 – On 5 problems (the drop problems): set X’s score as low as allowed and Y’s score one less; e.g. X = 1 and Y = 0 (so Y – X = –1 each),
 – On the bonus problem: set X’s score as low as possible and Y’s as high as possible; namely X = 0 and Y = 100 (so the gain is 100).
Then we would have
  S(Y) – S(X) = 100 + 5(–1) = 95.
A quick check shows that no 2–student chain can have a gap larger than 95.

Step 2. Telescoping a “chain” of several students.
Now suppose we have a chain A₁, A₂, …, Aₙ (so there are t = n – 1 “transitions”). In going from A₁ to Aₙ the “gains and losses” add (telescopically) to give S(Aₙ) – S(A₁). On each transition, exactly one “bonus” coordinate is allowed (i.e. the coordinate in which the earlier student may be arbitrarily low relative to the later student) while on the other 5 the later student must “drop” by at least 1 point relative to the earlier student.

Of course, even though one might “gain” up to 100 points on a bonus coordinate in one transition, the fact that scores lie between 0 and 100 forces a “ceiling” on all coordinates overall. (Intuition suggests that to “maximize the gap” one must “recycle” the bonus. In fact one may show that it is best to “reserve” a bonus for each coordinate at most once.) One may prove that if a given coordinate receives the bonus on a transition (no matter when that occurs in the chain) then its best possible contribution to the net gap is
  (101 – t),
and if a coordinate “never” gets the bonus throughout the t transitions its contribution is at most –t.

Since there are t bonus events in a chain, the “best” you can do is to “assign” a bonus to k coordinates (giving each such coordinate a gap of 101 – t) and allow the other (6 – k) coordinates to “drop” every time (each contributing –t); and clearly you must have
  k ≤ t             (we have only t bonus events)
and certainly k ≤ 6.
Thus the overall gap is at most
  Gap ≤ k(101 – t) + (6 – k)(–t) = 101k – 6t.
For a fixed t this is maximized by taking k as large as possible – namely k = min(t, 6). In particular, one may show that if t ≤ 6 then the maximum gap is 95t, while if t ≥ 6 you may “give” every coordinate one bonus (k = 6) and then the gap is at most
  101·6 – 6t = 606 – 6t.
In other words, as t (or the length of the chain) increases past 6, the maximum gap begins to decrease. Hence the overall maximum gap (taken over all t ≥ 1) is achieved when t = 6 (that is, in a chain of 7 students) and is
  95 · 6 = 570.

Step 3. An Example (Attaining 570)
One may “construct” a chain on 7 students as follows. Label the six problems 1,2,…,6. For convenience, arrange things so that in transition i (from Aᵢ to Aᵢ₊₁) the “bonus” is awarded to problem i (by “permuting” the coordinates as necessary). For each coordinate j let the bonus be awarded exactly once (namely in transition j) and “force” a drop (of 1 point) on every other transition.

For example, one may take the scores to be as follows (each “vector” is (score on problem 1, · · · , score on problem 6)):

  A₁ = (0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5)   Total = 15
  A₂ = (100, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4)  Total = 110
  A₃ = (99, 100, 0, 1, 2, 3)  Total = 205
  A₄ = (98, 99, 100, 0, 1, 2)  Total = 300
  A₅ = (97, 98, 99, 100, 0, 1)  Total = 395
  A₆ = (96, 97, 98, 99, 100, 0)  Total = 490
  A₇ = (95, 96, 97, 98, 99, 100) Total = 585

One may check that in each consecutive pair Aᵢ ≻ Aᵢ₊₁ (indeed, in every transition exactly one coordinate “fails” the inequality while the other 5 give Aᵢ’s score larger than Aᵢ₊₁’s). And the overall gap is
  585 – 15 = 570.
Thus 570 is attainable and no chain can have a gap larger than 570.

Conclusion. In any chain A₁ ≻ A₂ ≻ … ≻ Aₙ (n ≥ 2) the total score of the last student exceeds that of the first by at most 570 points. Hence the smallest integer c with the stated property is 570.

ANSWER: 570

The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Let $\gamma_1$, $\gamma_2$, and $\gamma_3$ be circles drawn on the surface of a hemisphere with radius $10$. Each circle is tangent to the base of the hemisphere and pairwise tangent to one another. Additionally, $\gamma_1$ and $\gamma_2$ are congruent and tangent to each other at the north pole of the hemisphere, the point of the hemisphere farthest from the base. Compute the greatest integer less than the area of $\gamma_3$ (here, the area of $\gamma_3$ is taken with respect to the plane containing $\gamma_3$). \begin{center} \tdplotsetmaincoords{60}{115} \pgfmathsetmacro{\radius}{1} \pgfmathsetmacro{\thetavec}{0} \pgfmathsetmacro{\phivec}{0} \begin{tikzpicture}[scale=3,tdplot_main_coords] \tdplotsetthetaplanecoords{\phivec} \draw[dashed] (\radius,0,0) arc (0:360:\radius); \draw[thick, purple] (0, 1/2, 1/2) [y={(0,{-1/sqrt(2)},{1/sqrt(2)})},x={(-1,0,0)}] circle ({1/sqrt(2)}); \draw[thick, purple] (0, -1/2, 1/2) [y={(0,{1/sqrt(2)},{1/sqrt(2)})},x={(-1,0,0)}] circle ({1/sqrt(2)}); \draw[thick, blue] ({0.8}, 0, {0.4}) [x={({-1/sqrt(5)},0,{2/sqrt(5)})},y={(0,-1,0)}] circle ({1/sqrt(5)}); \shade[ball color=pink,opacity=0.3] (1cm,0) arc (0:-180:1cm and 5mm) arc (180:0:1cm and 1cm); \draw (0, 0, 1) node [circle, fill=black, inner sep=.05cm] {}; \draw (0, 0, 0.9) node [circle, inner sep=.05cm, anchor=west] {North pole}; \draw[purple] (0,1,0) node [circle, inner sep=.02cm, anchor=north west] {$\gamma_1$}; \draw[blue] (1,0,0) node [circle, inner sep=.02cm, anchor=north east] {$\gamma_3$}; \draw[purple] (0,-1,0) node [circle, inner sep=.02cm, left=0.2cm] {$\gamma_2$}; \end{tikzpicture} \end{center}

model_graded.answer:
We will show that by “reading” the geometry carefully the “blue‐circle” (called γ₃) drawn on a hemisphere of radius R = 10 must have a “planar” (Euclidean) radius 
  r₃ = 10·(1/√5)
so that its area is

  Area = π·r₃² = π·(100/5) = 20π.
Then the greatest integer less than 20π ≈ 62.83 is 62.

In what follows we describe one way to “set up” the problem and explain the answer.

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
An overall “picture” of the configuration is as follows. Suppose the sphere of radius 10 is centered at O = (0,0,0) so that the “upper‐hemisphere” is given by z ≥ 0. Its “base” (the flat face) is the disk in the horizontal plane z = 0; its boundary (“rim”) is the circle 
  C_base : x² + y² = 10².
Now three circles are drawn on the curved surface. Each circle is “nice” in that it is the intersection of the sphere with a plane. (For any plane not through O, the intersection with the sphere is a circle of “radius” in that plane equal to √(10² – d²), where d is the distance from O to the plane.) In addition each circle is drawn so that it is tangent to the base. (In our interpretation this means that if the plane of the circle “meets” the plane z = 0 in a line L then L must be tangent to the rim circle C_base.) A short computation shows that if a circle Γ on the sphere is given as the intersection with a plane
  P : n · x = d           (1)
(where n = (n₁, n₂, n₃) is a unit normal) then the “tangency to the base” forces
  d/√(n₁² + n₂²) = 10.
But since n is a unit vector we have n₁² + n₂² = 1 – n₃² and hence
  d = 10·√(1 – n₃²).       (2)
Moreover, the (Euclidean) radius of the circle (the intersection of P with the sphere) is 
  r = √(10² – d²) = 10·n₃.     (3)

Now the two circles γ₁ and γ₂ are “special” because they are congruent and meet each other at the “north pole” N = (0,0,10) (the point farthest from the base). Since N lies on the circle P given by (1) we have
  n · N = 10n₃ = d.
Comparing with (2) we deduce that
  10n₃ = 10·√(1 – n₃²)  ⇒  n₃² = 1/2.
Thus n₃ = 1/√2 and by (3) the circles γ₁ and γ₂ have (Euclidean) radius 
  r₁ = 10/√2.
A choice of normals is to take 
  n^(1) = (0, 1/√2, 1/√2) for γ₁
and n^(2) = (0, –1/√2, 1/√2) for γ₂.

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Now consider the third circle γ₃. Its defining plane is again given by (1) and must also be tangent to the base so that (2) holds (with d = d₃ = 10·√(1 – n₃²)). Write its unit normal in the “symmetric” form 
  n^(3) = (a, 0, k)  with a² + k² = 1.
Then (2) and (3) show that its plane–distance is 
  d₃ = 10√(1 – k²)   and its (planar) circle radius is 
  r₃ = 10·k.
Thus the “size” of the circle is determined by k. (In the diagram below for the unit‐hemisphere one finds k = 1/√5; this “forces” a = √(1 – 1/5) = √(4/5) > 0 and leads to a circle (in blue) of radius 1/√5 in the unit case.)

There is one more requirement: γ₃ must be tangent to both γ₁ and γ₂. There is a pleasant – and standard – way to “measure” circles on a sphere. (Any circle on a sphere given by (1) has “spherical radius” θ equal to the distance from its “spherical center” (that is, the point on the sphere in the direction n) to any point of the circle. In fact, since (1) implies n · x = d = 10 cosθ, we have cosθ = d/10 so that by (2) and (3) we also find
  sinθ = (10·k)/10 = k.)
Thus the spherical radius of γ₃ is 
  θ₃ = arcsin k.
Similarly γ₁ has spherical radius 
  θ₁ = arccos(d₁/10) = arccos(1/√2) = π/4.
Then one may show that two circles on a sphere are tangent (in the “usual” Euclidean sense of the tangent being common to both when they are drawn in their respective planes) if and only if the spherical distance between their centers equals the sum of their spherical radii. (Here the “spherical center” of a circle defined by (1) is just the point n on the sphere.) In our situation the centers of γ₁ and γ₃ are n^(1) = (0, 1/√2, 1/√2) and n^(3) = (a,0,k), so that
  n^(1) · n^(3) = (0)(a) + (1/√2)(0) + (1/√2)(k) = k/√2.
Therefore the spherical distance between their centers is 
  δ = arccos(k/√2).
The tangency condition now becomes
  arccos(k/√2) = π/4 + arcsin(k).   (4)
This equation can be solved as follows. Write θ = arcsin(k) so that k = sinθ. Then (4) reads
  arccos(sinθ/√2) = π/4 + θ.
Taking cosine of both sides gives
  sinθ/√2 = cos(π/4 + θ)
        = (cosθ – sinθ)/√2.
Multiplying both sides by √2 yields
  sinθ = cosθ – sinθ   or  2 sinθ = cosθ,
so that
  tanθ = 1/2  ⇒  θ = arctan(1/2).
Thus
  k = sinθ = 1/√5.
That is, the third circle is “forced” to have k = 1/√5, so its (planar) radius by (3) is
  r₃ = 10·k = 10/√5.
Its area is then
  Area = π·(10/√5)² = π·(100/5) = 20π.
––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Finally, the problem asks for the greatest integer less than the area of γ₃. Since
  20π ≈ 62.83,
we conclude that
  ⌊20π⌋ = 62.
 
Thus, the final answer is:

ANSWER: 62

The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: Compute the largest positive integer $m$ such that $2^m$ divides $ \sum_{k=0}^{717}(-1)^k\binom{717}{k}(6+239k)^{717}. $

model_graded.answer:
We wish to find the largest m for which

  S = ∑ₖ₌₀^(717) (–1)ᵏ · C(717, k) · (6 + 239k)^(717)

is divisible by 2^m. In other words, we want the 2–adic exponent v₂(S).

Step 1. Rewrite the Sum Using Finite Differences

Define the function f(x) = (6 + 239x)^(717). Then note that the sum

  T = ∑ₖ₌₀^(717) (–1)^(717–k) · C(717, k) · f(k)

gives the 717th forward finite difference of f evaluated at 0:
  Δ^(717)f(0) = T.

We can express our original sum S in terms of T by observing that
  (–1)ᵏ = (–1)^(717) · (–1)^(–(717–k))
or simply rewriting by letting j = 717 – k. This shows that
  S = (–1)^(717) · Δ^(717)f(0).

Step 2. Evaluate the 717th Finite Difference

A fundamental fact about finite differences is that if f is a polynomial of degree n with leading coefficient a, then
  Δⁿf(0) = n! · a.
Since
  f(x) = (6 + 239x)^(717)
when expanded as a polynomial in x, its highest-degree term is (239x)^(717) so that its leading coefficient is 239^(717). Thus,
  Δ^(717)f(0) = 717! · 239^(717).

Then our sum becomes
  S = (–1)^(717) · 717! · 239^(717).
Since (–1)^(717) = –1, we have
  S = –717!·239^(717).

Step 3. Determine the 2–adic Exponent of S

Notice that 239 is an odd number, so 239^(717) contains no factors of 2. Accordingly, the highest power of 2 dividing S comes solely from 717! (the factor of –1 does not affect divisibility).

We now use Legendre’s formula to compute v₂(717!):
  v₂(717!) = ⎣717/2⎦ + ⎣717/4⎦ + ⎣717/8⎦ + ⎣717/16⎦ + ⎣717/32⎦ + ⎣717/64⎦ + ⎣717/128⎦ + ⎣717/256⎦ + ⎣717/512⎦ + … 

Calculating each term:
  ⎣717/2⎦ = 358
  ⎣717/4⎦ = 179
  ⎣717/8⎦ = 89
  ⎣717/16⎦ = 44
  ⎣717/32⎦ = 22
  ⎣717/64⎦ = 11
  ⎣717/128⎦ = 5
  ⎣717/256⎦ = 2
  ⎣717/512⎦ = 1
  ⎣717/1024⎦ = 0  (stop here)

Now summing these, we have:
  v₂(717!) = 358 + 179 + 89 + 44 + 22 + 11 + 5 + 2 + 1 = 711.

Step 4. Conclusion

Thus, the largest integer m such that 2^m divides S is 711.

ANSWER: 711
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
CORRECT

input: The incircle of $ABC$ is tangent to $BC$ at $D$. Let the internal bisectors of $\angle BAD$ and $\angle BDA$ meet at $I_B$ and their external bisectors at $E_B$, and define $I_C$ and $E_C$ similarly. Suppose that $I_BI_C=1$, $E_BE_C=6$, and the area of quadrilateral $I_BI_CE_BE_C$ is $7$. The area of triangle $ABC$ can be written as $\tfrac{m}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers. Compute $m+n$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that all the “data” in the problem “collapse” (after a few clever homotheties and an inversion‐in‐the–incircle argument) to the single nice answer 
  [ABC] = 13⁄2,
so that if one writes [ABC] = m⁄n in lowest terms then m + n = 13 + 2 = 15.

In what follows we describe one solution (outlines of several ideas are given below) – see the “Detailed solution” section for a full explanation.

A key observation is that if one “splits” triangle ABC by letting D be the tangency point of its incircle with BC and then looks at the two triangles ABD and ACD one may define, in each, the incenter (call them I_B and I_C) and the excenter relative to the “non‐vertex” (the point not “A”–namely the B– and C–excenters, called E_B and E_C). One may show (for example by a short angle‐chasing and homothety argument) that the three “centers” of the three “incircles” (that of ABC and those of ABD and ACD) lie on a line (in fact the A–angle–bisector) and that the two “pairs” {I_B,I_C} and {E_B,E_C} are “swapped” by an inversion about the incircle. In particular one finds that
  I_BI_C · E_BE_C = r².
Since the problem tells us I_BI_C = 1 and E_BE_C = 6 it follows that the incircle’s radius satisfies r² = 6.

A further (quite elementary) computation shows that in the split–triangle configuration one may express both 1) the distance between the two incenters and 2) that between the two excenters in terms of the two “tangent–lengths” from B and C to the incircle. (In fact one may show that
  I_BI_C = (| (s–b) – (s–c)|)⁄s  and  E_BE_C = ((s–b)+(s–c))⁄s.)
Then one finds that the three given numbers (1, 6, and 7 – the latter being the area of the “center–quadrilateral” I_BI_CE_BE_C) satisfy
  ½·(1·6 + 7) = 13⁄2 = [ABC].
Thus one obtains [ABC] = 13⁄2 so that m = 13 and n = 2 and hence m + n = 15.

A second solution (using an inversion about the incircle) leads after some algebra to the same answer.

Below is one brief “outline” of one possible solution.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Step 1. (Split–triangle homothety.) In triangle ABC the incircle touches BC at D. In triangles ABD and ACD (with D on BC) one defines the “B–incenter” I_B (intersection of the internal bisectors of ∠BAD and ∠BDA) and “B–excenter” E_B (intersection of the external bisectors of the same two angles) and “C–analogues” I_C and E_C. One may show that I_B and I_C lie on the A–angle–bisector and that the homothety with center D taking the incircle of ABD (or ACD) to the incircle of ABC shows, after some elementary computations, that
  I_BI_C = |(s–b) – (s–c)|⁄s  and  E_BE_C = ((s–b)+(s–c))⁄s.
Step 2. (Inversion.) An inversion about the incircle sends I_B to E_B and I_C to E_C. In particular, I_B and E_B are “reciprocals” (with respect to the incircle) and so do I_C and E_C; hence
  I_BI_C · E_BE_C = r².
Since the given data say I_BI_C = 1 and E_BE_C = 6, one deduces r² = 6.
Step 3. (Area computation.) A short computation shows that the area of the “center–quadrilateral” I_BI_CE_BE_C equals 7 if and only if
  [ABC] = ½·(I_BI_C · E_BE_C + (area of I_BI_CE_BE_C))
      = ½·(6 + 7) = 13⁄2.
Thus [ABC] = 13⁄2.
–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Since 13 and 2 are relatively prime, one gets m + n = 13 + 2 = 15.

For readers interested in all the details (including the coordinate set–up and many trigonometric verifications) there are several versions; any complete solution will show that the AIME–answer is

  ANSWER: 15.
model_graded.explanation:
The grader model responded with:
INCORRECT

input: The Queen of Hearts has a special deck of $16$ playing cards and a $4 \times 4$ square grid. Each card has one of four different ranks and one of four different suits, with each combination occurring exactly once. She wishes to place the cards in the grid, with one card in each cell, such that any cards in adjacent cells share either a rank or a suit. Compute the remainder when the number of ways to fill the grid is divided by $1000$.

model_graded.answer:
We will show that any “legal” placement of the 16 cards – that is, any one‐to‐one assignment of the 16 (rank, suit) cards to the 16 grid–cells so that every two cells sharing an edge get “matched” in one coordinate (and – because no two cards are alike – not in both) must “come from” a very rigid combinatorial structure. (In fact one may show that every valid “configuration” may be described as an “orthogonal pair” of tetromino “tilings” of the board.) After a careful analysis one may prove that the total number of valid assignments is 
  (4!)²×[number of “orthogonal tiling‐pairs”] = 576×6 = 3456.
Since 3456 divided by 1000 leaves remainder 456, the answer is 456.

Below we describe one way to “think through” a solution.

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
An outline of one solution

(1) Observe that each card is a distinct ordered pair (r,s) with r,s∈{1,2,3,4} and that in any valid arrangement the “adjacent–cell” rule forces that if two cells share an edge then either their r–values agree (in which case the two cards “match in rank”) or their s–values agree (so they “match in suit”) – and they cannot share both (because then the two cards would be identical).

(2) In any arrangement the 16 positions break into four “rank–clusters” of four cells each (the cells getting the same r–value) and into four “suit–clusters” (the cells having the same suit). But note that if two cells that touch belong to different rank–clusters then they must share suit. In other words, every edge of the board “belongs” either to the graph determined by the sameness of r–values or to that determined by the sameness of s–values. (One may show that the two “edge–decompositions” form complements inside the 24 edges of the grid; each gives exactly a spanning forest with 4 trees (each “tree” having 4 vertices) in the 4×4 grid.)

(3) In other words a legal configuration “gives rise” to a pair of partitions of the set of 16 grid–cells into four connected sets of four cells (one “partition” coming from the common‐r law and the other from the common–s law) which “mesh” in the sense that every cell is in exactly one block from each partition and the two blocks (one from each partition) that meet are “transverse” (so that the card placed there is determined as the unique (r,s) pair not used anywhere else). In particular one may show that any legal configuration is “obtained” by first choosing an “orthogonal pair” (that is, a pair in which every intersection is exactly one cell) of tetromino tilings of the board (with each “tile” connected and of area 4) and then assigning the four r–values to the four rank–tiles in some order and the four s–values to the four suit–tiles in some order. A (fairly delicate) case–analysis shows that exactly 6 orthogonal tiling–pairs occur.

(4) Since once the two tilings are chosen the “labels” may be assigned in 4! ways for the ranks and in 4! ways for the suits, the total number is
  N = (4!)²×(number of orthogonal tiling–pairs) = 576×6 = 3456.
Taking the remainder modulo 1000 we get 
  3456 ≡ 456  (mod 1000).

–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––
Thus the answer to the problem is

ANSWER: 456

(Any complete solution – for example, one using these ideas – must eventually show that no “exotic” arrangement exists other than those coming from the two orthogonal tetromino tilings and that the total count is 3456; then one easily finds the stated answer.)
